Halfway down the Rue Saint-Denis, near the Rue du Petit-Lion, stood a charming old house that let historians reconstruct old Paris. Dilapidated walls displayed timber patterns and plaster cracks. A weather-warped triangular roof jutted over the street, sheltering the entrance and dormer; the upper story used overlapping planks to avoid overburdening the fragile structure.

On a rainy March morning a young man in a cloak stood beneath a shop awning across from the house, studying it with the zeal of an antiquary. The relic of sixteenth-century civic life showed many peculiarities. He ignored the first floor’s narrow boarded windows and focused on the third floor’s modest sash windows, poorly made and dimmed by green-tinted glass. Occasionally he glanced down at the shop, where small details amused him.

A large wooden beam, supported by four pillars, bore layers of paint and a comical depiction of a cat playing racket, which pleased the young man. The caricatured cat, racket in paw and an oversized ball, had grown more absurd with age. The faded sign read “Guillaume, Successor to Master Chevrel,” a remnant of a whimsical past when such signs lured customers.

Dressed in a stylish cloak and white silk stockings, he looked as if he had just come from a wedding or ball; white gloves and disheveled hair hinted at recent festivities. Despite market gardeners hurrying by, the street was unusually still. His striking features—pale face set off by a dazzling white cravat and expressive eyes—drew the attention of the shopkeepers at the “Cat and Racket.”

Suddenly three apprentices appeared at the dormer window, laughing and teasing him, playfully sprinkling him with shaving cream; he shrugged off his annoyance. Then a young girl’s face appeared at the window, her beauty like a Raphael Virgin—innocent and unembarrassed. On seeing him she withdrew and the window slammed shut, leaving him disappointed.

Meanwhile the shutters of the “Cat and Racket” opened and a manservant hung the cloth sign reading “Guillaume, successor to Chevrel.” The shop’s exterior, guarded by iron bars, hid packages wrapped in brown linen. Despite its old-fashioned look, Monsieur Guillaume was known for having the best-stocked establishment among Parisian cloth merchants and a reputation for honesty; he secured profits without flattery or costly gifts, though fellow traders warned against his notary, notorious for heavy discounts.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The old merchant stood at his shop threshold on the Rue Saint-Denis, awakening to the morning bustle. Monsieur Guillaume, in traditional attire, watched a curious stranger inspect his business. His patient, shrewd face reflected years of experience and a staunch attachment to old customs. He ran a strict household and maintained a rigorous regime for his three apprentices, who feared his vigilance.

As the apprentices began their day, Guillaume noticed the stranger’s anxious glances and suspected ulterior motives. The eldest stepped outside and thought he saw Mademoiselle Augustine leaving in haste; he hailed a cab, which eased the apprentices’ worries about a prank played on her.

Guillaume reprimanded them for idleness, recalling how he had worked harder in his youth. Though the apprentices came from reasonably comfortable backgrounds and paid for their board, he demanded a strong work ethic, contrasting his order and economy with modern shops where youths expected quick wealth. The shop itself was meticulously kept, a testament to his values. The apprentices were not permitted to join the family at dessert, a sign of Madame Guillaume’s frugality, though they still attended Mass with the family.

The household was governed by strict traditions set by Madame Guillaume. The elder daughter, Virginie, mirrored her mother’s piety and sternness; Augustine, eighteen, was delicate and charming but overshadowed by maternal expectations. Their lives centered on domestic duties with little time outside, so they treasured family gatherings and the occasional visitor.

Augustine secretly yearned for more than their cloistered existence, feeding a desire for passion with romantic novels. That longing deepened Joseph Lebas’s affection for her; Joseph, the eldest apprentice, earned a modest salary after years of service. Guillaume regarded Joseph as a suitable match for Virginie, though Joseph’s true feelings were for Augustine. Guillaume mused about his daughters’ futures in a changing world where suitable suitors might become scarce.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
From that day the old merchant, grieving his eldest daughter's decline, remembered marrying Mademoiselle Chevrel under circumstances resembling Joseph Lebas and Virginie. Marrying his daughter would settle a sacred debt—repay an orphan for a past kindness. Joseph Lebas, thirty-three, noted the fifteen-year gap between him and Augustine and Monsieur Guillaume’s rule that the elder daughter must marry first. The warm-hearted, tall assistant suffered in silence.

To understand, rewind months before the story begins. One evening a young painter paused outside the darkened shop of the Cat and Racket, seized by a scene any artist would covet. The unlit shop seemed cavernous; a dining room lit by a hanging lamp cast a warm, Dutch-like glow. The family—Augustine and a chubby maid among them—formed a modest, striking tableau. Fresh from Rome and weary of Italian grandeur, the painter hungered for simple nature. He had sought the meditative maiden of his pictures; the living scene stirred deep admiration. Augustine, pensive and scarcely touched by food, was illuminated by the lamp into something almost angelic. Overwhelmed, he left unable to eat or sleep.

The next day he retreated to his studio to capture that spell. He visited the Cat and Racket repeatedly, sometimes in disguise, to study Augustine. For eight months he devoted himself to love and art, neglecting friends and previous pursuits. One morning his friend Girodet pierced his isolation to ask about his Salon submission. The artist produced two masterpieces; Girodet, recognizing his friend's passion, embraced him.

“You are in love?” Girodet asked; the artist nodded. They agreed great work springs from such feeling. Girodet warned against showing the pieces at the Salon, doubting the public would grasp their depth. Still, the works were exhibited and caused a sensation. The Interior sparked a genre shift, while the portrait became unforgettable, earning acclaim and offers the artist refused.

These public events felt remote from the monastic life on Rue Saint-Denis. During a visit to Madame Guillaume, the notary’s wife mentioned the exhibition, rousing Augustine’s curiosity. With her cousin’s help she obtained permission to visit the Louvre. Seeing the portrait, she trembled: it was herself, and she felt terror mixed with excitement. The artist stood nearby and whispered, “You see how love has inspired me,” leaving her stunned.

Desperate to escape the crowd, Augustine urged her cousin to leave, but they were carried toward the celebrated painting. Overcome, she wept at the sight. In a brief, silent exchange she gestured to the artist; he acknowledged her with a nod. That moment kindled both guilt and exhilaration. The Salon’s chaos and the artist's gaze intoxicated her; new emotions surged where none had been before.

As they left, the painter glowed with joy; Augustine, caught in the whirlwind, realized she was loved. She feigned a headache to avoid discussing the exhibition, yet her mother’s plans to visit the Salon filled her with dread. That night she reflected, feeling a void in her mundane life and longing for the elegance she had always desired. The thought of being the wife of a genius filled her with hope despite her modest upbringing. The next day her parents returned from the Salon disappointed: the painter had removed the two pictures, and Madame Guillaume had lost her shawl. Still, news of the paintings’ disappearance and the lost shawl revealed to Augustine a sensitivity she had not known she possessed.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
On the morning after a ball, Theodore de Sommervieux, who had won Augustine’s heart, found it hard to declare his love because of the household’s constraints. Augustine sat in the counting-house between Mademoiselle Virginie and Madame Guillaume and was closely watched by her mother, making any approach difficult. Fearing a rival among the assistants, Theodore bribed a maid to arrange discreet exchanges and they agreed to meet during Mass at Saint-Leu.

Meanwhile the Cat and Racket household was absorbed in stock-taking, with Monsieur Guillaume overseeing inventory and accounts. The family busied themselves verifying goods; Augustine’s absent-mindedness passed unnoticed. After weeks of work the stock-taking ended and the family enjoyed an evening out to see Cendrillon while the apprentices celebrated elsewhere.

The next morning Guillaume resumed his routine and summoned his assistant Joseph Lebas to discuss the profitable results. Impressed by Joseph’s contributions, Guillaume proposed making him a partner. Overwhelmed, Joseph confessed his love for Augustine; Guillaume first misunderstood, thinking he meant Virginie. Once corrected, Guillaume agreed to consider Joseph’s feelings and suggested Joseph accompany Augustine to Mass.

At breakfast tension rose as Madame Guillaume and Virginie exchanged glances at a nervous Joseph. Madame Guillaume, unaware of the budding romance between Augustine and Theodore, chastised Augustine for inattention at church and sent her to her room, prompting a heated discussion between her parents about their daughter’s future.

Summoned before them, Augustine confessed she loved Theodore de Sommervieux, emphasizing his respectable background. Monsieur Guillaume voiced concern about the uncertainties of artists, but Augustine defended Theodore as wealthy and of good family, which intrigued her father. Then Madame Roguin arrived with news of Theodore’s recent honors and fortune, advocating for the match. Her persuasive account began to sway parents who had been reluctant about their daughter marrying above her station.

As the day progressed the atmosphere shifted from conflict to acceptance. Monsieur Guillaume informed Joseph of the developments. By evening the family gathered to celebrate the potential union; Theodore presented a painting of the Cat and Racket as a symbol of shared happiness. Monsieur Guillaume consented to the marriage, though he stressed financial prudence and advised Augustine to consult him on money matters.

In the end the lovers found solace in mutual affection, and the household, once tense, embraced the prospect of a joyful future together, relieved and hopeful.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
A few months after a memorable Sunday, the high altar of Saint-Leu saw two contrasting weddings. Augustine and Theodore were radiant; Virginie, dressed simply, followed her sister. Monsieur Guillaume had tried to have Virginie married before Augustine, but the priests focused on the more elegant bride. Neighbors praised Virginie for making a sensible match, while others envied Augustine for marrying an artist of rank. An old fan-maker warned that such extravagance would lead to ruin, and Monsieur Guillaume felt justified in his cautious approach to settlements. After the ball and supper, Monsieur and Madame Guillaume returned home, Monsieur and Madame Lebas to their old residence, and the joyous artist carried Augustine to their well-adorned apartment.

Their first year together was blissful. Theodore showered Augustine with affection; she, lost in love, believed her devotion would always charm him and often revealed a naïveté about the world. As the year passed, Theodore returned to work—especially with a child expected—and reconnected with friends, frequenting the Duchesse de Carigliano's gatherings for the social validation of being seen with a beautiful wife.

Augustine enjoyed society but soon realized her lack of sophistication compared to Theodore's artistic circle. Her admiration for his work was genuine but superficial, and a disconnect grew. Theodore, once captivated, began to feel strained by their differing intellectual realms. He hoped the studio environment might inspire Augustine, but she struggled to adapt; her innocence clashed with the artists’ freer manners at gatherings.

Gradually Theodore's affection waned and Augustine felt the weight of his indifference. Despite trying to fulfill her duties and reignite his love, she sensed a widening distance. Three years into their marriage, she became consumed by sorrow. The artist's increasing enthusiasm for the Duchesse only deepened her despair, as she feared being supplanted by a more experienced woman.

Determined to bridge the gap, Augustine resolved to educate herself in poetry and art. Her attempts, however earnest, met frustration: she could not cultivate the natural wit and ease Theodore admired, and her efforts often backfired, leaving her feeling more inadequate.

In desperation she sought refuge with her family, returning to her childhood home where Virginie and brother-in-law Joseph Lebas ran their drapery with a steady contentment that contrasted sharply with her turmoil. Joseph's practical, businesslike advice only heightened her conflict, showing a different, more mundane kind of happiness that made her envy them yet doubt herself.

Ultimately Augustine returned home burdened by uncertainty, longing for the love she once shared with Theodore, like a patient trying every remedy in hope of healing a fractured marriage.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The elderly couple welcomed their daughter warmly; her visit brightened their monotonous lives. For four years they had drifted, dwelling on past business successes and failures, notably the Lecocq bankruptcy. Monsieur Guillaume checked on his shop, the Cat and Racket, and traded memories with former competitors, while Madame Guillaume, now on the Army's clothing consulting board thanks to her son-in-law, filled their home with extravagant, often tasteless decor. Their nostalgia and stagnation struck Augustine as a stark contrast to her own turbulent experiences, leaving her both proud and appalled at their vacuous existence.

Despite her inner turmoil, Augustine displayed her recent achievements and aired marital grievances, which her parents eagerly absorbed. Madame Guillaume was especially curious about life with Theodore, Augustine's husband, a painter. Augustine mentioned his late nights and unconventional habits, prompting her mother’s disapproval of his lifestyle and profession. The couple’s incredulity grew as Augustine defended Theodore, sparking a heated debate about genius and marital responsibility.

When Augustine hinted at divorce, her parents were shocked into action; Monsieur Guillaume was ready to support her. Overwhelmed by their concern and unable to convey the depth of her suffering, Augustine rejected separation, realizing her pain could scarcely be understood by them. She felt isolated, convinced that only those who had shared similar experiences could truly empathize.

Resolved to win back Theodore’s affection by other means, Augustine decided to consult the Duchess de Carigliano—not to reclaim his heart outright but to learn how to captivate him. Approaching the Duchess’s opulent home, she felt both envy and admiration for its elegance. Meeting the Duchess, Augustine was struck by the woman’s beauty and charm but also sensed rivalry.

The Duchess responded with a mix of sympathy and shrewd counsel, stressing the importance of a composed facade and control when dealing with men of genius. She advised cultivating mystery and not revealing the full measure of one’s affections, framing such guardedness as strategy rather than mere manipulation. Initially disheartened, Augustine absorbed the Duchess’s lessons, recognizing that her love for Theodore might require a different approach.

The Duchess gave Augustine a portrait of herself painted by Theodore, believing it could rekindle his interest. Filled with hope, Augustine resolved to embody the image in the painting. When Theodore returned, she presented the portrait; his reaction was fury and jealousy, igniting a tumultuous confrontation.

The quarrel escalated, exposing the depths of Theodore’s passion and insecurity and culminating in a violent outburst. The aftermath left Augustine shattered; her dreams of mending their marriage lay in ruins. The next morning, her mother found her consumed by despair amid the remnants of the confrontation.

Augustine’s tragic fate was sealed as she succumbed to the pressures of her turbulent life, dying young. Her story stands as a poignant reminder of the struggles women face pursuing love and fulfillment, especially when entangled with the complexities of artistic genius. The inscription on her grave reflects that tragedy, prompting those who knew her to ponder the challenges of balancing passion and stability in relationships.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
###### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###### ###### ###### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ######

############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

The Comte de Fontaine, a loyal royalist from Poitou, had served the Bourbon cause in the La Vendée. Despite noble lineage and sacrifices, he was financially ruined when his estate was confiscated. He married Mademoiselle de Kergarouet, a noblewoman without fortune, and after the second revolution moved to Paris with his large family in search of prospects. Initially disillusioned, he received unexpected honors—a military promotion and royal decorations—that rekindled hopes for favor. He soon discovered, however, that his sacrifices were largely unrecognized and he felt marginalized as new aristocrats rose.

Though he contemplated returning to his estate, the turmoil of 1815 drew him back into service; he became a trusted adviser to Louis XVIII, valued for his wit and political acumen. The Fontaines then began to prosper: his sons obtained important posts and his daughters made advantageous marriages, if primarily into lesser nobility.

Emilie, the youngest, was spoiled and the center of Parisian attention. Her beauty and charm made her much sought-after, yet she remained indifferent, asserting superiority over suitors of lower birth. Despite the Count’s efforts to arrange matches, Emilie’s high standards and caprice provoked family conflict. She dismissed candidates for trivial flaws, repeatedly frustrating his plans.

As the political landscape shifted and the King’s health declined, the Comte intensified efforts to secure a suitable husband for Emilie to cement the family’s status. Her unreasonable expectations produced a string of rejections that left him increasingly desperate and strained relations among siblings and in-laws who were themselves adjusting to new social standing. Through the turmoil he clung to the hope that royal favor and a proper match would validate his sacrifices and restore the family’s honor, yet Emilie’s continued disdain left the Fontaine future uncertain.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Nature had given Emilie de Fontaine the exact features for playing Célimène. Tall and slender, she could adopt dignity or coquettishness at will; a long neck hinted at scorn, fine black hair and arched brows suggested pride. She had perfected feminine gestures that could signify contempt or charm, and a melodious voice that could woo or sharply silence indiscretion. Her alabaster complexion rarely betrayed emotion. Many of the young men she scorned accused her of merely performing; she answered that she inspired desire only to subject it to her caprice.

Monsieur de Fontaine recognized, too late, that adoration had spoiled his daughter. Constant praise had inflated her pride and self-confidence, nurturing the selfishness of a pampered child. Her youthful charms concealed these defects, but her father perceived them and tried to instill practical wisdom. His counsel met caprice and ironic dismissal; his gentle advice slid off her heart like water from marble. He came to think her affection often condescending, like a child demanding attention before returning to play.

Emilie sometimes showed affection for her parents but frequently withdrew, jealous of any love they shared with others, including her siblings. She blamed solitude on fate rather than seeing that happiness comes from within. Though determined to resist her sisters’ marriages, she felt pangs of envy at their contentment. Her unpredictability led her mother to suspect slight madness.

Such pride is common among beautiful young women of wealth, who imagine mothers cannot understand youthful dreams and suspect maternal envy. This breeds secret tears and revolts against perceived tyranny. In such struggles they devise elaborate life plans, picturing a perfect partner to meet exacting criteria; with experience many later find happiness unexpectedly and learn ideals rarely match reality.

Emilie’s conditions for a suitor were stern: he must be a peer of France, a soldier, decorated with an Order so they could be treated with respect. He must also be slender, for she deemed corpulence unromantic. Her disdain for those who failed these standards was plain, and Monsieur de Fontaine feared her eccentricities would invite ridicule and leave her indecisive.

During the winter of Charles X’s reign he redoubled efforts to find matches, hosting gatherings to attract suitors. His reputation as a corrupter of legislative integrity grew, yet he remained earnest. After a season of matchmaking he felt hopeful, only to meet Emilie’s unyielding whims.

One morning he summoned her to discuss her future, arranging his study to present a dignified front. Emilie greeted him with a light kiss lacking warmth. He raised the subject of marriage and urged a wise choice for her happiness. She reminded him of their prior agreement about suitors, then mocked settling for anything less than a peer and found fault with every candidate he suggested.

Exasperated, Monsieur de Fontaine declared he had fulfilled his duty and that the choice now rested with her. He warned that marital happiness depended on mutual respect rather than mere wealth or rank. Emilie, touched by his sincerity, feigned warmth to soothe him, yet her pride endured. At a family gathering she reiterated her refusal to marry unless the man were a peer; her uncle joked that she was waiting for the Duc de Bordeaux to grow up, but she forbade further talk of marriage.

As summer approached the family retired to the country house. Emilie wished to attend the Bal de Sceaux, drawn by the crowd and the anonymity it offered. The ball, lively and mixed in social class, revealed to her that aristocrats mingled with the rest. While watching the festivities she noticed a striking young man who stood apart, radiating elegance and dignity. His presence stirred her imagination and she felt immediate attraction.

She approached him, but he remained aloof. Her exaggerated gestures and laughter failed to win his attention. Frustrated, she followed his gaze and saw him watching another dancer, a pale girl named Clara. Intrigued and piqued, Emilie trailed the pair as they left the ball; though attentive to Clara, the young man twice caught Emilie’s eye, arousing a mix of jealousy and curiosity.

Days later, while riding with her uncle, Emilie met Lady Dudley and Monsieur Vandenesse and discovered the young man she admired was not what she had hoped. Disappointed, she tried to conceal her feelings but remained drawn to him. Her uncle, sensing her agitation, teased about her attachment and promised an introduction, which intensified Emilie’s interest. Fantasies bloomed: the romance of the unknown and the possibility of an ideal future consumed her.

As days passed her infatuation grew, fed by imagination and youthful yearning. She remained unaware of the complexities and compromises that love and marriage would demand, still blinded by her exacting ideals.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The next morning, before Emilie woke, her uncle hurried to Chevreuse and, recognizing the young man he had insulted the day before, greeted him with the polished courtesy of the old court.

“Who would have guessed I’d meet the son or grandson of one of my best friends at seventy-three? I’m a vice-admiral; dueling’s no matter to me. I had too much grog and ran you down. Shake hands; I’d sooner endure a hundred rebuffs from a Longueville than bring his family regret.”

The young man, though reserved, returned the Count’s cordiality and they rode together. The admiral joked about duels, shot a pistol into a birch to show he feared none, and praised the young man when he fired close to the mark. On the road he quizzed him about trifles that marked a gentleman—debts, pleasures, heraldry—teasing about mistresses and extravagance. Longueville replied with composed manners: he paid punctually, lived independently, and showed the polish of a fine education. The Count declared he would introduce him to society at the Pavillon Planat.

At the Villa Planat, the announcement “Monsieur de Longueville” sharpened curiosity into attention. He arrived simply but elegantly, at ease in the Receiver-General’s mansion, well-spoken and surprisingly knowledgeable—able even to discuss naval architecture when the admiral broached it. His manners suggested training at the École Polytechnique; pressed about his profession, he said he had renounced the Engineers to preserve his independence. He declined dinner courteously, explaining he was caregiver to a delicate sister.

The family was struck. He charmed without ostentation, and though he aroused Mademoiselle de Fontaine’s interest—Emilie had dressed to attract him—his measured attention left her both delighted and wounded. She usually displayed her charms for newcomers, but now seemed simpler and more guarded; when roused by her uncle she quipped that perfection often hid defects—“Those who please everybody please nobody”—a remark showing caution and pride.

Over the next fortnight Longueville called repeatedly. Emilie, used to being the center of interest, found herself drawn out of pride as an external force attracted her. He was modest and discreet: he never spoke of his family and skillfully evaded her probes. He accepted her hospitality—his knowledge of painting, his piano and voice—but revealed little about his rank. Once they sang a duet and the guests could not tell whether he was a professional. His reserve both tormented Emilie and deepened her interest.

The family wished to meet Clara Longueville, and an informal dance was proposed to welcome her. When Clara and her brother arrived, Emilie felt genuine pleasure for Clara and lavished attention, though tinged with envy: Clara combined ease and modesty and, while amiable, gently reproved Emilie’s earlier disdain for commoners. Clara confessed she had come out of affection for her brother, and her frank courtesy reassured Emilie. The exchange—Clara’s mild rebuke and Emilie’s relieved response—brought the two girls closer.

With autumn the attachment between Emilie and Longueville quietly deepened. Their life became a sequence of small intimacies: aimless conversations that skirted deeper feeling, shared sunsets and daisy gatherings, and passionate duets where music voiced what words did not. They learned each other’s ways, testing limits, revealing little and withholding more. Longueville’s silence remained both balm and barrier; Emilie cherished his attention and resented the mystery, yet the bond made her gentler, more indulgent, and less self-centered.

Throughout, the Count and household watched and speculated, charmed by Longueville’s discretion and worldliness. His blend of talent, reserve, and secrecy kept his background uncertain and his admirers hopeful. Emilie surrendered to the tide of feeling, delighting in their private intimacies, even as unanswered questions about his rank and history lingered at the edges of their romance.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
##### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #####
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

For effusive souls, twilight — that soft, silent hour between day and night — invites contemplation of art. During one such hour a young painter, absorbed in finishing a large canvas, falls from his ladder, strikes his head, and loses consciousness.

He wakes to the gentle voice of a young woman and her mother, who had rushed to his aid. As he regains his senses he is struck by the girl's beauty, precisely matching the ideal of feminine grace he has long imagined. The older woman, resembling a refined marquise, tends him kindly and advises on his recovery. They accept his thanks but, by natural reserve, depart without pressing for further acquaintance.

After they leave, the porter’s wife identifies them as Mademoiselle Leseigneur and her mother, long-time residents of the building. The painter, Hippolyte Schinner, recently moved into the attic studio after winning some recognition. He is determined to provide better for his mother, who sacrificed much for him. Despite growing success he remains reserved and guarded about his private life.

During convalescence Hippolyte repeatedly returns in thought to the two women, especially the young girl's image, which lingers. When well enough to return to his studio he is eager to visit them, feeling a bond with their situation; he imagines they share a kind of misfortune that draws him close.

At their modest home he notes signs of poverty but feels no contempt, only empathy. Mademoiselle Leseigneur greets him politely; he is struck by the contrast between their humble surroundings and the elegance they maintain. The room contains remnants of past luxury — faded upholstery and well-worn ornament — yet everything is meticulously kept.

The conversation begins stiffly but soon turns to art, allowing Hippolyte to speak freely about his thoughts and feelings. Madame Leseigneur reveals how dear a portrait of her late husband, a naval officer, is to her, though the canvas is deteriorating. Hippolyte offers to paint a new portrait, hoping to forge a closer connection; the offer is accepted with graceful gratitude, and he senses the deep emotional weight behind it.

As they talk, Adelaide steps out briefly and returns with candles to light the room, shifting the atmosphere. Then an unexpected visitor arrives — a man who seems acquainted with Adelaide. His presence stirs jealousy in Hippolyte, who wants to know the nature of their relationship.

The newcomer is an older gentleman in a coat whose style recalls a bygone era; he carries an air mixing nobility with libertinism, suggesting a complicated past. His exchange with the women reveals layers of their lives, leaving Hippolyte to ponder the dynamics and histories hinted at by their manners and circumstances.

Throughout the evening Hippolyte struggles with feelings for Adelaide — admiration tangled with uncertainty. The contrast in social standing weighs on him, yet he is drawn to her spirit and grace, conscious of the slender hope for connection amid the shadows of their respective worlds.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
A remarkable figure stood beside the “Louis XIV’s light infantry” specimen — Bonapartists’ nickname for those remnants of the Monarchy. A lean, aged man, he seemed a faded reflection of the old order: a worn coat, dull hair powder, a feebler intellect, the very image Rivarol’s quip about Champcenetz suggested.

This silent old man puzzled the company and the painter. He neither spoke nor was spoken to; his relation to the gallant host—friend, relative, or mere companion—remained unclear. His presence invited curiosity but offered no answers, especially about any link to the host’s deceased wife.

The least dilapidated of the group approached Madame de Rouville, kissed her hand, and sat beside her; another man bowed and took a seat nearby. Adelaide leaned over the old gentleman’s chair, echoing a famous painting’s pose, though his familiarity seemed to irritate her. “Are you sulky with me?” he asked, casting a shrewd glance at Schinner, the painter, as if to judge his standing.

“This is our neighbor,” Madame de Rouville introduced Hippolyte, the celebrated painter. The old man noted the artist’s red ribbon of distinction against his own hard-earned honors. Hippolyte, captivated by Adelaide’s beauty, grew lost in admiration and briefly forgot their squalor. Absorbed, he answered only shortly; the old gentleman lamented his card losses, Adelaide offered help, but he insisted she stay where he could see her. As the game ended, the gentleman complained of bad luck; the silent figure echoed the time, and Hippolyte left, entranced by the first stirrings of love.

The next day Hippolyte felt an overwhelming urge to see Adelaide. Hesitant until afternoon, he awkwardly requested the Baron’s portrait. Alone, Adelaide asked him to fetch it, revealing her modesty. Their shared glance conveyed more than words; Hippolyte took the portrait, promising its quick return.

In the following days, their relationship blossomed with laughter and shared thought. Schinner, eager to learn piquet, often lost, while an unspoken bond between Hippolyte and Adelaide deepened. They became inseparable: Adelaide’s affection grew, Hippolyte’s jealousy flared when the old Count showed undue familiarity. Adelaide worried Hippolyte’s social life harmed his health; their love matured without grand gestures, built on mutual trust and delicate exchanges. Within two months their lives were intertwined: Hippolyte visited daily, and their connection deepened.

The old gentleman’s visits waned as Hippolyte took his place at the card table, sharing in the losses. Yet Hippolyte couldn’t shake concerns over Madame de Rouville’s finances. He spent two months on the portrait, calling it one of his best, but the Baroness’s silence puzzled him. One day Adelaide visited his studio and was awestruck by the work; their charged moment overflowed with feeling. Hippolyte, overwhelmed, declared his desire to marry her, but the old vice-admiral arrived, praised the painting, and hinted at funds to pay for both portraits.

As the evening went on, Hippolyte noticed Madame de Rouville’s unusual excitement during cards and suspected a gambling habit. He began to question the nature of their relationship, wondering if deception was afoot. After a night of torment, he discovered his purse missing and suspected Adelaide and her mother of theft. Despite his heartbreak, he clung to hope of a misunderstanding. The next day a friend mentioned rumors about Adelaide and her mother, deepening his unease. Torn between love and doubt, he resolved to confront his feelings and visit Madame de Rouville.

Seeing Adelaide pale and sad, he felt ashamed of his suspicions. Their talk revealed mutual longing, and he realized his mistake. The evening turned sweet as they played cards, but his fears returned when he noticed Adelaide’s secretive glances at her mother. During the game, Adelaide discreetly placed a new purse before Hippolyte, revealing her thoughtful attempt to replace the lost one. Overwhelmed, he grasped the depth of their bond and impulsively declared his intention to marry her.

Just then the old vice-admiral arrived, announcing his promotion and expressing support for the young couple. The evening ended with a sense of unity as the families came together, dispelling lingering doubts. The tale closes on love, trust, and reconciliation after misunderstanding, promising a brighter future.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############

######## MIDDLE OF THE STORY ########
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

In October 1800, a foreigner named Bartolomeo di Piombo stood outside the Tuileries with his wife and young daughter. Over sixty and grave, he was deeply troubled; his wife tried to keep a calm face for their child. Passersby were drawn by their despair, but Bartolomeo’s fierce gaze discouraged sympathy.

After a moment’s thought he made a desperate decision: he handed a dagger to his wife and declared he would confront the Bonapartes. He approached the palace but was stopped by a soldier who demanded a written request. Bartolomeo persisted until Lucien Bonaparte, minister of the interior, arrived, recognized him, and brought him inside to meet his brother, Napoleon.

Napoleon dismissed his aides to speak privately with Bartolomeo, who sought asylum and confessed he had killed the Portas—a family that had burned his vineyard and murdered his son. He explained that, in vengeance, he had slain all seven members of the Portas, a revelation that shocked Napoleon. Bartolomeo complained of the Bonaparte brothers’ earlier indifference and reminded them that he had once protected their family.

Now the leader of a nation, Napoleon felt bound by law and could not openly endorse private vengeance. Still, he acknowledged the Corsican tradition of vendetta and offered Bartolomeo a chance to live in Paris with financial assistance, warning that private justice would not be tolerated there.

Bartolomeo accepted, pledged loyalty to the Bonapartes, and appeared hopeful about his future in Paris. Afterward Lucien inquired into their finances and learned they had arrived with nothing. He gave them money and promised further help, noting that Bartolomeo’s Corsican property would not suffice to support them in the capital.

Fifteen years later, events stemming from Bartolomeo’s arrival in Paris would unfold—events that require the context of his earlier struggles and decisions.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Servin, a distinguished artist, was the first to open a studio for young girls who wished to paint. He taught about forty pupils and was married to a general’s dowerless daughter. At first mothers accompanied their daughters, but they soon trusted Servin’s principles and left them alone.

To avoid class complaints he accepted only young ladies from wealthy families and refused aspiring professional artists who would have demanded more thorough instruction. His carefulness and the quality of his teaching made him celebrated among the aristocracy; when a girl showed talent mothers recommended Servin without hesitation. He became synonymous with feminine art—Herbault for bonnets, Leroy for gowns—and, despite his ties to Parisian elites, maintained an independent, patriotic wit.

The studio itself was arranged with pupils in mind. The attic entrance above his apartment was sealed and reached by a small spiral staircase. The large room was lit through panes veiled with green linen shades; dark-gray walls were hung with caricatures and plaster models, a blend of ordered display and artistic clutter.

On a bright July day the studio buzzed with young girls at easels, each with distinct expressions and styles. Among them sat Ginevra Piombo, modestly dressed and apart, visibly anxious. The girls divided into two cliques—daughters of wealthy merchants and aristocrats—led by Amelie Thirion, vain and pretentious. Though they laughed and chatted as they worked, creating a lively atmosphere, jealousy simmered. Ginevra, most talented and beautiful, drew the aristocrats’ envy. To ostracize her, Amelie moved Ginevra’s easel away from the others, provoking whispers.

The political climate after the Bourbons’ second return sharpened these tensions. Ginevra, a devoted Bonapartist, faced contempt from royalist peers; Amelie’s slight was both personal and political. Yet Ginevra remained focused on her work, seemingly unaware of—or indifferent to—the growing hostility.

A mysterious sound behind a partition shifted attention. Ginevra discovered Servin was hiding a political fugitive, an officer of the Imperial Guard. The revelation brought excitement and concern for the man’s safety; Ginevra resolved to protect him, preferring to be near him rather than risk his exposure. Her calm indifference to Amelie’s insult only deepened the others’ bafflement and jealousy.

Madame Servin’s arrival interrupted the brewing conflict. She announced her husband could not attend and implied she knew of the hidden man. Sensing the tension, Ginevra began quietly humming a Corsican song to mask the officer’s breathing.

Throughout the session Ginevra stayed absorbed, sketching the officer’s head with a fervor that surprised even her. Servin praised her talent; the other girls, curious, were drawn to her sheet. Ginevra deftly deflected attention from the drawing, protecting the secret. Amelie, suspicious, tried to uncover the mystery but was foiled by Ginevra’s quickness. Their rivalry intensified: Ginevra’s talent and composure only heightened Amelie’s envy.

Servin, aware of the undercurrent, engaged each pupil but kept an eye on Ginevra. Their tacit understanding deepened a quiet bond, and Ginevra felt empowered to help the fugitive. As the day ended her resolve to keep the secret and aid the officer’s escape hardened. Outside, political turmoil loomed; inside, the girls navigated rivalries and friendships, united by their art.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
When the painter and Ginevra believed they were alone, Servin knocked on the garret door, revealing a tall young officer in an Imperial uniform, his arm in a sling and his face pale with pain. He recoiled at seeing Ginevra, but Servin reassured him, introducing her as the daughter of a loyal friend of the Emperor, the Baron di Piombo. Recognizing her patriotism, the soldier relaxed.

"You are wounded," Ginevra observed.

"It's nothing, mademoiselle," he replied. "The wound is healing."

Cries in the street announced a death sentence: "Condemned to death!" The officer turned pale at the name "Labedoyere," collapsing onto a stool. In despair he lamented that while Labedoyere faced death for their cause, he was hiding.

Ginevra blocked his path as he tried to leave. "Can you restore the Emperor?" she asked. "Do you expect to raise that giant who could not maintain himself?"

"What can I do?" he replied, feeling alone after losing his protector. He said he was willing to die, that an honest man's life was worth more than two traitors', and even contemplated selling his life to the executioner.

Alarmed, Ginevra offered her savings, explaining that her father was wealthy and would not blame her for helping a soldier. She urged him to wait until the storm passed, suggesting he could escape and serve elsewhere.

Her words, filled with maternal grace and hope, touched him. He admired her beauty and felt a rush of emotion. "You are an angel of goodness," he said, then his thoughts returned to Labedoyere.

In silence they shared a moment of understanding, their acquaintance deepening into a bond. Servin asked if he could save Labedoyere; the officer said he could only avenge him. Ginevra felt a surge at the thought of vengeance and recognized the officer's devotion to Napoleon.

As the sun set on the officer's face, Ginevra felt a magical connection, recalling childhood memories. Captivated by his presence, she smiled at him as she left, igniting a spark of hope.

The next day Ginevra returned to the studio. The officer, now called Luigi, told stories of the retreat from Moscow and the disaster at Waterloo. Ginevra listened, her admiration growing. They spoke Corsican, which deepened their bond.

Days passed and Ginevra's classmates noticed her absence from social gatherings, blaming her relationship with Luigi. Mademoiselle Thirion, worried by the rumors, withdrew from class, and attendance declined. Ginevra, lost in thoughts of Luigi, remained oblivious.

One day Laure, a fellow student, confided that her mother insisted she leave the studio because of the gossip around Ginevra and Luigi. Shocked, Ginevra reassured Laure they would meet again. Laure, sad to go, left, and Ginevra felt a pang of loss.

As Ginevra and Luigi's bond deepened, they shared unspoken love, each recognizing the other's worth. One evening Luigi signaled to Ginevra but, seeing Laure, retreated. Unaware of tension, Ginevra spoke with Laure and learned the others had left because of scandal.

Ginevra confronted Servin, revealing the young ladies had spread falsehoods about her relationship with Luigi. Servin dismissed the rumors, focused on his exhibition. Luigi entered, jokingly claiming responsibility for Servin's lost students; the painter playfully suggested marriage.

Their silence spoke volumes—the first acknowledgment of their love. Servin encouraged their union, celebrating the joy of love. Ginevra proposed to help Luigi secure a pardon, confident in her father's influence.

Meanwhile Ginevra's parents grew anxious about her late returns. Bartolomeo paced, concerned for his daughter. Though used to her late hours, he and his wife felt unease. Bartolomeo, a man of integrity who prioritized his family's well-being over appearances, reflected on their close-knit dynamic built on love and mutual respect. Ginevra's upbringing had instilled loyalty and passion, shared with her father and their political commitment to the Emperor.

When Ginevra finally arrived home, her father embraced her, relieved. The family shared a warm moment; their love was evident. Ginevra's heart swelled with affection for her parents, knowing they were her greatest supporters.

In the studio, Ginevra and Luigi's relationship blossomed amid challenges. Their love, forged in adversity, became a source of strength, uniting them against the world. As they navigated complexities, they remained steadfast in their commitment to one another, embodying the resilience of their shared ideals.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“Here she is, my Ginevra, Ginevrettina, Ginevrola, mia Ginevra bella!” cried the old man.

“Oh, father, you hurt me!” Ginevra exclaimed, but he set her down gently. She reassured her mother that she had only pretended to escape. The baroness recovered; Piombo, rubbing his hands in old-court fashion, looked pleased. The two elderly parents seemed revived.

“Now, to dinner!” Piombo called, taking Ginevra’s hand. “Your mother says you spend more time at the studio. Painting appears more important than us,” he teased.

“Oh, father!” she protested.

“Ginevra is preparing a surprise for us,” her mother suggested.

“A picture of your own! Will you show us?” Piombo asked.

“Yes, I’m busy at the studio,” Ginevra answered slowly. Her mother fretted. “You look pale,” she said.

“No! It shall never be said that Ginevra Piombo acted a lie,” Ginevra declared. Her parents looked astonished. “I love a young man,” she confessed, lowering her eyes.

“Is he a prince?” Piombo asked ironically, making his wife tremble.

“No, father, he is a young man without fortune,” she replied gently.

“Is he handsome?” he pressed.

“He is very unfortunate,” she said.

“What is he?” Piombo persisted.

“Labedoyere’s comrade; he was proscribed and hidden by Servin,” she explained.

“Servin is a good fellow,” Piombo acknowledged, “but you wrong to love any man but your father.”

“It does not depend on me,” Ginevra replied softly.

“I thought my Ginevra would remain faithful to me until I die,” Piombo lamented. “I did not expect our love to find a rival.”

“Did I ever reproach you for your devotion to Napoleon?” she countered. “You left me for months on missions. I endured your absence. Life has necessities.”

“You don’t love me for myself; your reproaches show your egotism,” she said.

“Dare you blame your father’s love?” he shouted.

“Father, I don’t blame you,” she answered gently. “You have grounds for your egotism, as I have for my love. I have never left your side for fifteen years; it has been my pleasure to charm your life. Is it ingratitude to desire a husband who will protect me?”

“What! You reckon benefits with your father?” Piombo asked dangerously. A dreadful silence followed. Bartolomeo cried out, “Oh! stay with us! I cannot bear to have you love another man. Ginevra, you will not have long to await your liberty.”

“But, father, I need not leave you; we will both love you. You will come to know the man to whom you bequeath me,” she said.

“Oh! Ginevra, why did you not marry when Napoleon offered?” Piombo lamented.

“They loved me to order,” she replied. “I did not wish to leave you alone.”

“You don’t wish to leave me alone, yet you marry! I know you, my daughter; you would cease to love us,” he said.

Elisa stood motionless. “We have no longer a daughter; she wishes to marry,” Piombo declared, sinking, hands raised to heaven. Ginevra, expecting a violent outburst, met instead a sudden gentleness.

“Father,” she said tenderly, “you shall never be abandoned by your Ginevra. Love me for my own sake. If you knew how he loves me! He would never make me unhappy!”

“Comparisons already!” Piombo shouted. “I cannot endure the thought of your marriage. If he loved you as you deserve, he would kill me; if he does not love you, I would kill him.”

Ginevra met his gaze calmly. “Oh! to love you! What man is worthy of such a life?” he continued. “Who is worthy to be your husband?”

“He,” Ginevra replied, “he of whom I am not worthy.”

“How can he know you enough to love you?” Piombo demanded.

“Father,” she said impatiently, “whether he loves me or not, if I love him—”

“You love him?” he interrupted. She nodded.

“You love him more than you love us?” he pressed.

“The two feelings cannot be compared,” she replied.

“Is one stronger?” he asked.

“I think it is,” she admitted.

“You shall not marry him,” Piombo declared, voice shaking the window panes.

“I shall marry him,” Ginevra replied calmly.

“Oh, God!” cried her mother. Piombo, seated, wore an icy sternness. He looked at Ginevra and pleaded, “No! You will not marry him. Say nothing more tonight—let me think otherwise. Do you wish to see your father on his knees, his white hairs prostrate before you? I supplicate you—”

“Ginevra Piombo does not break her word,” she answered. “I am your daughter.”

“She is right,” the baroness said. “We are sent into the world to marry.”

“Refusing to obey an unjust order is not disobedience,” Ginevra asserted.

“No order can be unjust from your father’s lips,” he countered. “Why do you judge my actions? My repugnance is counsel from on high, perhaps to protect you from great evil.”

“The only evil would be if he did not love me,” she replied.

“Always he!” Piombo exclaimed.

“Yes, always,” she affirmed. “He is my life, my good, my thought. Even if I obeyed you, he would remain in my soul. To forbid me to marry him is to make me hate you.”

“You love us not!” Piombo shouted.

“Oh!” Ginevra shook her head.

“Then forget him; be faithful to us. After we are gone—you understand?”

“Father, do you wish me to long for your death?” she asked.

“I shall outlive you. Children who do not honor their parents die early,” he warned.

“All the more reason I should marry and be happy,” she replied. This cool reasoning troubled Piombo; Ginevra shuddered and sprang onto his knee, embracing him. “Oh, yes, yes, let me die first! I could never survive you, my father!” she cried.

“Oh! my Ginevra, my own Ginevra!” Piombo answered, his anger melting.

“It was time you ceased,” the baroness said, trembling.

“Poor mother!” Ginevra said.

“Ah! Ginevretta! mia bella Ginevra!” Piombo exclaimed, playing with her as if she were a child. They had moments of joy, but Ginevra’s attempts to introduce Luigi met playful refusals from her father.

The next day she was more affectionate, thanking her father as if for consent. That evening she sang and played, saying they needed a man’s voice for the nocturne. A week later, her mother signaled her to come closer. “I have persuaded your father to receive him,” Elisa whispered.

“Oh! mother, how happy you have made me!” Ginevra replied. That day she returned home on Luigi’s arm. The officer emerged from hiding for the second time: Ginevra had appealed to the Duc de Feltre; Luigi’s name was restored on the officer’s list. Warned of her father’s potential disapproval, Luigi was anxious to meet him; Ginevra sensed his fear, which deepened her love.

“How pale you are!” she remarked.

“Oh! Ginevra, if it concerned my life only—” he began.

Though Bartolomeo had been told of the formal introduction, he remained seated, stern and intimidating.

“Father,” Ginevra said, “I bring you someone you will be pleased to see—a soldier who fought beside the Emperor at Mont-Saint-Jean.”

Piombo rose, casting a sidelong glance. “Monsieur is not decorated,” he remarked sarcastically.

“I no longer wear the Legion of Honor,” Luigi replied timidly.

Embarrassed by her father’s rudeness, Ginevra pulled out a chair for Luigi. Madame Piombo, noting her husband’s softening, tried to be cordial. “Monsieur’s resemblance to someone we knew in Corsica, Nina Porta, is surprising,” she observed.

“Nothing could be more natural,” Luigi replied. “Nina was my sister.”

“Are you Luigi Porta?” Piombo asked.

“Yes,” Luigi confirmed. Bartolomeo staggered as if struck; he needed support from his wife. The two old parents left the room in silence, abandoning their daughter in horror.

Luigi, bewildered, looked at Ginevra, who had turned pale and stared toward the door through which her parents had exited. The solemnity of the moment filled her with dread. “What misery in a word!” she exclaimed.

“In the name of our love, what have I said?” Luigi asked.

“My father never spoke of our tragic history, and I was too young to know,” Ginevra explained.

“Are we in vendetta?” Luigi asked, trembling.

“Yes. My mother said the Portas killed my brother and burned our house. My father then massacred their family. How did you survive? You were tied to the bed before they set fire to the house.”

“I do not know,” Luigi replied. “I was taken to Genoa at six and raised by an old man named Colonna. I was told I had enemies and advised to take Luigi as my surname to evade them.”

“Go, go, Luigi!” Ginevra urged. “Stay here; you are safe in my father’s house, but once you leave, danger awaits! My father has two Corsicans in his service, and they will hunt you.”

“Ginevra,” he said, “does this vendetta exist between us?”

She smiled sadly and nodded. “Oh, Luigi, our love must be pure and sincere to give me strength for the path ahead. But it promises happiness for our lives, does it not?”

Luigi squeezed her hand. Ginevra realized true love transcends mere words; their destiny was sealed. She foresaw a difficult struggle but felt no inclination to abandon him. With resolve, she helped him reach his modest lodging.

Returning home, Ginevra felt a calm resolve. She greeted her parents, who were preparing dinner, with gentle eyes, noticing her mother’s tear-stained face. They rose from the table in silence, unable to speak. In the gloomy salon, Piombo tried to speak but faltered; he rang for a servant. “Pietro, light the fire; I am cold,” he ordered.

Ginevra trembled, watching her father’s internal battle. Both suffered in silence, nursing wounded pride and anger. The love that had once brought them joy now felt like a burden. Ginevra’s gaze reproached her father, while Piombo’s reflected deep pain.

On Ginevra’s birthday, her mother, desperate to mend the rift, gathered them in Bartolomeo’s study. Ginevra sensed her mother’s intentions and smiled sadly. At that moment two notaries arrived with witnesses. Bartolomeo eyed them warily. The older notary introduced himself and began the civil formalities for her marriage.

“Monsieur le baron, we have come to fulfill the law regarding your daughter’s marriage,” he stated.

Piombo’s anger flared. “Are there laws in France that destroy paternal authority?” he demanded. “Which tear a daughter from her father? Which deprive an old man of his last consolation?” He interrupted the notary’s calm explanations with cry after cry.

The notary’s composure only inflamed Piombo. In a sudden movement, he seized a dagger from the wall and rushed toward Ginevra. Witnesses intervened; he pushed them aside, fury raw. Seeing him approach, Ginevra knelt before him. “No, no! I cannot!” he cried, throwing the dagger away. “Have mercy! Give me Luigi! I ask for your consent upon my knees,” she pleaded, voice trembling.

Bartolomeo pushed her away. “Go. The wife of Luigi Porta cannot be a Piombo. I have no daughter. My Ginevra Piombo is buried here,” he said, pressing his heart. “Leave my house, unhappy girl,” he added, ushering her out.

“Luigi!” Ginevra cried, rushing to his humble abode. “My Luigi, we have no fortune but our love.”

“Then I am richer than kings!” he exclaimed.

“My father and mother have cast me off,” she said sadly.

“I will love you in their place,” he promised.

“Then let us be happy—we WILL be happy!” she declared, voice full of mixed joy and dread.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The day after Ginevra was expelled from her father's house, she sought refuge with Madame Servin until her marriage to Luigi. Madame Servin received her coldly, resentful of the trouble Ginevra had caused her husband. Proud yet shocked by this selfishness, Ginevra rented a room near Luigi, who devoted himself to her and painted a bright future that lifted her spirits despite her father's harshness.

One morning she received trunks of essentials from her mother, who included a letter urging her to reconsider the marriage. The letter, full of love and sorrow, deeply moved Ginevra. Just as she felt compelled to return home, Luigi arrived and her resolve to abandon him faded. She chose to bury her grief for the sake of their love.

On the wedding day Ginevra was alone; Luigi brought ordinary witnesses to sign the marriage certificate. Their plain presence contrasted with the usual joy of a wedding, and Ginevra's beauty silenced them, creating a somber mood. As they walked to the mayor's office, they passed other, more festive wedding parties, highlighting the difference in their situation.

Inside, they sat together while the festive atmosphere around them made their own union feel overshadowed. The mayor's clerk noted the absence of Ginevra's parents, and when the mayor inquired, the clerk confirmed their protest. The bureaucratic formality felt oppressive, a reminder of family disapproval; the ceremony seemed reduced to a legal act.

After signing the registers, Ginevra and Luigi left the office with mixed relief and sadness. Luigi expressed his gratitude, and Ginevra felt a measure of happiness despite the circumstances. They hurried to the church, where an old priest married them in a modest chapel amid grander weddings. The priest's remarks about their parents' absence stung, but a sergeant's blessing comforted Ginevra.

Once married they moved into a modest apartment. Luigi embraced Ginevra, calling this their true wedding. They explored the space, which Luigi had provisioned thoughtfully with artistic supplies for Ginevra. She was delighted by these details, and they reveled in their shared home, feeling they had found paradise despite limitations.

In the early days they indulged in their love, often losing themselves in each other. Reality soon set in and they recognized the need to work. Ginevra began copying paintings and gained clients, while Luigi, after initial struggles, used his handwriting skills to offer copying services. His efforts paid off; he eventually had enough work to hire assistants, providing a stable income for both.

This period marked the happiest time of their lives, filled with love and labor. Even though Ginevra sometimes felt pangs of remorse for the family happiness she had sacrificed, they lived contentedly. To mark an anniversary, Ginevra painted a portrait of herself for Luigi, capturing the essence of their love. Another year passed in quiet, simple happiness.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
At the start of 1819, picture-dealers urged Ginevra to produce original works instead of copies as competition grew. Madame Porta lamented not taking up genre painting earlier, which might have improved her standing. Ginevra experimented with portraiture but faced stiff competition from artists in greater need. Though she and Luigi had modest savings, they grew anxious about their future.

Through the winter Luigi labored relentlessly against a shrinking market for writing. Unable to afford help, he worked longer hours for the same pay. Ginevra painted several commendable works, but dealers avoided lesser-known artists; low prices failed to sell her paintings.

Their household deteriorated: love and happiness were overshadowed by looming poverty. They concealed worries from each other—Ginevra lavishing affection while Luigi hid his fears—clinging to each other and the hope that love would sustain them. One night Ginevra woke to find Luigi gone and discovered him working late to avoid burdening her. Moved, she began coloring lithographs through the night while Luigi toiled in his garret; both pretended to sleep to shield the other from anxiety.

On another evening, exhausted and feverish, Luigi opened his window and saw Ginevra's lamp across the courtyard, realizing she too was working. When he confronted her, she said she could not be idle while he suffered. They shared a tender moment, watching dawn together and feeling briefly hopeful.

Their struggles deepened when Ginevra gave birth to a son, Bartolomeo. The joy of parenthood briefly masked their financial burdens, but soon they were selling possessions and jewels. Poverty stripped them of comfort and pride, escalating their desperation. Months later Ginevra, frail and worn, struggled to care for her sickly child. Luigi, feeling helpless, sought aid but found none; he sold Ginevra's paintings and remaining furniture for a meager sum that only prolonged their hardship. Ginevra showed remarkable strength, managing household duties and caring for the boy while often starving herself to provide for Luigi.

One evening she offered Luigi her last piece of bread and urged him to eat. Realizing her sacrifice, he was consumed by despair and left to find help. In desperation he decided to sell himself as a military substitute, hoping the money would save Ginevra and their child. After securing an advance, he hurried home, only to find Ginevra asleep with their child. When he roused her, she smiled, then saw Bartolomeo cold and lifeless. Luigi, horrified, sought aid, but it was too late. In delirium, Ginevra spoke her love for Luigi and sorrow over their lost child, then died in his arms, leaving him devastated.

As Luigi mourned, Ginevra's parents sat at home reflecting on their daughter, whom they had not seen in years. Memories surged as they noticed the empty chair that had once belonged to her; Elisa Piombo spoke of hoping to rekindle Bartolomeo's love for Ginevra. Just then a man arrived with news of the tragedy. The old couple was shattered, realizing their daughter was gone; Bartolomeo’s death and Ginevra’s loss left only memories and grief.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
##### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ##### ################################################ MIDDLE OF THE STORY.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

Many tales, rich in situation or dramatic twist, can be told artfully or simply without losing charm. But some human experiences demand a delicate touch. Certain details—almost anatomical—require a nuanced rendering of thought. Portraits must carry a soul, and some things are expressed only through secret harmonies shaped by time, circumstance, or an inner moral inclination.

Such revelations are necessary for this simple story, aimed at the grave and reflective who find sustenance in tender emotions. If the writer, like a surgeon at a dying friend’s side, feels reverence for the subject, should not the reader share that feeling? Many recognize the vague sadness that surrounds us, a gentle suffering that can be oddly pleasing. If the reader has mourned a dear one, is alone, or the day is waning, they may continue; otherwise they should set this book aside. Those who have never buried a beloved relative will not grasp these pages, which may seem fragrant to some and colorless to others. The reader must have known the luxury of tears and the silent pangs of memory, evoking both regret for loss and smiles for past joys.

The writer assures that this narrative is a true story, worthy of the reader's emotional investment, if they possess any.

In contemporary France, language reflects as many idioms and idiosyncrasies as there are individuals. It amuses to hear differing interpretations of the same person from various Parisians. Ask a Practical man about Madame Firmiani: he measures her by means—a fine house in the rue du Bac, well-furnished salons, an income of one hundred thousand francs a year, and a husband who was once receiver-general—concluding they are solid people with nothing to criticize.

A Lounger knows her through society: her salon is respectable, noted for excellent tea and exclusive gatherings where the best appear. An Egotist dismisses such salons as dull, thinking a mind should avoid trivial conversation. A Student calls her enchanting and wishes to introduce you; a Fop notes faded beauty but concedes lingering charm and remnants of allure. A Connoisseur admires her impressive painting collection.

A Spiteful woman warns against visiting her, calling her a dangerous siren who provokes jealousy. An Attache recalls her beauty from a decade earlier. A Distinguishing observer remarks on her elite connections despite modest household circumstances. A Duke claims not to know her, recognizing only those presented at court. A Ninny mistakenly asserts she used to sing at the Opera, while two old ladies discuss her lineage, revealing ties to the noble Cadignan family.

An Original assures you her salon is safe, since any scoundrels there are of high quality. An Observer describes her as a beautiful woman who rarely rises to greet guests, a mystery married to a mythical husband; he praises her charm and conversational skill, hints at many admirers but insists none are favored. A Contradictor claims she was once Murat’s mistress, while an Envious person dismisses her wealth as coquetry that ruined a young man’s prospects—an indignation whose persistence is puzzling.

Each portrait is partial and telling: the Practical man uses property and rank; the Lounger, hospitality and exclusivity; the Egotist, disdain; the Student, romance; the Fop, faded beauty; the Connoisseur, art; the Spiteful, rivalry; the Attache, memory; the Distinguishing, connections; the Duke, court recognition; the Ninny, error; the old ladies, lineage; the Original, quality of companions; the Observer, elusive charm; the Contradictor and Envious, rumor and resentment.

In sum, Madame Firmiani is a complex figure seen through many lenses, each shaped by the observer’s habits and biases. The narrative invites readers to explore these interpretations while reflecting on the deeper emotional truths—reverence, loss, memory, and the quiet harmonies—that connect them all.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
In January 1824 opinions about Madame Firmiani varied so widely that no clear picture emerged: widow or wife, wise or foolish, virtuous or not, rich or poor, beautiful or plain. This multiplicity, like lithographic blocks producing different images from slight variations, shows how society distorts a reputation.

That winter Monsieur de Bourbonne, an elderly provincial magnate from Touraine, became intrigued. Practical and observant from managing an estate, he traveled to Paris to learn more, but gossip only deepened his confusion. A man of honor, he cherished his nephew Octave de Camps and had high hopes for him; he was irritated when Octave sold an estate without consulting him. A distant relative then claimed Octave had squandered his fortune on Madame Firmiani and been reduced to teaching mathematics—a report that troubled the old man.

Still, he refused to assume the worst and went to Paris determined to investigate. Using connections in the faubourg Saint-Germain, he gathered information and resolved to meet Madame Firmiani under the name de Rouxellay to discover the truth of her relationship with Octave. Choosing a moment when Octave was occupied, he soon confirmed that his nephew’s financial troubles were indeed real.

Monsieur de Rouxellay was no typical provincial uncle; a former member of the King’s guard, he knew society’s manners and politics. Entering Madame Firmiani’s salon with the Marquise de Listomere, he expressed pity for Octave, wondering how a woman of such luxury could be involved with a man in such straits. Yet upon seeing Madame Firmiani his anger softened. In the salon’s atmosphere her beauty shone; the elegance of her presence captivated him and made him rethink his initial judgments.

Though thought to be twenty-five, she was nearer twenty-eight, having married the invisible Firmiani at sixteen. In that age she combined beauty, wealth, musical talent, intelligence, and wit, and was esteemed in high society—admired even by rivals. But her popularity made her a target for malicious gossip: many envied her apparent happiness and propriety, and unfounded rumors persisted, revealing the dangers of societal suspicion.

This brief portrait hints at her true nature: a complex blend of qualities that inspire admiration and intrigue. Her beauty, grace, and natural charm made her a figure of fascination, capable of stirring deep emotions and reflecting the multifaceted nature of womanhood and the human experience.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The former guardsman lingered after the others had gone, and Madame Firmiani found him still seated as the clock struck two in the morning.

“Madame,” he began as she rose, signaling he should leave, “I am the uncle of Monsieur Octave de Camps.”

Madame Firmiani sat down again, visibly moved. The old Planter could not tell whether her pallor came from shame or pleasure—women hide their joys, and the more delicate they are, the more they conceal them. Monsieur de Bourbonne misread her agitation.

“Well, monsieur?” she asked with a look that disarmed men.

“Someone in my province told me my nephew has ruined himself for you, living in a garret while you enjoy wealth. Forgive my rustic honesty; I thought you should know.”

“Stop, monsieur,” she replied, lifting her hand. “I know all that. You are too polite to press me if I ask you not to, and too gallant to pretend you can question me. It would be absurd to defend myself. Believe my contempt for money, though I married a wealthy man without a fortune. Your nephew’s fortune or lack of it is irrelevant; I welcome him as my friend. I have no patience for gossip. My guardian angel has kept me from dishonesty.”

Her voice rose, then she ended with the composed piquancy of Celimene teasing the Misanthrope.

“Madame,” Monsieur de Bourbonne said, his voice trembling, “I am an old man, almost a father to Octave; forgive me. I swear as a gentleman your answer shall stay here.” He placed his hand on his heart. “Do you love Octave?”

“To another I’d answer with a look,” she said. “But to you—what would you think of a woman who answered plainly? Confessing love is an effort, even when one is certain of being loved; it’s a reward for him. To say it to another—” She left the sentence unfinished, rose, bowed, and retired to her rooms.

“Ah! the mischief!” he thought. “What a woman! She is either sly or an angel.” He went off in his hired coach.

The next morning Monsieur de Bourbonne climbed to Octave’s rooms; the young professor, astonished to see him after a sleepless night, listened as his uncle scolded him.

“You rascal!” the old man cried, sitting down. “Since when is it fashionable to mock uncles with twenty-six thousand francs a year? Have I not been a good uncle? I’m making you an estate in Touraine, but you sell property and live like a lackey!”

“Uncle—”

“I’m not talking about uncles; I’m talking about nephews. Confess: have you been gambling? Lost money at the Bourse? Say, ‘Uncle, I’m a wretch,’ and I’ll forgive you. But if you lie, I’ll sell my property and return to youthful ways.” He kissed his fingers. “I saw your Madame Firmiani yesterday—she is charming. Have you ruined yourself for her?”

“Yes, uncle.”

“Ha! Women used to be cleverer at ruining men, but this one—she’s a piece of the last century.”

“Uncle,” Octave said tenderly, “you are mistaken. Madame Firmiani deserves esteem and every adoration.”

“Youth, youth! Always the same!” cried Monsieur de Bourbonne. “Tell your old tale. But remember, I’ve had my share.”

“My dear uncle, here is a letter that will explain nearly everything,” Octave said, producing it from an elegant portfolio—her gift. “Read it and you’ll know a Madame Firmiani unknown to the world.”

“I don’t have my spectacles; read it aloud.”

Octave began: “My beloved—”

“Hey, then you are still intimate with her?” the uncle interrupted.

“Of course.”

“You haven’t parted from her?”

“Parted!” Octave repeated. “We are married.”

“Heavens!” cried Monsieur de Bourbonne. “Then why do you live in a garret?”

“Let me continue.”

Octave struggled through the letter with emotion: “‘My beloved Husband, you ask why I am sad. I cannot deceive you; this may be a misfortune, for a wife should be gay and caressing. I long to love you forever; a woman’s glory is the man she loves. But my angel has fallen. The tale you told me has tarnished my joy. I feel humiliated by you—whom I thought the most honorable of men. I must have deep confidence to make this avowal; it costs me much. How can you, knowing your father unjustly deprived others of their property, keep it? You told me in the room where our love was born; you are a gentleman and think yourself noble, and I am yours! I try to find excuses in your youth and thoughtlessness. Perhaps you never thought seriously about fortune and integrity. Oh! how your laugh wounded me. Reflect on that ruined family always in distress, poor young girls who have reason to curse you; an old father who says each night, ‘We might not be starving if that man’s father had been honest.’’”

“Good heavens!” cried Monsieur de Bourbonne. “Surely you didn’t tell that woman about your father’s affair with the Bourgneufs? Women know more about wasting a fortune than making one.”

“They know about integrity. But listen: ‘Octave, no power on earth can change the principles of honor. Look into your conscience and ask it what name you give to the action by which you hold your property.’” Octave paused; his uncle dropped his head.

“‘I will not tell you all the thoughts that assail me; they reduce to one: I cannot respect the man who knowingly is smirched for a sum of money, whatever the amount. Five francs stolen at play or five times a hundred thousand gained by a legal trick are equally dishonoring. I feel degraded by the very love that was my joy. There rises in my soul a voice my tenderness cannot stifle. I have wept to feel that I have more conscience than love. Were you to commit a crime, I would hide you from justice, but my devotion could go no farther. Love, to a woman, means boundless confidence united to reverence. I have always conceived love as a fire purifying noble feelings. I have one thing to say: come to me poor, and my love shall be redoubled. If not, renounce it. Should I see you no more, I shall know what it means. I do not wish you to make restitution because I urge it. Consult your conscience. An act of justice ought not to be a sacrifice made to love. I am your wife, not your mistress; it is less a question of pleasing me than of inspiring true respect. If I am mistaken, if you can show me your right to the property, consider and decide by listening to your conscience. A man who truly loves a woman respects the sanctity of her trust too deeply to dishonor himself. I blame myself for what I have written; a word might have sufficed, and I have preached to you! Scold me; I wish to be scolded—but not much, only a little. Dear, between us, the power is yours—you alone should perceive your own faults.’”’

“Well, uncle?” Octave asked, tears in his eyes.

“There’s more; finish it.”

“Oh, the rest is for a lover’s eyes only,” Octave smiled.

“Yes, right, my boy,” the old man softened. “I too have loved. But why do you teach mathematics?”

“My dear uncle, I am your nephew; isn’t that as good as saying I dipped into my father’s capital? After reading this letter, a revolution happened within me. I paid my entire debt of remorse in one day. I cannot describe it. As I drove in the Bois, a voice said, ‘That horse is not yours’; when I ate, it said, ‘You have stolen this food.’ I rushed to Madame Firmiani.
