“I would give ten years of my old age,” said the duke, “to offer you that jewel.”

“Ah, here comes the audacious giver!” cried Modeste as Canalis rode up. “La Brière rode at full gallop from Havre?”

“Does your secretary take such liberties?” she asked, turning pale, and threw the whip to Françoise with scorn. “Give me your whip, papa.”

“Poor Ernest, half-dead with fatigue!” said Canalis, overtaking her. “You are pitiless, mademoiselle. The poor fellow said, ‘I have but this one chance to remain in her memory.’”

“And should you think well of a woman who could take presents from half the parish?” she returned.

She received no answer; Canalis’s inattention attributed to horse’s feet. His admiration seemed too intense to be natural. At a cross-road overlooking the Seine, Canalis let Modeste and the duke pass, then joined the colonel.

“Monsieur le comte,” he said, “you are an open-hearted soldier. I am twenty-nine, without landed estates, full of ambition. Mademoiselle Modeste pleases me. The question of money is of the utmost importance. I have about thirty thousand francs a year; if my wife brought me as much more, I should be about equal. Shall you give Mademoiselle a million?”

“We have not reached that point,” said the colonel cautiously.

“Then suppose we go no further. Give me your word of honor to say nothing, even to Mademoiselle Modeste, because my circumstances may change and I may ask for her without dowry.”

“I promise,” said the colonel. “People exaggerate fortunes. I await accounts of my agents; the sale of my merchandise and ship is not yet concluded. I did say to La Brière in Paris I would guarantee two hundred thousand francs in ready money. I wish to entail my estates and enable grandchildren to inherit my arms and title.”

Canalis barely listened. They reached a table-land with a view of the Seine valley. “Butscha was right; God is the greatest landscape painter,” he said.

Modeste remarked on fictitious wants and vanities; Canalis called it a bucolic written on tablets of gold. The colonel invited the duke and Canalis to dine in riding-dress. That evening Modeste gave La Brière’s whip back: “Please ask Monsieur de La Brière to keep it until you know whether we are rich enough to buy it back.” The colonel kissed her forehead approvingly.

Canalis, alarmed at the indecency of his earlier conduct and its consequences, tried to excuse himself to Modeste, pleading jealousy and the delicacy of a poet’s sensibility. “I attach more value to words and looks than ordinary men,” he said, “and demand infantile security.” Modeste countered, calling his demand excessive and resenting his mention of the duchess. “I admire you, my dear poet,” she said lightly, but rebuked his presumption. Their intercourse cooled; Canalis left early, and the field opened to the duke. Butscha, walking with Madame Latournelle, whispered in Modeste’s ear, “Was I right?”

“Alas, yes,” she answered. “Anger got the better of me.”

“Well, so much the better. When you are both so angry you can’t speak, I will make him desperately in love until you are deceived yourself.”

“Come, Butscha; he is a great poet; he is a gentleman; he is a man of intellect.”

“Your father’s millions are more to him than all that,” answered Butscha.

“Eight millions!” cried Modeste.

“My master, who sold his practice, is going to Provence to attend to buying lands your father’s agent suggested. The purchase is four millions; your father has agreed. You are to have a dot of two millions and another million for a Paris establishment. Count up.”

“If it hadn’t been for that comedian Canalis you would have kept his whip thinking it came from me,” said Butscha, advocating La Brière.

Modesty’s reaction sealed matters. Canalis, sleepless, composed a letter to the Duchesse de Chaulieu explaining his absence and his interference on La Brière’s behalf. He described Mademoiselle Modeste as “a rather pale, insignificant, thread-papery little thing” who liked literature and had coquetted with the duchess’s Melchior. He asked Eleonore to verify facts through banker Mongenod about Monsieur Mignon’s fortune and to help La Brière by transmission of a father-in-law’s title. His confidential reminiscences were too intimate.

Meanwhile Butscha, under the name Jean Jacmin, had received and replied to a letter from his fictitious cousin Philoxene, and the duchess, wounded by Melchior’s silence, dictated and read replies and then summoned Mongenod to inquire about Monsieur Mignon’s fortune. Finding evidence that Modeste had wealth, Eleonore—who had felt betrayed and angry—quickly changed to generosity after reading Canalis’s letter: if he had not been faithless, she would give him the girl and her millions—provided Mademoiselle Mignon was as ugly as Canalis had written.

The duke arrived. The duchess, eager, wrote to Diane de Maufrigneuse and to Mademoiselle d’Herouville to arrange an invitation and hinted to secure the hunt. Eleonore sent Canalis a sharp answer:

My dear poet,—Mademoiselle de La Bastie is very beautiful; Mongenod has proved she has millions. I thought of marrying you to her; I am displeased at your want of confidence. You are a child. The duke thinks you less than a gentleman, reflecting on your mother’s honor. Now I intend to see things for myself and will manage to have you invited to Rosembray.

Canalis flung the letter at Ernest across breakfast. “That’s the two thousandth love-letter I have had from that woman,” he cried. “Marry, try your luck! The worst marriage is better than this halter.”

Caught between two lies, Canalis was at a loss. “Play by rule, and you lose!” he muttered, then resolved that if he must retire, his ten years of fidelity to Eleonore would ensure some good match. The hunt would unite passions and prove a truce among adversaries. Canalis wished to appear courteous and simply charming; he talked finances with Gobenheim and war with the colonel, biasing them in favor of La Brière. The d’Herouville ladies continued their intrigues to secure Modeste’s dot. Invitations from the Duc de Verneuil and the Master of the Hunt extended to the Basties to stay at Rosembray for a grand hunt.

La Brière, full of dark presentiments, craved Modeste’s presence. The duke arrived to arrange Modeste’s departure; the colonel invited the suitors to breakfast the morning of the start. Canalis planned to reconquer Modeste and throw over the duchess, La Brière had resolved to bid eternal farewell, and each suitor prepared a last plea. The colonel pressed Modeste for decision.

“Our position with the d’Herouville family will be intolerable at Rosembray,” he said. “Do you mean to be a duchess?”

“No, father,” she answered.

“Then do you love Canalis?”

“No, papa, a thousand times no!” she exclaimed; the colonel rejoiced.

“Ah, I chose in Paris the man of fixed principles and sound moralities,” he said slyly.

“I was not even thinking of him, papa. Please leave me liberty to refuse the duke myself; after a glimpse of court life at Rosembray I will tell you my secret.”

“Monsieur de La Brière,” cried the colonel, “I hope you are going to this hunt?”

“No, colonel, I have come to take leave; I return to Paris—”

“You have no curiosity,” said Modeste.

“If that is all, you must stay to please me; I wish it,” said the colonel, moving away to meet Canalis and leaving daughter and suitor together.

“Mademoiselle,” said La Brière, raising his eyes, “let me carry away with me your forgiveness. My life can never be happy; it will be full of remorse—”

“Before we part forever,” said Modeste interrupting, “I wish to ask you one thing; though you once disguised yourself, I think you cannot be so base as to deceive me now. Will you be frank?”

La Brière related the scene in the poet’s study and the challenge that followed. “You have said enough,” said Modeste, restraining emotion. “If you have not my heart, monsieur, you have at least my esteem.” These words gave him a violent shock; staggering, he leaned against a tree. Modeste left him but returned, taking his hand. “What is the matter?” she asked.

“Forgive me—I thought you despised me.”

“But,” she answered, distant, “I did not say that I loved you.”

She left him. Yet the esteem she’d given filled him with joy. Modeste saw in La Brière’s sensitivity the possibility of a love that might fulfill the ideal every woman hopes for.

Canalis approached Modeste later. “In spite of the little value you attach to my sentiments, my honor is concerned in effacing a stain under which I have suffered,” he said, and let her read lines from the duchess’s letter that proved Mongenod’s inquiry and intention to marry Modeste to La Brière—he tore that passage from the letter and gave the fragment to Modeste. “I cannot let you read the rest,” he added; “but a girl who could accuse me of ignoble motives is capable of suspecting collusion. Ah, Modeste,” he added with tears, “your poet has as much poetry in his heart as in his mind. Suspend judgment until you have seen the duchess.”

“Oh, dear!” she said; “it seems they are all angels—and not marriageable; the duke is the only one that belongs to humanity.”

Butscha appeared with a parcel. He had been busy with Spanish bits and had shown a piece to the colonel, who thanked him generously. The dwarf departed like a man who’d heard of a rich uncle.

“My dear father,” Modeste said to the colonel in the salon, “I would like that beautiful whip—suppose you ask Monsieur de La Brière to exchange it for your picture by Van Ostade.” She looked furtively at Ernest as the colonel made the proposition; she thought, “He will be at the hunt.”

Curiously, Modeste’s three lovers each went to Rosembray with hearts full of hope. Rosembray—bought by the Duc de Verneuil—was a chateau of the grand order, approached by an avenue of elms, presenting a courtyard with orange trees and a façade with fluted pilasters and a pediment carved by Coysevox. Its park extended to immense ponds, gardens in the French style, and thirty acres of woodland contiguous with other forests, royal and state—a noble landscape fitting the drama to come.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Modeste’s arrival at Rosembray created a sensation. A carriage in the liveries of France drew up, followed by the grand equerry, the colonel, Canalis, and La Briere on horseback, an outrider in full dress ahead, six servants including Negroes and a mulatto behind, and the colonel’s britzka bearing two waiting-women and luggage. Four horses drew the carriage; the postilions were dressed with the exacting elegance enforced by the grand equerry. Dazzled by the display and the little Versailles before her, Modeste feared appearing awkward before the duchesses and regretted having wished for a hunt.

As she alighted she saw a plump, calm old man in a frizzed blond wig, with a fatherly smile and a monastic cheerfulness—the Duc de Verneuil, master of Rosembray. The duchess, spare, erect, and devout, the only daughter of a deceased rich chief-justice and mother of four, resembled Madame Latournelle with an abbatial grace.

“Mademoiselle d’Herouville” kissed the duchess and presented Modeste. “We have heard so much of you,” said the duchess. The Duc de Verneuil welcomed the Comte de La Bastie with admiration, and complimented Modeste as a treasure.

The duchess took Modeste into a vast salon where a dozen women grouped about the fireplace; the men lingered on the terrace except Canalis, who respectfully went to the superb Eleonore. The Duchesse de Chaulieu, at an embroidery frame, was showing Mademoiselle de Verneuil how to shade a flower.

If Modeste had pricked her finger she could not have felt a sharper sting than Madame de Chaulieu’s cold, contemptuous stare. For an instant Modeste saw nothing but that one woman and saw through her. Eleonore’s radiance masked a menacing, implacable reserve; Canalis’s attempts at intimacy slid off her like oil. Modeste’s beauty, rivaling Diane de Maugrigneuse herself, kindled Eleonore’s carefully restrained wrath.

All the women rushed to the windows to see the new wonder alight from the carriage, accompanied by her three suitors. Diane exclaimed, “She is divine! where in the world does she come from?” and they returned to their seats, pretending composure though Eleonore’s heart brimmed with poisoned feeling.

“Mademoiselle d’Herouville murmured to the Duchesse de Verneuil that Eleonore received her Melchior ungraciously,” someone commented. “The Duchesse de Maugrigneuse thinks there is a coolness between them,” added Laure de Verneuil.

“Why so?” Modeste asked Helene.

“The great poet,” said the pious duchess, motioning her daughter to silence, “left Madame de Chaulieu without a letter for more than two weeks after claiming to go to Havre for his health—while she was trying to get him the Legion of Honor command and a post at Baden.”

“Oh, that was shameful in Canalis; he owes everything to her,” said Mademoiselle d’Herouville.

“Why did not Madame de Chaulieu go to Havre?” Modeste asked innocently.

“My dear,” replied the Duchess de Verneuil, “she would let herself be cut to pieces without a cry. Look at her—she is regal; her head would smile, like Mary Stuart’s, after it was cut off.”

“Did she not write to him?” Modeste persisted.

“Diane says,” the duchess, prompted to speak, informed her, “that in answer to Canalis’s first letter she sent a cutting reply a few days ago.”

Modeste flushed on Canalis’s account; she longed, not to crush him, but to take a sharp revenge. She looked haughtily at the Duchesse de Chaulieu.

“Monsieur Melchior!” she said.

All the women sniffed and glanced from the duchess, who was whispering to Canalis over the embroidery frame, to the young girl daring to interrupt what society expected to be a lovers’ meeting. Diane nodded as if to say Modeste was right. The women, enraged at a woman of fifty-six still handsome enough to claim the dues of youth, smiled at each other in shared malice. Melchior’s look to Modeste was feverish; the duchess, with a lowered head and lioness movement, sent red, epigrammatic glances that stabbed the poet.

“Monsieur Melchior!” Modeste repeated, asserting her right to be heard.

“What, mademoiselle?” demanded Canalis. He rose reluctantly, stalled between the embroidery frame and the fireplace where Modeste sat with the Duchesse de Verneuil. Bitter reflections assailed him: obeying Modeste would end everything with his protectress; refusing would confess his dependence and squander the fruits of his scheming. Modeste’s beauty and money versus Eleonore’s rights and influence made his hesitation agonizing, like a matador’s peril in the arena.

“Mademoiselle d’Herouville hurried me from the carriage, and I left behind me,” Modeste said to Canalis, “my handkerchief—”

Canalis shrugged.

“And,” she continued, ignoring his gesture, “I had tied into one corner of it the key of a desk which contains the fragment of an important letter; have the kindness, Monsieur Melchior, to get it for me.”

Between an angel and an enraged tiger—between Modeste’s imperious youth and Eleonore’s controlled wrath—Canalis, turned livid, no longer hesitated. He was about to comply and thus compromise himself irretrievably when La Briere appeared at the salon door, seeming to him like an archangel.

“Ernest, here, Mademoiselle de La Bastie wants you,” the poet hastily said, returning to his chair.

Ernest darted to Modeste without ceremony, saw only her, took the commission with joy, and sped from the room with the secret approbation of the assembled women.

“If you speak to her, if you ever look at her, all is over between us,” the duchess had whispered to Canalis, not satisfied with Ernest’s intervention; “and remember, if I am not present, I leave eyes behind that will watch you.”

She rose—a woman of medium height but slightly too stout, still retaining beauty—and moved toward Diane de Maugrigneuse, stepping daintily like a deer. Beneath her plumpness showed the delicacy and nervous vigor that controlled and vitalized the flesh. None but women of long descent know, as Eleonore did, how to be majestic despite buxom tendencies. A philosopher might pity Philoxene yet admire the careful elegance with which Eleonore wore a morning dress like a queen’s ease. Her abundant undyed hair was simply plaited; she bared her snowy throat and shoulders and displayed the celebrated hand and arm with pardonable pride. Modeste and the duchess’s other rivals were forced to admit, “She eclipses us.” Eleonore was one of the rare grandes dames whose nobility of bearing and practiced arts defied dissection.

Madame de Chaulieu bowed to Helene and her aunt and, in a pure, even tone, said to Diane, “Is it not time to dress, duchess?” She left accompanied by her daughter-in-law and Mademoiselle d’Herouville. As she passed, she spoke softly to the old maid, who pressed her arm and murmured “You are charming,” a phrase meaning gratitude for a service rendered. Mademoiselle d’Herouville then returned to the salon to resume her rôle as spy, and her first glance apprised Canalis that the duchess had made her threat good. The poet recognized his diplomacy was insufficient for such a struggle; compelled to a humbler course, his wit now served to salvage what he could.

When Ernest returned with Modeste’s handkerchief, the poet seized his arm and led him to the terrace.

“My dear friend,” he said, “I am not only the most unfortunate but the most ridiculous man; get me out of the hornet’s nest into which I have run. Modeste is a demon; she sees my difficulty and laughs at it. She has just spoken to me of a fragment of a letter of Madame de Chaulieu which I foolishly gave her; if she shows it I can never make my peace with Eleonore. Go at once and ask Modeste to send me back that paper, and tell her, from me, that I make no pretensions to her hand. Count on her delicacy and propriety as a young girl to behave as if we had never known each other. I beg her not to speak to me; I implore her to treat me harshly—though I hardly dare ask her to feign a jealous anger, which would aid my interests amazingly. Go; I will wait here for an answer.”
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
On re-entering the salon, Ernest de La Briere found the Vicomte de Serizy, a young officer of the Havre guard who had come from Rosny to announce that Madame must be present at the opening of the Chambers. The importance of this constitutional solemnity—when Charles X delivered his speech with the royal family, Madame la Dauphine and Madame in their gallery—made the choice of emissary a mark of attention to Diane, adored by the charming Vicomte, son of a minister, gentleman in ordinary, and sole heir to an immense fortune. The Duchesse de Maufrigneuse tolerated his attentions chiefly to contrast his youth with Madame de Serizy’s age; whispered chronicles said she had once deprived Lucien de Rubempre of his heart.

“You will do us the pleasure, I hope, to remain at Rosembray,” said the severe duchess to the young officer.

While scandal was discussed, the devout lady closed her eyes to the duke’s carefully chosen guests; these excellent women endured much under the pretense of reclaiming lost sheep. “We reckoned without our constitutional government,” said the grand equerry; “and Rosembray, Madame la duchesse, will lose a great honor.”

“We shall be more at our ease,” replied a tall thin old man of about seventy-five, dressed in blue cloth and wearing his hunting-cap by permission of the ladies. Resembling the Duc de Bourbon, he was the Prince de Cadignan, Master of the Hunt, one of the last great French lords. Just as La Briere tried to slip behind the sofa to speak with Modeste, a man of thirty-eight, short, fat, and common in appearance entered.

“My son, the Prince de Loudon,” said the Duchesse de Verneuil to Modeste, who could not restrain her amazement at seeing the heir of a historic name reduced to such a commonplace figure.

“Gaspard,” called the duchess; he came, and she motioned to Modeste, “Mademoiselle de La Bastie, my friend.”

The heir presumptive, whose marriage with Desplein’s daughter had lately been arranged, bowed without seeming struck by her beauty. Modeste could compare this modern youth with the old reverence she had seen: the Prince de Cadignan’s homage to womanhood seemed as much to royalty as to femininity. The Duc de Rhetore, eldest son of the Duchesse de Chaulieu, bowed cavalierly. The contrast of fathers and sons suggested that young men no longer felt themselves great beings and dispensed with the duties of greatness. The fathers retained the politeness of vanished grandeur, like mountain-tops still gilded when twilight fills the valley.

Ernest at last slipped a word into Modeste’s ear, and she rose immediately.

“My dear,” thought the duchesse, mistaking Modeste for someone going to dress, and pulling a bell-rope, “they shall show you your apartment.”

Ernest accompanied Modeste to the foot of the grand staircase, presenting the plea of the luckless poet and trying to touch her feelings by describing Melchior’s agony.

“You see, he loves—he is a captive who thought he could break his chain.”

“Love in such a rapid seeker after fortune!” retorted Modeste.

“Mademoiselle, you are at the entrance of life; you do not know its defiles. The inconsistencies of a man who falls under the dominion of a woman much older than himself should be forgiven; he is really not accountable. Think how many sacrifices Canalis has made to her. He has sown too much seed of that kind to resign the harvest; the duchess represents to him ten years of devotion and happiness. You made him forget all that, and unfortunately, he has more vanity than pride; he did not reflect on what he was losing until he met Madame Chaulieu here today. If you really understood him, you would help him. He is a child, always mismanaging his life. You call him a seeker after fortune, but he seeks very badly; like all poets, he is a victim of sensations—childish, easily dazzled by anything that shines, and pursuing its glitter. He used to love horses and pictures, and he craved fame—well, he sold his pictures to buy armor and old Renaissance and Louis XV furniture; just now he is seeking political power. Admit that his hobbies are noble things.”

“You have said enough,” replied Modeste; “come,” she added, seeing her father, whom she called with a motion of her head to give her his arm; “come with me, and I will give you that scrap of paper; you shall carry it to the great man and assure him of my condescension to his wishes, but on one condition—you must thank him in my name for the pleasure I have taken in seeing one of the finest German plays performed in my honor. I have learned that Goethe’s masterpiece is neither Faust nor Egmont—” and, seeing Ernest’s puzzled look, she added, “It is Torquato Tasso! Tell Monsieur de Canalis to re-read it.” She smiled. “Repeat to your friend word for word what I say; it is not an epigram, it is the justification of his conduct—with this difference, that he will, I trust, become more and more reasonable, thanks to the folly of his Eleonore.”

The duchess’s head-woman conducted Modeste and her father to their apartment, where Francoise Cochet had already put everything in order; the colonel was astounded when he heard there were thirty other apartments in the chateau decorated with the same taste.

“This is what I call a proper country-house,” said Modeste.

“The Comte de La Bastie must build you one like it,” replied her father.

“Here, monsieur,” said Modeste, giving the bit of paper to Ernest; “carry it to our friend and put him out of his misery.”

The word our friend struck the young man’s heart. He looked at Modeste to see if there was anything real in the community of interests she seemed to admit, and she, understanding perfectly what his look meant, added, “Come, go at once, your friend is waiting.”

La Briere colored excessively, and left the room in a state of doubt and anxiety less endurable than despair. For the true lover the path that approaches happiness is like the narrow way Catholic poetry calls the entrance to Paradise—a dark passage echoing with the last cries of earthly anguish.

An hour later this illustrious company was assembled in the salon; some played whist, others conversed; the women had their embroideries, and all awaited the announcement of dinner. The Prince de Cadignan drew Monsieur Mignon out upon China and his campaigns under the empire, making him speak of the Portendueres, the L’Estorades, and the Maucombes, Provencal families; he blamed him for not seeking service and assured him nothing would be easier than to restore him to his rank as colonel of the Guard.

“A man of your birth and your fortune ought not to belong to the present Opposition,” said the prince, smiling.

This society of distinguished persons not only pleased Modeste but enabled her, during her stay, to acquire a perfection of manners she might otherwise have lacked. Seeing these women was an education; Modeste had the instinct to appropriate the distinctive qualities of Madame de Maufrigneuse and Madame de Chaulieu. Whereas a bourgeois would have ridiculed or made absurd their ways by clumsy imitation, a well-born, well-educated, right-minded young woman like Modeste fell naturally into connection with them and saw at once the differences separating the aristocratic world from the bourgeois world, the provinces from the faubourg Saint-Germain. She caught almost imperceptible shadings and perceived the grace of the grande dame without doubting she could acquire it. She noticed also that her father and La Briere appeared infinitely better in this Olympus than Canalis. The great poet, abdicating his real power—the mind—became nothing more than a courtier seeking a ministry, intriguing for an order, flattering the galaxy. Ernest de La Briere, without ambitions, could be himself; while Melchior became, to use a vulgar expression, a mere toady, courting the Prince de Loudon, the Duc de Rhetore, the Vicomte de Serizy, or the Duc de Maufrigneuse, like a man not free to assert himself, as Colonel Mignon did, proud of his campaigns and of Napoleon’s confidence. Modeste noted the strained efforts of the man of real talent seeking witticisms or compliments to please these grand personages. In a word, to Modeste’s eyes the peacock had plucked out his tail-feathers.

Toward the middle of the evening the young girl sat down with the grand equerry in a corner of the salon. She led him there purposely to end a suit she could no longer encourage if she wished to retain her self-respect.

“Monsieur le duc, if you really knew me,” she said, “you would understand how deeply I am touched by your attentions. It is because of the profound respect I feel for your character, and the friendship which a soul like yours inspires in mine, that I cannot endure to wound your self-love. Before your arrival in Havre I loved sincerely, deeply, and forever one who is worthy of being loved, and my affection for whom is still a secret; but I wish you to know—and in saying this I am more sincere than most young girls—that had I not already formed this voluntary attachment, you would have been my choice, for I recognize your noble and beautiful qualities. A few words your aunt and sister have said to me as to your intentions lead me to make this frank avowal. If you think it desirable, a letter from my mother shall recall me, on pretense of her illness, tomorrow morning before the hunt begins. Without your consent I do not choose to be present at a fete I owe to your kindness, and where, if my secret should escape me, you might feel hurt and defrauded. You will ask why I have come here at all. I could not withstand the invitation. Be generous enough not to reproach me for what was almost a necessary curiosity. But this is not the chief, not the most delicate thing I have to say you. You have firm friends in my father and myself—more so than perhaps you realize; and as my fortune was the first cause that brought you to me, I wish to say—but without intending to use it as a sedative to calm the grief gallantry requires you to testify—that my father has thought over the affair of the marshes, his friend Dumay thinks your project feasible, and they have already taken steps to form a company. Gobenheim, Dumay, and my father have subscribed fifteen hundred thousand francs, and undertake to get the rest from capitalists who will feel it in their interest to take up the matter. If I have not the honor of becoming the Duchesse d’Herouville, I have almost the certainty of enabling you to choose her, free from all trammels in your choice, and in a higher sphere than mine. Oh! let me finish,” she cried, at a gesture from the duke.

“Judging by my nephew’s emotion,” whispered Mademoiselle d’Herouville to her niece, “it is easy to see you have a sister.”

“Monsieur le duc, all this was settled in my mind the day of our first ride, when I heard you deplore your situation. This is what I have wished to say to you. That day determined my future life. Though you did not make the conquest of a woman, you have at least gained faithful friends at Ingouville—if you will deign to accord us that title.”

This careful, charming speech moved the grand equerry to tears; he seized her hand and kissed it.

“Stay during the hunt,” he said; “my want of merit has accustomed me to these refusals; but while accepting your friendship and that of the colonel, you must let me satisfy myself by the judgment of competent scientific men, that the draining of those marshes will be no risk to the company you speak of, before I agree to the generous offer of your friends. You are a noble girl, and though my heart aches to think I can only be your friend, I will glory in that title, and prove it to you at all times and in all seasons.”

“In that case, Monsieur le duc, let us keep our secret. My choice will not be known, at least I think not, until after my mother’s complete recovery. I should like our first blessing to come from her eyes.”
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“Ladies,” said the Prince de Cadignan, as the guests prepared to separate for the night, “I know that several of you propose to follow the hounds with us tomorrow, and it becomes my duty to tell you that if you will be Dianas you must rise, like Diana, with the dawn. The meet is for half-past eight o’clock. I have seen many women display greater courage than men, but for a few seconds only; and you will need a strong dose of resolution to keep you on horseback the whole day, except for a halt for breakfast, which we shall take, like true hunters and huntresses, on the nail. Are you still determined to show yourselves trained horse-women?”

“Prince, it is necessary for me to do so,” said Modeste, adroitly.

“I answer for myself,” said the Duchesse de Chaulieu.

“And I for my daughter Diane; she is worthy of her name,” added the prince. “So, then, you all persist in your intentions? However, I shall arrange, for the sake of Madame and Mademoiselle de Verneuil and others who stay at home, to drive the stag to the further end of the pond.”

“Make yourself quite easy, mesdames,” said the Prince de Loudon, when the Royal Huntsman had left the room; “that breakfast ‘on the nail’ will take place under a comfortable tent.”

At dawn the next day the weather promised a fine hunt: pale gray vapor gave way to blue the northwest wind would clear. As the party left the chateau, the Master of the Hunt, the Duc de Rhetore, and the Prince de Loudon rode ahead, noting the chateau’s white mass against autumn foliage.

“The ladies are fortunate in their weather,” remarked the Duc de Rhetore.

“Oh, in spite of all their boasting,” replied the Prince de Cadignan, “I think they will let us hunt without them!”

“So they might, if each had not a squire,” said the duke.

Their attention was drawn by a loud altercation at the forest of Rosembray, the meet. The cause was plain: the Prince de Loudon, enamored of English ways, had brought his own British hunting establishment and placed it under the Master of the Hunt. One of his men, a small fair Englishman—insolent, phlegmatic, neatly dressed—had posted himself on one side. John Barry wore a scarlet frock with buttons bearing the De Verneuil arms, white breeches, top-boots, a striped waistcoat, and carried a small hunting-whip and brass horn. He was first whipper-in to fifteen horses and sixty English hounds, which had cost the Duc de Verneuil much.

John found himself forestalled by three other whippers-in in charge of two royal packs brought in carts. They were the three best huntsmen of the Prince de Cadignan, a marked contrast to the Englishman in character and costume. Each wore a broad three-cornered hat above a swarthy, wrinkled face; lean, sinewy, vigorous—men for whom sport was passion. They bore immense horns of Dampierre, wound with green worsted, but chiefly controlled their hounds by eye and voice. Those noble animals, marked white, black, or liver, had distinct looks; some young ones yelped impatiently, the old scarred hounds lay quiet with ears to the earth.

As the Englishman came up, the royal dogs and huntsmen looked at each other as though to say, “If we cannot hunt by ourselves his Majesty’s service is insulted.”

Jests grew fiercer between Monsieur Jacquin La Roulie, the old French whipper-in, and John Barry. The two princes guessed the subject from afar, and the Master of the Hunt, setting spurs to his horse, brought it to an end with authority.

“Who drew the wood?”

“I, monseigneur,” said the Englishman.

“Very good,” said the Prince de Cadignan, proceeding to take Barry’s report.

Dogs and men became silent and respectful before the Royal Huntsman, as all recognized his dignity. The prince laid out the day’s work; a hunt is like a battle, and the Master of Charles X.’s hounds was their Napoleon. Thanks to the admirable system he had introduced into French venery, he could concentrate on the science and strategy of it. He assigned driving the stag to water to the Prince de Loudon’s establishment, expecting the royal hounds to send the stag into the Crown forest on the horizon before the chateau. He soothed his old huntsmen’s pride by giving them the most arduous task, while employing the Englishman at his specialty, letting the two systems face each other and show their strengths.

“Does monseigneur wish us to wait any longer?” said La Roulie.

“I know what you mean, old friend,” said the prince. “It is late, but—”

“Here come the ladies,” said the second whipper-in.

At that moment sixteen riders approached, four ladies at the front with green veils. Modeste, accompanied by her father the grand equerry and La Briere, rode in advance beside the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse, escorted by the Vicomte de Serizy. Behind them rode the Duchesse de Chaulieu, flanked by Canalis, on whom she smiled without rancor. When they reached the open where the huntsmen in red coats and brass bugles, surrounded by hounds, made a tableau, the Duchesse de Chaulieu, despite her embonpoint, rode up to Modeste, deeming it more dignified than avoiding the young woman she'd ignored the evening before.

When the Master of the Hunt finished his compliments on their punctuality, Eleonore observed the magnificent whip glittering in Modeste’s little hand and graciously asked to see it.

“I have never seen anything more beautiful,” she said, showing it to Diane de Maufrigneuse. “It suits its possessor,” she added, returning it.

“You must admit, Madame la duchesse,” answered Mademoiselle de La Bastie, with a tender malicious glance at La Briere, “that it is a rather strange gift from a future husband.”

“I should take it,” said Madame de Maufrigneuse, “as a declaration of my rights, in remembrance of Louis XIV.”

La Briere’s eyes filled; for a moment he dropped his reins; a second glance from Modeste bade him not betray his happiness. The hunt began.

The Duc d’Herouville said in a low voice to his fortunate rival, “Monsieur, I hope you will make your wife happy; if I can be of use, command me—I should be glad to contribute to the happiness of so charming a pair.”

This great day, on which such vast interests of heart and fortune were decided, caused but one anxiety to the Master of the Hunt—whether the stag would cross the pond and be killed on the lawn before the house; huntsmen of his calibre are chess-players who can foresee checkmate. The happy old man attained his wish; the run was magnificent, and the ladies were freed from his attendance for the hunt two days later—which, however, proved rainy.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The Duc de Verneuil’s guests stayed five days at Rosembray. On the last day the Gazette de France announced Baron de Canalis’s appointment as commander of the Legion of Honor and minister at Carlsruhe.

Early in December, after Desplein’s operation, Madame de La Bastie regained her sight; on seeing Ernest de La Brière she pressed Modeste’s hand and whispered, “I should have chosen him myself.”

By late February Latournelle had signed the deeds for the Provence estates, and the La Bastie family obtained the king’s signature to the marriage contract and the ordinance transmitting their title and arms to La Brière, who thereupon took the name La Brière‑La Bastie. Letters‑patent entailed the La Bastie estate at the end of April. La Brière’s witnesses at his marriage were Canalis and the minister he had served five years as secretary; the bride’s were the Duc d’Herouville and Desplein, whom the Mignons remembered with gratitude after showing their regard.

Later in this history we shall again meet Monsieur and Madame de La Brière‑La Bastie; readers will see how sweet and easy married life with an educated, intelligent woman can be. Modeste, avoiding the follies of pedantry, is the pride and happiness of her husband, her family, and all who surround her.

############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############

######## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ######## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################ ######## ################################################ ############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ############# ################################################
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

France, and especially Brittany, still shelters towns little touched by the nineteenth century’s progress. Poor roads and erratic communication with Paris make modern civilization a spectacle to be observed rather than embraced. These places keep old manners alive: a moral archaeologist studying people would find echoes of Louis XV’s time in Provence, of Louis XIV’s in Poitou, and even older survivals in Brittany. Most such towns once were capitals of petty feudal states—counties or duchies absorbed by the crown or divided by failures of male lines—and, disinherited from active life, their heads remained titles without substance.

In the last thirty years even these relics have begun to fade. Modern industry, aimed at masses, ignores the individualized creations of former artisans; products replace works. Public buildings are now retrospects; the monuments of modern industry are quarries, saltpeter mines, cotton factories. Soon these old cities will survive only in prints and drawings.

One town that still embodies the feudal type with rare fidelity is Guérande. Its name evokes images for painters and thinkers: this jewel of feudality commands the tides and dunes at the summit of a triangle with Croisic and Batz. Few towns save Vitré and Avignon preserve so intact a medieval character.

Guérande is ringed with stout walls; its moats are full, battlements entire, loopholes free of vegetation, ivy never mantling the towers. Three gates remain with portcullis rings; one enters by an iron-clamped drawbridge which, though never raised now, could be. The mayoralty was reproached in 1820 for planting poplars along the moat to shade the promenade, justifying it by noting the esplanade had long been a mall under elms. The houses keep their original frontages: none have added stories or been plastered, some rest on wooden columns forming arcades beneath which pedestrians still pass on bending planks. Merchant houses are small and low, fronts veneered with slate; carved window-casings and pillars display grotesque faces and fantastic beasts—relics prized by painters for their dusky tones and half-blurred features.

The streets are as they were four centuries ago, except that the population no longer throngs them. Social movement is so dead that a traveler may walk sadly alone beneath boarded mullioned windows closed against the window-tax. A street ends at a postern flanked by a masonry wall, beyond which rises a luxuriant bouquet of trees—one of the richest vegetations in France. Under the postern’s arch no voice comes from city life; the landscape is seen through casemate loop-holes once occupied by halberdiers and archers, like a belvedere arranged for a point of view. Every step recalls usages of long-past times; the stones themselves seem to speak. Medieval ideas and superstitions persist. A gendarme in modern uniform seems an anachronism; contemporary clothing is scarce and adapted to fixed customs and physiognomies.

The public square remains full of Breton costumes that artists flock to draw: white linen of paludiers (salt-gatherers) contrasting with peasants’ blues and browns and the sacredly preserved jewelry of women. These classes—paludiers, peasants, sailors with jerkins and varnished caps—stand as distinct castes, recognizing their distance from bourgeoisie, nobility, and clergy. Lines are clearly marked; revolutionary ideals found the masses too rugged to plane. The immutability attributed by science to zoological species seems to hold for Breton human nature. Even after 1830 Guérande remained apart—essentially Breton, fervently Catholic, silent and self-contained—a place where modern ideas gain little headway.

Its geography explains this. The town overlooks salt marshes producing Guérande salt, which Bretons credit for the excellence of their butter and sardines. It is connected by two roads only: one from Savenay, stopping at Saint-Nazaire; another from Vannes linking with Morbihan. Land communication is limited to an arrondissement road and from Saint-Nazaire by water with Nantes. The mail-coach does not ply the eighteen miles between the village and Guérande; not three coach passengers a year pass along it. These obstacles discourage travelers; government moves slowly; inhabitants willingly endure separation from the rest of France. Guérande, at the continent’s extreme end, leads nowhere; glad to be ignored, she thinks and cares for herself. The immense product of her salt‑marshes, which yields a tax of not less than a million francs to the Treasury, is chiefly managed at Croisic, a peninsula reached over quicksands that erase tracks at night and by boats crossing the sea arm that makes Croisic’s port.

Guérande is the Herculaneum of feudality—preserved by non-destruction rather than by life. Arriving from Croisic after crossing dreary salt-marshes, one feels a strong sensation at sight of its vast fortifications; from Saint‑Nazaire its picturesque position and naive grace please as much. The surrounding country is ravishing: hedges full of flowers, honeysuckles, roses, box, and other plants; like an English garden designed by a great hand. This rich nature, untrod, framed by an African-like oceanic desert banked by sand where paludiers in white move among melancholy swamps, yields a landscape of contrasts: gardens and dunes, flowers and barren sea.

Thus Guérande produces upon the mind the effect of a sleeping-draught. It is as silent as Venice. There is no public conveyance beyond the springless wagon of a carrier who bears travelers, merchandise, and occasionally letters to and from Saint-Nazaire. Bernus, the carrier in 1829, was the factotum of the community: he went and came when he pleased, ran errands for all. The arrival of a carriage—perhaps a lady or an invalid bound for Croisic for sea-bathing—is still a great event. Peasants come on horseback, many with commodities in sacks for barter, buying distinctive Breton bridal jewels, white linen, or cloth.

For ten miles round, Guérande is always GUÉRANDE—the town where a famous treaty was signed in 1365, the coast’s key, which may boast, like Batz, of a splendor lost in time. Jewels, linen, cloth, ribbon, and hats are made elsewhere but attributed to Guérande by buyers. Artists and some bourgeois who come feel, as at Venice, a desire—soon forgotten—to end their days amid its peace, walking the beautiful mall from gate to sea. This image lingers in memory: crowned with towers and clasped by her girdle, her flower-strewn robe floating, the golden mantle of dunes enfolding her; she fills the mind like an enchanting woman whose presence lingers in a fold of the heart.

Near the church stands a mansion to the town what the town is to the region—an emblem of the past, the symbol of a great thing destroyed. This mansion belongs to the noblest local family, the du Guaisnics, who in du Guesclin’s day were as superior in antiquity and fortune as Trojans to Romans. The Guaisnics (du Glaicquin), from which du Guesclin comes, are as old as Brittany’s granite; they are Bretons, Celts—once perhaps Druids gathering mistletoe and sacrificing on dolmens. Today this pure race—equal to the Rohans though not princes before Hugues Capet—possesses only two thousand francs a year, its mansion in Guérande, and the little castle of Guaisnic. All baronial lands are pledged to farmers and bring in sixty thousand francs despite ignorant cultivation. The du Guaisnics remain nominal owners, unable for two centuries to pay off advances; they are in the position of the crown toward its engagistes before 1789. Once the fiefs under Guaisnic were worth fifty thousand francs a year; but a vote of the National Assembly suppressed seigneurs’ dues on inheritance. Thus, a family of little account in France, laughed at in Paris, is in Guérande the whole of Brittany. In that town the Baron du Guaisnic is one of the great barons of France, a man above whom there is but one—the King. The name du Guaisnic, full of Breton significance (roots explained in The Chouans), has been altered in administration to du Guenic.

At the end of a silent, damp lane stands an arch wide enough for a horseman—proof that carriages did not exist when it was built. The granite arch frames an oak gate studded with enormous nails in geometric figures. The semicircular arch is carved with the Guaisnics’ arms as clean-cut as though the sculptor had just laid down his chisel. The escutcheon delights heraldry lovers by its simplicity, proving the family’s pride and antiquity: gules, a hand proper gonfaloned ermine, with a sword argent in pale, and the motto FAC. A baronial coronet crowns this simple badge; the hand holds the sword with a proud, chivalrous turn. The depth of carving, sheltered from weather by the arch, matches the depth of the motto in the family soul. To those who know the Guaisnics this fact is touching.

When the gate opens, a somewhat vast courtyard appears: stables on the right, kitchen and offices on the left. The house is freestone from cellar to garret. The courtyard facade has a portico with a double range of steps, its wall covered with carved vestiges now effaced, yet an antiquary can still discern the Hand bearing the sword. Granite steps are disjointed, grasses and moss grow through fissures, yet solidity remains. The oak, nail-studded door opens to a noble hall, at the end of which another door opens to a portico leading to the garden.

This hall is marvelously preserved. Three-foot chestnut wainscot, Spanish leather wall coverings with relief figures and fading gilding, and a wooden painted ceiling with faint gold, red flowers, and green foliage suggest great age—perhaps dating to or restored in Louis XI’s reign. The immense carved stone chimney-piece contains giant wrought-iron andirons; it could hold a cart-load of wood. Furniture is wholly oak, each article bearing the family arms. Three English guns fit for chase or war, three sabres, game-bags and fishermen’s utensils hang from wall nails.

A dining-room communicates with the kitchen by a door cut through a corner tower; this tower corresponds on the courtyard facade with another tower at the opposite corner where a spiral staircase leads to the two upper stories. The dining-room is hung with fourteenth-century tapestries whose banderols’ orthography proves their age, framed in bands of oak black as ebony. Projecting rafters are enriched with foliage, the spaces between painted blue with garlands of golden flowers. Two old buffers face each other; on shelves, as in 1200, old goblets, an ancient embossed soup-tureen, two silver salt-cellars, many pewter plates and pitchers of gray and blue pottery with arabesque designs and the du Guaisnics’ arms appear, covered by hinged pewter lids. The chimney-piece here is modernized—evidence the family has inhabited this room within the last century. A mirror is let in the back of the new carved Louis XV mantel; a tall tortoiseshell clock inlaid with brass stands on the velvet-covered mantel, flanked by silver candelabra of singular design. A large square table fills the center; chairs are turned wood covered with tapestry. By a garden window is an odd lamp: a glass globe about the size of an ostrich egg with a wick drawing nut-oil through a brass reed. Windows into the garden and courtyard are mullioned with hexagonal panes, draped by heavy crimson silk valances once called brocatelle.

Each of the two upper stories has but two rooms: the head of the family’s bedroom and the children’s. Guests lodged beneath the roof; servants above kitchens and stables. The pointed roof, lead-protected at angles, has noble pointed windows on each side, one looking down on the courtyard, the other on the garden. These windows rise almost to the roof with slender casings now crumbling under salty vapors. Above each arch with stone crossbars turns a vane of a noble.

A precious detail worth noting: the spiral-stair tower is placed at the corner of a great gable wall with no window; the staircase descends to a little arched door opening on a gravelly yard between house and stables. This tower is repeated on the garden side by another five-sided tower ending in a cupola containing a bell-turret instead of being roofed like its sister with a pepper-pot. These towers connect on the first floor by a stone gallery supported by brackets each ending in a grotesque human head; the gallery has an exquisite balustrade. From the gable hangs a stone dais like those crowning saints’ statues at church portals. One can picture a woman walking this balcony, gazing over Guérande at sunshine gilding the sands and shimmering on the ocean. The gable wall is flanked by varied towers; the opposite gable adjoins the next house. Harmony sought by architects of those days is maintained on the courtyard facade by a tower communicating between dining-room and kitchen, the same as the staircase tower except it stops at the first upper story, surmounted by a small open dome beneath which stands a now blackened statue of Saint Calyste.

The garden is magnificent for so old a place. It covers half an acre, its walls espaliered; the space divided into vegetable squares bordered with cordons of fruit-trees tended by the man-of-all-work, Gasselin, who also grooms horses. At the farther end is a grotto with a seat; in the middle, a sun-dial; paths are graveled. The garden facade lacks towers corresponding to those on the courtyard, but a slender spiral column rises from ground to roof that once bore the family banner—an iron socket at its summit where a few weak plants straggle. This detail, with vestiges of sculpture, proves to a practiced eye that a Venetian architect built the mansion: the graceful staff is a signature revealing Venice, chivalry, and thirteenth-century delicacy. Trefoils with four leaves instead of three, present in the ornamentation, point to the Venetian school’s semi-Saracenic influence, a deviation from the Christian art faithful to the Trinity.

If this ancient dwelling attracts the imagination, one may ask why such marvels are not renewed. Because today mansions are sold, pulled down, and their grounds turned into streets. No one is sure the next generation will possess the paternal dwelling; homes are more like inns; whereas in former times building was done for a family in perpetuity. Hence the grandeur of these houses: faith in self, as well as faith in God, produced prodigies.

From the ground-floor description and the account of the family’s manners and physiognomy, the arrangement of the upper rooms may be imagined. For the last fifty years the du Guaisnics have received friends in these two rooms; in them, and in the courtyard and external accessories, the spirit, grace, and candor of old noble Brittany still survive. Without the town’s topography and the house’s minute depiction, the surprising figures of the family might be less understood; frames have thus preceded portraits. Everything influences persons: public buildings affect those who dwell nearby. It would be difficult to be irreligious in the shadow of a cathedral like Bourges. Surrounded by reminders of destiny, souls are less apt to stray. Such was our immediate grandfathers’ thought, now abandoned by a generation soon to have no signs, no distinctions, and whose manners and morals change with each decade. If you do not expect now to find the Baron du Guaisnic sword in hand, all here written would be falsehood.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Early in May 1836 the Guenic household comprised Monsieur and Madame du Guenic, Mademoiselle Zéphirine du Guenic (the baron’s elder sister), and the only son, twenty-one-year-old Gaudebert-Calyste-Louis—by family usage; the father was Gaudebert-Calyste-Charles. Saint Gaudebert and Saint Calyste were ever invoked as patrons of the Guenics.

The Baron du Guenic had left Guérande at the first Vendée and Brittany uprisings, fighting through the war with Charette, Cathelineau, La Rochejaquelein, d’Elbée, Bonchamps, and the Prince de Loudon. Prudently, before departing he sold his property to his sister, Mademoiselle Zéphirine. After the leaders’ deaths the baron, miraculously spared, refused to submit to Napoleon and continued resisting until 1802; defeated and nearly captured, he returned to Guérande, went to Croisic, and crossed to Ireland, faithful to the old Breton hatred for England.

The people of Guérande feigned ignorance of him; in twenty years not a word betrayed him. Mademoiselle collected the rents and forwarded them by fishermen. Monsieur du Guenic returned to Guérande in 1813 as quietly as if he had been but at Nantes. During his stay in Dublin the old Breton, well past fifty, fell in love with an Irishwoman, Fanny O’Brien, daughter of a noble but poor family; she was twenty-one. He went to France for marriage papers, returned to Ireland, and about ten months later, in early 1814, brought his wife to Guérande. She gave him Calyste on the same day Louis XVIII landed at Calais—hence the final name Louis.

The old Breton was now seventy-three; his long guerrilla warfare, exile, and sea crossings had prematurely aged him, making him seem much older. As head of the house he suited the worn grandeur of their dwelling, built in the days when a court still reigned at Guérande.

Monsieur du Guenic was tall, straight, wiry, and lean, his oval face furrowed with wrinkles like a Rembrandt—sunken, weathered by outdoor life and watching his country. Enough remained to touch the soul: a firm brow, solemn lines, a rigid nose bearing scars of conflict, declaring intrepidity, uncalculating faith, strict obedience, uncompromising fidelity, and steadfast love. In him, Breton granite had become man.

The baron had no teeth; his drawn lips lent a threatening and proud expression. His chin seemed to reach for his humped nose, betraying energy and Breton resistance. His marbled skin suggested a sanguine temperament fit to bear fatigue. Abundant white hair fell in curls on his shoulders. In his black eyes, despite an extinguished face, still glittered the last flames of a generous, loyal soul. Eyebrows and lashes were gone; shaving had become difficult, so he let his beard grow fan-shaped. His hands—large, broad, hairy—were those of a soldier and guerrilla, hands that had handled sword and oar, fought among thorns and marshes; recent marks showed he had again joined the Vendée. These hands embodied the Guenic motto: Fac!

His forehead, temples, and receding hair added majesty to the noble ruin. His countenance, material and Breton, presented a certain savagery and stoic calm, resembling Huguenot impassibility—a repose where animal nature predominated and thought was relative; thought lived more in the heart than the head and led to acts rather than speculation. He had faiths and sentiments inborn, so institutions and religion supplied much of his thought. He reserved his mind for action, drawing thought from his heart as he would draw his sword—sincerity shining on his scutcheon. That practical mindset explained the sale to his sister before the war—provision against death, confiscation, and exile. The nobility of character in these two old people—the sister lived wholly for the brother—cannot be understood by selfish modern morals. Not an archangel could find a selfish thought in them. In 1814, when the rector of Guérande urged the baron to go to Paris to claim recompense from the Bourbons, Mademoiselle cried, “Oh, fy! does my brother need to hold out his hand like a beggar?” “It would be thought I served a king from interest,” said the baron. “Besides, it is for him to remember. Poor king! he must be weary indeed of those who harass him.”

This loyal servant, who had spent life and means on Louis XVIII, obtained the rank of colonel, the cross of Saint-Louis, and a stipend of two thousand francs a year—news the baron accepted as the king’s remembrance. No one undeceived him; the gift had been made by the Duc de Feltre. To show gratitude he held a siege at Guérande against General Travot; refusing to surrender the fortress, he later evacuated into the woods with a band of Chouans who remained armed until the second Bourbon restoration. Guérande still remembers that siege.

The Baron du Guenic was as illiterate as a peasant; he could read, write, and cipher a little, knew military art and heraldry, but outside his prayer-book had read scarcely three volumes in his life. His clothing was always the same: stout shoes, ribbed stockings, greenish velveteen breeches, a cloth waistcoat, and a loose coat with a collar from which hung the cross of Saint-Louis. A noble serenity reigned on his face; for the last year or so he had often fallen into somnolent pauses, a prelude to death that did not alarm his wife, blind sister, or friends—these silences seemed natural: the baron had done his duty.

In this ancient mansion the absorbing interests were the fortunes of the dispossessed elder branch, the future of the exiled Bourbons, the Catholic religion, and political innovations in Brittany. The one personal attachment was the family’s devotion to the only son, Calyste, heir and sole hope of the du Guenic name.

Some years earlier the old Vendean had a return of youth and took to training his son in manly exercises fit for a gentleman ready to take arms. When Calyste was sixteen, his father took him to the marshes and forest, teaching the rudiments of war by the chase, setting examples in horsemanship and marksmanship, indifferent to fatigue, intrepid before obstacles, and bidding his son follow him into danger.

So, when the Duchesse de Berry landed for her restoration attempt, the father judged it right to take his boy and join her, practicing the family motto. The baron started at night, saying nothing to his wife, taking his son under fire as if to a fête, accompanied by Gasselin, his sole vassal. The three were absent three months without sending news to the baroness, who read the Quotidienne with trembling, nor to the blind sister, whose nerve never faltered. The three guns on the walls had seen recent service. The baron, deeming the enterprise useless, left the region before the affair of La Pénissière; otherwise the house of Guenic might have ended in hecatomb.

On a stormy night after parting from Madame, the father, son, and servant returned, surprising the baroness and Mademoiselle, though the blind sister recognized their steps in the lane. The baron looked around the anxious circle seated by the antique lamp and said in a tremulous voice, while Gasselin replaced guns and sabres, in words of feudal simplicity: “The barons did not all do their duty.” Then he kissed his wife and sister, sat in his old arm-chair, and ordered supper for his son, Gasselin, and himself. Gasselin had thrown himself before Calyste to protect him and received a sabre cut on his shoulder; the women scarcely thanked him. The baron and guests uttered neither curses nor complaints about conquerors—silence a Breton trait. In forty years no one ever heard contumely from the baron about his adversaries; it was for them to do their duty as he did his. This silence indicated an unalterable will.

This last exertion—a flash of waning energy—had caused the present weakness and somnolence. The fresh defeat and exile of the Bourbons, as dramatic in defeat as in their brief restoration, were a bitter sorrow.

About six in the evening, when the baron, who customarily dined at four, fell asleep while his wife read the Quotidienne, his head rested against the arm-chair back by the fireplace on the garden side.

Near that gnarled ancient tree and before the fire, the baroness sat—a type common in England, Scotland, and Ireland: milk-white, golden-haired, curls wound as if by angels, a heavenly light in her hair. Fanny O’Brien was one of those sylphs—strong in tenderness, invincible under misfortune, soft in voice, pure in her blue eyes; her skin silken to the touch, beauty beyond painter or word. Still handsome at forty-two, many would have been glad to wed her on that autumn evening as she watched the display of flowers and fruit.

She held the paper in dimpled fingers; half reclining, feet out to warm, she wore a black velvet gown against the chill. Her hair, in English ringlets with the rest twisted to the crown and pinned, gleamed like burnished gold. She braided a short nape-lock—a racial sign—tucking it into the coif, a small vanity showing the care she took; it was her pride to please the old baron. When a woman displays coquetry at home rather than outside, she is a noble mother and wife; she loves without calculation, does good in secret, and worships. In Fanny a Virgin’s halo seemed to preserve her beauty from time. Her skin had warm pearly tones; her broad, well-modeled brow caught the light; turquoise eyes shone with incomparable sweetness; soft lashes and slightly sunken temples lent mute melancholy. Her aquiline thin nose suggested high birth; pure lips wore happy smiles of inexhaustible loving-kindness. She had gained slight embonpoint lately, but hips and a slender waist remained pleasing. Autumn had added noble rounded arms, finer skin, and fuller outlines. Above all, her open serene face and clear blue eyes expressed boundless sympathy and angelic tenderness.

At the other hearth, in an arm-chair, the octogenarian sister sat knitting stockings—a craft needing no sight. Both eyes had cataracts; she refused an operation despite her sister-in-law’s entreaties. Secretly she said it was want of courage; truly, she would not let her brother spend twenty-five louis for her. That sum saved would benefit the household.

These two older persons threw the baroness’ beauty into relief. Mademoiselle Zéphirine, deprived of sight, was unaware of age’s changes in her face. Her pale hollow countenance, white sightless eyes giving an appearance of death, and three or four solitary projecting teeth framed by a little brown printed cotton hood quilted and trimmed with a ruche, seemed severe. She wore a cotillon over a quilted petticoat (a real mattress hiding double louis-d’or) and pockets sewn to a belt she unfastened each night and put on each morning like a separate garment. Her body was encased in a casaquin of Brittany, a spencer with a pleated collar; the washing of which caused her only dispute with her sister-in-law—she changed it only once a week. From the large wadded sleeves came two withered yet vigorous arms and brownish-red hands that knitted tirelessly; at times she would thrust a long needle between hood and hair to poke her white locks, then thrust it back carelessly. She was straight as a steeple; her erect, imposing carriage might be an old coquetry proving pride a necessary passion. Her smile was gay. She, too, had done her duty.

As soon as the baroness saw her husband asleep, she ceased reading. A ray of sunshine crossed the old room, burnishing black furniture, touching carvings, and brightening the oak table; Fanny’s voice and that light seemed to make music in the blind sister’s heart. The ray waned into twilight; the baroness fell into deep meditation, a silence noted by Mademoiselle for the last two weeks, unexplained and unasked about. Mademoiselle continued to knit; silence deepened until the needles’ clicking was plainly heard.

“You have dropped the paper, sister, but you are not asleep,” said Mademoiselle slyly.

Mariotte then entered to light the lamp, placed it by the fire, fetched her distaff and ball of thread, and sat on a stool in the window recess to spin. Gasselin busied himself about the offices, tended the horses, readied the stable, and fed the two hunting-dogs; their joyful barking was the last noise to awaken echoes in the dark walls. The two dogs and two horses were the remaining vestiges of the house’s chivalry. An imaginative man sitting on the portico steps might have quivered at the hounds’ baying and the horses’ neighing.

Gasselin was a short, thick Breton with black hair and a sun-browned face—silent, slow, obstinate as a mule, yet steady. Forty-two, he had been in the household twenty-five years, hired by Mademoiselle at fifteen on hearing of the marriage and the baron’s probable return. He considered himself part of the family, had played with Calyste, loved the horses and dogs, and wore a blue linen jacket with flapping pockets, matching waistcoat and trousers, blue stockings, and stout hob-nailed shoes; in cold he donned a goatskin.

Mariotte, also over forty, was to woman what Gasselin was to man—same complexion and figure, lively black eyes. Curiously they never married; perhaps it seemed improper, they were so like a brother and sister. Mariotte’s wages were ninety francs a year; Gasselin’s, three hundred. But no thousands elsewhere would have tempted either to leave the Guenic household. Both were under Mademoiselle’s orders, who, from the war to the baron’s return, had ruled the house. When she learned the baron would bring home a mistress, she feared losing the household sceptre, yet she was agreeably surprised to find in Fanny O’Brien a woman of high birth who disliked petty domestic cares and preferred plain fare to elaborate self-indulgence. Fanny could do all duties, was strong in privation but without taste for commonplace work. When the baron, at his wife’s request, begged his sister to continue in charge, the old maid kissed the baroness like a sister, made a daughter of her, adored her, and rejoiced to keep control of household management. She ran the house on an almost inconceivable economy, relaxing only for great events—lying-in, Calyste’s needs, and similar occasions.

Though the two servants were used to the stern regime and needed no orders—masters’ interests being their own—Mademoiselle insisted on overseeing everything. She knew, without looking, how many nuts were in the barn or oats in the bin, carrying a boatswain’s whistle on her belt to summon Mariotte with one note and Gasselin with two.

Gasselin’s greatest happiness was tending the garden, producing fine fruits and vegetables; with so little else to do he would have been lost without it. After grooming horses and cleaning the ground-floor rooms, he weeded and watched for a field-mouse or the grub of the cockchafer, joyfully presenting his catch to his masters. He liked going to Croisic on fast-days to buy fish cheaper than at Guérande.

Thus no household was more united in interests, more bound together than this noble family devoted to duty. Masters and servants seemed made for one another. For twenty-five years there had been neither trouble nor discord. The only griefs were the boy’s petty ailments, the only terrors the events of 1814 and 1830. If the same tasks were performed at the same hours, and food obeyed seasons and times, this monotony, like nature’s, varied only by cloud and sun, and was sustained by affection in all hearts—the more fruitful because it sprang from natural causes.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
When night had fairly fallen, Gasselin entered the hall and asked his master respectfully if he was still needed.

“You can go out, or go to bed, after prayers,” replied the baron, waking, “unless Madame or my sister—”

The two ladies signaled assent. Gasselin knelt as his masters rose to kneel on their chairs; Mariotte also knelt before her stool. Mademoiselle du Guenic then said the prayer aloud. After it was over, someone rapped at the door on the lane and Gasselin opened it.

“I dare say it is Monsieur le curé; he usually comes first,” said Mariotte.

Everyone recognized the rector’s foot on the portico steps. He bowed respectfully to the three occupants and addressed them with the unctuous civility of a priest. To the somewhat absent-minded greeting of the mistress of the house he replied with ecclesiastical inquiry.

“Are you anxious or ill, Madame la baronne?” he asked.

“Thank you, no,” she replied.

Monsieur Grimont, about fifty, of middle height, lost in his cassock from which issued two stout shoes, showed a plump visage and generally pale skin with yellow spots. His dimpled hands and placid abbatial face suggested a Dutch burgomaster; his straight black hair and lively brown eyes recalled a Breton peasant. His gaiety, that of a man whose conscience is calm, admitted a joke. He lacked the unease of rectors whose power is contested; seen through Guerande, even the most irreligious traveler would recognize him as the sovereign of that Catholic town. Yet before the du Guenics he lowered his spiritual superiority; in their salon he was a chaplain in his seigneur’s house. In church his benediction always favored the Guenic chapel, where their mailed hand and device adorned the keystone.

“I thought Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel had already arrived,” said the rector, sitting and taking the baroness’s hand to kiss it. “She is getting unpunctual. Can it be that dissipation is contagious? I see Monsieur le chevalier is again at Les Touches this evening.”

“Don’t say anything about those visits before Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel,” cried the old maid eagerly.

“Ah! mademoiselle,” remarked Mariotte, “you can’t prevent the town from gossiping.”

“What do they say?” asked the baroness.

“The young girls and the old women all say he is in love with Mademoiselle des Touches.”

“A lad of Calyste’s make is playing his proper part in making the women love him,” said the baron.

“Here comes Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel,” said Mariotte.

The gravel in the courtyard crackled under discreet footsteps; a page with a lantern accompanied the coming lady. Seeing him, Mariotte moved her stool to the great hall to talk with him by the lantern’s gleam, economizing the hall’s single oribus candle.

This elderly demoiselle was thin, dried-up, yellow as parchment, wrinkled, with gray eyes, prominent teeth, and the hands of a man. Short, slightly crooked, possibly hump-backed, she stirred a great mass of petticoats when reaching a pocket; a loud jingling of keys and money echoed among her garments. She wore, dangling from one side, the keys of a good housekeeper, and from the other a silver snuff-box, thimble, knitting-needles, and other resonant implements. Instead of Zephirine’s wadded hood she had a green bonnet—once fresh, now yellowing—of a fashion preserved by her nieces and renewed every five years at Nantes. Her gowns, too, were made by her nieces to an immutable pattern. She used the old cane with its short hook and bore the ermine of ancient Breton dukes. In her and her sister the illustrious house of the Pen-Hoels ended. Her younger sister had married a Kergarouet who, despite disapproval, added Pen-Hoel to his name and called himself Vicomte de Kergarouet-Pen-Hoel.

“Heaven has punished him,” said the old lady; “he has nothing but daughters, and the Kergarouet-Pen-Hoel name will be wiped out.”

Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel had about seven thousand francs a year from rents. She had come into her property at thirty-six and managed it herself, inspecting it on horseback and displaying the firmness often seen in deformed persons. Her avarice was admired locally; she kept one woman-servant and the page, and her yearly expenses, excluding taxes, did not exceed a thousand francs. Thus she attracted the caresses of the Kergarouet-Pen-Hoels, who spent winters at Nantes and summers on the Loire estate. She was supposed ready to leave her fortune to whichever niece pleased her best; every three months one of the four demoiselles de Kergarouet-Pen-Hoel (the youngest twelve, the eldest twenty) came to spend a few days with her.

A friend of Zephirine du Guenic, Jacqueline de Pen-Hoel, devoted to the Breton grandeur of the du Guenics, had planned since Calyste’s birth to transmit her property to the chevalier by marrying him to whichever niece the Vicomtesse de Kergarouet-Pen-Hoel would bestow. She dreamed of buying back some of the best Guenic property from the farmer engagistes. When avarice has an object it ceases to be a vice; it becomes a means of virtue. Perhaps Zephirine knew Jacqueline’s plan. Perhaps even the baroness, whose soul was occupied by love for her son and tenderness for his father, guessed it, seeing with what wily perseverance Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel brought her favorite niece, Charlotte de Kergarouet, now sixteen. The rector, Monsieur Grimont, was certainly in her confidence; he helped the old maid invest her savings.

But even had Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel possessed three hundred thousand francs in gold, the du Guenics would never have shown any attention that could be construed as seeking her fortune. From true Breton pride, Jacqueline, glad of the supremacy accorded to Zephirine and the du Guenics, always showed herself honored by her relations with Madame du Guenic and her sister-in-law. She even concealed the sacrifice she made each evening in allowing her page to burn in the Guenic hall that singular gingerbread-colored candle called an oribus still used in western France.

Thus this rich old maid was nobility, pride, and grandeur personified. At the moment of this narrative, the Abbe Grimont has revealed that on the evening when the old baron, the young chevalier, and Gasselin secretly departed to join MADAME (to the terror of the baroness and the great joy of all Bretons) Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel had given the baron ten thousand francs in gold—an immense sacrifice—to which the abbe added another ten thousand, a tithe collected by him—charging the old hero to offer the whole, in the name of the Pen-Hoels and of the parish of Guerande, to the mother of Henri V.

Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel treated Calyste as if her intentions gave her rights over him; her plans seemed to authorize supervision. Not strict in gallantry—she had the indulgence of old women of the old school—she held in horror modern ways. Calyste might increase her esteem by a few Breton adventures but would lose it considerably if she saw him entangled in what she called innovations. She might disinter a little gold to pay for the results of a love-affair, but if Calyste drove a tilbury or spoke of a visit to Paris she would deem him dissipated and a spendthrift. To her, novel ideas meant the overthrow of crop succession, ruin under the name of improvements, mortgaged lands as the inevitable result of experiments. Prudence was the true method of making fortune; good management meant filling granaries and waiting for a rise rather than risking experiments. By singular chance she often made lucky sales that confirmed her principles. She was thought maliciously clever but was not quick-witted; methodical as a Dutchman, prudent as a cat, and persistent as a priest, those qualities in routine-bound Brittany were practically equivalent to intellect.

“Will Monsieur du Halga join us this evening?” asked Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel, taking off her knitted mittens after the usual exchange of greetings.

“Yes, mademoiselle; I met him taking his dog to walk on the mall,” replied the rector.

“Ha! then our mouche will be lively to-night. Last evening we were only four.”

At the word mouche the rector rose and took from a drawer a small round osier basket, a pile of ivory counters yellow with use, and a greasy pack of cards. He arranged counters before each player and set the basket beside the lamp with infantine eagerness and the manner of a man accustomed to this service.

A firm knock at the outer gate echoed through the mansion. The page opened, and presently the long, lean, methodically clothed Chevalier du Halga, former flag-captain to Admiral de Kergarouet, outlined himself in black on the portico.

“Welcome, chevalier!” cried Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel.

“The altar is raised,” said the abbe.

The chevalier was a man in poor health, who wore flannel for his rheumatism, a black-silk skull-cap to protect his head from fog, and a spencer to guard his chest from sudden gusts. He always carried a gold-headed cane and a little bitch named Thisbe. Fussy as a fine lady and speaking low to spare his voice, he had in earlier days been among the most intrepid officers of the old navy, winning the confidence of de Suffren and the friendship of the Comte de Portenduere. His splendid conduct as flag-captain to Admiral Kergarouet was written on his scarred face. Now no one would imagine the voice that ruled storms and the eye that compassed the sea.

The chevalier never smoked, never swore; he was gentle and tranquil as a girl, as concerned about Thisbe’s caprices as an elderly dowager.
