He moved as an invalid, walking as if stepping on eggs, complaining of wind, sun, or mist, yet showed the whitest teeth in the reddest gums—a fact reassuring as to his maladies, which were rather expensive, consisting of four daily meals of monastic amplitude. His frame, like the baron’s, was bony and indestructibly strong, covered with a parchment-like skin retaining the tawny color from India, whence, however, he did not bring back facts or ideas. He had emigrated with his friends, lost his property, and now ended his days with the cross of Saint-Louis and a pension of two thousand francs from the Invalides de la Marine. The slight hypochondria that made him invent imaginary ills is explained by actual suffering during the emigration. He served in the Russian navy until Emperor Alexander ordered him employed against France; he then resigned and lived at Odessa near the Duc de Richelieu, with whom he returned to France. The duke obtained for this relic of the old Breton navy the pension that enabled him to live. On the death of Louis XVIII he returned to Guerande and eventually became mayor of the city.

For the last fifteen years the rector, the chevalier, and Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel had regularly passed their evenings at the hôtel de Guenic, where other noble neighbors also came. It will be readily understood that the du Guenics were at the head of the faubourg Saint-Germain of the old Breton province, where no member of the new administration sent down by the government was ever allowed to penetrate. For the last six years the rector coughed when coming to the crucial words, Domine, salvum fac regem. Politics in Guerande remained at that point.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Mouche is played with five cards to each player and one turned up for trumps. Each round a player may play or abstain; abstaining costs only his stake unless there are forfeits in the basket. If he plays and wins a trick he is paid pro rata to the stake; if he wins none he is mouche, paying the whole stake and swelling the next basket. Those who decline throw down their cards; their play is null. Players may exchange cards with the remainder of the pack in sequence, so the first two sometimes take most of it. The turned-up trump belongs to the dealer, who is last and may exchange it for any card in his hand. One powerful card, the knave of clubs, is called Mistigris.

The game provokes cupidity, diplomacy, and poker faces. At the hotel du Guenic each player took twenty counters representing five sous, making the stake five farthings—a significant sum for this company. In extraordinary luck fifty sous could be won—more than anyone in Guerande spent in a day. Consequently Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel played mouche with the passion of a big-game hunter. Mademoiselle Zephirine, who shared with the baroness, attached equal importance, treating one farthing for a chance at five as a serious investment.

An 1825 convention stated the game should cease when anyone losing ten sous wished to retire; politeness forbade continuing without him. The chevalier and the baron, however, found a way round this: when all but one wished to continue, du Halga, a rich free-spending sailor, would offer ten counters to Zephirine or Jacqueline, recompensing them if they won. The baron made similar offers under the pretext of continuing the game. The misers accepted, though not without pressing—a maidens’ custom—but only after the baron and chevalier had won, or the offer would insult them.

Mouche became brilliant when a Demoiselle de Kergarouet passed through with her aunt. The Kergarouets insisted on being called simply Kergarouet. The aunt treated the niece to mouche at du Guenic and ordered her to look amiable—easy in Calyste’s presence, whom the four Kergarouet girls adored. Brought up in modern ways, they cared little for five sous; stakes rose. These evenings excited the old blind sister, who signaled stakes to the baroness by pressing her foot a number of times. To play or not when the basket was full provoked inward struggle—cupidity versus fear. If Charlotte de Kergarouet was lucky, her aunt would nevertheless lecture her on reserve and modesty for appearing too decisive.

These nightly games, carried on for twenty years, were sometimes interrupted by gossip or political discussion. Players often sat half an hour talking with cards fanned on their stomachs. If a counter was missing from the basket, everyone declared they had put in their share. Usually the chevalier made up the deficiency and was accused of being absent-minded about his health; when he paid, Zephirine and Jacqueline fretted they themselves might have forgotten. The company enjoyed the small suspicions. Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel would almost always accuse the rector of cheating when he won the basket.

“It is singular,” he replied, “that I never cheat except when I win the trick.”

Sometimes talk of royal misfortunes would distract the baron, and evenings could end without gain or loss but with emotion and complaints that mouche was dull. Once, after the Kergarouets arrived, they tried whist and boston at the baroness’s urging but could not understand them; on departure the new games were voted head-splitters and the company returned to mouche—tradition triumphing in Brittany.

While the rector dealt, the baroness asked the Chevalier du Halga the same health questions as the night before. The chevalier made it a point to invent new ailments; he never complained of real wounds but of fancied pains—the doctors have found no remedy for illnesses that don't exist. “Yesterday the trouble was, I believe, in your legs,” said the rector. “It moves about,” said the chevalier. The exchange, with light banter from Zephirine and Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel, displayed the chevalier’s old wit and a life rich in memories.

The conversation turned to the death of the Duc de Lenoncourt, and the baron remarked he would be next; his wife gently rebuked him. The company noticed the baron’s melancholy and afterward would say, “Monsieur du Guenic was sad to-night.” The phrase “The Baron du Guenic fails” opened many conversations.

“How is Thisbe?” asked Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel. “The poor thing is like her master,” the chevalier replied, mimicking the dog and exposing his hand—an attempt by a neighbor to see if he had Mistigris or the trump. The baroness observed the rector’s nose grow pale—his sign of holding Mistigris—because of the pleasure that card gave him.

“You had company today,” said the chevalier. “Yes, a cousin of my brother-in-law,” she replied; she told of the marriage of the Comtesse de Kergarouet, a Demoiselle de Fontaine. The chevalier, who knew the admiral, recognized the name. She then astonished them with tales about Mademoiselle des Touches—so strange she could hardly believe them; if true, Calyste would not be so constantly with her. The baron pricked up his ears.

But Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel suddenly had Mistigris and could not continue. “Your play, monsieur le baron,” she said. Zephirine returned to knitting; the rector, informed about Calyste and Mademoiselle des Touches, refrained. The baron inquired again: “What is so extraordinary?” “She smokes,” Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel said. The chevalier commented dryly. Asked about her property, the old maid said she was squandering it.

“The game is mine!” cried the baroness; she had a long run of trumps and Mistigris. The victory horrified Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel, who forgot Calyste and Mademoiselle des Touches for a moment. By nine only the baroness and rector remained; the others had gone to bed. The chevalier, as usual, accompanied Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel home, commenting on Zephirine’s delight in engulfing her gains. He had also noticed the baroness’ abstraction.

At Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel’s door she confided the cause: “Calyste is lost unless we marry him promptly. He loves Mademoiselle des Touches, an actress!” “Then send for Charlotte.” “I have; my sister will receive my letter tomorrow,” she replied.

From this sketch of a normal evening one may imagine the hubbub excited throughout Guerande by any stranger’s arrival, stay, or passage.

When silence came from the baron’s and his sister’s rooms, the baroness looked at the rector, who toyed pensively with the counters. “I see that you begin to share my anxiety about Calyste,” she said. “Did you notice Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel’s displeased looks to-night?” he asked. She had the best intentions for Calyste; she loved him as though he were her son. His conduct in the Vendée beside his father and Madame's praises of his devotion had increased that affection. Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel intends to execute a deed of gift leaving her property to whichever niece Calyste marries. The rector acknowledged the mother’s hope for a richer Irish match but said it was wise to have two strings to the bow—Pen-Hoel’s savings and well-managed land were not to be despised. He then described Mademoiselle des Touches’ life: a stage-woman and writer, frequenter of theatres and artists, squandering her fortune on pamphleteers, painters, and musicians, under the false name Camille Maupin. She had turned Les Touches into an improper paradise of dissipation; more wine was drunk there in weeks than in all Guerande in a year. Last year the Demoiselles Bougniol let lodgings to men suspected of being Blues; they sang wicked songs and spent time at Les Touches. And this is whom Calyste adores; he would gallop to Nantes for an infamous book but not for the Church. She is not a royalist; if Calyste were called to fight for the cause and she wanted to keep him, the chevalier might let his own father go without him.

“Oh, no!” exclaimed the baroness. The rector feared to test Calyste; the mother would suffer too much. “I will see Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel tomorrow,” he promised. “Don’t be too uneasy. The harm may not be as great as they say. I will find out the truth. Jacqueline trusts me. Calyste is our child and pupil—he will not let the devil inveigle him nor disturb his family’s plans. One fault is not vice.”

“You are only informing me of details,” said the baroness. “Was I not the first to notice the change in my Calyste? A mother keenly feels being second in her son’s heart. This crisis is a trial of motherhood. I had hoped Calyste would love someone noble and beautiful—not a stage-player, a masquerader, an author who feigns sentiments, a creature who will deceive and make him unhappy! She has had adventures—”

“With several men,” said the rector. “Yet this impious creature was born in Brittany! She dishonors her land. I shall preach upon her next Sunday.”

“Don’t do that!” cried the baroness. “The peasants and paludiers might rush to Les Touches. Calyste is Breton; something dreadful might happen if he defended her as he would the Blessed Virgin.”

“It is now ten o’clock; I must bid you good-night,” said the abbé, lighting his lantern. “Who could have told me that a young man brought up by you, trained by me to Christian ideas, a fervent Catholic, a child who has lived like a spotless lamb, would plunge into such mire?”

“But is it certain?” asked the mother. “How could any woman help loving Calyste?”

“What proof other than her staying on at Les Touches? In twenty-four years since coming of age she has never stayed so long; her visits were formerly few and short.”

“A woman over forty!” exclaimed the baroness. “In Ireland they say such a woman is the most dangerous mistress for a young man.”

“As to that I have no knowledge,” replied the rector, “and shall die in ignorance.”

“And I, too, alas!” said the baroness. “I wish I had loved deeply, so I might counsel and comfort Calyste.”

The rector did not cross the little court-yard alone; the baroness accompanied him to the gate, hoping to hear Calyste’s step. They heard the rector’s cautious tread grow fainter and the parsonage door close behind him.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
V. CALYSTE

The poor mother returned to the salon, deeply distressed to find the whole town aware of what she had thought private. She trimmed the wick of the lamp, took up her worsted-work, and waited for Calyste. The baroness hoped to induce her son to come home earlier and spend less time with Mademoiselle des Touches, but in vain. Day by day Calyste’s visits to Les Touches increased and he came in later and later; the night before the moment we follow he returned at midnight.

The baroness stitched with the rapidity of someone absorbed in thought. Curiosity and wounded maternal pride combined in her reflections: what spell did these daughters of Baal cast to make men forget family and duty? Sometimes she longed to meet the woman and judge her soberly. Her mind measured the danger the rector described—what modern ideas might do to the one child she had raised so carefully, as guileless as a girl and beautiful as a spring morning.

Calyste, the splendid product of old Breton and Irish blood, had been nurtured with strict care. After her initial hands-on guidance, Fanny had entrusted him to the rector of Guerande for the rest of his education. The Church, as reverent as a mother, gave him the seminary training the abbé himself had known: Latin and Greek, circumspect history, geography, limited French writers, and the seminary philosophy. Fanny taught him English; a mathematician among the household staff gave lessons. He was ignorant of modern literature and the sciences’ recent advances. At sixteen, when he began the abbé’s philosophy, he remained essentially unchanged—innocent, fervent in his Catholicism, neither over-pious nor ridiculous.

For such a son the baroness imagined a quiet, respectable life. She expected to inherit an aunt’s two or three thousand pounds sterling; combined with the Guenics’ small fortune, she could find Calyste a wife bringing twelve or fifteen thousand francs a year. Charlotte de Kergarouet, a rich Irish girl, or any decent heiress would have suited her. Fanny knew nothing of passionate love; to her, marriage was a matter of fortune, religion, property, and duty. Her anxieties combined maternal love and practical interest: would some modern woman—Mademoiselle des Touches—ruin the patient care of twenty years and the family’s future? She prayed for a long life to see her plans bear fruit; Mademoiselle du Guenic shared that hope, now threatened by Camille Maupin.

Midnight passed; one struck; Calyste was still absent. When his steps sounded in the lane joy drove out anxiety; the poor mother flew to the gate, opened it, and welcomed him. He reproached her softly for sitting up; she kissed him and tried to read his face for the evening’s events. Calyste’s beauty moved her—he resembled her in hair, mouth, hands, and skin, with the black, intense eyes of his father. Though his beard had not come and his features were almost girlish, his strength was Herculean. Fatigue shadowed his brow, which his mother attributed to excitement.

The baroness’s bitter thought struck: those cheeks, that innocent brow, might now belong to another woman; passion would tarnish that purity. Money from London relations allowed velvet garments despite modest means; several wealthy aunts had kept the family creditable and hoped to find Calyste an heiress.

When he entered, she asked if he had been at Les Touches. He answered curtly that he had. The baroness postponed fuller inquiry. Mothers sense the worst but begrudge losing the dependency of their children; conflicting feelings of fear and joy warred in Fanny. Calyste was indulgently allowed to sleep late; household rules bent to his whims. At mid-day he still slept; Mariotte served a tray in his bed. At one, as they sat in the salon, Calyste appeared dressed. The baroness feared, the old sister whistled with impatience, and Mariotte announced he would probably dine at Les Touches.

The rector had spread grave charges about Mademoiselle des Touches—that for a year she had changed Calyste and corrupted him by making him read “all sorts of books.” The baron joked about nicknames and duels of past days while the old sister condemned educated people; their conversation betrayed generational and cultural divides. Mariotte argued that if he loved her, what harm? There were practical considerations: Camille had thirty thousand francs a year and was handsome. The baron exploded at the notion of a Guenic marrying a des Touches; social status and family pride flared. The baroness, from religion and custom, found Camille’s literary life, stage connections, and open genius scandalous.

When Calyste returned to the room Mariotte had withdrawn. The baroness, trembling, accused Les Touches of being improper and of spoiling Calyste; Camille Maupin had made him read books and had taken “a man’s name.” Calyste knelt and replied that Camille, or Félicité des Touches, had rejected him eighteen months earlier. She had gently laughed, called herself old enough to be his mother, said a woman of forty would commit a sort of crime by loving a minor, and refused him. She had comforted him when he wept and offered friendship; she was generous in heart if not in conventional ways. He had resigned himself on learning she loved another. “Do not repeat calumnies,” he begged; Camille was an artist and genius; her exceptional life could not be judged by ordinary rules.

Fanny, religious and provincial, could not excuse a woman who abandoned the Church’s gentle tenets. To write plays and go to the theatre, to consort with artists and musicians, to roam the world—such acts, to her, signaled a failure of duty to God and society. Calyste protested passionately: Camille was his friend and he would give his life for her. The baroness recoiled—his life was theirs too. The old sister and the baron showed puzzled and differing reactions: the baron amused, the sister solemnly counseled education yet warned of the evil of abandoning duty. Calyste begged for patience; that night he promised to explain all and that, when she knew everything, she would bless Mademoiselle des Touches. He left; the family watched him through the gate. The discussion, exceptional in that tranquil house, unsettled the baroness deeply. Camille Maupin seemed to her like the Revolution entering their calm domestic world.

The baron minimized the matter: Mademoiselle des Touches was reputed dark, strong, about forty; of course a young man would fall in love with a woman of such force. If she were a saint she would not accept Calyste—he joked—and resolved to visit her himself. His projected visit, given the character of Camille’s biography, promised adventures.

VI. BIOGRAPHY OF CAMILLE MAUPIN

Guerande, which had seen Calyste’s frequent visits to Les Touches for months, concluded that Mademoiselle Félicité des Touches was passion-struck and had bewitched him. Rumors swelled: why did an old woman rule that angel so absolutely? The rector and other local notables further alarmed each other with accounts of the singular life of the writer who hid behind the masculine name Camille Maupin. In their eyes she was a monstrous creature, part siren, part atheist, violating social law designed to check women’s infirmities.

Camille Maupin was the nom de plume of Félicité des Touches, a Breton woman whose literary virility had once made readers suppose Camille a man. Her early plays, published in 1822, stirred literary controversy; she followed with more plays and a notable novel. Explaining why Félicité took a man’s name and kept her freedom—more excusable than the famous Madame de Staël—helps justify the attention Calyste gave her.

Félicité became an orphan in 1793. Her father, a royalist major of the guards, was killed on 10 August; her brother was massacred at Les Carmes; her mother died of grief; a nun aunt initially took her in. Madame de Faucombe later took the orphan to Faucombe near Nantes, where convent sisters lived with her. Revolutionary upheaval seized the nuns and child; only Thermidor released them. Faucombe, an elderly great-uncle, took charge. He was a man of archaeology and antiquarian pursuits and left Félicité largely to her own devices. Neglected by his young wife, she grew up more like a boy in his library than like a conventionally educated girl. She read voraciously and formed an immense, undigested store of knowledge: theoretical life without the lived innocence of childhood.

By eighteen she was well-read in many subjects, her imagination and intellect fed rather than stifled. Her learning, excessive for her age and sex in provincial Nantes, did not corrupt her chastity but gave her a deformed intellectual maturity: a mind trained by passion for books rather than conventional feminine training. Faucombe was delighted; Félicité helped him and even ghost-wrote three of his books. The strain harmed her health; doctors ordered exercise and society. She became an admirable horsewoman and recovered.

At eighteen she entered society and, though admired for beauty, she felt cold to adulation. Hurt by family mockery, she resolved to be coquettish and musical, and sent for Steibelt for twelve thousand francs’ tuition. In Paris she studied harmony and composed music for two operas largely known to be by others—an example of her behind-the-scenes genius. Province wearied her, so she returned to books, music, and Faucombe. In 1812, at twenty-one, she took control of her fortunes: fifteen thousand francs a year from Les Touches, twelve thousand from Faucombe’s estate, plus a capital of three hundred thousand francs saved during her minority.

She learned to manage money and invest shrewdly—in the Grand-Livre during the retreat from Moscow—raising her income to fifty thousand francs a year. At twenty-one she had the will and economic sense of a much older man. She stayed to nurse Faucombe through illness and death, then, a Royalist, she went to Paris at the Restoration. The Bourbons’ return and political unrest shaped her early public life; she bought a fine house in rue Mont Blanc, installed herself in 1815, and gathered around her artists, writers, and publicists—creating a salon mixing rank with letters, similar to Baron Gérard’s.

Habitually accustomed to controlling her life and rejecting conventional marriage, she valued independence and felt disgust for maternity’s demands. The peculiarities of her upbringing—no parental domestic education, a guardianship of an archaeologist, self-formed intellect—explained the anomalies of Camille Maupin: she had never played with dolls and was mentally formed by books and solitude.

By 1817 she sensed the physical and moral toll of obstinate celibacy, the fear of losing youth, and the need to choose between marriage and freedom. She wanted both admiration and liberty. At forty she retained an almost youthful presence; Nature had favored her with complexion and bearing that resisted time.

Described physically, Félicité was Breton but with a luminous, ivory-like skin by candlelight, dark abundant hair, a broad forehead, and deep, immortal eyes, sometimes scintillating, sometimes dull with meditation. Her features combined severity, strength, and a classical profile: straight nose, mobile nostrils expressive of irony or anger, an arched mouth with a thin upper lip and full lower—capable of disdain and tenderness. Her neck and arms were athletic; her hands delicate. Her presence was more sad and serious than graceful; she listened more than she spoke, startling others by the weight of silence and attention. Educated observers saw something of Cleopatra or Isis in her. She was leonine, self-sufficient, and potentially unconquerable: a woman of intellect whose soul could seem abnormal to those expecting conventional feminine charm. She was the living image of an Isis—respected, feared, and sometimes dreaded for the height of her mind.

Her beauty, fortune, and social position furthered her fame. In 1817 she opened her house to the cultivated: artists, authors, scientists, and discerning women. Mothers hoped to marry their sons into her fortune, peers brought their womenkind, diplomats came for conversation. She had seen and risen above the trivialities of provincial life. After a first love that ended in disillusion, she sought refuge in music and letters. A later, pivotal relation with a man of original mind—an author and critic who took her to Italy—shaped the identity of Camille Maupin. He taught her to appreciate art in Italy, encouraged study of German and English literature, and styled her taste and frankness. In Rome in 1820 he deserted her for an Italian; that betrayal hardened her contempt for men and set her on the public literary path. Misfortune, as Napoleon said, is often the midwife of genius.

Back in Paris she worked with Conti, the great musician, writing librettos. Encouraged by success, she published plays in 1822 that caused a literary stir, then a novel recounting her betrayed love—one of the period’s masterpieces, dangerous like “Adolphe.” Why she took the mask of Camille Maupin—whether to escape fame or to lend herself a man’s name for criticism—remains debated. But her salon, wit, sound judgement, and solid erudition made her an authority. She played the part of a worldly woman: languid when she wished, coquettish at times, attentive to dress and the light nothings that charm women and poets. She was the intellectual’s Ninon, adoring art and surrounding herself with artists of all kinds. Her heart was generous, often duped by sorrow; she had pity for suffering and contempt for the prosperous, and since 1830 she had presided over a close circle of friends who loved and esteemed each other. She could joke about Camille Maupin and George Sand—her “brother Cain”—without jealousy.

From 1817 to 1834 she visited Les Touches intermittently. Her first stay after the 1818 disillusion found the house uninhabitable; she lodged in the village intending to contemplate herself like a cloistered nun. She later furnished Les Touches with Parisian goods transported to Croisic and across sands; public curiosity about these luxuries brought visitors and a modest income to the porter and gardener. After that she did not return for two years, until after Italy and Conti. By the time of our story her Parisian fame preceded her and letters addressed simply to “Camille Maupin, Les Touches” arrived at the post.

In conservative, Catholic Brittany her singular life naturally provoked scandal and rumor, reaching Abbé Grimont, who saw in her life the dissolution of social mores. Yet Félicité continued to live as she pleased, the center of a small literary world, neither immoderately notorious nor wholly private.

During the present stay at Les Touches she was not alone. Claude Vignon, a bitter and powerful writer known for scornful superiority, was her guest. She had entertained him and many authors, journalists, artists, and men of the world for seven years. She knew Vignon’s laziness, need, and disgust for things but seemed, at least apparently, inclined to marry him—whether from ambition, fear of age, or the desire to anchor a man of superior mind with her fortune. She had brought him from Paris perhaps to study him and decide her course. Yet she misled both Calyste and Claude: she was not thinking of marriage. Her heart had been struck, late but violently, by the sun of young love; too late she had discovered passion could still light her life.

Thus she found herself, in a life resembling a convent of reflection, entangled in an emotion both dangerous and irresistible—the very situation that had alarmed Fanny and the town. The scene at Les Touches, where this inner tumult unfolded, became the quiet but fateful setting of the romance about to involve Calyste and the extraordinary Camille Maupin.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
A few hundred yards from Guerande the soil of Brittany ends and the salt‑marshes and sandy dunes begin. We descend into a desert left by the sea along a rugged road through a ravine that has never seen a carriage. This desert contains waste tracts, ponds of unequal size, muddy banks where salt is made, and a little arm of the sea that separates the mainland from Croisic. Geographically Croisic is really a peninsula; but as it clings to Brittany only by beaches to Batz—barren quicksands hard to cross—it may be called an island.

Where the road from Croisic to Guerande turns off stands a country house surrounded by a large garden, remarkable for twisted pines—some shaped like sun‑shades, others stripped of branches, their reddened trunks exposed where bark has peeled. These trees, bent by hurricanes and wind, prepare the mind for the strange sight of marshes and dunes resembling a stiffened ocean. The house, built of slaty stone with granite courses, is plain architecturally: a regular wall with small leaded windows below and larger panes above; an enormously high pointed roof with two gables and two large dormers on each side, and circular windows under each gable, one facing the sea and the other Guerande. One façade looks to the road to Guerande, the other to the desert ending at Croisic and, beyond, the open sea. A brook escapes through an opening in the park wall by the road and is lost in the sands.

The gray tones of the house harmonize with its landscape. The park is an oasis in the desert; at its entrance a mud‑hut shelters custom‑house officials. The house, without much land (most of the estate lies in Guerande), derives income from the marshes and a few outlying farms—over ten thousand francs a year. Such is the fief of Les Touches, from which the Revolution lopped feudal rights. The paludiers, however, still call it “the château,” and would say “seigneur” if the fief were not now in the female line. When Felicité set about restoring Les Touches she, artist that she was, did not alter the desolate exterior that gives it a prison look. The only change was an entrance gate enlivened by two brick columns supporting an arch beneath which carriages pass into the court where she planted trees.

The ground floor follows the plan of most country houses built a hundred years earlier and seems erected on ruins of an old castle formerly perched there. A large paneled entrance hall, turned by Felicité into a billiard‑room, opens onto an immense salon with six windows and the dining‑room. The kitchen communicates with the dining room through an office. Camille has shown noble simplicity in arranging this floor, avoiding splendid decoration. The salon, painted gray, is furnished in old mahogany with green silk coverings. The dining room contains four great mahogany buffets, horsehair chairs, and fine Audran engravings in mahogany frames. The old wooden staircase with heavy balusters is covered with a green carpet.

Above are two suites separated by the staircase. Mademoiselle des Touches took the one that looks toward the sea and the marshes, making of it a small salon, a large chamber, and two cabinets—one a dressing room, the other a study and writing room. The other suite houses two guest apartments, each with bedroom, antechamber, and cabinet. The servants’ rooms are in the attic. Guest rooms are furnished simply; a certain fantastic luxury is reserved for Felicité’s private apartment. In that sombre, melancholy habitation overlooking the sombre landscape she assembled the most fantastic creations of art she could find. The little salon is hung with Gobelin tapestry framed in marvelously carved oak. Windows are draped with heavy silken hangings of a past age: brocade shot with crimson and gold against green and yellow, gathered into mighty pleats and trimmed with fringes, cords, and tassels fit for a church. This salon contains a chest or cabinet worth seven or eight thousand francs, a carved ebony table, a secretary with many drawers inlaid with ivory arabesques bought in Venice, and other noble Gothic pieces—pictures and choice workmanship bought in 1818 when their value was unsuspected. Her bedroom is strictly Louis XV: a carved white bedstead with Cupids scattering flowers, a canopy, blue silk hangings, the Pompadour dressing table, duchesse, chaise‑longue, a stiff little sofa, silk window curtains lined with pink satin and tied with silken ropes, and a Savonnerie carpet—those elegant dainties of eighteenth‑century women.

Her study, entirely contemporary, contrasts with the Louis XV gallantries and offers a charming collection of mahogany furniture; it resembles a boudoir. The bookshelves are full, but trivialities of a woman’s existence clutter it; amid them one perceives, with uneasy surprise, pistols, a narghile, a riding whip, a hammock, a rifle, a man’s blouse, tobacco, pipes, a knapsack—a bizarre combination that paints Felicité.

Every great soul entering that room would be struck by the peculiar beauty of the landscape spreading its broad savanna beyond the park, the last vegetation on the continent. The melancholy squares of water, divided by little paths of white salt crust along which the salt‑makers pass—dressed in white—to rake the salt into mulons; a space whose saline exhalations prevent birds from crossing and stifle botanical effort; sands where the eye is soothed only by one hardy plant bearing rosy flowers and the Chartreux pansy; that lake of salt water, the sandy dunes, the view of Croisic like a miniature town afloat upon the sea; and the ocean tossing its foaming fringe upon the granite rocks—this sight uplifts and saddens; the sublime produces a regretful yearning for things unknown yet perceived by the soul on far heights. These wild harmonies are for great spirits and great sorrows.

This desert scene, where at times the sun’s rays reflected by water and sands whiten the village of Batz and ripple pitilessly on Croisic’s roofs, filled Camille’s dreaming mind for days. She seldom looked to the cool, refreshing groves and meadows around Guerande. Her soul struggled to endure a horrible inward anguish.

No sooner did Calyste see the vanes of the two gables shooting up beyond roadside furze and distorted pines than the air seemed lighter; Guerande was a prison to him; his life was at Les Touches. Who will not understand a youth’s attraction? A love like Cherubin’s had flung him at the feet of a person who was a great and grand thing to him before he thought of her as a woman, and it had survived Felicité’s repeated and inexplicable refusals. This sentiment—more a need of loving than love itself—had not escaped Camille’s analysis; hence, perhaps, her rejection—a generosity unperceived by Calyste.

At Les Touches the ravished eyes of the ignorant young countryman beheld the riches of a new world and heard another language. He listened to the poetic sounds of the finest music of the nineteenth century, where melody and harmony blend; he saw works of modern painters, the French school now heir to Italy, Spain, and Flanders; he read works of imagination, creations of modern literature that impressed his unused heart. In short, the great Nineteenth Century appeared to him in collective magnificence, its critical spirit and desires for renovation—its vast efforts nearly all raised on the scale of the giant who cradled the century in his banners and sang hymns with the lullaby of cannon.

Initiated by Felicité into these things, Calyste gratified the taste for the glorious, powerful at his age, and that artless admiration, the first love of adolescence, always irritated by criticism. He listened to charming Parisian raillery, graceful satire, and awakened a thousand ideas that might have slumbered forever in the soft torpor of his family life. For him Mademoiselle des Touches was the mother of his intellect. She was so kind; a woman is always adorable to a man whom she inspires to love, even when she seems not to share it.

At the present time Felicité was giving him music lessons. To him the grand lower apartments and her private rooms above, so coquettish and artistic, were vivified by a spirit, a supernatural atmosphere, strange and undefinable. The modern world with its poesy sharply contrasted with the dull patriarchal world of Guerande; the two systems faced one another—on one side a thousand developments of Art, on the other the sameness of uncivilized Brittany. No one will therefore wonder that the poor lad, bored by provincial pleasures, quivered as he approached the house, rang the bell, and crossed the court. Such emotions rarely assail a mature man.

As the door opened, Calyste hearing the piano supposed Camille was in the salon; but in the billiard‑hall he no longer heard it. Camille, he thought, must be playing on a small upright she had brought from England and placed in her salon. He ran up the stairs where the thick carpet smothered his steps; but he slowed as he neared the top, perceiving something unusual about the music. Felicité was playing for herself only; she was communing with her own being.

Instead of entering, the young man sat on a Gothic seat covered with green velvet on the landing beneath a window framed in carved wood. Nothing was more mysteriously melancholy than Camille’s improvisation; it seemed the cry of a soul de profundis—from the depths of a grave. The heart of the young lover recognized the cry of despairing love, the prayer of a hidden plaint, the groan of repressed affliction. Camille had varied and lengthened the introduction to the cavatina “Mercy for thee, mercy for me!” which is nearly the whole of the fourth act of Robert le Diable. She suddenly sang the words in a heart‑rending manner, then as suddenly interrupted herself. Calyste entered and saw why. Poor Camille Maupin! poor Felicité! She turned to him with a face bathed in tears, took out her handkerchief, dried them, and said simply, without affectation, “Good‑morning.” She was beautiful in her morning gown: a red chenille net on her head through which coils of black hair shone, a short upper garment like a Greek peplum revealing cambric trousers with embroidered frills and pretty Turkish slippers, red and gold.

“What is the matter?” cried Calyste.

“He has not returned,” she replied, going to a window and looking out upon the sands, sea, and marshes.

This explained all. Camille was awaiting Claude Vignon.

“You are anxious about him?” asked Calyste.

“Yes,” she answered, with a sadness the lad was too ignorant to analyze.

He started to leave.

“Where are you going?” she asked.

“To find him,” he replied.

“Dear child!” she said, taking his hand and drawing him toward her with one of those moist glances that are the best recompense to a youthful soul. “You are distracted! Where could you find him on that wide shore?”

“I will find him.”

“Your mother would be in mortal terror. Stay. Besides, I choose it,” she said, making him sit on the sofa. “Don’t pity me. The tears you see are the tears a woman likes to shed. We have a faculty that is not in man—that of abandoning ourselves to our nervous nature and driving our feelings to an extreme. By imagining certain situations and encouraging imagination we end in tears, and sometimes in serious illness. The fancies of women are not actions of the mind; they are of the heart. You have come just in time; solitude is bad for me. I am not the dupe of his professed desire to go to Croisic and see the rocks and dunes and salt‑marshes without me. He meant to leave us alone together; he is jealous, or rather he pretends jealousy, and you are young, you are handsome.”

“Why not have told me this before? What must I do? Must I stay away?” asked Calyste, barely restraining his tears, one rolling down his cheek and touching Felicité deeply.

“You are an angel!” she cried. Then gaily she sang the “Stay! stay!” of Matilde in Guillaume Tell, taking all gravity from that princessly answer to a subject. “He only wants to make me think he loves me better than he does,” she said. “He knows how much I desire his happiness,” she went on, looking attentively at Calyste. “Perhaps he feels humiliated to be inferior to me there. Perhaps he suspects you and means to surprise us. But even if his only crime is to enjoy himself without me and not associate me with the ideas this new place gives him, is that not enough? Ah! I am no more loved by that great brain than I was by the musician, by the poet, by the soldier! Sterne is right; names signify much; mine is a bitter sarcasm. I shall die without finding in any man the love that fills my heart, the poesy that is in my soul—”

She stopped, arms pendant, head back on the cushions, eyes fixed on a pattern of the carpet. The pain of great minds has something grandiose and imposing; it reveals a vast extent of soul whose affections belong to a people and so affect a world.

“Why did you reject my—” said Calyste; but he could not finish. Camille’s beautiful hand laid upon his interrupted him.

“Nature changed her laws in granting me a dozen years of youth beyond my due,” she said. “I rejected your love from egotism. Sooner or later the difference in our ages must have parted us. I am thirteen years older than he, and even that is too much.”

“You will be beautiful at sixty,” cried Calyste, heroically.

“God grant it,” she answered, smiling. “Besides, dear child, I want to love. In spite of his cold heart, his lack of imagination, his cowardly indifference, and the envy which consumes him, I believe there is greatness behind those tatters; I hope to galvanize that heart, to save him from himself, to attach him to me. Alas! I have a clear‑seeing mind, but a blind heart.”

She was terrible in her self‑knowledge. She suffered and analyzed her feelings as surgeons explain the fatal advance of disease. Camille knew the passion within her as those men knew their anatomy.

“I have brought him here to judge him, and he is already bored,” she continued. “He pines for Paris; the nostalgia of criticism is on him; he has no author to pluck, no system to undermine, no poet to drive to despair, and he dares not commit some debauch in this house to lift the burden of his ennui. Alas! my love is not real enough, perhaps, to soothe his brain; I don’t intoxicate him! Make him drunk at dinner tonight and I shall know if I am right. I will say I am ill, and stay in my room.”

Calyste turned scarlet from neck to forehead; even his ears burned.

“Oh! forgive me,” she cried. “How can I heedlessly deprave your girlish innocence! Forgive me, Calyste—” She paused. “There are some consistent natures who say at a certain age: ‘If I had my life to live over again, I would do the same things.’ I, who do not think myself weak, say, ‘I would be a woman like your mother, Calyste.’ To have a Calyste, oh! what happiness! I could be humble and submissive—and yet I have done no harm except to myself. But alas! dear child, a woman cannot stand alone in society except in a primitive state. Affections not in harmony with social or natural laws escape us. Suffering for suffering, as well be useful where we can. What care I for those children of my cousin Faucombe? I have not seen them these twenty years, and they are married to merchants. You are my son, who have never cost me the miseries of motherhood; I shall leave you my fortune and make you happy—so far as money can do so, dear treasure of beauty and grace that nothing should ever change or blast.”

“You would not take my love,” said Calyste, “and I shall return your fortune to your heirs.”

“Child!” answered Camille, in a guttural voice, letting tears roll down her cheeks. “Will nothing save me from myself?” she added presently.

“You said you had a history to tell me, and a letter to—” said the generous youth, wishing to divert her thoughts; but she would not let him finish.

“You are right to remind me of that. I will be an honest woman before all else. I will sacrifice no one—Yes, it was too late yesterday, but today we have time,” she said cheerfully. “I will keep my promise; and while I tell you that history I will sit by the window and watch the road to the marshes.”

Calyste arranged a great Gothic chair for her near the window and opened one sash. Camille, sharing an oriental taste with her illustrious sister author, took a magnificent Persian narghile given her by an ambassador. She filled the nipple with patchouli, cleaned the mouthpiece, perfumed the goose‑quill she attached to it, set fire to the yellow leaves, placed the vase with its long neck enameled in blue and gold at some distance, and rang the bell for tea.

“Will you have cigarettes?—Ah! I am always forgetting that you do not smoke. Purity such as yours is so rare! The hand of Eve herself, fresh from the hand of her Maker, is alone innocent enough to stroke your cheek.”

Calyste colored; sitting down on a stool at Camille’s feet, he did not see the deep emotion that for a moment seemed to overcome her.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“I promised you this tale of the past, and here it is,” Camille began, and she told Calyste that the letter he had received came from the Marquise de Rochefide. The Rochefides, a newer noble house than the Casterans, had sought a prestigious connection for their son; the Comtesse de Montcornet recommended Béatrix-Maximilienne-Rose de Casteran, the youngest daughter whose father intended to marry off his daughters without dowries to preserve his estate for his son. Béatrix, born and raised at the château de Casteran, married in 1828 at twenty. Camille called her what provincials name “original”—independent, enthusiastic, passionately artistic—a temperament dangerous for a woman, liable to lead her “to the edges of abysses” where only men have the support to keep balance.

The dowager de Casteran approved the match with Rochefide, who seemed superior in every respect. The marriage linked the Rochefides to other aristocratic families and nearly secured a peerage for Rochefide’s son under Charles X, lost after the July revolution. Rochefide, foolish and vain, mistook Béatrix’s refinement and reserve for frigidity and reverted to bachelor habits. As many had advised, he assumed a proud, artistic wife would be content with salon life; but he was not merely foolish—he combined vanity with a niggling jealousy that flared into cruelty. Camille analyzed how this temperament and Béatrix’s admiration for him produced rivalry: Béatrix wanted a salon of her own to draw away Camille’s circle. She managed only superficial success. Camille thought Béatrix thirsted for celebrity but acknowledged the marquise had grandeur of soul, regal pride, clear ideas, and a rare ease with art, theology, and music—though with some pose, as if she wished to appear versed in arcana.

Camille sketched Béatrix’s appearance: an ethereal blonde, slender and upright, with a long, pointed face, a daring forehead, pale sea-green eyes, a clever nose, a disdainful mouth, pale cheeks, a somewhat stout chin, an exquisite waist and shoulders, but an underdeveloped bust. Her carriage and nobility of bearing made her striking; in the right dress—cherry velvet and lace—she could seem divine. Camille admitted Béatrix was not faultlessly beautiful, but she left an ineffaceable impression.

Félicité showed Calyste a Mieris painting; Camille jested that blondes have charms brunettes lack and warned Calyste not to fall for Béatrix by her portrait alone. Camille described Béatrix as severe and elegant, sometimes revealing a Southern passion; an angelic flash might appear and vanish. For three years after her marriage she judged the world from her own height; the July revolution and social change unsettled her. She embraced some new intellectual currents while wanting to protect the nobility; finding limited place for individual superiority in the new order, she chose personal happiness over fading social power.

At Camille’s salon she met Gennaro Conti, a celebrated Neapolitan composer whose voice people adored as a soul rather than mere sound. Béatrix fell into mad passion and took him from Camille. The act, painful to Camille, was fair play: Béatrix confided in her and made Camille arbiter of the choice. Camille admired Béatrix’s conduct—she retained dignity as a marquise and showed a grandeur of womanhood that men often fail to see. Yet Camille confessed she had been faithful to Conti in admiration and curiosity; she had tested him, knowing him charming but vain and hypocritical in matters of the heart. Conti, Camille argued, was a consummate charlatan of feeling—insincere, theatrical, insatiable for applause; he posed as fanatically devoted to art while often acting selfishly. Still his warmth and chivalry deceived many women. Having probed and seen his false sentiment, Conti now hated Camille for seeing through him. She had told Béatrix nothing.

Camille had even received Conti and Béatrix together in her salon to keep the affair discreet, forcing Conti to dissemble. Béatrix soon told Camille she was leaving for Italy with Conti. Camille was not surprised: Béatrix regarded herself united to him and, tired of social constraints, chose escape to love. Conti, elated, was warned by Camille that he loved himself more than any woman. Despite a scene of fury from him, she lent him money for the journey. Béatrix left Rochefide a note and went to Italy for two years; she wrote charming letters to Camille, who was the only woman she trusted. Conti, short of funds, accepted an offer to write a French opera; Béatrix wrote from Genoa that they might soon return to Paris. Camille handed Calyste Béatrix’s letter to read.

Claude Vignon then arrived unexpectedly, casting an ominous presence. He was thirty-seven, prematurely bald, once handsome but now haggard; he resembled Raphael in youth but had thickened features, a sharpened nose, drawn expressions from weariness or solitude. His pale blue eyes were clouded. Camille described him as a critic whose intellect penetrated everything but whose energy failed outwardly; his criticism demolished but did not create. He was simple yet wily, indulgent in excess, and prone to paralysis of action—an arch-critic who contemplated without acting. Camille liked the idea of making Claude more active; he fancied himself a politician and writer but habitually let opportunity slip away.

Small talk followed, with veiled jests about entrances “by the door” or window-scaling as a lover’s badge. Claude’s sardonic manner disturbed Calyste, who felt uneasy. Claude interrupted Calyste’s earnestness about the letter with mocking comments about love, angering the young man. Calyste, rash and sincere, was reproached by Camille and Félicité, who tried to shield him. He left in a storm of emotion, kissing Camille’s hand and weeping; Claude smirked that the youth would love much. Félicité announced Madame de Rochefide might arrive, perhaps with Conti, which interested Claude, who had seen a vessel off Croisic that could bring them.

At home Calyste brooded on Béatrix’s letter and Camille’s portrait. The household—his mother Fanny, Aunt Zéphirine, and servants—created a cozy domestic scene. Calyste read the letter again and imagined Béatrix as Camille had described. He told his mother how Béatrix had opened his mind, teaching him about Parisian life, music, literature, Italian, and social nuances; she had become, in effect, the mother of his intellect. Fanny feared the change yet softened, moved by his devotion. Aunt Zéphirine urged a prudent, bourgeois path: marry a good Breton girl, live tranquilly to secure the estate and heirs—practical counsel that contrasted with Calyste’s romantic ardor. Calyste flared against domestic plans, exclaiming he could not be confined to a married monk’s life; his imagination had been inflamed by Parisian civilization, books and women; he longed for passion, beauty, freedom. His mother, seeing his earnestness, embraced him and blessed his pursuit of happiness while pleading prudence.

After dinner Calyste returned to Les Touches under pretext of bringing back the letter. Claude Vignon and Félicité remained; Félicité indulged Claude’s gourmandise and the dinner displayed Camille’s Parisian taste, a contrast with the Guénic family’s simpler comforts. Calyste presented the letter; Claude read only the first page and observed that Béatrix’s attachment to Conti seemed bound more by pride than passion: “She is held to Conti now by pride only; she is condemned to love him quand même.” Camille protested that Béatrix’s nature was too grand to change and reminded them Conti would accompany her.

Claude’s cynical remarks pierced Calyste. When Claude casually suggested Béatrix would be a suitable match for Calyste, the youth was wounded. Félicité admonished Claude not to plant dangerous ideas in the impressionable young man. Claude, enjoying wordplay and satire, pursued a discussion about youth’s infatuations with older women—arguing young men often adore older, semi-dowager women because such women possess experience, savoir-faire, dignity, and absolute devotion that captivates: their love, he said, is vast and unrelieved by vanity—the deep, memorable passions. Camille’s evident pleasure in the praise flattered but also complicated the circle; Félicité felt both flattered and wary.

The evening continued with wit and anecdotes; Claude and Félicité described Parisian life to Calyste, who was charmed—intellect held immense seduction for someone “all heart.” Claude predicted the Marquise and Conti might land at Croisic the next day, perhaps on the Danish vessel he had seen; Camille colored at the suggestion.

Calyste arrived home late to his anxious mother, insisting he had never had so delightful an evening: genius, he rhapsodized, could create life and choice among women. His mother kissed him and reminded him that love might suffice. Calyste pledged he would love in her stead for those who might have adored him. Fanny counseled him to choose noble women if he must pursue love, and bade him be prudent though she could not bind his heart.

Thus the scene closed with Calyste torn between domestic duty and the new passionate horizons opened by Béatrix’s letter and Camille’s salon—a youth intoxicated by ideas, love, and the promise of Paris, watched over by a family that loved him and feared for his future.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
What young man full of restrained life and emotion would not go to Croisic to see Madame de Rochefide land, and to examine her incognito? Calyste surprised his parents by leaving at dawn without waiting for breakfast. He moved with an agility almost new to him and kept close to the walls of Les Touches lest he be seen. He was ashamed of his ardor and afraid of being laughed at; Félicité and Vignon were so perspicacious. Young men imagine their foreheads transparent.

At the shore, by a stone embankment before a shelter for bad crossings to Croisic, two boats arrived laden with trunks and chests that marked travellers of distinction. In one boat a maid in a straw bonnet with a green veil and a man—only a maid and a footman—were present.

“Are you going over to Croisic, Monsieur Calyste?” a boatman asked; annoyed at being called by name, Calyste shook his head.

A chest covered with tarred cloth labeled MME. LA MARQUISE DE ROCHEFIDE caught his eye. The name shone before him like a talisman; he fancied there was something fateful in it. In the desert of his newly awakened desires his fancy fastened on the first woman presented. Béatrix thus inherited the love Camille had rejected.

As the luggage landed Calyste glanced toward Croisic for another boat that might show him Béatrix—already the marble statue on which to hang his garlands. He stood with arms folded, lost in meditation. Remarkably, we often subject ourselves to sentiments by our own volition, deliberately bind ourselves; chance has less to do with it than we think.

“Are you expecting someone to meet you?” the maid asked.

“We were told they would fetch us from Les Touches. If they don’t come,” she added, “I don’t know how Madame la marquise will manage to dress for dinner. You had better go and find Mademoiselle des Touches. Oh! what a land of savages!”

Calyste felt he had blundered.

“Is your mistress going to Les Touches?” he inquired.

“She is there; Mademoiselle came for her this morning at seven o’clock. Ah! here come the horses.”

Calyste started toward Guérande with the lightness of a chamois, doubling to avoid retracing his steps or meeting servants of Les Touches. He met two of them on the narrow causeway through the marsh.

“Shall I go in, or shall I not?” he thought when the pines of Les Touches came in sight. He was afraid, and continued to Guérande, where he finished his excursion on the mall and reflected.

“She has no idea of my agitation,” he said to himself.

His capricious thoughts were grapnels fastening his heart to the marquise. He had known none of these mysterious terrors and joys with Camille. Such vague emotions rise like poems in the untutored soul. Warmed by the first fires of imagination, souls like his pass through preparation and may reach in silence the heights of love without having met the object of their efforts.

Presently Calyste saw the Chevalier du Halga and Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoël walking together on the mall. He heard them say his name and slipped aside to listen. The chevalier advised Pen-Hoël, if Charlotte de Kergarouët came, to keep her four or five months so the girl might fall in love with Calyste by daily contact, allowing them to marry next winter. “If you say two words about it to Charlotte she’ll say four to Calyste,” he added; “a girl of sixteen can certainly carry off the prize from a woman of forty.”

The old people turned away and Calyste heard no more. Remembering what his mother had told him, he saw Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoël’s intention. The idea of a girl thus imposed upon him sent him with greater ardor into his imaginary love. He had never fancied Charlotte; he now felt repugnance at the thought. Calyste, raised in modest means and accustomed to restrained life, cared little for fortune; his thoughts belonged to the marquise. What was that little Charlotte beside the portrait Camille had drawn—more a sister than a rival?

He did not return home until five. His mother handed him a letter from Mademoiselle des Touches:

My dear Calyste,—The beautiful marquise has come; we count on you to help us celebrate her arrival. Claude, always sarcastic, declares that you will play Bice and that she will be Dante. It is for our honor as Bretons, and yours as a du Guenic to welcome a Casteran. Come soon.
Your friend, Camille Maupin.
Come as you are, without ceremony; otherwise you will put us to the blush.

Calyste gave the letter to his mother and departed.

“Who are the Casterans?” said Fanny to the baron.

“An old Norman family, allied to William the Conqueror,” he replied. “They bear on a shield tierce fessed azure, gules and sable, a horse rearing argent, shod with gold. That beautiful creature for whom the Gars was killed at Fougères in 1800 was the daughter of a Casteran who made herself a nun and became an abbess after the Duc de Verneuil deserted her.”

“And the Rochefides?”

“I don’t know that name. I should have to see the blazon,” he replied.

The baroness was somewhat reassured that the Marquise de Rochefide was of noble birth, but she felt her son exposed to new seductions.

Calyste walked along with violent yet soft inward movements; his throat was tight, his heart swelled, his brain was full, fever possessing him. He tried to walk slowly, but some superior power hurried him. Such impetuosity is common in young men: a subtle fire makes Nature and woman seem radiant.

He found the company assembled in Camille’s little salon. It was about six; the setting sun cast a ruddy light chequered by trees; twilight, beloved of women, spread through the room.

“Here comes the future deputy of Brittany,” said Camille as Calyste raised the tapestry portière—“punctual as a king.”

“You recognized his step just now,” said Claude to Félicité in a low voice.

Calyste bowed low to the marquise, who inclined her head; he did not look at her but took the hand Claude Vignon held out and pressed it.

“This is the celebrated man of whom we have talked so much, Gennaro Conti,” said Camille, not replying to Claude’s remark.

She presented a man of medium height, thin, with chestnut hair, eyes almost red, and a freckled white skin, whose head so resembled Lord Byron’s that description seemed superfluous. Conti was proud of the resemblance.

“I am fortunate,” he said, “to meet Monsieur du Guenic during the one day that I spend at Les Touches.”

“It was for me to say that to you,” Calyste replied with a certain ease.

“He is handsome as an angel,” said the marquise in an undertone to Félicité.

Standing between the sofa and the two ladies, Calyste heard the words. He seated himself and looked furtively at the marquise. In the soft half-light he saw, reclining on a divan, a white serpentine shape that thrilled him. Félicité had done her friend a service; the marquise was much superior to Camille’s unflattering portrait. Had Béatrix wound blue-bells into her hair to set off her creped curls? The circles at her eyes, showing fatigue, were mother-of-pearl; her skin dazzled with fine blue veins. The delicacy of her features, diaphanous forehead, and long graceful neck lent many expressions. Her waist might be spanned by the hands; bare shoulders sparkled like a white camellia. A transparent fichu revealed the bosom's outline. A white muslin gown strewn with blue flowers, large sleeves, and shoes with crossed strings over stockings showed a charming knowledge of dress. Silver filigree ear-rings harmonized with the soft curls starred with blue-bells.

Calyste’s eager eye took in these beauties at a glance and carved them on his soul. The fair Béatrix and the dark Félicité might have sat for contrasting portraits: force and feebleness of womanhood in full development. In a moment—at first sight—Calyste was seized with a love that crowned his hopes and fears. Mademoiselle des Touches had awakened his nature; Béatrix inflamed heart and thought. He felt a sudden power to conquer; he looked at Conti with an envious, gloomy rivalry he’d never felt for Claude Vignon. He strove to control himself; the inward tempest fell as soon as Béatrix’s eyes turned to him and her soft voice sounded. Dinner was announced.

“Calyste, give your arm to the marquise,” said Camille, taking Conti with her right hand and Claude Vignon with her left, drawing back to let the marquise pass.

The descent of that ancient staircase was to Calyste like going into battle. His heart failed him; he had nothing to say; a slight sweat pearled on his forehead and wet his back; his arm trembled so that as they reached the lowest step the marquise asked, “Is anything the matter?”

“Oh!” he replied muffled, “I have never seen any woman so beautiful as you, except my mother, and I am not master of my emotions.”

“But you have Camille Maupin before your eyes.”

“Ah! what a difference!” said Calyste ingenuously.

“Calyste,” whispered Félicité behind him, “did I not tell you that you would forget me as if I had never existed? Sit there,” she said aloud, “beside the marquise, on her right, and you, Claude, on her left. As for you, Gennaro, I retain you by me; we will keep a mutual eye on their coquetries.”

Camille’s peculiar accent struck Claude Vignon, who observed her closely and never ceased to examine Mademoiselle des Touches during dinner.

“Coquetries!” replied the marquise, taking off her gloves and showing her beautiful hands; “the opportunity is good, with a poet,” and she motioned to Claude, “on one side, and poesy the other.”

By candlelight Béatrix seemed even more beautiful. The gleam made her forehead satiny, lit the spangles of her eyes, and turned her curls to gold. She threw back her thin gauze scarf, disclosing a beautiful nape and graceful neck—unlike Camille’s, the sign of another character.

Calyste had trouble pretending to eat; nervous motions deprived him of appetite. The ardor of the heart, restrained by moral reserve, led to inward conflict—the long respectful hesitations and tender debatings of the pure-hearted. Studying furtively the marquise’s beauty, Calyste felt oppressed by her majesty; dwarfed by the hauteur of certain glances, the imposing expression of an aristocratic face, and the pride women express in slight motions. The false position she had assumed forced an imposing manner—an art of the great world fatal to vulgar women.

Félicité’s eyes made Béatrix aware of the inward adoration she inspired in the youth beside her, and that it would be unworthy to encourage it. She now and then gave him repressive glances that fell upon his heart like an avalanche of snow. The unfortunate young fellow turned to Félicité with a look in which she could read the tears he was suppressing. She asked in a friendly tone why he ate nothing; the question piqued him and he forced himself to eat and take part in the conversation.

But Madame de Rochefide paid little attention to him. Mademoiselle des Touches, speaking wittily of her journey to Italy, related many incidents that made Claude Vignon, Conti, and Félicité laugh.

“How far such a woman is from me! Will she ever deign to notice me?” thought Calyste.

Camille was struck with his expression and tried to console him with a sympathetic look. Claude intercepted and became sarcastically gay, telling Béatrix that love existed only by desire; that most women deceived themselves in loving; that they loved for reasons unknown to men and often to themselves; that the best among them were artful.

“Keep to books, and don’t criticise our lives,” said Camille, glancing imperiously at him.

The dinner ceased to be gay. Claude’s sarcasm made the two women pensive. Calyste felt pain amid the happiness of looking at Béatrix. Conti searched the marquise’s eyes to guess her thoughts. When dinner was over, Mademoiselle des Touches took Calyste’s arm, gave the other two men to the marquise, and let them pass so she could be alone with the young Breton a moment.

“My dear Calyste,” she said, “you are acting in a manner that embarrasses the marquise; she may be delighted with your admiration, but she cannot accept it. Pray control yourself.”

“She was hard to me, she will never care for me,” said Calyste, “and if she does not I shall die.”

“Die! you! My dear Calyste, you are a child. Would you have died for me?”

“You have made yourself my friend,” he answered.

After coffee, Vignon asked Conti to sing. Camille sat at the piano. Together she and Gennaro sang the duet Dunque il mio bene tu mia sarai, the last duet of Zingarelli’s Romeo e Giulietta, a most pathetic modern page. The passage Di tanti palpiti expresses love in all its grandeur. Calyste, sitting in the same arm-chair in which Félicité had told him the story of the marquise, listened in rapt devotion. Béatrix and Vignon were on either side of the piano. Conti’s sublime voice blended with Félicité’s; both had often sung this piece and used all their gift. The music became a poem of divine melancholy, the farewell of two swans to life. When it was over, all present were under feelings that defy vulgar applause.

“Ah! music is the first of arts!” exclaimed the marquise.

“Camille thinks youth and beauty the first of poesies,” said Claude Vignon.

Mademoiselle des Touches looked at Claude with vague uneasiness. Béatrix, not seeing Calyste, turned to see the music’s effect—more for Conti’s sake than Calyste’s; she saw a white face bathed in tears. At the sight, and as if seized by sudden pain, she turned back quickly and looked at Gennaro. Music had arisen before Calyste, touching him until he stood in presence of creation. He was dumbfounded by Conti’s genius. Despite Camille’s warnings about the musician, Calyste believed now in the beauty of the soul expressed by such love. How could he rival such an artist? What woman could cease to adore that genius? That voice entered the soul like another soul.

The poor lad was overwhelmed by poesy and despair. He felt himself of no account; this ingenuous admission of his nothingness was readable on his face mingled with admiration. He did not observe the gesture with which Béatrix, moved by the contagion of a true feeling, called Félicité’s attention to him.

“Oh! the adorable heart!” cried Camille. “Conti, you will never obtain applause of one-half the value of that child’s homage. Let us sing this trio. Béatrix, my dear, come.”

When the marquise, Camille, and Conti arranged themselves at the piano, Calyste rose softly, flung himself on a sofa in the bedroom, whose door stood open, and sat with his head in his hands, plunged in meditation.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
X. DRAMA

“What is it, my child?” Claude Vignon asked as he entered the bedroom and took Calyste’s hand. “You love; you think you are disdained; but it is not so. The field will be free to you in a few days and you will reign—beloved by more than one.”

“Loved!” Calyste cried, springing up. “Who loves me here?”

“Camille,” Claude answered.

“Camille loves me? And you—what of you?”

Claude paused, weary, then confessed he was tired of life though not bold enough to leave it. He had felt a conviction for days and returned from the marshes bitter: finding Calyste with Camille had wounded him. He would soon seek an explanation with her and probably leave Les Touches—perhaps with Conti—and strange passions would follow. He warned Calyste to be careful entering such dangerous contests.

Calyste, stunned to learn Camille loved him while he believed himself pledged to Beatrix, sat undecided. He could not reconcile Camille’s love with Beatrix’s hold. He heard, without entering, Claude confront Felicite:

“You came to Paris last year desperately in love with Calyste,” Claude said. “Frightened by consequences, you took me as a screen to hide your feelings and leave yourself free to follow your adoration. That plan was grand and ignoble; you should have chosen a common man or one distracted enough to be deceived. You thought me simple because I am a man of talent, not genius—and I have divined you. You have never been loved, Maupin; you will not be after rejecting that beautiful fruit.”

“Why has love fled me?” she murmured.

“Because you are not lovable,” he answered. “You do not yield; your strength repels those who might struggle with you. You are grand and sublime; bear the consequence: men are fatigued by you.”

Camille protested, but Claude declared he’d leave to stop serving as her screen. He pronounced their superior minds condemned to solitude: “Lord God, Thou hast made me powerful and solitary.”

Calyste then appeared. Camille, visibly moved and more beautiful for it, flushed. Claude told Calyste plainly that Felicite loved him—not as herself but with the strange delight women take in the sufferings born of longing—“in some sense a man,” he added sardonically. He advised Calyste to use Beatrix’s attraction as a shield for Camille; the young man’s passion endangered the older woman. Camille, humiliated and weeping, could not meet their eyes. Calyste, struck, fell at her feet and kissed her hands.

“Do not abandon me, Claude,” Camille implored.

Claude argued there was no danger now: Calyste’s passion for the marquise would serve better as a barrier than Claude himself. Rising with a dominating look, Camille declared she could conceive a grandeur of love without hope: “Do not love me, Calyste; but I will love you as no woman will!” Claude knelt and kissed her hand.

Calyste returned to Guerande dazed, scarcely pitying Camille though later imagining her moans. At home gloom reigned; his mother, sewing by lamplight, noticed his agitation. He embraced her passionately.

“Camille loves me, but I love her no longer,” he told his mother.

The next day Calyste told Gasselin to watch for Mademoiselle des Touches’ carriage and, learning it had passed, saddled horses. His family guessed mischief—some thought he rode to see Charlotte de Pen-Hoel, a proposed match. Calyste denied that Felicite would obstruct any marriage. With affectionate farewells he rode off.

He arrived at the port just as Camille and the marquise waved to a steamer. Beatrix, poised in straw and muslin, gave Calyste an imperious look; Camille’s smile made the marquise sense the vulgarity of such conduct. Camille took Calyste’s arm and left Beatrix to watch the vessel. Soon Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel, the Vicomtesse de Kergarouet, and Charlotte arrived; Camille offered them places in her carriage, and Calyste conveyed the message. Charlotte, who had nourished boyhood hopes of Calyste, found him reserved and embarrassed.

The party passed through Guerande before a crowd. Calyste stayed near the ladies, giving his arm and bidding Camille and Beatrix farewell. He kissed Felicite’s hand; the marquise, with arms crossed, withheld hers. At the du Guenics, dinner and cards followed; all assumed Calyste free and nearly engaged to Charlotte. He alone was silent, torn between provincial ties and witty, sophisticated Parisians. His mother urged him to accept Camille’s carriage; he ran to Les Touches.

XI. FEMALE DIPLOMACY

Calyste reached Les Touches as Camille and Beatrix left the grand salon after dinner. He offered his arm to Felicite, who pressed it and chided him for abandoning the viscountess. Conversation revealed social links; Beatrix remarked the Kergarouet girl would suit Calyste, but Camille quickly denied any marriage would occur.

Mortified by Beatrix’s coolness toward Charlotte, Calyste grew silent. Camille, seeing his despair, accused Beatrix of coldness and suggested she had driven him toward the marquise. Beatrix’s calm, slightly distrustful manner wounded him. Camille took him into her chamber and shut the door.

“My child,” she said, “if you want to succeed with Beatrix, you must seem to love me still. You are too candid to play a part, but deception is the only way to win her. I will take it on. Come every day; spend mornings with me and study—four hours daily; in the evenings, we will be together. Obey me blindly. If you break our agreement and speak privately with the marquise, you will lose her forever.”

Calyste, trusting her, kissed her hand; Camille did not restrain tears. He left believing that in a week Beatrix would love him. That evening at home he resumed gaiety; the card players praised him. He returned to Les Touches regularly, studying, bringing books, and participating in the ruse.

After Calyste left, Camille, exhausted, confessed to Beatrix: “I am forty and I love him!” She lamented lost youth and the shame of being loved from pity. She asked Beatrix, who loved Conti, to be noble and help retain Calyste. Flattered and moved, Beatrix resolved to behave generously—giving Calyste a cool shoulder while secretly delighting in inspiring a man’s love.

The next morning Calyste found Camille at the window, arranging small signals to draw the marquise’s attention. Camille urged him to stay to dinner but refuse in a way that showed regret; Beatrix had dressed charmingly. These staged scenes became a comedy of feminine diplomacy: Camille played the jealous, self-sacrificing woman; Beatrix, alternating pride and curiosity, aimed to subdue her hurt with superiority. The duel between the two women used snares, feints, false generosity, and crafty confidences—Camille’s words both exposed and disguised her passion, poisoning Beatrix against her.

For six days Camille concentrated every artifice on her end. She praised Calyste’s purity and beauty to Beatrix, extolled his faith, and let the marquise perceive both the nobility and the danger of the boy. Beatrix, torn between jealousy and generosity, kept a gentle indifference that tormented Calyste. He had promised to obey Camille, but his love’s fury threatened the plan. He swore to see Beatrix and speak to her, craving to read her eyes and breathe her presence.

One morning he slipped into the garden and surprised Beatrix; their conversation was awkward. She told him he had neither pleased nor displeased her, and when he declared he no longer loved Camille she asked why he visited her every morning. Camille, appearing angrily, drew Calyste away with reproaches. Beatrix, stunned by Camille’s dramatic abnegation, could not comprehend the part she was being asked to play—yet the abnegation was precisely the extremity of feminine grandeur Camille could reach.

At breakfast the marquise resolved to stifle any rising love. Though not harsh, she remained gently indifferent, tormenting Calyste. Camille proposed an excursion, but Beatrix refused, fearing complications: Conti was due to return to fetch her. The thought of Conti’s return turned Calyste pale. Despite Camille’s urgings and his entreaties, Beatrix remained inflexible, showing the obstinacy Camille had warned about. Calyste left Les Touches plunged into the despair that follows intense love, beginning to think of ways to force an explanation with Beatrix.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
When Calyste reached home he shut himself in his room until dinner and returned there afterward. At ten his mother, uneasy at his absence, found him amid blotted, half-torn sheets: he was composing a letter to Béatrix, stung by doubts about Camille after the marquise’s manner in the garden.

No first love-letter is polished; it pours from the heart and repeats itself. Calyste’s first finished missive, which he read aloud to his astonished mother, filled their old mansion with the glow of his passion.

In essence he threw himself at the marquise’s feet. He told Béatrix he loved her as one loves a dream; seeing her intensified his devotion. He spoke little of mere beauty and more of understanding and adoration: he had studied her delicacy, divined her desires, and felt himself worthy of her notice. He begged to be believed in his submission, patience, and mute adoration; he asked no return unless she would deign a word of pity. Camille’s arguments that stagnant love is wretched only freed him to love Béatrix without expectation. He declared that, even if forbidden to love, he would adore her whatever she did; he could endure martyrdom for a single word of praise. He warned that if she persisted in cold silence he would suppose she feared him, and he entreated her to be herself—charming, witty, gay, tender—rather than the proud statue he both worshipped and feared. He begged merely for consolation, for a pity that would crown his sacrifice; asked if he might return to Les Touches the next day; and proposed an excursion to Croisic and Batz, saying that refusal would be taken as an answer.

There were four more pages of similar impassioned repetition. Though unlikely to move Madame de Rochefide, the letter made his mother weep.

“My child, you are not happy,” she said softly. The raging poem terrified the baroness; for the first time she read a love-letter. Calyste, perplexed how to send it, followed his mother into the salon with the letter burning in his pocket.
