The Chevalier du Halga was there; Charlotte de Kergarouet fluttered, trying to use Calyste’s apparent indifference for her own ends. Calyste wandered like a distracted butterfly. When the card game ended he asked the Chevalier in a whisper how best to send a letter secretly to one’s mistress.

“Slip it into the maid’s hand with a louis or two,” the chevalier suggested—though better, he said, to give the letter oneself. Calyste snatched his hat and ran to Les Touches, appearing like an apparition in the little salon where Camille and Béatrix sat together. He flung himself beside the marquise, took her hand, and slipped the letter in so swiftly that Félicité did not perceive it. His heart tingled as Béatrix pressed his hand and, without interrupting her words, slipped the letter into her glove.

“You fling yourself on a woman’s dress without mercy,” she laughed; Félicité reproved him as wanting in common sense. Calyste kissed Camille’s hand, then idly ran his fingernail over the piano keys. Camille, surprised by his exuberance, signed for him to come near; she probed him gently, but he excused himself because his mother expected him, and left Les Touches at eleven, after facing Camille’s piercing glance.

That wakeful night Calyste paced Guerande’s main street many times for an answer. At last the marquise’s waiting-woman brought a note, which he read in the garden grotto.

Béatrix’s reply was firm, measured, and meant to be a lesson in restraint. She began by calling him “a noble child,” but a child nonetheless; she reminded him he was bound to Camille, who adored him and could give him the perfections which distinguished her. Whatever he might imagine, Béatrix insisted, Camille was young and rich in heart; Béatrix herself was older, full of doubts and a slave to passions that made her unfit to be the source of his happiness. A man’s declaration of love to her, she said bluntly, is an insult: her situation forbids accepting such homage.

She painted the consequences: to yield would debase her, humiliate Camille, and invite bitter ridicule. She offered a poignant, even violent image—if she were seen humiliated and betrayed, she imagined herself capable of killing the man who had brought her low. Such motives, she claimed, were delicate and womanly, and men like Calyste could not be expected to grasp them. Camille’s love for him, she argued, is a barrier too high to be overleaped; none but a devil would fail to recoil before such treachery. For the sake of the finer feelings that govern women, she refused him.

Then she turned to Calyste’s future and spoke like a guardian: his mother was pure and spotless and would fulfil the ideal of the woman his wife should be. She promised she would not mock him by sending him back to little Charlotte without thought; instead, she would commend him to some “divine young girl” worthy of his love. She warned that if she were his wife his life would be blighted—she would be jealous, ready to be wretched over trifles, incapable of bearing the slow changes of ten years. She distrusted her own temper and feared she would be an “unreasonable tyrant” in private, however charming outwardly.

Felicité, Béatrix said, was inexhaustibly tender; she would marry him to some young girl even at the cost of her own suffering, would smooth difficulties and could make him her heir by selling an acre in Paris if necessary to free his estate in Brittany. In short, Camille could secure him a wife and happiness that Béatrix judged herself unfit to confer. For herself she held out a friendly hand, proposing to be a sister to him and to end the correspondence between Les Touches and Guerande that she deemed absurd.

Stirred by her son’s strange exhibitions, the baroness could not sit quietly. After listening to Calyste’s first letter and Béatrix’s reply, she rose and went to him humbly yet boldly to demand a favor she felt entitled to ask. When he read Béatrix’s letter aloud they saw no malice in the marquise’s cautious epistle. “She is a noble, grand woman!” the baroness said with moist eyes. “I will pray for her. I did not know a woman could abandon her husband and child and yet keep a soul so virtuous. She is worthy of pardon.”

“Have I not every reason to adore her?” cried Calyste.

“But where will this love lead you?” said the baroness. “How dangerous are women with noble sentiments! There is less to fear from the bad. Marry Charlotte de Kergarouet and release two-thirds of the estate. By selling a few farms, Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel can secure that result in the marriage contract and help you manage your property. You could leave your children a great name and estate.”

“Forget Béatrix!” said Calyste, muffled, eyes on the ground.

He left to write an answer.

Madame du Guenic, who had taken Béatrix’s letter into her heart, sought counsel to understand it and Calyste’s hope. She found the Chevalier du Halga on the mall and recounted, without naming names, the affair and the letters. The chevalier, with the air of a man versed in gallantry, compared Calyste to a thoroughbred horse reconnoitring a barrier and declared him a lucky dog. The baroness, terrified and impulsive, ran to Calyste’s room in search of him. He was absent; she saw an unsealed letter addressed to Madame de Rochefide on the table and, compelled by invincible curiosity, read it. The indiscretion punished her: Calyste’s second letter revealed how deep the gulf into which his passion hurled him.

In that second letter Calyste abandoned whatever reserve remained and wrote with a clearer-eyed resolution. He declared that the Guenic name no longer mattered to him; his name was Béatrix; her happiness and life were his fortune; his estates might remain mortgaged for generations—his true property was her heart. The thought of marrying another convulsed him. He vowed to marry no one but her and would wait twenty years if need be; he believed she would remain ever beautiful and that his mother, a saint who had never loved, had learned from Béatrix what she had missed. He said Béatrix had taught him to love his mother better and placed her above all rivals.

Regarding Camille, he begged Béatrix to tell her that Camille was not loved by him; since seeing Béatrix, Camille had become a sister, a friend, a comrade—only entitled to the rights of friendship. He minimized Camille’s womanhood, saying he had perceived in Camille the habits and airs of a man—she swims, hunts, smokes, drinks, rides, writes and analyzes hearts—and that Camille lacked the particular graces of womanhood present in Béatrix. From the first moment he had felt Béatrix was his.

He begged to elope: to flee somewhere where she was known to no one, where only God and he might reach her heart; Ireland, he suggested, was full of castles and the family could lend one. A boat and a few sailors, and they would be safe. He insisted Béatrix had never been loved and that a “sacred love” could wipe out the past; he said he loved her so truly he could even wish her doubly shamed if such a trial would sanctify her. He asked for one day of happiness—one walk by the sea, her foot on his sands would sanctify them—such a passing alms would be an eternal riches to him.

The baroness let the letter fall before finishing it. She knelt and prayed to God to save her son’s reason, to remove his madness. Calyste entered.

“What are you doing, mother?” he asked.

“I am praying to God for you,” she answered simply, turning tearful eyes on him. “I have committed the sin of reading that letter. My Calyste is mad!”

“A sweet madness!” he said, kissing her.

“I wish I could see that woman,” she sighed.

“Mamma,” Calyste answered, “we shall take a boat to-morrow and cross to Croisic. If you are on the jetty you can see her.”

So saying, he sealed his letter and departed for Les Touches. What especially terrified the baroness was that Calyste’s second letter showed instinctive feeling and a clear-eyed resolution that read as if the worldly chevalier might have dictated it. Where the first letter had been the raw overflow of boyish passion, the second combined passion with a practical readiness to act—renouncing family and name, proposing elopement, minimizing Camille’s claim, and asserting a willingness to endure any scandal for Béatrix. That blend of resolve and romantic extremity made the baroness fear both for his future and for the entanglements that might ensue.

Meanwhile, at Les Touches, people were watching. Calyste’s appearance there, his boldness in pressing the letter into Béatrix’s hand, had not escaped observation. Mademoiselle des Touches noted there was “something between them.” Félicité reproved him for his lack of common sense; the marquise remained impenetrable, answering with calm refusal rather than pique. The chevalier, who enjoyed such matters as sport, thought Calyste a lucky dog. Charlotte de Kergarouet, who had her designs on his fortune, was anxious, and gossip in Guerande spread: “Something very extraordinary is happening at the du Guenics,” Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel told her niece.

Béatrix’s letter, while refusing his passion, did not scorn him. She treated him with stern kindness, explained the reasons that made love between them impossible, and offered to help secure him a respectable match through Camille and Félicité. Calyste, too impassioned to see the prudence of her refusal, answered like a man ready to renounce fortune and family for love. His mother, torn between admiration for Béatrix’s nobility and terror at her son’s folly, prayed and tried to reason with him toward safer counsel—marriage to Charlotte and prudent management of the estate.

The chapter thus closes on a tableau of conflicting loyalties and temperaments: Calyste’s youthful, almost feverish readiness to sacrifice everything for an idealized woman; Béatrix’s sober, proud refusal grounded in delicacy and the obligations of social sentiment; Camille’s silent proprietorship of his heart; Félicité’s enduring tenderness; and the baroness’s maternal anguish. Calyste’s passion, escalated by his second letter into a deliberate plan to elope or to wait indefinitely, alarms the family for the danger it poses both to his estate and to his reason.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
One of the greatest pleasures for small minds is deceiving a great soul and setting snares for it. Beatrix felt herself far inferior to Camille Maupin—not only in talent but in the deeper matter of the heart: passion.

When Calyste hurried to Les Touches with the impetuosity of first love, the marquise delighted in being the object of that affection. She did not want to share it; she fancied herself noble in repressing her caprice and persuaded herself she matched Camille’s devotion, imagining she sacrificed for a friend. Her vanity—the coquetry that formed so much of her charm—was flattered by Calyste’s love. Attacked by such seduction, she resisted while admiring her own virtue.

The two women reclined on the little salon divan; the room was harmonious and fragrant, windows open to a southerly breeze that stirred the salt lake and made the sun glitter on the sands. Their souls, however, were agitated. Camille, bruised by the machinery she had set in motion, guarded herself against the clever friend she had admitted. To preserve secrets and remain aloof she sought refuge in nature; she cheated her aching heart by finding God in that sky and rock. When an unbeliever perceives God, he flings himself into Catholicism as a complete system.

That morning Camille bore the halo of thoughts born of those researches after a night of struggle. Calyste stood before her like a heavenly image. The beautiful youth she had secretly worshiped seemed a guardian angel who lifted her above suffering. Yet an air of triumph about Beatrix disturbed her. No woman gains advantage without making it noticed; the dumb moral contest between them—each hiding a secret and believing herself generous—was strange.

Calyste arrived, a letter held between hand and glove, ready to slip to Beatrix. Camille, noting a subtle change in her friend’s manner, watched Beatrix in a mirror as Calyste entered. Such moments are crucial for women: even the most guarded betray themselves in a look. Too much reserve or too little, a luminous gaze, the lowering of an eyelid—all reveal what is hardest to hide. Women have a genius for nuance; they can read a rival from head to foot. Two women observing one another enact one of life’s choicest comedies.

“Calyste has committed some folly,” thought Camille, detecting in each guest an indefinable air of mutual understanding.

Beatrix no longer feigned indifference; she regarded Calyste as her own. Calyste was even more transparent; he colored like someone guilty or happy. He announced he had come to arrange the excursion for the next day.

“Then you really intend to go, my dear?” Camille asked.

“Yes,” said Beatrix.

“How did you know it, Calyste?” Camille asked.

“I came here to find out,” he replied, prompted by a look from Madame de Rochefide who did not want Camille to learn of their correspondence.

“They have an agreement,” thought Camille, catching that look. A horrible discomposure spread over her face and frightened Beatrix.

“What is the matter, my dear?” she cried.

“Nothing. Well then, Calyste, send my horses and yours across to Croisic, so we may drive home by way of Batz. We will breakfast at Croisic and be home for dinner. You must arrange the boat. Let us start by half-past eight. You will see some fine sights, Beatrix, and one very strange one; you will see Cambremer, a man who does penance on a rock for having wilfully killed his son. Oh, you are in a primitive land, moved by different sentiments than ordinary mortals. Calyste shall tell you the tale; it is a drama of the seashore.”

She went into her bedroom, stifling. Calyste gave his letter to Beatrix and followed Camille.

“Calyste, you are loved, I think; but you are hiding something from me; you have done some foolish thing,” Camille said gravely.

“Loved!” he exclaimed, sinking into a chair.

Camille glanced into the next room; Beatrix had disappeared—odd, since women seldom leave a room with the man they admire unless sure of seeing him again or for advantage. Mademoiselle des Touches wondered, “Can he have given her a letter?” but thought the innocent Breton incapable of such boldness.

“If you have disobeyed me, all will be lost through your own fault,” she warned. “Go now and make your preparations for tomorrow.”

She made a gesture Calyste did not resist.

As he walked toward Croisic to hire boatmen, fears entered Calyste’s mind. Camille’s speech foreshadowed something fatal; he believed this prescient warning. When he returned four hours later, tired and expecting to dine at Les Touches, he found Camille’s maid at the door to tell him that neither her mistress nor the marquise could receive him that evening. Calyste, surprised, wished to question her, but she bade him hastily good-night and closed the door.

Six o’clock struck in Guérande as Calyste entered his house; Mariotte gave him his belated dinner, after which he sat gloomy and pensive. Alternations of joy and despair—extinctions of hope after seeming certainties of being loved—bruised the young soul that had flown so high that the fall was dreadful.

“Does anything trouble you, my Calyste?” his mother asked.

“Nothing,” he replied, his eyes lacking their usual light.

It is not hope but despair that measures our ambitions. The finest poems of hope are sung in secret; grief appears unveiled.

“Calyste, you are not nice,” teased Charlotte after vain attempts to cheer him.

“I am tired,” he said, rising and bidding them good-night.

“Calyste is much changed,” remarked Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel. The baroness, noting the marquise would be at Croisic, declared she wished to see her on the jetty.

While Calyste tried to guess what had closed Les Touches’ doors to him, a scene between Camille and Beatrix unfolded that would decide the morrow.

Calyste’s last letter had stirred in Madame de Rochefide emotions hitherto unknown; women are rarely the object of love so young, guileless, sincere, and unconditional. Beatrix, who had loved more than she had been loved, suddenly desired to be a tyrant. Yet rereading the letter, a cruel idea pierced her.

What were Calyste and Camille doing alone since Claude Vignon’s departure? If Calyste did not love Camille, and Camille knew it, how did they spend their mornings? Memory flashed certain of Camille’s speeches into Beatrix’s mind; a grinning devil seemed to show her, like a magic mirror, that heroic woman’s gestures that now took a different meaning. Instead of being Camille’s equal, was she crushed? Instead of outwitting her, had she been outwitted? Was she merely a toy, a diversion Camille granted her child, whom she loved with extraordinary, nonvulgar devotion?

To Beatrix such a thought was a thunderclap. She reviewed the past week minutely; the roles each had played unrolled before her; she felt horribly belittled. In her fury of jealous anger she fancied Camille’s conduct an intention of vengeance against Conti. Once on that path of doubt she did not pause. She paced, sat, tried to decide, unable to; she remained prey to indecision until dinner, when she rose hastily and went downstairs without dressing. Seeing her in a morning gown with chilling air and taciturn manner, Camille felt a crisis had come. Beatrix’s look foretold the hostilities of an embittered heart.

Camille instantly left the room and gave the order surprising Calyste; she feared he might arrive amid the quarrel and wished to fight alone without witnesses. Beatrix alone would infallibly succumb. Camille knew the other’s soul was barren and her pride petty and obstinate.

The dinner was gloomy. Camille was gentle and kind; she felt superior. Beatrix was hard and cutting; she felt managed like a child. The battle began with glances, gestures, half-spoken phrases—insufficient to enlighten servants but enough to prepare an observer for the storm. When they rose, Camille offered her arm maliciously to Beatrix, who pretended not to see and sprang up the stair alone. After coffee, Mademoiselle des Touches dismissed the footman with “You may go”—a brief sentence serving as their signal.

“The novels you make, my dear, are more dangerous than those you write,” the marquise began.

“They have one advantage, however,” Camille replied, lighting a cigarette.

“What is that?” asked Beatrix.

“They are unpublished, my angel.”

“Is the one in which you are putting me to be turned into a book?”

“I’ve no fancy for the role of Oedipus; I know you have the wit and beauty of a sphinx, but don’t propound conundrums. Speak out plainly, my dear Beatrix.”

“When, to make a man happy, amuse him, please him, and save him from ennui, we let the devil help us—”

“That man would reproach us later for our efforts and think them prompted by the genius of depravity,” interrupted Camille, taking the cigarette from her lips.

“He forgets the love that carried us away; that’s our sole justification—but men are unjust and ungrateful,” Beatrix continued. “Women know each other; they know how proud and noble their minds are, and—frankly—how virtuous! But, Camille, I’ve just recognized criticisms of your nature you’ve sometimes complained of. You have something of the man about you; you behave like a man; nothing restrains you; if you haven’t all a man’s advantages, you have his spirit and his contempt for women. I have no reason to be satisfied with you and am too frank to hide my dissatisfaction. No one has ever given or will give so cruel a wound as the one I now suffer. If you are not a woman in love, you are one in vengeance. It takes a woman of genius to find the most sensitive spot in another woman’s delicacy. I speak now of Calyste and the trickery—you have employed against me. To what depths have you descended, Camille Maupin! and why?”

“More and more sphinx-like!” said Camille, smiling.

“You want me to fling myself at Calyste’s head; but I am still too young for that. To me, love is sacred; love is love with all its emotions, jealousies, and despotisms. I am not an author; I cannot see ideas where the heart feels sentiments.”

“You think yourself capable of loving foolishly!” said Camille. “Make yourself easy; you have plenty of sense. You calumniate yourself; your nature is cool enough to let your head judge your heart.”

The marquise colored; she darted a venomous look and found sharper arrows. Camille smoked composedly as Beatrix launched a furious tirade and even attacked Camille’s age.

“Is that all?” said Félicité when Beatrix paused, letting smoke exhale.

“Do you love Calyste?”

“No; of course not.”

“So much the better,” Camille replied. “I do love him—far too much for my peace of mind. He may perhaps have had a passing fancy for you; for you are enchantingly fair, while I am as black as a crow; you are slim and willowy, while I have portly dignity; in short, you are young!—that’s the final word, and you have not spared it to me. You have abused your advantages as a woman against me. I have done my best to prevent what has now happened. However little woman you may think me, I am woman enough not to allow a rival to triumph unless I choose to help her.” This apparently innocent remark cut the marquise to the heart. “You take me for a very silly person if you believe all that Calyste tries to make you think of me. I am neither so great nor so small; I am a woman, and very much of a woman. Come, put off your grand airs, and give me your hand!” Camille took Madame de Rochefide’s hand. “You do not love Calyste, you say; that is true, is it not? Don’t be angry; be hard, cold, and stern to him tomorrow; he will end by submitting, especially after certain reproaches I mean to make. Still, Calyste is a Breton, and very persistent; if he continues to court you, tell me frankly, and I will lend you my little country house near Paris, where you will find comforts, and where Conti can come out and see you. You said Calyste calumniated me. Good heavens! what of that? The purest love lies twenty times a day; its deceptions prove its strength.”

Camille’s superb disdain frightened Beatrix, who did not know how to answer. Camille dealt another blow.

“I am more confiding and less bitter than you,” she said. “I don’t suspect you of attempting to cover by a quarrel a secret injury that would compromise my life. You know me; I shall never survive the loss of Calyste, but I must lose him sooner or later. Still, Calyste loves me now; of that I am sure.”

“Here is what he answered to my letter urging him to be true to you,” said Beatrix, holding out Calyste’s letter.

Camille read it; her eyes filled with tears; presently she wept as women weep in their bitterest sorrows.

“My God!” she said, “how he loves her! I shall die without being understood—or loved,” she added.

She sat with her head on Beatrix’s shoulder; her grief was genuine, the same terrible blow the Baronne du Guénic had felt reading that letter.

“Do you love him?” she asked, looking fixedly at Beatrix. “Have you that infinite worship for him which triumphs over all pain, survives contempt, betrayal, the certainty he will never love you? Do you love him for himself and for the joy of loving him?”

“Dear friend,” said the marquise tenderly, “be happy, be at peace; I will leave this place tomorrow.”

“No, do not go; he loves you, I see that. Well, I love him so much I could not endure to see him wretched. I had formed plans and projects; but if he loves you, all is over.”

“And I love him, Camille,” said the marquise with naïveté and coloring.

“You love him and yet you cast him off!” cried Camille. “Ah! that is not loving; you do not love him.”

“I don’t know what fresh virtue he has roused in me, but certainly he has made me ashamed of myself,” said Beatrix. “I would I were virtuous and free, to give him something better than the dregs of a heart and the weight of my chains. I do not want a hampered destiny for him or myself.”

“Cold brain!” exclaimed Camille, horrified. “To love and calculate!”

“Call it what you like,” Beatrix replied, “but I will not spoil his life, or hang like a millstone round his neck to become an eternal regret. If I cannot be his wife, I shall not be his mistress. He has— you will laugh? No? Well, then, he has purified me.”

Camille cast the most sullen, savage look female jealousy can cast.

“On that ground, I believed I stood alone,” she said. “Beatrix, those words must separate us forever; we are no longer friends. Here begins a terrible conflict between us. I tell you now; you will either succumb or fly.”

So saying, Camille bounded into her room, showing the maddened-lioness’s face to astonished Beatrix, then raised the portière and looked back in.

“Do you intend to go to Croisic tomorrow?” she asked.

“Certainly,” replied Beatrix proudly. “I shall not fly, and I shall not succumb.”

“I play above board,” replied Camille; “I shall write to Conti.”

Beatrix turned as white as her gauze scarf.

“We are staking our lives on this game,” she murmured, not knowing what to say or do.

The violent passions roused by this scene cooled during the night. Both argued with themselves and returned to the temporizing courses attractive to most women—an excellent scheme between men and women, but fatally unsafe among women alone. In the midst of this tumult Mademoiselle des Touches listened to that great Voice whose counsels subdue the strongest will; Beatrix heard only the promptings of worldly wisdom; she feared society’s contempt.

Thus Félicité’s last deception succeeded; Calyste’s blunder was repaired, but a fresh indiscretion might yet be fatal to him.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
It was the end of August, and the sky was magnificently clear. Near the horizon the sea had a molten-silver tint; on the shore it rippled in tiny waves. A glowing vapor from the sun on the sands produced an almost tropical atmosphere. Salt gleamed like bunches of white violets on the marsh surface. The patient paludiers, dressed in white against the sun and armed with long rakes, had been at their posts since morning. Some leaned on the low mud-walls dividing the holdings, watching the salt-making known to them from childhood; others played with their wives and children. The custom-house officers smoked their pipes with calm.

The scene had an exotic air; a Parisian suddenly transported here would hardly guess he was in France. The baron and baroness, who had feigned interest in the salt harvest, stood on the jetty admiring the silent landscape where only the sea sounded, and where boats and the cultivated green girdle of earth made a rare charm on the ocean shore.

“Well, my friends, I wanted to see the marshes of Guérande once more before I die,” said the baron to the paludiers gathered at the marsh entrance.

“Can a Guénic die?” replied one.

The party from Les Touches arrived by the narrow path. The marquise walked first alone; Calyste and Camille followed arm-in-arm, Gasselin bringing up the rear.

“There are my father and mother,” Calyste said to Camille.

The marquise stopped. Madame du Guénic felt the strongest repulsion for Béatrix, though Béatrix was well dressed: a wide Leghorn hat with bluebells, crimped hair, a gray wool gown and a blue sash gave her the air of a princess disguised as a milkmaid.

“She has no heart,” thought the baroness.

“Mademoiselle,” Calyste said to Camille, “this is Madame du Guénic, and this is my father.” Then to the baron and baroness: “Mademoiselle des Touches, and Madame la Marquise de Rochefide, née de Casteran, father.”

The baron bowed; Mademoiselle des Touches made a respectful bow full of gratitude to the baroness.

“That one,” Fanny thought, “really loves my boy; she seems to thank me for bringing him into the world.”

“I suppose you have come to see, as I have, whether the harvest is a good one. But I believe you have better reasons,” said the baron to Camille. “You have property here, I think.”

“Mademoiselle is the largest owner,” said one of the paludiers, “and may God preserve her to us, for she’s a good lady.”

The parties bowed and separated.

“No one would suppose Mademoiselle des Touches to be more than thirty,” said the baron. “She is very handsome. And Calyste prefers that haggard Parisian marquise to a sound Breton girl!”

“I fear he does,” replied the baroness.

A boat awaited at the jetty steps; the party embarked in silence. The marquise was cold and dignified. Camille had lectured Calyste on his disobedience, making the situation plain. Calyste, in black despair, cast glances at Béatrix where anger and love struggled. Not a word was spoken during the short passage from the jetty of Guérande to the port of Croisic, where salt is discharged and peasant-women carry it away in earthen jars on their heads. These women, barefoot with short petticoats, sometimes let their kerchiefs fly open; some wearing only shifts appeared more dignified, their modest bearing seeming nobler with less clothing.

The little Danish vessel had just finished lading; the landing of two handsome ladies excited curiosity among the salt-carriers. To avoid their remarks and to serve Calyste, Camille sprang toward the rocks, leaving him to follow with Béatrix. Gasselin kept some distance behind his master.

The Croisic peninsula is flanked by granite rocks whose fantastic shapes defy easy comparison. The rocks form interminable arabesques; the sea in storm polishes them. Under a natural vaulted roof, like a bold effect art imitates, is a rocky hollow polished like a marble bathtub floored with white sand and tepid water safe for bathing. Cool little covered hollows sheltered by great portals, roughly carved but majestic, recall palaces imitating nature’s whims.

There is one rare thing here: a large box-bush, perhaps unique on such rocky shores, three miles from the harbor on the point of rocks that runs far into the sea. On a granite promontory, high and out of the spray, chance or birds have placed enough vegetable earth for this compact plant, whose roots indicate an existence of perhaps three hundred years. Above it the rock breaks off abruptly; the convulsion that caused this left marked traces.

The sea rushes to the foot of this cliff, which rises four or five hundred feet; scattered rocks at its base describe a semi-circle. It takes nerve to climb this little Gibraltar whose summit resembles old castle look-out towers. From it you see the clock towers and arid fields of Croisic, the sandy dunes stretching to Batz. Sardine fishers gave the rock a Breton name better left unsaid.

Calyste led Béatrix to this point, where the view is magnificent and where the granite’s sculpture is even more imposing close at hand.

Camille, like a wounded wild animal longing for solitude, threaded the fissures and caves of nature’s fortress. Not to be hampered in climbing, she wore trousers with frilled edges, a short blouse, a peaked cap, and carried a riding-whip. In this attire she looked handsomer than Béatrix; she wore a little crimson China crape shawl crossed on her bosom and tied behind like a child. For some time Béatrix and Calyste saw her flitting over peaks and chasms like a ghost; she tried to still inward sufferings by confronting imaginary peril.

Camille was first to reach the rock where the box-bush grew. She sat in the shade, lost in thought. What could a woman like her do with old age, having already drunk the cup of fame too eagerly? There, on that spot, a singular reflection—one of those chance accidents that open vast depths of thought to noble souls—decided her to take an extraordinary step to part forever from social life.

She drew from her pocket a little box of strawberry lozenges, ate several, and thought: the strawberries no longer exist, yet live in their qualities. Was it not so with ourselves? The ocean was an image of the infinite. No great spirit can face the infinite and admit immortality without the conviction of a future of holiness. The thought filled her. How petty then seemed the part she had been playing! There was no true greatness in giving Béatrix to Calyste. Thinking so, the earthly woman died within her; the noble and angelic being veiled by flesh arose. Her mind, knowledge, attainments, and false loves confronted the bounteous mother and comforter of troubled spirits—the Roman Church—poetic to poets, childlike with children, and profound to anxious souls. She saw her love for Calyste as a divine messenger who had turned earthly desire to divine love.

After walking in silence, Calyste could not refrain from comparing the ocean’s depth and purity with his love when Béatrix remarked on the ocean’s grandeur, unlike the Mediterranean.

“It is met by a rock!” said Béatrix, laughing.

“When you speak thus,” he answered with a sublime look, “I hear you, I see you, and I can summon the patience of the angels; but when I am alone, you would pity me if you could see me then. My mother weeps for my suffering.”

“Listen to me, Calyste; we must put an end to all this,” said the marquise, gazing down upon the sandy plain. “Perhaps this is the only propitious place to say these things, for never in my life did I see nature more in keeping with my thoughts. I have seen Italy, where all tells of love; Switzerland, where all is cool and fresh and tells of labor’s happiness; but I have never seen anything that so depicts the burning barrenness of my life as that arid plain dried by salt winds, corroded by spray, where fruitless agriculture struggles against the ocean. There, Calyste, you have an image of this Béatrix. Don’t cling to it. I love you, but I will never be yours in any way, for I have the sense of my inward desolation. No, you shall never see your idol diminished; she shall never fall from the height at which you have placed her. I now have a horror of love which disregards the world and religion. I shall remain in my present bonds; I shall be that sandy plain—without fruit or flowers.”

“But if you are abandoned?” said Calyste.

“Then I should beg my pardon of the man I have offended. I will never risk taking a happiness I know would quickly end.”

“End!” cried Calyste.

The marquise repeated the word in a tone that silenced him. This opposition roused in Calyste a mute inward fury known only to those who love without hope. They walked for several hundred steps in silence, looking neither at sea nor rocks nor plain.

“I would make you happy,” said Calyste.

“All men begin by promising that,” she answered. “They end by abandonment and disgust. I have no reproach to cast on him to whom I shall be faithful. He made me no promises; I went to him; but my only means of lessening my fault is to make it eternal.”

“Say rather,” he cried, “that you feel no love for me. I, who love you, know love cannot argue; it is itself; it sees nothing else. There is no sacrifice I will not make to you; command it, and I will do the impossible. He who despised his mistress for flinging her glove among the lions and ordered him to bring it back did not love—he denied the right to test our hearts and to yield only to our utmost devotion. I will sacrifice my family, my name, my future.”

“What an insult in that word ‘sacrifice’!” she said reproachfully, which made Calyste feel the folly of his speech.

“You are right,” he replied, letting fall a tear; “that word can only be said of the cruel struggles you ask of me.”

“Hush!” said Béatrix, struck by an answer in which, for the first time, Calyste had really made her feel his love. “I have done wrong enough; tempt me no more.”

They reached the base of the rock where the box grew. Calyste helped her ascend the steep slope; to the poor lad it was a precious privilege to support her. This turned his head; he saw nothing but Béatrix and clasped her round the waist.

“What!” she said with an imposing air.

“Will you never be mine?” he demanded, voice choked with tumult.

“Never, my friend,” she replied. “I can only be to you a Béatrix—a dream. But is that not sweet and tender? We shall have no bitterness, no grief, no repentance.”

“Will you return to Conti?”

“I must.”

“You shall never belong to any man!” cried Calyste, pushing her with frenzied violence.

He listened for her fall, intending to spring after her, but heard only a muffled sound: the tearing of some stuff and then the thud of a body. Instead of being flung headlong down the precipice, Béatrix had slipped eight or ten feet into the cavity where the box-bush grew; her gown caught on a rock point and, tearing slowly, lowered her onto the bush.

Mademoiselle des Touches, horrified, could not cry out but signed to Gasselin to come. Calyste leaned forward, his expression savage curiosity; he saw Béatrix’s position and shuddered. Her lips moved—she seemed to be praying; she thought she was about to die as the bush began to give. With youth’s agility Calyste slid down to the ledge below the bush, grasped the marquise and held her though both risked sliding into the sea. She had fainted; in that aerial spot he fancied her wholly his, and his first emotion was pleasure.

“Open your eyes,” he said, “and forgive me; we will die together.”

“Die?” she said, opening her eyes and unclosing pallid lips.

Calyste welcomed the word with a kiss, and felt her tremble convulsively with passionate joy. At that instant Gasselin’s hob-nailed shoes sounded above them. The old Breton was followed by Camille; together they sought a way to save the lovers.

“There is but one way, mademoiselle,” said Gasselin. “I must slide down there; they can climb on my shoulders; you must pull them up.”

“And you?” said Camille.

The man seemed surprised to be considered. “You must go to Croisic and fetch a ladder,” she answered.

Béatrix asked to be laid down; Calyste placed her on the narrow space between the bush and rock. “I saw you, Calyste,” Camille said from above. “Whether Béatrix lives or dies, remember that this must be an accident.”

“She will hate me,” he said, moist-eyed.

“She will adore you,” replied Camille. “But this puts an end to our excursion. We must get her back to Les Touches. Had she been killed, Calyste, what would have become of you?”

“I should have followed her.”

“And your mother? And me?” After a pause she added feebly.

Calyste stood pale and silent. Gasselin returned from a nearby farm with a ladder. By then Béatrix had recovered somewhat. With the ladder placed and Gasselin lowering Camille’s red shawl for her to grasp, Béatrix reached the top where the Breton took her in his arms and carried her to the shore like an infant.

“I should not have said no to death—but suffering!” she murmured to Félicité, weak.

The marquise’s prostration obliged Camille to have her taken to the farmhouse from which the ladder had been borrowed. Calyste, Gasselin and Camille made a litter from spare clothes on the ladder and carried Béatrix; the farmers gave her a bed. Gasselin rode to Croisic for a doctor, telling the boatman to row to the nearest landing.

Calyste sat on a stool answering with head motions and rare monosyllables; Camille’s uneasiness at Béatrix’s condition grew with Calyste’s unnatural state. The physician bled Béatrix; she improved and consented to embark, and by five o’clock they reached the jetty at Guérande whence she was carried to Les Touches. News of the accident had already spread widely.

Calyste passed the night at Les Touches, sitting at the foot of Béatrix’s bed with Camille. The doctor had assured them that only slight stiffness would remain. Through Calyste’s despair there came a gleam of joy: he was there at her feet, could watch her sleep or wake, study her pallid face and expressions. Camille recognized in Calyste a passion one feels but once—a passion that dyes a man’s soul and faculties when nothing else distracts him. She saw he would never see the real woman in Béatrix.

How guileless Calyste was: when he saw Béatrix’s green eyes turned to him with love, confusion, and mischief, he colored and turned away.

“Did I not say truly, Calyste, that you men promise happiness and end by flinging us down a precipice?” Béatrix jested sweetly.

Calyste knelt, took her moist hand, and kissed it humbly. “You have the right to reject my love forever,” he said. “I have no right to say a word.”

“Ah!” cried Camille, seeing Béatrix’s expression and comparing it to the results of her diplomacy, “love has a wit of its own, wiser than that of all the world! Take your composing-draught, my dear friend, and go to sleep.”

That night at Les Touches left ineffaceable marks on Calyste, who had read Indiana—the work of Camille’s rival—while Mademoiselle des Touches read theological mysticism. Félicité soothed Calyste with the assurance that unless a woman were a monster she must be flattered by being the object of such a crime.

“You would never have flung me into the water,” said Camille, brushing away a tear.

Toward morning Calyste, worn out, fell asleep in his arm-chair; the marquise watched his pale, charming face and heard him murmur her name as he slept.

“He loves while sleeping,” she said to Camille.

“We must send him home,” said Félicité, waking him.

No one at the hôtel du Guénic was anxious; Mademoiselle des Touches had written to the baroness about the accident.

Calyste returned to dinner at Les Touches and found Béatrix up and dressed, though pale and feeble. The harshness and coldness were gone. After an evening of music by Camille, who left Calyste free to take and press Béatrix’s hands, no storms occurred at Les Touches. Félicité completely effaced herself.

Cold, fragile, hard women like Madame de Rochefide—women whose necks have a feline turn—have pale souls; to melt them needs a thunderbolt. To Béatrix, Calyste’s fury of love and his mad action were that thunderbolt. She felt inwardly humbled; a true, pure love bathed her heart. She breathed a genial atmosphere of feelings hitherto unknown to her; she felt magnified, elevated—raised to the heaven Bretons place the Woman in. She relished the respectful adoration of the youth, whose happiness cost her little: a gesture, a look, a word satisfied him. The value Calyste placed on trifles touched her; to hold her gloved hand meant more to him than possession meant to another man. What a contrast between them!

Few women could resist such constant deification. Béatrix felt sure of being obeyed and understood; she could ask Calyste to risk his life for a caprice and he would do it without reflection. This gave her a noble and imposing air. She saw love in its grandeur and sought to remain forever the loftiest of women in his eyes.

Her coquetries grew persistent because she felt an inward weakness. She played the invalid for a week with charming hypocrisy, leaning on Calyste’s arm in languid dependence as they walked between house and garden on the velvet turf.

Before the Croisic excursion the two women had talked about love, laughing at the ways men declare it; they admitted the cleverest men, naturally the least loving, often go straight to the point. “They go to work like La Fontaine when he wanted to enter the Academy,” said Camille.

Madame de Rochefide had unbounded power to restrain Calyste by reminding him of his violence on the rocks; a look from her made his eyes fill with tears and he swallowed his prayers and arguments. By infernal coquetry she finally brought him to seek Camille’s advice.

Beatrix, armed with Calyste’s own letter, quoted his axiom that to love was the first happiness and to be loved came later; she used it to restrain his passion within respectful idolatry, which suited her. She liked the sweet hymns of praise and adoration that youth offers; she gave no definite answer. Love was not yet the question; he asked only permission to love. Calyste still clung to love’s spiritual side; that was his sublime devotion.

Nevertheless, driven to despair, Calyste complained to Camille.

“I meant to cure you by making you quickly understand her,” she replied; “but you have spoiled all. Ten days ago you were her master; today you are her slave. You will never have the strength to do as I advise.”

“What ought I to do?”

“Quarrel with her over her hardness. A woman is always over-excited when she discusses; let her be angry and ill-treat you, then stay away; do not return to Les Touches till she recalls you.”

In extreme illness there is a moment when the patient accepts cruel remedies. Calyste had reached that point. He followed Camille’s advice and stayed away two days; on the third he scratched at Béatrix’s door to let her know he and Camille were waiting breakfast for her.

“Another chance lost!” Camille said when she saw him reappear weakly.

During his absence Béatrix often looked through the window toward the road to Guérande. When Camille found her doing so she talked of the gorse’s golden blooms along the roadway, dazzling in the September sun.

The marquise kept them waiting long for breakfast; when she appeared, her dress showed evident intention to fascinate him and prevent another absence. After breakfast she walked with him in the garden and filled his simple heart with joy by expressing a wish to return to the rock where she had nearly perished.

“Will you go with me alone?” asked Calyste.

“If I refused, I should give you reason to think I found you dangerous. Alas! as I have told you, I belong to another, and I must be his only. I chose him knowing nothing of love. The fault was great, and bitter is my punishment.”

When she spoke thus with moist eyes, Calyste was filled with compassion that tempered his ardor; he adored her as he might a Madonna. Béatrix was undergoing an inward struggle, hesitating between herself and Calyste—between the world she hoped to re-enter and the young happiness offered to her. She began, therefore, to listen to his blind passion and allowed his soft pity to soothe her. Several times she was moved to tears as she listened and suffered him to commiserate her being bound to an evil genius like Conti. Camille had told Calyste of Béatrix’s misery in Italy and given him lectures he profited from.

“I will love you absolutely,” he said. “I have no triumphs of art to offer you; my only talent is to love you. My honor, my pride are in your perfections. No other woman can have merit in my eyes; you have no odious rival to fear. You are misconceived and wronged; for every wrong I will make you feel my comprehension day by day.”

She listened with bowed head, letting him kiss her hands and admitting silently that she was indeed an angel misunderstood.

“I am too humiliated,” she said. “My past has robbed the future of all security.”

It was a glorious day for Calyste when, arriving at Les Touches at seven, he saw Béatrix at a window watching for him, wearing the same straw hat from their memorable first excursion. For a moment he was dazzled; such small things of passion magnify the world.

They left Les Touches by the garden gate onto the dunes. Béatrix loved the sands, gathered hardy little plants with rose-colored flowers to mix with Chartreux pansies and divide with Calyste—those flowers were to be an eternal, dreadful relic.

“We’ll add a bit of box,” she said smiling.

They sat on the jetty awaiting the boat; Calyste told her of his juvenile escapade on the day of her arrival.

“I knew of your little escapade,” she said, “and it was the cause of my sternness to you that first night.”

During their walk her tone was lightly jesting with tenderness. Calyste had reason to think himself beloved. At a charming creek strewn with brilliant pebbles, when Calyste, at the summit of happiness, asked her plainly to fly with him to Ireland, she resumed her distant air, asked for his arm, and continued in silence to her Tarpeian rock.

“My friend,” she said mounting the magnificent block of granite where she made herself a pedestal, “I have not the courage to conceal what you are to me. For ten years I have had no happiness comparable to searching for shells with you and exchanging pebbles for the necklace I will treasure more than diamonds. I have been a little girl again, such as I was at fourteen or sixteen—when I was worthy of you. The love I have inspired in you has raised me in my own eyes. Understand these words.”

They reached the rock’s summit, seeing the vast ocean on one side and Brittany on the other. “But,” she continued, “I do not belong to myself; I am more bound by my own will than by law. You must be punished for my misdeed, but be satisfied to know we suffer together. Dante never saw his Beatrice again; Petrarch never had his Laura. Such disasters fall only on noble souls. But if I should be abandoned, if I fall lower into shame and ignominy and your Béatrix is cruelly misjudged, then, my adored child,” she said taking his hand, “to you she will still be first of all; you will see she rises to heaven as she leans on you; but then, if you wish to cast her down do not fail of your blow; after your love, death!”

Calyste clasped her round the waist and pressed her to his heart. As if to confirm her words, Béatrix laid a tender, timid kiss on his brow. Walking back like those who perfectly understand each other—she thinking she had gained a truce, he not doubting his happiness—both were deceived. Calyste, believing from Camille’s information that Conti might be pleased to part from Béatrix, looked to chance to arrange the future; Béatrix yielded to the vagueness of her position.

They reached Les Touches in bliss, entering by the garden gate whose key Calyste had taken. It was nearly six; the evening’s odors and sun harmonized with their feelings. Their steps were in unison, their silence full of mutual completeness. Having said all that could be said and physically tired, they walked on in silence.

Suddenly, at a turn, Béatrix was seized with a horrible trembling, the contagious horror caused by the sight of a snake, which Calyste felt before he saw the cause: on a bench beneath a weeping ash sat Conti, talking with Camille Maupin.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The marquise’s inward convulsive trembling betrayed more than she meant; a tragic scene unfolded in the souls of all present.

“You did not expect me so soon, I fancy,” said Conti, offering his arm to Beatrix.

The marquise, unable to refuse, let Calyste’s arm drop and took Conti’s. That humiliating transit overwhelmed Calyste, who flung himself on the bench beside Camille after exchanging the coldest salutations with his rival. At first he thought to attack Conti and reclaim Beatrix; but the woman’s violent trembling—her visible suffering—stayed him. He was struck dumb, as if by inevitability.

Madame de Rochefide and Conti passed before Calyste. As they passed, the marquise shot Camille one of those terrible glances in which women say everything. She avoided Calyste’s eyes and turned to Conti, who seemed to be jesting.

“What will they say to each other?” Calyste asked Camille.

“You don’t yet know the terrible rights an extinguished love gives a man over a woman,” Camille warned. “Beatrix could not refuse Conti. He’s jesting her about her new love; he must have guessed it from your manner.”

“Jesting her!” cried Calyste, starting up.

“Be calm,” Camille advised. “If he wounds her pride, she will crush him. But Conti is astute; he’ll manage her more cleverly. He’ll pretend to believe in her loyalty, represent you as an ambitious child wanting a marquise in love with him; he’ll insinuate, she’ll be forced to deny, and he’ll become her master again.”

“Ah! he does not love her. I would leave her free. True love must be chosen anew each moment. Why did he not stay away longer? A few more days and he would not have found her. What brought him back?”

“The jest of a journalist,” Camille said. “His opera has failed. Some remark—probably by Claude Vignon—stung him: ‘It is hard to lose fame and mistress at the same time.’ He’s weary of troubles, disgusted with life, regrets his public alliance with a marquise. He spoke to me of past happiness in a poem too clever to be true. He probably hoped to worm out your secret amid my flatteries.”

Calyste watched Beatrix and Conti approaching; he no longer listened to Camille. In talking with Conti, Camille had held the defensive; she revealed nothing of Calyste’s secret or Beatrix’s. She warned Calyste to distrust Conti—the most wily man she had known.

“My dear friend,” she said, “this is your most critical moment. You lack the caution you need; I fear you’ll be tricked.”

The dinner bell rang. Conti offered his arm to Camille; Calyste gave his to Beatrix. Camille stepped back to let the marquise pass, and Beatrix used the moment to press her finger to her lips, signaling Calyste the absolute necessity of discretion.

Conti was extremely gay at dinner; perhaps to probe Madame de Rochefide, who bore her part poorly. Had she been merely coquettish she might have deceived him; but her new love was real and showed. Far from increasing her embarrassment, Conti pretended not to notice. At dessert he steered the talk to women and praised the nobility of their constancy—the idea that many women who abandon a man in prosperity would sacrifice all in misfortune, clinging to their first lover. The praise, meant to wound, fell like incense on one bleeding heart. Camille and Beatrix alone saw the bitterness; they flushed but controlled themselves. After dinner they slipped into the great salon to be alone.

“It is dreadful to let Conti ride over me; yet I can’t defend myself,” Beatrix murmured. “The galley-slave is always a slave to his mate. I am lost; I must go back to my galleys! And it is you, Camille, who have cast me there. You brought him back a day too soon—or a day too late. I recognize your infernal talent as author. Your revenge is complete.”

“I may have said I would write to Conti, but to do it was another matter,” cried Camille. “I am incapable of such baseness. But you are unhappy, and I forgive the suspicion.”

“What will become of Calyste?” asked Beatrix, with naive self-conceit.

“Then Conti carries you off, does he?” Camille asked.

“Ah! you think you triumph!” cried Beatrix, anger and hatred contorting her face. Camille tried to hide satisfaction under false sympathy; the sparkle in her eyes betrayed her. They sat under a strong light on the divan where so many secret tragedies had begun, examining each other for the last time as if forever parted by undying hatred.

“Calyste remains to you,” said Beatrix; “but I am fixed in his heart, and no woman can ever drive me out of it.”

Camille answered with a cutting irony: “You reign, you love, and you depart!”—a sentence that struck the marquise to the heart.

Neither noticed that Conti and Calyste were absent. The composer had remained at table with his rival, begging him to keep him company to finish a bottle of champagne.

“We have something to say,” Conti added to prevent refusal.

Placed where he was, Calyste could not refuse. Once a little drunk, Calyste disclosed himself; Conti, with feigned charm, revealed his intentions. He declared that Beatrix loved him, but he had ceased to love her; he was not there to carry her off but to break off their relations and leave her the honors of the rupture. He advised Calyste to play the part of injured youth; men who get themselves dismissed produce regret and leave the woman feeling superior. He begged Calyste to take Beatrix, to make a pretended pitying show, to be rough and jealous so she would betray herself. He spoke of his new passion—an opera singer, Mademoiselle Falcon—and of marrying her, boasting he had exchanged a marquise for a queen.

Calyste, candid and content, could not hide his love for Beatrix, which was what Conti wanted. No man, however blasé, does not inflame when his love is threatened by rival. Questioned, Calyste related events of the past weeks; Conti received them with charming humor, concealing fury.

“Come, let us go upstairs,” Conti said. “Women are so distrustful; those two will think we cannot be together without tearing each other’s hair out; better to talk where they won’t listen. I’ll seem jealous; it will drive the marquise to betray herself.”

Calyste begged Conti to sing; the composer performed the famous cavatina “Pria che spunti l’aurora” with an extraordinary passion. As Conti sang, he looked intently at Beatrix, giving the words a cruel meaning. Camille, accompanying him, guessed the order conveyed, and Beatrix bowed her head. She looked at Calyste and was sure he had fallen into a trap despite her advice. When Calyste, evidently happy, came to bid her good-night and kissed her hand with confident triumph, the whole deceit was evident.

By the time Calyste reached Guerande, servants were packing Conti’s carriage; “by dawn” he was carrying Beatrix away with Camille’s horses to the first relay. The morning twilight allowed Madame de Rochefide to see Guerande, its towers whitened by the dawn. Melancholy possessed her; she was leaving the sweetest flower of her life—a pure love—sacrificing it to the laws of the world. The woman of the world obeyed those laws as many women sacrifice love to duty or pride. Read thus, her story resembled that of many women who choose notoriety over tranquil happiness.

The next day Calyste went to Les Touches at mid-day. From the window where Beatrix had watched, he saw Camille, who ran down and told him in one word: “Gone!”

“Beatrix?” thundered Calyste.

“You’ve been duped by Conti; you told me nothing, and I could do nothing for you.”

She led him to her little salon; he flung himself on the divan and burst into tears. Felicite smoked her hookah and said nothing—knowing words could not arrest such pain. Calyste sat torpid all day. Before dinner, Camille tried to speak after asking him to listen.

“Friend,” she said, “you caused me the bitterest suffering, and I had not your beautiful young life to heal myself. For me, life has no spring. I drew Beatrix’s portrait before she came; I wanted you to know her as she is, to keep you safe. Now listen to the truth. Madame de Rochefide is unworthy of you. The scandal of her fall was unnecessary; she did it deliberately to play a part in society. She is one of those women who prefer the celebrity of scandal to tranquil happiness; they seek to be talked about at any cost. Beatrix was eaten up with vanity. Her fortune and wit had not given her the feminine royalty she craved; she sought the celebrity of names like the Duchesse de Langeais. But the world gives its interest to real feeling only. Beatrix’s playing was judged second-rate. If she were sincere and tender, would she have run off with Conti this morning?”

Camille’s eloquence failed to change Calyste; he believed Beatrix absolutely. She forced him to dine; he could not swallow. Youth is uniquely vulnerable; later organs harden. After his burst of tears he settled into a calm resignation that alarmed Camille. Before he left, he asked to go into Beatrix’s bedroom, where he had seen her during her illness, and laid his head on the pillow where hers had lain.

“I am committing follies,” he said, grasping Camille’s hand and bidding her good-night in deep dejection.

He returned home among the usual company, sat with his mother, and spent the evening in her presence. The rector, the Chevalier du Halga, and Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel knew of Beatrix’s departure and rejoiced, hoping Calyste would return to simpler ways. No one guessed how fatal a first love might prove to so simple and true a heart.

For days Calyste went regularly to Les Touches—walking the lawn where he had walked with Beatrix, going to Croisic to stand on the fateful rock or lie in the box-bush. These solitary trips, silence, and gravity made his mother anxious. After two weeks of this caged-animal conduct, the poor lover ceased to cross the bay; he barely had strength to travel from Guerande to the spot where he had once seen Beatrix. The family, delighted at the absence of “those Parisians,” saw nothing fatal in the lad. The two old maids and the rector, pursuing their plan, kept Charlotte de Kergarouet—who nightly played coquetries on Calyste—hoping to secure him for marriage. During long evenings Calyste sat between his mother and the little Breton girl, observed as they discussed his mood. They mistook his lethargy for submission.

One evening Calyste, wearied, went to bed; the players dropped their cards and looked at each other, hearing his steps with anxiety.

“Something is the matter with Calyste,” said the baroness, wiping her eyes.

“Nothing is the matter,” replied Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel; “but you should marry him at once.”

“Do you think marriage would divert his mind?” asked the chevalier.

Charlotte arrived with unusual care. After a discourse on matrimony Calyste requested a few days’ grace. The old baron rubbed his hands and whispered the “good news” to the baroness.

Breakfast was gay; Charlotte sparkled. After the meal Calyste took Charlotte to the grotto; their parents watched from the window.

“They are watching us,” Charlotte said.

“They cannot hear us,” he replied.

“True; but they see us.”

“Let us sit down,” he said, taking her hand.

“Is it true your banner used to float from that twisted column?” she asked, thinking of owning the house. “You will make changes, won’t you, Calyste?”

“I shall not have time,” he said, taking her hands and kissing them. “I love someone you have seen and who loves me, and I cannot make any other woman happy; though from childhood you and I have been destined. But I shall wait.”

“And I, too,” Charlotte said, with tears. “You cannot long love a married woman who, they say, has gone off with a singer—”

“Marry, Charlotte,” Calyste interrupted. “With the fortune your aunt intends to give you, you could choose some better man than I. I have brought you here not to tell you what you know, but to entreat you, as a childhood friend, to take this rupture upon yourself and say you have rejected me. Say you do not wish to marry a man whose heart is not free; thus I shall be spared the sense that I have done you public wrong. You do not know how heavy life is now; I cannot bear the slightest struggle; I am like a man whose vital spark is gone. If it were not for the grief I should cause my mother, I would flung myself into the sea. Do not speak of this to anyone. Good-bye, Charlotte.”

He kissed her hair and left by the postern-gate to Les Touches, remaining near Camille till past midnight. Returning home at one, he found his mother waiting with worsted-work. Softly he clasped her hand and asked, “Is Charlotte gone?”

“She goes tomorrow, with her aunt, in despair,” answered the baroness. “Come to Ireland with me, my Calyste.”

“Many a time I have thought of flying there—” he began, then added, “With Beatrix.”

Some days after Charlotte’s departure, Calyste joined the Chevalier du Halga for a daily promenade. They sat with a view toward the Croisic rocks. Calyste was thin and pale; feverish shudders came and his eyes had the fixed brightness of obsession. The chevalier, the only companion who could share a few thoughts, was for Calyste like an apostle of his own religion.

“Have you loved many women?” Calyste asked.

“Only one,” the chevalier replied. “She was not free—she was the wife of my best friend. Ah! how I suffered! She loved me passionately until her death at forty-nine during the emigration at St. Petersburg. I have thought of bringing her here to lay her near me; she lives in my heart.”

The chevalier brushed away tears; Calyste took his hand.

“I care for this little dog more than life,” the old man said, pointing to Thisbe. “She is precisely like the one Madame l’Amirale held on her knees. I never look at Thisbe but I see her hands.”

“Did you see Madame de Rochefide?” Calyste asked.

“No,” replied the chevalier. “It is sixty-eight years since I have looked at any woman with attention—except your mother.”

Three days later the chevalier said, “My child, I have a hundred and forty louis laid by. When you know where Madame de Rochefide is, come and get them and follow her.”

Calyste thanked him, envying the old man’s steadfastness. But day by day he grew morose; he seemed to love no one; all things hurt him; he was gentle only to his mother. The baroness watched his decline with growing anxiety; by prayer and entreaty she made him swallow food. Toward late October he ceased to go to the mall; the chevalier’s appeals were vain.

“We can talk of Madame de Rochefide,” the old man said. “I’ll tell you my first adventure.”

“Your son is ill,” he confided to the baroness when efforts seemed useless.

Calyste answered that he was well; but like all youths in melancholy, he took pleasure in thoughts of death. He no longer left the house but sat in the garden on a bench, warming himself in pale sunshine, alone with his thought.

Soon after Calyste ceased even to go to Les Touches, Felicite asked the rector to come. The assiduity with which Abbé Grimont called at Les Touches became the town’s topic. Masters and servants noted Calyste’s increasing weakness, though none imagined youth might die of love. Even the chevalier had no example of such a death. They attributed Calyste’s thinness to lack of food. His mother implored him to eat. Calyste tried to please her, but nourishment taken against will only increased the slow fever consuming him.

In late October Calyste could no longer mount the stairs; his bed was placed in the lower hall, surrounded by family. They sent for the Guerande physician, who broke the fever with quinine and ordered exercise and amusement. The baron roused himself; with Calyste, Gasselin, and two dogs he set out for the forest, and they hunted for days. Calyste obeyed but had no spirit. Both grew dangerously ill and doctors from Nantes were summoned.

The baron had received a fatal shock on realizing Calyste’s change. With the lucidity of the dying he feared his line might perish. He prayed, his face turned to his son’s bed; at the least motion Calyste made, the old man trembled. The baroness no longer left the room; Zephirine sat knitting in unease and insisted on knowing everything. One night, convinced of doom, Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel told Zephirine she must resign herself to the baron’s death. The old blind woman fell to prayer with a fervor that gave her a new splendor; the others joined in.

At that moment the three doctors arrived. They plied Calyste with questions; they examined the baron and wondered he still lived. The Guerande doctor suggested taking Calyste to Paris for the best physicians, costing over a hundred louis.

“People die of something, but not of love,” Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel said.

“Alas! whatever the cause, Calyste is dying,” said the baroness. “I see all the symptoms of consumption around him.”

“Calyste dying!” cried the baron, opening his eyes and shedding two large tears—perhaps the only tears he had ever shed. He rose, took his son’s hand, and looked at him intently.

“What is it you want, father?” asked Calyste.

“That you should live!” cried the baron.

“I cannot live without Beatrix,” replied Calyste.

The old man sank into a chair.

“Oh! where could we get a hundred louis to bring doctors from Paris? There is still time,” cried the baroness.

“A hundred louis!” cried Zephirine. Without hesitation she thrust her hands into her placket, unfastened her petticoat, and let it fall; coins tumbled as she produced the savings she had sewn away. She had thirty-seven, forty-two—summing to one hundred and four louis. Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel stared; the Chevalier du Halga arrived and declared he had a hundred and forty louis saved for Calyste’s use. Mariotte, seeing wealth, locked the doors.

“Gold will not give him health,” said the baroness, weeping.

“But it can take him to Paris, where he can find her. Come, Calyste.”

“Yes,” cried Calyste, springing up, “I will go.”

“He will live,” said the baron in a shaking voice; “and I can die—send for the rector!”

News of the baron’s approaching end spread; peasants, paludiers, and servants knelt in the courtyard while the rector administered the last sacraments. The whole town felt the likely extinction of the old Breton race as a public calamity.

The solemnity affected Calyste deeply. His filial sorrow temporarily silenced the anguish of his love. During the baron’s last hour he knelt beside him, watching death come. The old man died in his chair before assembled family.

“I die faithful to God and his religion,” he said. “My God! as the reward of my efforts grant that Calyste may live!”

“I shall live, father, and I will obey you,” said the young man.

“If you wish to make my death as happy as Fanny has made my life, swear to me to marry.”

“I promise it, father.”

It was touching to see Calyste, or rather his shadow, leaning on the chevalier’s arm—a spectre leading a shade—follow the baron’s coffin as chief mourner. The church and square were crowded with country people from miles around.

But the baroness and Zephirine soon saw that despite his promise Calyste fell back into fatal stupor. On the day the family donned mourning the baroness took her son to a garden bench and questioned him. Calyste answered gently and submissively, but his replies proved only despair.

“Mother,” he said, “there is no life in me. What I eat does not feed me; the air does not refresh me; the sun feels cold; it seems to you to light that front of the house, but to me it is a blur. If Beatrix were here it would be dazzling. There is but one thing left that keeps its shape to my eyes—this flower,” and he drew from his breast the withered bunch the marquise had given him at Croisic.

The baroness dared not speak further; his words seemed the sign of madness rather than mere despair. She saw no hope.

The baron’s last hours had prevented the rector from bringing Camille to Calyste. But that day, while mother and son sat on the bench, Calyste quivered at seeing Felicite through the windows; she reminded him of Beatrix, and life revived. It was to Camille the poor mother owed this first motion of joy.

“Well, Calyste,” said Mademoiselle des Touches, “I want you to go to Paris with me. We will find Beatrix,” she added in a low voice.

The pale, thin face flushed and a smile brightened him.

“Let us go,” he said.

“We shall save him,” said Camille to the mother, who pressed her hands and wept for joy.

A week after the baron’s funeral, Mademoiselle des Touches, the Baronne du Guenic, and Calyste started for Paris, leaving the household in charge of old Zephirine.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Félicité’s care prepared a prosperous future for Calyste. Allied to the Grandlieu family—the ducal line would end in five daughters—she had written to the Duchesse de Grandlieu describing Calyste, his history, and her intentions. She had lately sold her rue du Mont-Blanc house for two million five hundred thousand francs. Her agent bought a rue de Bourbon house for seven hundred thousand francs; one million she intended to devote to recovering the du Guenic estates, and the remainder she wished to settle upon Sabine de Grandlieu. Félicité knew the Grandlieus’ plans: the youngest was to marry the Vicomte de Grandlieu (the heir); Clotilde-Frédérique, the second daughter, wished to remain unmarried in memory of Lucien de Rubempré without becoming a nun like her eldest sister; two sisters were already married; and Sabine, nearly twenty and the second youngest, was the only available daughter. Félicité resolved that Sabine should cure Calyste of his passion for Béatrix.

During the journey to Paris Mademoiselle des Touches revealed these arrangements to the baroness. The new rue de Bourbon house was being decorated and intended as Sabine and Calyste’s home if the plan succeeded.

The party stayed at the hôtel de Grandlieu, where the baroness was received with distinction due her rank as a du Guenic and the daughter of a British peer. Mademoiselle des Touches urged Calyste to see Paris, investigated Béatrix (who had disappeared abroad), and occupied him with diversions and amusements. The season for balls began, and the duchess and her daughters did the honors. Calyste thought Sabine resembled Madame de Rochefide, one of the handsomest and most charming girls in Parisian society; this fancied likeness made him attend willingly to Sabine’s coquetries. Sabine liked Calyste, and the winter of 1837 passed so that the young baron—his youth and health restored—listened without repugnance to his mother when she reminded him of the promise to his dying father and proposed marriage with Sabine. He outwardly agreed but kept within himself an indifference known only to the baroness, who trusted it would be conquered by a happy home.

On the day the families gathered in the Grandlieu salon to sign the marriage contract, when the notary Léopold Hannequin began preliminaries, Calyste—clouded—suddenly and curtly refused to accept the benefactions offered by Mademoiselle des Touches. Did he still rely on Félicité’s devotion to recover Béatrix? In the embarrassment of the assembled families, Sabine entered and handed him a letter from Mademoiselle des Touches, to be given on that occasion.

Calyste read:

Camille Maupin to Calyste.

Calyste—before I enter my convent cell I am permitted a last look upon the world I leave for a life of prayer and solitude. That look is for you, who have been my whole world these last months. My voice reaches you at the moment of a ceremony in which I cannot share.

On the day you stand before the altar giving your hand to a young and charming girl who can love you openly, I shall be at another altar in a convent at Nantes betrothed forever to Him who neither fails nor betrays. I do not write to sadden you, but to entreat you not to deny the service I wish to do you. Do not contest my rights so dearly bought.

If love is suffering, I have loved you indeed, my Calyste. But have no remorse; the only happiness I have known I owe to you; my pangs sprang from myself. Make me recompense by causing me an everlasting joy. Let poor Camille, who is no longer what she was, still be something in the material comfort you enjoy. Let me be like a fragrance in your life, unseen and not importunate.

To you, Calyste, I shall owe my eternal happiness; will you not accept a few paltry and fleeting benefits from me? Do not be wanting in generosity. Do you not see this as the last message of a renounced love? The world without you held nothing more for me; you made it the most awful of solitudes; you have brought Camille Maupin, the unbeliever and writer, bound before God.

I am now what I might have been—innocent, a child. I have washed my robes in tears of repentance; I can come before the altar to which my guardian angel, my beloved Calyste, has led me. I love you without self-seeking, as a mother loves her son, as the Church loves her children. I can pray for you and yours without wish except your happiness. If you knew the sublime tranquillity in which I live, risen above petty interests and doing our duty, you would enter your new life with unfaltering steps and never a look behind. Society cannot exist without the religion of duty; sacrifice your fancy to your duties as head of your race, husband, father. Raise the fallen standard of the old du Guenics; show what a gentleman is in honor and grandeur. Let me play a mother’s part to you; your own mother will not be jealous of this voice from a tomb, these hands uplifted to heaven imploring blessings. Today, more than ever, rank and nobility need fortune. Calyste, accept a part of mine, and make a worthy use of it. It is not a gift but a trust I place in you. I thought more of your children and the old Breton house than of you in offering you my Parisian profits.

“Let us now sign the contract,” said the young baron, returning to the company.

The Abbé Grimont, to whom Camille’s conversion was attributed, soon became vicar-general of the diocese.

The following week, after a ceremony at seven in the morning at Saint-Thomas d’Aquin, Calyste and Sabine left in their pretty travelling-carriage amid tears, embraces, and congratulations. Congratulations came from the witnesses and men; tears from the Duchesse de Grandlieu and Clotilde, who trembled thinking, “She is launched upon the sea of life! Poor Sabine! at the mercy of a man who does not marry entirely of his own free will.”

Marriage is not wholly pleasures; it involves temperamental compatibility, physical sympathies, and character harmonies. Mothers and marriageable daughters know the lottery’s terms and dangers—that is why women weep at a wedding while men smile; men think they risk nothing, women know, or nearly know, what they risk.

In a preceding carriage rode the Baronne du Guenic, to whom the duchess had said, “You are a mother, though you have only had one son; try to take my place to my dear Sabine.” On the bridal carriage sat a chasseur as courier; two waiting-maids and four postilions, decorated with ribbons and bouquets (which the duke bribed them to relinquish) completed the convoy. The duchess bade them farewell, hinting to Calyste that she understood him. Clotilde watched them depart, tearful, as the convoy clattered off toward Brittany.

It is singular that both artisans and great families set out publicly after marriage: the great hide themselves in carriages; the little people parade roads and woods. A moralist might debate which modesty is finer—the private inauguration of the domestic hearth or the public display. The love which begins a marriage is a jewel to be buried in the depths of the soul.

Who can relate a honeymoon? Much of it belongs to the bride. Many women will recognize that this uncertain period forecasts conjugal life. Young women of the faubourg Saint-Germain, like Sabine, are, if intelligent, well trained in manners and bearing; such upbringing, combined with virgin sincerity, made Sabine as interesting as Mademoiselle de Chaulieu. Her letters to her mother during the honeymoon show her mind and temperament.

Guerande, April, 1838.

To Madame la Duchesse de Grandlieu:

Dear Mamma,—You will understand why I did not write during the journey—our wits are like wheels. Here I am in the depths of Brittany at the hôtel du Guenic—a house carved like a sandal-wood box. Despite the family’s affection, I feel a desire to fly to you and confess things only a mother should know.

Calyste married with great sorrow. We all knew that, and you warned me of difficulties; but they are greater than you thought. Ah, my dear mother, what experience we gain in hours! All your counsels seemed fruitless; I love Calyste as if he were not my husband—that is, if I were married to another and traveling with Calyste, I should love Calyste and hate my husband.

Now imagine a man loved so completely, involuntarily, absolutely; my servitude is established despite your good advice. You told me to be grand, dignified, and self-respecting to win esteem and consideration that sanctify a woman. I remember your warnings about young wives who begin with compliance and familiarity that lead to indifference and contempt. “Remember that you are a Grandlieu!” Yes, I recall all that.

But could you have supposed I should begin with the catastrophe you feared?
