Madame Schontz had too much sense and knew men too well not to conceive great hopes. Rochefide allowed her five hundred francs a month, furnished shabby second-floor rooms in rue Coquenard costing 1,200 francs a year, and set himself to study Aurélie’s character while she, perceiving his intent, gave him a character to study. Rochefide was happy to find in her a woman of noble nature; he imagined her mother a Barnheim of Baden, and Aurélie’s upbringing confirmed the illusion. She spoke English, German, Italian, knew foreign literatures, could hold her own against second-class pianists, and behaved about her talents like a well-bred woman—she never mentioned them. She took up painting half in jest and produced a head that astonished. For amusement during her tenure at Saint-Denis she had advanced in the sciences; later life covered those seeds, yet she let Arthur take credit for their sprouting.

Aurélie began by showing disinterestedness equal to her other charms, which allowed this weak corvette to fix its grapnels on the larger vessel. But by the end of the first year she made ignoble noises in the antechamber with her clogs, arriving when Rochefide awaited her in a draggled gown. By this time she had persuaded her gros papa that her ambition was to obtain honorably a comfortable bourgeois existence; about ten months after their meeting the second phase of happiness declared itself.

She obtained a fine apartment in rue Neuve-Saint-Georges. Arthur, who could no longer conceal his fortune, gave her splendid furniture, a complete service of plate, 1,200 francs a month, a hired low carriage with one horse—and a tiger. Madame Schontz was not the least grateful; she knew the motive of his conduct and recognized the male rat’s calculations. Sick of restaurant life, Rochefide offered Aurélie forty francs a day to cover his dinner and that of a friend; she accepted.

Having cashed in all her moral letters of credit, she was listened to favorably when she asked for 500 francs more a month for dress to avoid shaming her gros papa, whose friends belonged to the Jockey Club.

“It would be a pretty thing,” she said, “if Rastignac, Maxime de Trailles, d’Esgrignon, La Roche-Hugon, Ronqueroles, Laginski, Lenoncourt, found you with a sort of Madame Everard. Besides, have confidence in me, papa, and you’ll be the gainer.”

Aurélie contrived to display new virtues in this second phase. She assumed the housekeeping role for which she claimed credit: she said she balanced two thousand five hundred francs a month without debt—a rare achievement in the faubourg Saint-Germain of the thirteenth arrondissement—and served dinners superior to Nucingen’s, with wines at twelve francs a bottle. Rochefide, amazed and delighted to host friends economically, declared as he caught her round the waist, “She’s a treasure!”

He soon hired a third of an Opera box for her and took her to first representations. He began consulting Aurélie and took credit for her clever sayings, which raised his reputation as an amusing man. He acquired certainty of being truly loved and for himself alone. Aurélie refused a Russian prince offering five thousand francs a month.

“You are a lucky man, my dear marquis,” cried old Prince Galathionne at the club. “Yesterday I tried to get Madame Schontz away from you, but she said: ‘Prince, you are not handsomer, but you are a great deal older than Rochefide; you would beat me, but he is like a father to me; can you give me one-tenth of a reason why I should change? I’ve never had the grand passion for Arthur that I once had for little fools in varnished boots and whose debts I paid; but I love him as a wife loves her husband when she is an honest woman.’ And thereupon she showed me the door.”

This speech, not exaggerated, increased the neglect and degradation in the hôtel de Rochefide. Arthur shifted his whole existence to Madame Schontz and prospered; after three years he had four hundred thousand francs to invest.

The third phase began. Madame Schontz became the tenderest of mothers to Arthur’s son; she fetched him from school, showered him with presents and pocket-money. She took part in managing Arthur’s property: she made him buy into the Funds when low—just before the treaty of London that overturned the ministry of March 1st—and Arthur gained two hundred thousand francs by that transaction; Aurélie did not claim a penny. Rochefide invested six hundred thousand in Bank of France stock and put half in Josephine Schiltz’s name. A little house in rue de La Bruyère was hired and given to Grindot, that great decorator, to make it a bonbon-box.

Henceforth Rochefide ceased managing his affairs. Madame Schontz received the revenues and paid the bills. Practically his wife and woman of business, she justified her position by making her gros papa more comfortable than ever; she had learned his fancies and gratified them as Madame de Pompadour gratified Louis XV. In short, Madame Schontz became absolute mistress. She began to patronize a few young artists and men of letters, newly famous and keen to make reputations with little to accomplish.

Her conduct, a triumph of tactics, revealed her superiority. A dozen young fellows amused Arthur, supplying witty sayings and opinions without questioning the mistress’s fidelity; they proclaimed her eminently intelligent. These living advertisements soon set Madame Schontz up as the most agreeable woman on the borderland between the thirteenth arrondissement and the rest. Her rivals—Suzanne Gaillard, Fanny Beaupré, Mariette, Antonia—spread droll calumnies about the beauty of those young men and Rochefide’s complacent reception of them. Madame Schontz, who could out-blague those ladies by three turns, told them once at Nathan’s supper, after recounting her fortune and success, “Do as much yourselves!”—a remark they remembered.

During this period she made Arthur sell his race-horses, through arguments perhaps derived from Claude Vignon, one of her habitués.

“I can conceive,” she said after lashing the horses with wit, “that princes and rich men should set their hearts on horse-flesh, but only for the good of the country, not for betting. If you had a stud farm and could raise a thousand or twelve hundred horses, and all of France’s horses entered into one great competition, it would be fine; but you buy animals as managers of theatres trade in artists; you degrade an institution to gambling; you make a Bourse of legs, as of stocks. It is unworthy. Don’t you spend sixty thousand francs sometimes merely to read in the newspapers: ‘Lélia, belonging to Monsieur de Rochefide, beat by a length Fleur-de-Genet the property of Monsieur le Duc de Rhetore’? You had much better give that money to poets, who would carry you to immortality in prose and verse.’”

By this prodding Rochefide saw the hollowness of the turf and economized sixty thousand francs; the next year Madame Schontz remarked, “I don’t cost you anything now, Arthur.”

Many rich men envied Rochefide and tried to entice Madame Schontz away, but, like the Russian prince, they wasted their old age.

“Listen to me,” she told Finot, now immensely rich. “I am certain Rochefide would forgive me a little passion if I fell in love, but one doesn’t leave a marquis with a kind heart like that for a parvenu like you. You couldn’t keep me in the position in which Arthur has placed me; he has made me half a wife and a lady, and that’s more than you could do even if you married me.”

This last point clinched the fetters of that happy galley-slave: the speech, of course, reached the ears for which it was intended.

The fourth phase had begun: habit, the final victory in these campaigns, which make the women of this class say of a man, “I hold him!” Rochefide, who had just bought the little hôtel in Mademoiselle Josephine Schiltz’s name (a trifle of eighty thousand francs), had reached, as the Duchesse de Grandlieu planned, the stage of finding vanity in his mistress—whom he now called Ninon II.—by boasting of her scrupulous honesty, excellent manners, education, and wit. He had merged his defects, merits, tastes, and pleasures into Madame Schontz, and was now, from lassitude, indifference, or philosophy, a man unable to change, clinging to wife or mistress.

In five years Madame Schontz had so consolidated her position that introductions to her had to be proposed before being granted. She refused to receive dull rich people and smirched people, admitting only certain great aristocratic names.

Ostensibly she possessed the three hundred thousand francs Rochefide had given her, invested and managed by a broker named Gobenheim—the only broker admitted to her house. Secretly she manipulated another little fortune of two hundred thousand francs, the result of savings over three years and constant movement of the known three hundred thousand—for she never admitted more than that known sum.

“The more you make, the less you get rich,” Gobenheim told her.

“Water is so dear,” she answered.

This secret hoard was increased by jewels and diamonds, which Aurélie wore for a month and then sold. When anyone called her rich, Madame Schontz replied that at the Funds’ interest three hundred thousand francs produced only twelve thousand, and she had spent as much in the hardest days of her life.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Such conduct implied a plan, and Madame Schontz had one. Jealous for two years of Madame du Bruel, she was consumed with the ambition to be married by church and mayor. Every social position has a forbidden fruit—some small thing magnified until it seems the weightiest concern. This ambition involved a second Arthur; none had yet discovered Rochefide’s secret rival. Bixiou fancied Leon de Lora the favored one; the painter suspected Bixiou, who, past forty, ought to be making a fate. Suspicions also fell on Victor de Vernisset, a Canalis-school poet desperately in love; he accused Stidmann, a young sculptor, of rivalry. That artist, charming and ambitious to be another Cellini, worked for jewellers and bronze manufacturers. Claude Vignon, the young Comte de la Palferine, Gobenheim, Vermanton—a cynical philosopher—all frequented the salon and were suspected, then proved innocent. No one fathomed Madame Schontz, certainly not Rochefide, who thought she preferred the young, witty La Palferine; she was virtuous from self-interest and wholly bent on making a good marriage.

Only one man of equivocal reputation was ever seen in Madame Schontz’s salon: Couture, a stock-gambler who had once ruined his brother’s speculations. Couture had been one of Madame Schontz’s earliest friends, and she alone remained faithful to him. The false alarm of 1840 swept away the last vestige of this speculator’s credit; Aurelie, seeing his ill-luck, made Rochefide play in the other direction. Glad to find a place at Aurelie’s table, Couture—who sometimes received a thousand-franc note from Finot, the cleverest or luckiest of parvenus—was shrewd enough to offer his hand and name to Madame Schontz. She studied him to see if the bold speculator had political power and sufficient gratitude not to desert his wife. Couture, about forty-three, half worn-out, did not redeem the unpleasant sonority of his name by birth; he said little of his past.

Madame Schontz was bemoaning the scarcity of eligible men when Couture presented to her a provincial, supplied with the two handles by which women take hold of such pitchers when they wish to keep them. To sketch this person is to paint a portion of the youth of the day.

In 1838, Fabien du Ronceret, son of a chief-justice of the Royal court at Caen (recently deceased), left Alençon, resigning his judgeship—an office his father had compelled him to hold—and came to Paris to make a noise there. A Norman ambition, difficult to realize on an income scarcely scraping eight thousand francs a year; his mother still held a life-interest in a valuable estate in Alençon. In earlier visits he had tried his rope like an acrobat and perceived the great vice of the social replastering of 1830. He meant to turn it to profit, following the example of the longest heads of the bourgeoisie. This requires a glance at one effect of the new order.

Modern equality, unduly developed in our day, has produced in private life—parallel to political life—three great divisions of the social I: pride, conceit, and vanity. Fools wish to pass for wits; wits want to be thought men of talent; men of talent want to be treated as genius. This tendency, which in the Chamber makes the manufacturer jealous of the statesman and the administrator jealous of the writer, leads fools to disparage wits, wits to disparage men of talent, and men of talent to disparage those who outstrip them. By pulling down recognized superiorities, a nation opens the sluice through which a torrent of secondary ambitions rushes, each meaner than the last, and the smallest resolves to lead. Democrats say there was an evil in the aristocracy, but they substitute a dozen armed, contending aristocracies—the worst of all. Proclaiming equality, they promulgate a declaration of the rights of Envy. We inherit the saturnalia of the Revolution transferred to the domains of the mind, industry, and politics; reputations won by toil, service, and talent are treated like privileges taken from the masses. Distinction is sought at any price—by ridicule, by affectation, by little public causes—creating fictitious dignities and a profusion of presidents, vice-presidents, and secretaries for societies whose numbers exceed the questions they pretend to solve. Society on a grand scale has been demolished to make a million little ones in the image of the defunct. These parasitic organizations reveal decomposition: are they not maggots in a dead body? All these societies are daughters of one mother, Vanity. Catholic charity or true beneficence proceed differently; they study and cure wounds rather than perorate in public meetings for the pleasure of perorating.

Fabien du Ronceret, without being superior, had divined—by the greedy common-sense peculiar to a Norman—the gain he could derive from this public vice. Every epoch has a character clever men exploit. Fabien’s mind, though not clever, was wholly bent on making himself talked about.

“My dear fellow, a man must make himself talked about, if he wants to be anything,” he said on parting from the king of Alençon, a certain du Bousquier, a friend of his father. “In six months I shall be better known than you are!”

So Fabien obeyed the spirit of his age. He made his debut in Bohemia, where he was known as “The Heir” because of premeditated prodigalities. Du Ronceret profited by Couture’s follies for the pretty Madame Cadine: during an ephemeral opulence he had arranged a delightful ground-floor apartment with garden in the rue Blanche. The Norman, who wanted luxury ready-made, bought Couture’s furniture and the improvements he was forced to leave—kiosk, rustic gallery, India mattings, potteries—though he called the result his den and never acknowledged the architect Grindot, the carvings of Stidmann, or the paintings of Leon de Lora—Fabien’s crowning vanity lied for the sake of magnifying himself.

He crowned the magnificence with a greenhouse built along a southern wall—not through any love of flowers but to attack public notice through horticulture. He had nearly reached his end. Elected vice-president of a floral society presided over by the Duc de Vissembourg, he wore the Legion of Honor ribbon at an exhibition, delivered an opening speech bought from Lousteau for five hundred francs and boldly pronounced it his own, and made a stir by presenting a flower given him by old Blondet of Alençon as the product of his greenhouse.

But this was nothing. The Heir, who wanted to be accepted as a wit, planned to consort with clever celebrities and reflect their fame—hard to do on eight thousand francs a year. He repeatedly asked Bixiou, Stidmann, and Leon de Lora to present him to Madame Schontz and allow him into her menagerie of lions. Failing these, he applied to Couture, for whose dinners he had so often paid that the late speculator felt obliged to recommend him to Madame Schontz, if only as an elegant unpaid footman whom mistresses employ to run errands when servants are lacking.

In three evenings Madame Schontz read Fabien like a book and said to herself, “If Couture does not suit me, I am certain of saddling that one. My future can go on two legs now.” This queer fellow whom everybody laughed at was really the chosen one—chosen, however, with an intention that made such preference insulting. The improbability of the choice kept suspicion away. Madame Schontz intoxicated Fabien with secret smiles and little scenes on the threshold when she bade him good-night, if Rochefide stayed. She often made Fabien a third with Arthur in her opera-box, excusing it by saying he had done her some service. Men have a natural conceit common as to women—the belief of being loved exclusively. Of all flattering passions none is more prized than that of a Madame Schontz: the man she makes the object of a love she calls “from the heart,” as distinct from another sort. A woman like her, who plays the great lady and whose intrinsic value is real, would be an object of pride to Fabien, who fell in love so that he never presented himself before her eyes except in full dress, varnished boots, lemon-kid gloves, embroidered shirt and frill, variegated waistcoat—in short, with all the external symbols of profound worship.

A month before the duchess’s conference with her confessor, Madame Schontz confided the secret of her birth and real name to Fabien, who did not at all understand the motive. A fortnight later she was surprised at his want of intelligence and exclaimed to herself, “Heavens! how stupid I am! he expects me to love him for himself.” Accordingly she took the Heir in her calèche to the Bois—she now had two little carriages—to discuss her future and declared she wished to marry.

“I have seven hundred thousand francs,” she said, “and if I could find a man full of ambition who understands my character, I would change my position; for do you know the dream of my life? To become a true bourgeoise, enter an honorable family, and make my husband and children truly happy.”

The Norman wished to be “distinguished” by Madame Schontz, but marrying her seemed a folly to a bachelor of thirty-eight whom the revolution of July had made a judge. Seeing his hesitation, Madame Schontz made him the butt of her wit and disdain and turned to Couture. Within a week Couture, whom she put upon the scent of her fortune, had offered his hand, heart, and future—three things of about the same value.

The manoeuvres of Madame Schontz had reached this stage when the Duchesse de Grandlieu began inquiries into the life of the Béatrix of the Place Saint-Georges.

Following the Abbe Brossette’s advice, the duchess asked the Marquis d’Ajuda to bring the celebrated Comte Maxime de Trailles—the archduke of Bohemia, political cut-throat and youngest of young men though fully fifty. D’Ajuda dined with Maxime at the club in the rue de Beuane and proposed afterward to go play dummy whist with the Duc de Grandlieu, who was feigning gout.

Maxime did not deceive himself about the invitation’s meaning; he felt certain the duke or duchess needed him. Club life—men playing cards with others they do not receive at home—is a telling sign of the age.

After several games the duke went to bed, leaving his wife tête-à-tête with Maxime and d’Ajuda. The duchess, seconded by the marquis, revealed her project and asked Maxime’s assistance while ostensibly seeking only his advice. Maxime listened without committing, waiting until she asked point-blank.

“Madame, I fully understand you,” he said, casting that shrewd, penetrating look by which great scamps compromise their interlocutors. “D’Ajuda will tell you that if any one in Paris can conduct that difficult negotiation it is I—of course without mixing you up in it; without it being even known that I have come here this evening. Only, before anything is done, we must settle preliminaries. How much are you willing to sacrifice?”

“All that is necessary.”

“Very well, then, Madame la duchesse. As the price of my efforts you must do me the honor to receive in your house and seriously protect Madame la Comtesse de Trailles.”

“What! are you married?” cried d’Ajuda.

“I shall be married within a fortnight to the heiress of a rich but extremely bourgeois family—a sacrifice to opinion! I imbibe the very spirit of my government and start upon a new career. Consequently, Madame la duchesse will understand how important it is to have my wife adopted by her and her family. I am certain of being made deputy by my father-in-law’s resignation, and I am promised a diplomatic post in keeping with my new fortune. I do not see why my wife should not be as well received as Madame de Portenduere in that society of young women. My wife is pretty, and I will undertake to uncotton-night-cap her. Will this suit you, Madame la duchesse? You are religious, and if you say yes, your promise, which I know to be sacred, will greatly aid my change of life. It will be one more good action to your account. Alas! I have long been the king of mauvais sujets, and I want to make an end of it. After all, we bear, azure, a wyvern or, darting fire... and we have been counts since Catherine de’ Medici.”

“I will receive and protect your wife,” said the duchess solemnly, “and my family will not turn its back upon her; I give you my word.”

“Ah! Madame la duchesse,” cried Maxime, visibly touched, “if Monsieur le duc would also deign to treat me with some kindness, I promise you to make your plan succeed without its costing you very much. But,” he paused, “you must take upon yourself to follow my instructions. This is the last intrigue of my bachelor life; it must be all the better managed because it concerns a good action.”

“Follow your instructions!” the duchess replied. “Then I must appear in all this.”

“Ah! madame, I will not compromise you,” Maxime protested. “I esteem you too much to demand guarantees. I merely mean that you must follow my advice. For example, it will be necessary that du Guenic be taken away by his wife for at least two years; she must show him Switzerland, Italy, Germany—in short, all possible countries.”

“You confirm a fear of my director,” said the duchess naively, recalling the Abbe Brossette’s prudence.

Maxime and d’Ajuda smiled at the idea of an agreement between heaven and hell.

“To prevent Madame de Rochefide from ever seeing Calyste again,” the duchess continued, “we will all travel—Juste and his wife, Calyste, Sabine, and I. I will leave Clotilde with her father—”

“It is too soon to sing victory, madame,” said Maxime. “I foresee enormous difficulties; though I shall no doubt vanquish them. Your esteem and protection are rewards which would make me commit the vilest actions, but these will be—”

“The vilest actions!” cried the duchess, whose piety disclosed selfishness.

“And you would share them, madame, inasmuch as I am only your agent. But are you ignorant of the degree of blindness to which Madame de Rochefide has brought your son-in-law? I know it from Canalis and Nathan, between whom she was hesitating when Calyste threw himself into the lioness’s jaws. Béatrix has contrived to persuade that serious Breton that she has never loved any one but him; that she is virtuous; that Conti was merely a sentimental head-love—in short, a musical love. As for Rochefide, that was duty. So, you understand, she is virgin!—a fact she proves by forgetting her son, whom for more than a year she has not tried to see. The little count will soon be twelve, and he finds in Madame Schontz a mother who is all the more a mother because maternity is, as you know, a passion with women of that sort. Du Guenic would let himself be cut in pieces and would chop up his wife for Béatrix; and you think it easy to drag a man from such credulity! Ah! madame, Shakespeare’s Iago would lose all his handkerchiefs. People mistake theatrical passion for true love. Love, Madame la duchesse, is not loving a noble woman; it is to say to oneself: ‘She whom I love is infamous; she deceives me; she is abandoned; she is hellish,’ yet we rush to her and find there the blue of heaven. That is how Molière loved, and how we, scamps that we are, love. As for me, I weep at the great scene of Arnolphe. Now, that is how your son-in-law loves Béatrix. I shall have trouble separating Rochefide from Madame Schontz; but Madame Schontz will no doubt lend herself to the plot; I shall study her interior. But as for Calyste and Béatrix, they will need blows of an axe, far deeper treachery, and so base an infamy that your virtuous imagination could never descend to it—unless indeed your director gave you a hand. You have asked the impossible; you shall be obeyed. But in spite of my settled intention to war with fire and sword, I cannot absolutely promise you success. I have known lovers who did not recoil before the most awful disillusions. You are too virtuous to know the full power of women who are not virtuous.”

“Do not enter upon those infamous actions until I consult the Abbe Brossette to know how far I may be your accomplice,” cried the duchess, with a naiveté that revealed the selfishness in her piety.

“You shall be ignorant of everything, my dear mother,” interposed d’Ajuda.

On the portico, as the marquis’s carriage drew up, d’Ajuda said to Maxime: “You frightened that good duchess.”

“But she has no idea of the difficulty of what she asks. Let us go to the Jockey Club; Rochefide must invite me to dine with Madame Schontz tomorrow, for to-night my plan will be made, and I shall have chosen the pawns on my chessboard to carry it out. In the days of her splendor Béatrix refused to receive me; I intend to pay off that score, and I will avenge your sister-in-law so cruelly that perhaps she will find herself too well revenged.”

The next day Rochefide told Madame Schontz that Maxime de Trailles was coming to dinner. That meant notifying her to display all her luxury and prepare the choicest food for this connoisseur emeritus, whom all women of Madame Schontz’s type revered. Madame Schontz considered her toilette and her house with the same care she would give to presenting to such a personage.

In Paris there are as many royalties as varieties of art, mental and moral specialties, sciences, and professions; the strongest and most capable of their practitioners has a majesty all his own and is respected by peers who know their art’s difficulties. Maxime was, in the eyes of rats and courtesans, an extremely powerful and capable man who had known how to make himself excessively loved. He was also admired by men who appreciate the difficulty of living in Paris on good terms with creditors; in short, he had no rival in elegance, deportment, and wit except the illustrious de Marsay, who often employed him on political missions. All this explains his interview with the duchess, his prestige with Madame Schontz, and the authority of his words in a conference he planned to hold on the boulevard des Italiens with a young man already known, though newly arrived, in the Bohemia of Paris.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The next day Maxime de Trailles summoned Finot and, having met Couture and Lousteau for breakfast at the Café Anglais, extracted information about Madame Schontz. Finot, who deferred to Maxime, complied; prompted by Finot and inadvertently by Lousteau, Couture revealed what Maxime wanted.

About one o’clock Maxime loitered on Tortoni’s portico with du Tillet, ostensibly occupied but awaiting the Comte de la Palferine. When the prince of Bohemia arrived, Maxime asked whether La Palferine had debts—fifty or a hundred thousand. The count admitted only six thousand; Maxime shrugged that small sums can hamper as much as great ones, praised the young man’s spirit, and offered help: twenty thousand francs and a scheme to make a woman love him within ten days. Flattered though feeling his inferiority, La Palferine accepted. Maxime instructed him to meet that evening at Madame Schontz’s and to report nightly at one a.m. on the boulevard de la Madeleine.

Maxime intended to exploit Madame d’Espard’s dislike of Beatrix. He called at the Hotel de Grandlieu and found the duchess, Madame du Guenic, and Clotilde upset: Calyste had not returned, and Beatrix was distraught. Urging secrecy, Maxime proposed a plot needing twenty thousand francs and perhaps offices—hinting at a receiver-generalship as inducement.

Madame Schontz had invited Claude Vignon, Couture, Fabien du Ronceret (the Heir), Bixiou, Leon de Lora, La Palferine, and Nathan—nine guests of talent or ambition. Anxious to seem youthful, Madame Schontz wore a guipure pelerine and blue velvet, her celebrated head coiffed and opals flashing. Maxime arrived, praised Rochefide’s good fortune through her, and bantered about art and lorettes; Aurélie insisted she saw herself as an artist and as progenitrix of honest men.

Claude Vignon expressed literary opinions and Maxime teased him about Camille Maupin. The guests chatted; privately Maxime handed La Palferine the twenty thousand-franc notes, telling him not to economize and directing Nathan to present him to Madame la Marquise de Rochefide two nights hence. Maxime urged a drunken display and winning charm; La Palferine pledged to report nightly.

Fabien du Ronceret sat at table; Maxime scorned his waiter-like dress but learned he had ties to d’Esgrignon. The dinner was lavish. After the meal Maxime confided to Couture a scheme: if Couture wanted steadiness, Madame de Rochefide could obtain a receiver-generalship for him—Aurélie’s million would secure it—if Couture gave Maxime his vote. Couture pledged allegiance. Maxime then drove La Palferine home.

That night Madame Schontz and Fabien flirted; when Arthur Rochefide surprised them, Aurélie assumed wounded virtue and threatened to marry Fabien, humiliating Arthur. Maxime advised Aurélie how to force the issue—rouse Arthur’s jealousy, make him feel unloved, demand a dramatic parting—promising Fabien a chief-justiceship and the Legion of Honor soon. The scene instigated domestic warfare: Arthur woke to Aurélie’s icy sarcasm; she accused him of scheming to reconcile with Madame de Rochefide. He protested; she threatened to leave him and refused his offers, preferring to cultivate Fabien.

Rochefide, in despair, sought Maxime’s counsel at the club. Maxime suggested Arthur show less obvious devotion and perhaps turn toward another woman, Madame Antonia, to recover self-respect and entice his wife back. Though Arthur wished to remain faithful, Maxime urged a practical plan. Rochefide hurried home but found Aurélie had contrived a compromising scene: Fabien’s hat had been left and the maid’s clumsy removal suggested intimacy. Arthur, devastated, submitted.

Within a week La Palferine transformed: he paid debts, adopted a carriage, joined clubs, and published a successful novelette. Nathan, who promoted him to Madame de Rochefide, told Beatrix that La Palferine adored her. Ten days after Maxime’s arrangement, the count made his debut at Beatrix’s house while Calyste was at a party. Nathan primed her curiosity with artful reticence and lauded La Palferine’s prowess, implying his skill as a lover, which sharpened Beatrix’s interest. The law of contraries worked: courtesans sometimes yearn for virtue, and virtuous women secretly desire libertinism; Nathan exploited this, and Beatrix began to question whether her upbringing had blinded her.

The next day Calyste seemed to Beatrix a loyal but unimaginative gentleman; after the dazzling soirée his plainness failed to satisfy her appetite for brilliance. She feared a meeting between Calyste and La Palferine; Maxime’s maneuver soon forced public humiliation. At the opera, where Beatrix sat in an obscure box, she exited and was recognized on the crowded staircase; whispers and stares left her isolated. La Palferine, offering his arm, escorted her, sat with her in her carriage, and rode home beside her, to Calyste’s stupefaction. In the salon the count conversed brilliantly, ignoring Calyste, who seethed. La Palferine left on a witty note, ensuring the lovers’ quarrel.

Maxime, watching from the Jockey Club, checked on Aurélie’s progress. Madame Schontz, pleased, had managed to eject Arthur: she led Fabien to believe she would reform and marry him, and Fabien, intoxicated and ambitious, embraced the plan. Maxime promised Fabien the offices. Two days later Charles-Edouard told Maxime, “It is done.” Aurélie had turned Arthur out; Maxime consoled the marquis and suggested reconciliation could be bought with three hundred thousand francs and a new match arranged for him.

Meanwhile the duchess learned Calyste planned to leave for Italy; Sabine wanted to follow and even bought pistols. Maxime counseled silence, promising d’Ajuda that Madame de Rochefide would not leave Paris but would soon separate from Calyste. He arranged for his own marriage to be accepted publicly and urged the duchess to travel to Italy soon.

Beatrix, now alternately consumed by passion and disdain, demanded Calyste abandon Sabine to prove his devotion. Entrenched, she thought him unlikely to break with the Grandlieus because Sabine’s fortune mattered. Yet Calyste, driven by despair, obtained a passport and funds from his mother, and nine days after La Palferine’s opening success he resolved to force reconciliation. He arrived at Beatrix’s house intending to oust La Palferine and depart with her. Antoine, the old footman, sought to shield her by telling La Palferine she was out, but when La Palferine appeared Calyste watched him depart in a carriage; Beatrix, frantic, sent La Palferine a long signed letter of devotion—an unprecedented confession.

When La Palferine called next, Beatrix, ensnared and overjoyed, embraced him. He proposed a condition: she must be publicly reconciled with Rochefide, restore her social honor, return to the Hôtel de Rochefide and play the grand lady in public; she must achieve this by political means and persistence. He had already arranged the reconciliation; she had instructed La Palferine to insist on it. Calyste, repeatedly refused by Antoine and by La Palferine’s household, left letters for La Palferine and begged to be heard. Eventually La Palferine and Maxime confronted him and, with cold civility, showed him the six unopened letters he had sent: La Palferine had no wish to cause scandal, and he disclosed that Beatrix had taken him up in her carriage after the opera and had insisted on bringing back Rochefide. Rochefide had been living with Madame Schontz, who had left Paris, and had returned with Aurélie to Nogent-sur-Marne while the Hôtel de Rochefide would be restored for the marquise’s triumphant return. La Palferine advised Calyste to resume his life with Sabine after three months and not to regret Beatrix: she was vain, incomplete both for vice and virtue, and would have abandoned him without remorse.

Calyste left stunned but relieved to have his disillusionments pierced. Three days later the Duchesse de Grandlieu found Calyste content at home with Sabine, planning the layette; Sabine whispered to the duchess, “I am loved, mother, and forever!”

############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############

MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ##########
####################
MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #########
################################################
MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

If the French have as great an aversion to traveling as the English have a propensity for it, both have reasons. Everywhere may be found something better than England; but the charm of France is hard to find elsewhere. Other countries have admirable scenery, sometimes greater comfort, grandeur, and noble manners; but the life of the brain, the talent for conversation, the instinctive understanding that is half the French language, exists nowhere but in Paris. A Frenchman whose raillery meets little comprehension withers abroad. Emigration is contrary to French instincts. Many have confessed a strange pleasure on finding their native customs even at a foreign custom-house.

This preamble recalls the delight Parisians feel when, abroad, they find an oasis of Parisian life: French food that suggests Rue Montorgueil’s pleasures, wines that elsewhere seem mythical, and that critical, unspoken wit which is half the language. In 1836, when the Sardinian Court was at Genoa, two Parisians could imagine themselves still in Paris when they found a palazzo on the last fold of the Apennines, between San Tomaso and the lighthouse, taken by the French Consul-General. It was one of those magnificent Genoese villas once built by patrician millions.

An early night in Genoa after a morning’s torrential rain, the sea and sky clear, silence on the quay and in the groves, the stars and the Mediterranean waves, with the perfumed air and the luxury of sipping a sorbet while a woman sits opposite—such hours are peculiarly of Italy and the Mediterranean.

Around the Consul’s table sat the Marquis di Negro, the Marquis Damaso Pareto, the Consul-General with his beautiful wife and two sleepy children, the French Ambassador and his wife, a secretary who fancied himself crushed and mischievous, and two Parisians come to take leave: the celebrated landscape painter Leon de Lora and the critic Claude Vignon. Predominant among the guests was a celebrated woman, Mademoiselle des Touches, known to literary circles as Camille Maupin, who had been traveling in Italy with Leon de Lora and had come to Genoa again to aid his studies.

Camille had accompanied the painter from Florence and Rome; she was returning to France by the Cornice to Marseilles. She had left Leon to the care of the diplomat and the Genoese marquises, and, though pressed by the Ambassador—an accomplished man of letters—she resisted any exhibition of intimacy. Yet she had consented to a farewell day at the Consul’s villa, sacrificing one of those rare liberated days enjoyed so sparingly by women of the world.

Etiquette had been set aside; curiosity about whether Camille’s manly talent impaired her grace, or whether “trousers showed beneath her petticoats,” had drawn many. After dinner till the collation at nine, conversation had been merry and grave, lightened by Leon de Lora’s sallies—he being considered one of Paris’s roguish wits—but literature had scarcely been touched. Eventually, as is natural among French talkers, the conversation turned to the perennial subject of woman’s sin: when a woman sins, who is to blame?

The three women present—the Ambassadress, the Consul’s wife Onorina Pedrotti, and Camille—were unsparing towards the fallen woman. The men argued that a woman might retain some virtues after a fall. Leon teased, and the Consul-General, rising from the table to speak to his wife about sending for a black pocket-book, announced he would tell a story in which he had a part. “Begin by dissecting a corpse,” he said; everyone leaned forward to hear.

He began: at twenty-two, having taken his law degree, his old uncle, the Abbe Loraux, then seventy-two and Cure of the White Friars, determined to provide him with a protector and career. The Abbe, who had raised him after his mother’s death, said he could not leave him a fortune, but he could present him to “Comte Octave,” a statesman recently made a minister by the Dauphiness and a man of learning and quiet life. The Abbe promised that Comte Octave would offer the young man a salary and rooms, and recommended that the Abbe be invited to dine every Monday. Thus was the narrator, Maurice de l’Hostal, introduced to the Count.

Maurice described himself as innocent and pious, groomed at the College Saint-Louis and Saint-Sulpice, weaned almost from literature by college discipline but hungry for it. He had led a modest life on his uncle’s hundred francs a month and been shy of famous people. To enter Comte Octave’s service, to have a hundred louis a year, was independence; he also sought a protectress who might save him from youthful folly.

Comte Octave’s house was vast, like a state mansion—large halls, marble staircases, colonnades, a neglected grandeur that made the approach solemn. His study opened onto an immense garden. The Count, in a gray flannel morning coat, with a harsh, hollow, refined face, small bright bluish eyes, a large mouth capable of irony and kindness, a forehead intimidating in its expanse, and hair silvered and neatly dressed, struck the young man as both forbidding and compelling. Maurice detected in him what he would later call subterranean caverns of sorrow and suppressed passion beneath a mask of benign politeness and stoical resignation.

The Count, who slept little and rose early, was pious without ostentation and devoted to public business. He had an air of melancholy that seemed not from despondency but from a deep and ineffaceable sorrow. He was industrious and selfless, devoted to public duties and religion. Maurice perceived in him not merely an eccentric man but one carrying secret wounds. Although outwardly austere and reserved, the Count’s private moments revealed him as a sufferer—sometimes he would break into tears, sometimes be unexpectedly tender, sometimes cold and surly; his behavior varied and seemed to shelter some hidden catastrophe.

The Count’s house and habits bore evidence of neglect: cobblestones hidden by grass, burnished surfaces fallen to decay, and servants whose carelessness made the palace shabby. Maurice set about improving matters—replacing worthless servants, bringing in better cooks and a competent staff—so that the house regained dignity and comfort. He thereby glimpsed the Count’s neglect of his own fortunes: from an income of about 160,000 francs he carelessly spent much on extravagant household retainers and charity. Maurice’s reforms saved large sums and convinced him of his usefulness.

Their relationship deepened. The Count made Maurice his companion in work and society, sending him in his place to many functions. Maurice’s appreciation grew into affection; he became a sonlike figure. He learned the Count’s methods, drafted reports which the Count corrected, and felt rewarded when the Count praised him and, once, kissed his forehead, saying Maurice was no longer an apprentice and might, if life did not change, become like a son.

Maurice noted oddities: the Count sometimes left hurriedly; sometimes he spoke fervently to an old woman in a street, making Maurice suspicious; sometimes he spent long hours staring at goldfish in a marble basin, or walking the garden, rubbing his hands as if to wear away the skin. These behaviors hinted at an inner drama—some passion or sorrow that his public devotion, his piety, and his work tried to keep at bay.

The Count’s sadness was profound but not bankrupting; it was a severe chastisement of the soul. He was a man of spirit, able to pity and admire, to keep silence while suffering terribly. Maurice had to ask: What catastrophe could have blasted this learned, pious, and upright statesman? Had he loved wrongly? Was there a scandalous liaison? The Count’s habits of secretiveness and sudden absences suggested more than mere eccentricity.

Maurice, with the curiosity of the devoted, observed the Count closely and endeavored to help where he could. He reorganized the household, curtailed waste, and made the Count’s home livable, thereby earning his trust and gratitude. He described, with affection and awe, the complexity of the Count’s character: a public man of duty and simplicity, a private man consumed by pain and longing, a man whose religion blended with personal torment. The Count’s interior drama fascinated and haunted Maurice, and he watched for signs, trying to discern the cause of a misery so ill-suited to a life of conscience and service.

The Consul-General broke off his narrative at this point, the story hanging on the revelation of the Count’s secret sufferings.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“On a certain Monday,” he resumed, “M. le Président de Grandville and M. de Serizy (then Vice‑President of the Council of State) met at Comte Octave’s house. They formed a committee of three; I was the secretary. The Count had already secured my appointment as Auditor to the Council of State. All documents for their inquiry into the political matter privately submitted to them lay on a long table in the library. They had trusted the Count to examine the papers preliminarily. To avoid carrying all the papers to M. de Serizy as president, they decided to meet first in the Rue Payenne. The Cabinet at the Tuileries attached great importance to this work; its chief burden fell on me—and to it I owed my appointment that year as Master of Appeals.

“Though the Comtes de Grandville and de Serizy, whose habits resembled my patron’s, never dined away, we were still discussing the matter late when the servant told me, ‘MM. the Cures of Saint‑Paul and of the White Friars have been waiting in the drawing‑room for two hours.’

It was nine o’clock.

“‘Well, gentlemen, you find yourselves compelled to dine with priests,’ said Comte Octave. ‘I do not know whether Grandville can overcome his horror of a priest’s gown—’

“‘It depends on the priest.’

“‘One of them is my uncle, and the other is the Abbé Gaudron,’ said I. ‘Do not be alarmed; the Abbé Fontanon is no longer second priest at Saint‑Paul—’

“‘Well, let us dine,’ replied the Président de Grandville. ‘A bigot frightens me, but there is no one so cheerful as a truly pious man.’

We went into the drawing‑room. The dinner was delightful. Men of real information—politicians whose business gives consummate experience and practice of speech—are admirable storytellers. With them there is no medium: they are either heavy or sublime. A statesman's fun, faceted like a diamond, is sharp, sparkling, and full of sense. Confident the proprieties would be observed, my uncle let his wit play—refined, gentle, penetrating, elegant, like men used to concealing thoughts under a black robe. There was nothing vulgar nor idle in this light talk, which I compare, for its effect, to Rossini’s music.

The Abbé Gaudron was, as M. de Grandville said, more Saint Peter than Saint Paul: a peasant full of faith, square on his feet and tall; his ignorance of the world and literature enlivened conversation with guileless amazement and unexpected questions. They began discussing a plague‑spot of social life—adultery. My uncle noted the contradiction the legislators of the Code, still shaken by the Revolution, had set between civil and religious law, which he said was at the root of the mischief.

“‘In the eyes of the Church,’ said he, ‘adultery is a crime; in those of your tribunals it is a misdemeanor. Adultery drives to the police court in a carriage instead of standing at the bar to be tried. Napoleon’s Council of State, too tender toward erring women, was inefficient. Should they not have harmonized civil and religious law, and sent the guilty wife to a convent, as of old?’

“‘To a convent!’ said M. de Serizy. ‘They must first have created convents, and in those days monasteries were being turned into barracks. Besides, think of what you say, M. l’Abbé—give to God what society would have none of?’

“‘Oh!’ said the Comte de Grandville, ‘you do not know France. They were obliged to leave the husband free to take proceedings: well, there are not ten cases of adultery brought up in a year.’

“‘M. l’Abbé preaches for his own saint, for it was Jesus Christ who invented adultery,’ said Comte Octave. ‘In the East, the cradle of the human race, woman was merely a luxury, regarded as a chattel; no virtues were demanded of her but obedience and beauty. By exalting the soul above the body, the modern family in Europe—a daughter of Christ—invented indissoluble marriage, and made it a sacrament.’

“‘Ah! the Church saw the difficulties,’ exclaimed M. de Grandville.

“‘This institution has given rise to a new world,’ the Count went on with a smile. ‘But the practices of that world will never be those of a climate where women are marriageable at seven years of age, and more than old at five‑and‑twenty. The Catholic Church overlooked the needs of half the globe.—So let us discuss Europe only.

“‘Is woman our superior or our inferior? That is the real question so far as we are concerned. If woman is our inferior, by placing her on so high a level as the Church does, fearful punishments for adultery were needful. And formerly that was what was done. The cloister or death sums up early legislation. But since then practice has modified the law, as is always the case. The throne became a hotbed for adultery, and its increase marks the decline of Catholic dogma. Today, where the Church demands only sincere repentance, society marks rather than executes. The law still condemns but no longer terrifies. Thus two moral standards exist: the world and the Code. Where the Code is weak, as I admit with our dear Abbé, the world is audacious and satirical; few judges would not have committed the faults they thunder against. The world, which contradicts law in its rejoicings, habits, and pleasures, is severer than Code or Church: it punishes a blunder after encouraging hypocrisy. The whole marriage law needs reconstruction. French law would be perfect if it excluded daughters from inheriting.’

“‘We three among us know the question very thoroughly,’ said the Comte de Grandville with a laugh. ‘I have a wife I cannot live with. Serizy has a wife who will not live with him. As for you, Octave, yours ran away from you. So we three represent every case of the conjugal conscience, and, no doubt, if ever divorce is brought in again, we shall form the committee.’

Octave’s fork dropped on his glass, breaking it and his plate. He turned as pale as death and flashed a thunderous glare at M. de Grandville, hinting at my presence, which I caught.

“‘Forgive me, my dear fellow. I did not see Maurice,’ the Président went on. ‘Serizy and I, after being the witnesses to your marriage, became your accomplices; I did not think I was committing an indiscretion in the presence of these two venerable priests.’

M. de Serizy changed the subject, listing all he had done to please his wife without success. He concluded that human sympathies cannot be regulated; social law is best when nearest natural law. Nature disregards affinities of souls; her aim is propagation. Hence the Code was wise to leave latitude to chance. Preventing daughters from inheriting while male heirs existed was an excellent provision—whether to hinder degeneration, or to make households happier by abolishing scandalous unions and favoring moral qualities and beauty.

“‘But then,’ he exclaimed, lifting his hand with a gesture of disgust, ‘how are we to perfect legislation in a country which insists on bringing together seven or eight hundred legislators!—After all, if I am sacrificed,’ he added, ‘I have a child to succeed me.’

“‘Setting aside all the religious question,’ my uncle said, ‘I would remark to your Excellency that Nature only owes us life, and that it is society that owes us happiness. Are you a father?’ my uncle asked.

“‘And I—have I any children?’ said Comte Octave in a hollow voice, and his tone made such an impression that there was no more talk of wives or marriage.

After coffee, the two Counts and two priests slipped away, seeing poor Octave had fallen into melancholy and did not notice. My patron sat in an armchair by the fire, like a man crushed.

“‘You now know the secret of my life,’ he said when we were alone. ‘After three years of marriage, one evening I found a letter in which the Countess announced her flight. The letter had dignity; women preserve virtues even when committing that sin. The story now is that she went abroad on a ship that wrecked; she is supposed dead. I have lived alone seven years. Enough for this evening, Maurice. We will speak of my situation when I have grown used to speaking of it. Chronic disease needs time to accustom to improvement—which often seems another aspect of the complaint.’

I went to bed agitated; the mystery seemed more obscure. I foresaw some strange drama, for there could be no vulgar difference between the woman the Count could select and his character. The events driving the Countess from so noble, amiable, perfect, loving a man must have been singular. M. de Grandville’s remark was like a torch thrown into caverns I had long walked; though the flame dimly lit them, I perceived their extent. I could imagine the Count’s sufferings without knowing their depths. His sallow face, parched temples, overwhelming studies, moments of absentmindedness, the smallest details of this married bachelor’s life stood out in luminous relief during the hour of mental questioning—the twilight before sleep—into which I gave myself.

Oh, how I loved my poor master! He seemed sublime. I read a melancholy poem; I saw perpetual activity in a heart I had accused of torpor. Must not supreme grief at last stagnate? Had this judge, with so much power, ever revenged himself? Was he feeding on her long agony? Is it not remarkable in Paris to keep anger seething for ten years? What had Octave done since this misfortune—the separation of husband and wife is a great misfortune in our day, when domestic life has become a social question unlike before?

We allowed days to pass on watch, for great sorrows have a diffidence of their own; but at last, one evening the Count said in a grave voice:

“‘Stay.’
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“This, as nearly as may be, is his story.

“My father had a ward, rich and lovely, who was sixteen when I came back from college to live in this old house. Honorine, brought up by my mother, was just awakening to life. Full of grace and childish ways, she dreamed of happiness as one dreams of jewels; perhaps happiness was to her the jewel of the soul. Her piety had a puerile side; religion itself was poetry to her ingenuous heart. She looked to the future as a perpetual fête. Innocent and pure, no delirium had disturbed her dream. Shame and grief had never tinged her cheek. She did not even inquire into the cause of her involuntary emotions on a fine spring day. She felt weak and destined to obedience, and awaited marriage without wishing for it. Her smiling imagination knew nothing of the corruption which literature depicts; she was ignorant of the world and its dangers. The dear child had suffered so little that she had not developed courage. Her guilelessness would have led her to walk among serpents like the ideal Innocence painters invent. We lived together like two brothers.

“After a year, by the basin in the garden, throwing crumbs to the fish, I asked her, ‘Would you like us to be married? With me you could do whatever you please, while another man would make you unhappy.’

“‘Mamma,’ she said to my mother, ‘Octave and I have agreed to be married----’

“‘What! at seventeen?’ my mother protested. ‘No, you must wait eighteen months; if eighteen months hence you like each other, well, your birth and fortunes are equal; you can have a suitable marriage and a love match.’

“When I was six-and-twenty and Honorine nineteen, we were married. Respect for my parents—old folks of the Bourbon Court—kept us from making the house fashionable; we lived as children. I went into society; I initiated my wife into fashion; I regarded it as my duty to instruct her.

“I recognized afterwards that marriages under such conditions carry a rock against which many affections are wrecked. The husband becomes a pedagogue and love perishes beneath the rod that sooner or later gives pain; a young, handsome wife, discreet and laughter-loving, will not accept any superiority beyond her natural grace. Perhaps I was wrong. During the difficult beginnings of a household I perhaps assumed a magisterial tone; or I trusted too entirely to that artless nature. I kept no watch over the Countess, in whom revolt seemed impossible. Alas! neither in politics nor domestic life has it been settled whether empires and happiness are wrecked by too much confidence or too much severity. Perhaps I failed to perceive Honorine’s girlish dreams? Who can tell while happy days last what precepts he has neglected?

“I remember only the broad outlines of his self-reproaches, made in the good faith of an anatomist seeking a cause missed by his brethren; his indulgence struck me as worthy of Jesus rescuing the woman taken in adultery.

“It was eighteen months after my father’s death—my mother followed in a few months—when the fearful night came which surprised me with Honorine’s farewell letter. What poetic delusion had seduced my wife? Was it sensual? Misfortune’s magnetism? Genius? I could not know. The blow was so terrible that for a month I remained stunned. Afterwards reflection counselled me to continue in ignorance; Honorine’s misfortunes have since taught me too much. So far, Maurice, the story is commonplace enough; but one word will change it all: I love Honorine; I have never ceased to worship her. From the day she left me I have lived on memory; one by one I recall the pleasures for which Honorine no doubt had no taste.

“Oh!” he cried, seeing my amazement, “do not make a hero of me; do not think me such a fool as the Colonel would say, as to have sought no diversion. Alas, my boy! I was either too young or too much in love; I have not in the whole world met another woman. After frightful struggles I tried to forget; money in hand, I stood on the threshold of infidelity, but the memory of Honorine rose before me like a white statue. I recalled the infinite delicacy of that exquisite skin, the ingenuous face, the heavenly fragrance, the light in her eyes, the prettiness of her movements, and I fled as one would from violating a tomb. At consultations, in Court, by night, I dream incessantly of Honorine; only by excessive strength of mind do I attend to business. This is the secret of my labors.

“I felt no more anger with her than a father feels seeing his child in danger. I understood I had made a poem of my wife, a poem I delighted in so intoxicatedly that I fancied she shared the intoxication. Ah! Maurice, indiscriminating passion in a husband is a mistake that may lead to any crime in a wife. I had left all the faculties of this child, loved as a child, unemployed; I had perhaps wearied her with my love before the hour of loving had struck for her. Too young to see that in the constancy of the wife lies the germ of the mother’s devotion, she mistook this first test of marriage for life itself, and the refractory child cursed life, unknown to me, out of modesty perhaps. In that cruel position she was defenseless against any man who stirred her deeply. I, so wise a judge, who have a kind heart but whose mind was absorbed, understood too late these unwritten laws of the woman’s code: I read them by the light of the fire that wrecked my roof. Then I constituted my heart a tribunal; by the law the husband is judge: I acquitted my wife and condemned myself. Love seized me as a mean, despotic passion which comes over some old men. To this day I love the absent Honorine as a man of sixty loves a woman he must possess, and yet I have the strength of a young man. I have the insolence of the old man and the reserve of a boy. Society laughs at such a desperate conjugal predicament. Where it pities a lover, it ridicules a husband who cannot keep his wife under the canopy of the Church and the Maire’s scarf. I had to keep silence.

“Serizy is happy. His indulgence allows him to see his wife; he can protect and defend her; as he adores her, he knows all the joys of a benefactor. ‘I remain married only for my wife’s sake,’ he told me.

“But I—I have nothing; I have not even to face ridicule, I who live solely on a love which is starving! I who can never find a word to say to a woman of the world! I who loathe prostitution! I who am faithful under a spell! But for my religious faith, I should have killed myself. I have thrown myself into hard work as into a gulf, and come out alive, fevered, sleepless!—I cannot repeat all his words; passion gave him an eloquence above the pleader’s, and I, like him, felt my cheeks wet with tears. After a pause, he finished with this revelation:

“This is the drama of my soul, but it is not the living drama acted in Paris. The interior drama interests nobody. I know it; one day you will admit it. No one can burden his heart with another’s pain; the measure of suffering is in ourselves. You can imagine me calming despair by contemplating a miniature in which I see and kiss her brow and lips, breathe the whiteness of her skin; or leaping with hope and then writhing under despair, trampling Paris to quell irritation by fatigue. My life is a continual paroxysm of fear, joy, and dejection.

“As to the drama—it is this. You think I am occupied with Council of State, Chamber, Courts, politics. Seven hours at night suffice for all that; my faculties are over-wrought. Honorine is my real concern. To recover my wife is my only study: to guard her in her cage without her suspecting she is in my power; to satisfy her needs, supply the little pleasures she allows herself, to be about her like a sylph without letting her know, for if she knew the future would be lost—that is my life. For seven years I have never gone to bed without seeing the light of her night-lamp, or her shadow on the curtains.

“She left my house taking nothing but the dress she wore that day. The child carried magnanimity to folly. Eighteen months after her flight her lover deserted her, appalled by the cold, cruel aspect of poverty—the coward! The man doubtless had counted on the easy life abroad. Honorine had sixty thousand francs a year of her own. The wretch left the dear creature expecting an infant and without a penny. In November 1820 I found means to get the best accoucheur to pose as a humble suburban apothecary. I induced the parish priest to supply her needs as charity. To hide my wife, secure her against discovery, find a devoted housekeeper to be my confidante—this was a task worthy of Figaro! You may suppose that to find where my wife had taken refuge I only had to make up my mind.

“After three months of desperation rather than despair, I resolved to devote myself to Honorine with God only in my secret. Love must have its daily food. Ought I not to protect this child, whose guilt flowed from my imprudence, against fresh disaster—to fulfil my part as guardian angel? At seven months the infant died, happily for her and me. For nine months more my wife lay between life and death, deserted when she most needed a manly arm; but this arm,” he said, holding out his, “was extended over her head. Honorine was nursed as she would have been at home. When, on recovery, she asked by whom she had been assisted, she was told ‘By the Sisters of Charity, by the Maternity Society, by the parish priest.’

“This woman, whose pride is almost a vice, has shown resistance in misfortune, which on some evenings I call obstinacy. Honorine was bent on earning her living. For five years I have lodged her in the Rue Saint-Maur in a charming little house where she makes artificial flowers and articles of fashion. She believes she sells them to a shop, paid so well that she makes twenty francs a day; in six years she suspected nothing. She pays about a third of true cost, so on six thousand francs a year she lives as if she had fifteen thousand. She is devoted to flowers, and pays a gardener a hundred crowns, who costs me twelve hundred in wages, and sends me a bill for two thousand francs every three months. I have promised him a market-garden on land I hold in the name of a clerk. The smallest indiscretion would ruin him. Honorine has a little house, a garden, and a splendid hothouse for five hundred francs a year. There she lives under the name of her housekeeper, Madame Gobain, an old woman of discretion I found, whose affection Honorine has won. But their zeal is kept hot by the promise of reward. The porter and his wife cost me dreadfully dear for the same reasons. However, for three years Honorine has been happy, believing she owes her comfort to her own toil.

“Yes,” he cried, reading the question in my eyes, “I have made the attempt. My wife had been in the Faubourg Saint-Antoine. One day, when I thought of reconciliation, I wrote a letter, re-written twenty times! I went from the Rue Payenne to the Rue de Reuilly like a condemned man. It was dark and foggy; I met Madame Gobain who was to tell me of my wife. Honorine, on recognizing my writing, had thrown the letter into the fire without reading it. What a dagger-stroke to a man who delights in the trickery by which he gets fine Lyons velvet cheaply, a pheasant, a fish, fruit, for a tenth of their value, for a woman ignorant enough to think two hundred and fifty francs ample wages for a cook fit for a bishop.

“You have sometimes found me rubbing my hands in a kind of happiness. Well, I had just succeeded in some ruse worthy of the stage—I had sent her an Indian shawl by a purchaser as the property of an actress who had hardly worn it. My life can be summed up in two words: I love and I wait! I have in Madame Gobain a faithful spy on the heart I worship. I go every evening to chat with the old woman to hear all Honorine has done; a single exclamation might betray the secrets of that soul which is wilfully deaf and dumb. Honorine is pious; she attends church and prays, but she has never been to confession or taken Communion; she foresees what a priest would tell her. She will not listen to advice or an injunction to return to me. This horror of me overwhelms me; I have never done her the smallest harm. I have been kind. Granting I may have been hasty in teaching her, that my man’s irony may have hurt her girlish pride, is that reason for persisting in a determination only implacable hatred could inspire? Honorine has never told Madame Gobain who she is; she keeps absolute silence as to her marriage, so the worthy woman can never speak a word in my favor. The others know nothing; they live in awe of the Prefect of Police and their respect for a Minister’s power. Hence it is impossible for me to penetrate that heart; the citadel is mine, but I cannot get in. An act of violence would ruin me for ever.

“How can I argue against reasons I do not know? Should I write a letter and have it copied and laid before Honorine? That would risk a third removal. The last cost me fifty thousand francs. The purchase was made in the name of the secretary whom you succeeded. The unhappy man, who did not know how lightly I sleep, was detected by me opening a box with the private agreement; I coughed, and he panicked; next day I compelled him to sell the house to the man in whose name it now stands, and I turned him out.

“If it were not that I feel my noblest faculties satisfied—if my part were not that of divine fatherhood; if I did not drink in delight by every pore—there are moments when I should believe I was a monomaniac. Sometimes at night I hear the jingling bells of madness. I dread the violent transitions from faint hope to complete despair. A few days since I was considering the horrible end of Lovelace and Clarissa Harlowe, and saying to myself, if Honorine were the mother of a child of mine, must she not necessarily return under her husband’s roof?

“I have such faith in a happy future that ten months ago I bought and paid for one of the handsomest houses in the Faubourg Saint-Honoré. If I win her back I will not allow her to see that house again nor the room from which she fled. I mean to place my idol in a new temple. That house is being made a marvel of taste. I have been told of a poet who, mad with love for an actress, bought the handsomest bed without knowing how she would reward him. Well, one of the coldest of lawyers was stirred to the depths by that anecdote. The orator of the Legislative Chamber can understand the poet who feeds his ideal on material possibilities. I love as a poet—as an emperor!’

“As I heard the last words, I believed Count Octave’s fears were realized; he rose and walked, gesticulating, then stopped as if shocked by his own vehemence. ‘I am very ridiculous,’ he added after a long pause, looking at me as if craving pity.

“‘No, monsieur, you are very unhappy.’

“‘Ah yes!’ he went on. ‘From my speech you must believe in the intensity of a physical passion which for nine years has absorbed me; but that is nothing compared with the worship I feel for the soul, mind, and heart in that woman. By retrospection I now see the graces of Honorine’s mind and heart, which I paid little heed to while happy. Day by day I appreciate the extent of my loss, discovering the exquisite gifts of that capricious and refractory young creature who has grown so strong and proud under poverty and the shock of cowardly desertion. And that heavenly blossom is fading in solitude and hiding! The law we spoke of,’ he went on with bitter irony, ‘is a squad of gendarmes—my wife seized and dragged away by force! Would that be to triumph over a corpse? Religion has no hold on her; she craves its poetry and prays, but she does not listen to the Church’s commandments. I have exhausted mercy, kindness, love; I am at my wits’ end. Only one chance of victory is left—the cunning and patience with which bird-catchers at last entrap the wariest birds. Hence, Maurice, when M. de Grandville’s indiscretion betrayed to you the secret of my life, I regarded that incident as a decree of fate. Have you enough affection for me to show me romantic devotion?’

“‘I see what you are coming to, Monsieur le Comte,’ said I; ‘I guess your purpose. Your first secretary tried to open your deed box. I know the heart of your second—he might fall in love with your wife. Can you devote him to destruction by sending him into the fire? Can any one put his hand into a brazier without burning it?’

“‘You are a foolish boy,’ replied the Count. ‘I will send you well gloved. It is no secretary of mine that will be lodged in the Rue Saint-Maur in the little garden-house I have at his disposal. It is my distant cousin, Baron de l’Hostal, a lawyer high in office...’

“After a moment the gate bell rang, and a carriage came in. The footman announced Madame de Courteville and her daughter. The Count’s cousin, the widow of a judge, had a daughter and no fortune. What could a woman of nine-and-twenty be in comparison with a young girl of twenty as lovely as an ideal mistress?

“‘Baron, Master of Appeals till you get something better, and this old house settled on her—would you not have enough reasons for not falling in love with the Countess?’ he whispered, taking my hand and introducing me to Madame de Courteville and her daughter.

“I was dazzled, not so much by these advantages as by Amélie de Courteville’s beauty and her costuming designed to secure a marriage.

“But I will not talk of myself,” the Count resumed.

“Three weeks later I went to live in the gardener’s cottage, cleaned, repaired, and furnished with the celerity explained by three words: Paris; French workmen; money! I was as much in love as the Count could desire as a security. Would prudence in a young man of five-and-twenty be equal to the part I undertook, involving a friend’s happiness? I confess I counted on my uncle’s advice, for I had been authorized by the Count to take him into confidence if I deemed it necessary. I engaged a garden; I devoted myself to horticulture; I worked frantically, turning up the soil, appropriating the ground to flowers. Like maniacs of Holland or England, I gave out I was devoted to one flower and especially grew dahlias. You will understand that my conduct, even in small details, was laid down by the Count, whose intellect was directed to the trifling incidents of the tragi-comedy in Rue Saint-Maur. As soon as the Countess had gone to bed, at about eleven, Octave, Madame Gobain, and I sat in council. I heard the old woman’s report: everything the Countess had done. He inquired into her meals, occupations, frame of mind, the flowers she aimed to imitate. I understood what love in despair may be when it is the threefold passion of heart, mind, and senses. Octave lived only for that hour.

“During two months, while my garden work lasted, I never set eyes on the little house where my fair neighbor dwelt. I had not inquired whether I had a neighbor, though the Countess’ garden was divided from mine by a paling, along which she had planted cypress trees already four feet high. One morning Madame Gobain announced as disastrous news the intention, expressed by an eccentric neighbor, to build a wall between the gardens at the end of the year. I will say nothing of the curiosity which consumed me to see the Countess! The wish almost extinguished my budding love for Amélie. A wall would cut away three-quarters of the hatchet-shaped plot and leave Honorine’s garden a narrow alley.

“The Countess was in despair.

“‘My good Gobain,’ she said, ‘what sort of man is this florist?’

“‘I do not know whether it is possible to tame him. He seems to have a horror of women. He is the nephew of a Paris curé. I have seen the uncle once; a fine old man of sixty, very ugly but amiable. It is possible the priest encourages his nephew’s love of flowers, as they say in the neighborhood, that nothing worse may happen----’

“‘Well?’ asked the Countess.

“‘He is a little cracked,’ said Gobain, tapping her head.

“A harmless lunatic is the only man no woman distrusts in matters of sentiment. You will see how wise the Count had been in choosing this disguise for me.

“‘What ails him then?’ asked the Countess.

“‘He has studied too hard,’ replied Gobain; ‘he has turned misanthropic. If you want to know all that is said about him----’

“‘Madmen frighten me less than sane folks; I will speak to him myself! Tell him I beg him to come here. If I do not succeed, I will send for the curé.’

“The day after, as I walked my graveled path, I caught sight of the half-open curtains on the Countess’ first-floor window and a woman’s face peeping out. Gobain called me. I glanced at the Countess’ house and, by a rude shrug, expressed, ‘What do I care for your mistress?’

“‘Madame,’ said Gobain, ‘the madman bid me leave him in peace, saying that even a charcoal seller is master in his own premises, especially when he has no wife.’

“‘He is right,’ said the Countess.

“‘Yes, but he ended by saying, “I will go,” when I told him he would distress a lady living in retirement who found her solace in growing flowers.’

“The next day a signal from Gobain informed me that I was expected. After the Countess’ breakfast, when she walked before her house, I broke out some palings and went towards her. I had dressed as a countryman in old gray flannel trousers, heavy wooden shoes, shabby shooting coat, a peaked cap, a ragged bandana, hands soiled with mould, and a dibble in my hand.

“‘Madame,’ said the housekeeper, ‘this good man is your neighbor.’

“The Countess was not alarmed. I saw at last the woman whose conduct and her husband’s confidences had made me so curious. It was early May. The air was pure, the spring verdure and fragrance a fitting setting for this creature of sorrow. As I saw Honorine I understood Octave’s passion.

“Her pallor struck me by its peculiar tone of white—for there are as many tones of white as of red. Her skin showed the blood in blue veins; at the slightest emotion a rosy flush came. The sunshine through the acacias shed on Honorine a pale gold like that Raphael and Titian enwrap their Virgins in. Her brown eyes expressed tenderness and vivacity; their brightness seemed reflected through long lashes. By lifting her eyelids she could spellbind; there was feeling, dignity, terror, or contempt in her way of raising or dropping those veils of the soul. She could freeze or give life by a look. Her light-brown hair, carelessly knotted, outlined a poet’s brow, high, powerful, dreamy. The mouth was voluptuous. All the lines and forms of the head had a stamp of nobleness defying time.

“Though slight, Honorine was not thin; her figure might revive love when it believed itself exhausted. She perfectly represented the word mignonne—pliant like a kitten. Her small feet made a light inherent sound that harmonized with the rustle of her dress, producing a feminine music stamped on the heart. Her gait bore the quarterings of her race with pride, so even a working man would make way in the street. Gay and tender, haughty and imposing, she remained a child. But a child who might be as strong as an angel; once hurt, she would be implacable.

“Coldness on that face must be death to those she had smiled on. Inhaling the perfume of violets about her, I understood how the memory of this wife arrested the Count on the threshold of debauchery, and how impossible to forget a creature who was a flower to the touch, the eye, and the soul. Honorine inspired chivalrous devotion irrespective of reward. A man seeing her must say to himself: ‘Think, and I will divine your thought; speak, and I will obey. If my life sacrificed in torments can procure you one day’s happiness, take my life; I will smile like a martyr.’

“Many women study expression and succeed in producing similar effects; in her it was all outcome of delightful nature. If I tell you this, it is because her soul, thoughts, and heart’s exquisiteness are what we are concerned with, and you would blame me if I had not sketched them.

“I was near forgetting my part as a half-crazy lout, clumsy and hardly chivalrous.

“‘I am told, madame, that you are fond of flowers?’

“‘I am an artificial flower-maker,’ she said. ‘After growing flowers I imitate them, like a mother painting her children. That is enough to say I am poor and unable to pay for the concession I desire from you.’

“‘But how,’ said I, grave as a judge, ‘can a lady of such rank ply so humble a calling? Have you, like me, good reasons for employing your hands so as to keep your brain from working?’

“‘Let us stick to the question of the wall,’ she said with a smile.

“‘We have begun at the foundations,’ I replied. ‘Must I not know which of us ought to yield to the other in behalf of our suffering, or, if you choose, our mania? Oh! what a charming clump of narcissus!’

“She had made a perfect museum of flowers and shrubs, arranged as an artist. The retired garden breathed comforting scents and suggested sweet thoughts and graceful pictures. It showed the sign-manual of our true character stamped on all when nothing compels hypocrisy. I looked at the narcissus and at the Countess, pretending to be more in love with the flowers than with her to keep up my part.

“‘So you are fond of flowers?’ she asked.
