################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

Whenever you have seriously looked at the exhibition of sculpture and painting since the revolution of 1830, have you not felt uneasiness and sadness at the sight of those long overcrowded galleries? Since 1830, the true Salon no longer exists. The Louvre has been taken again—this time by a populace of artists who have maintained themselves there.

In former days, when the Salon showed only the choicest works, it conferred the highest honor on those exhibited. Among two hundred selected paintings, the public could still choose: a crown was awarded to the masterpiece by unseen hands. Eager discussions arose about some picture. Abuse showered on Delacroix, on Ingres, contributed as much to their fame as praise. Today, neither the crowd nor the criticism grows impassioned about the products of that bazaar. Forced to make the selection for itself, which the examining jury once made, the public's attention soon tires and the exhibition closes. Before 1817 the pictures admitted never went beyond the first two columns of the long gallery of the old masters; but in that year, to the astonishment of the public, they filled the whole space. Historical, high-art, genre paintings, easel pictures, landscapes, flowers, animals, and water-colors—these eight specialties could scarcely offer more than twenty pictures a year worthy of attention; the public cannot give its attention to more. The more artists increase, the more careful and exacting the jury of admission should be.

The true character of the Salon was lost as soon as it spread along the galleries. The Salon should have remained within fixed limits of inflexible proportions, where each specialty could show its masterpieces. A decade's experience has shown the excellence of the former institution. Now, instead of a tournament we have a mob; instead of a noble exhibition, a tumultuous bazaar; instead of a choice selection, a chaotic mass. What is the result? A great artist is swamped. Decamps' "Turkish Cafe," "Children at a Fountain," "Joseph," and "The Torture" would have redounded far more to his credit if the four pictures had been exhibited in the great Salon with the hundred good pictures of that year, than his twenty among three thousand others, jumbled in six galleries.

By some strange contradiction, ever since the doors opened to everyone there has been talk of unknown and unrecognized genius. When, twelve years earlier, Ingres' "Courtesan," Sigalon's, Gericault's "Medusa," Delacroix's "Massacre of Scio," Eugene Deveria's "Baptism of Henri IV." were admitted by celebrated artists accused of jealousy, the world saw that young vigorous palettes existed and no such complaint was made. Now, when the veriest dauber can send his work, all the talk is of genius neglected! Where judgment no longer exists, there is nothing judged. But whatever artists may do now, they will return in time to examination and selection, which present their works to the crowd for whom they work. Without selection by the Academy there will be no Salon, and without the Salon art may perish.

Ever since the catalogue has grown into a book, many names have appeared in it that remain in obscurity, in spite of ten or a dozen pictures attached to them. Among these names perhaps the most unknown to fame is an artist named Pierre Grassou, from Fougeres, called simply "Fougeres" among his brother-artists, who now holds a place, as they say, "in the sun," and who suggested the rather bitter reflections by which this sketch of his life is introduced—reflections applicable to many other members of the tribe of artists.

In 1832, Fougeres lived in the rue de Navarin, on the fourth floor of one of those tall narrow houses which resemble the obelisk of Luxor, with an alley, a dark stairway with dangerous turnings, three windows only on each floor, and, within, a courtyard, or, to speak more correctly, a square pit. Above the three or four rooms occupied by Grassou of Fougeres was his studio, looking toward Montmartre. The studio was painted brick-color for a background; the floor was tinted brown and well frothed; each chair had a bit of carpet bound round the edges; the simple sofa was as clean as that in the bedroom of some worthy bourgeoise. All these things denoted the tidy ways of a small mind and the thrift of a poor man. A bureau held studio implements, a table for breakfast, a sideboard, a secretary; in short, all the articles necessary to a painter, neatly arranged and very clean. The stove participated in this Dutch cleanliness, made more visible because the little changing northern light flooded the vast apartment. Fougeres, being merely a genre painter, did not need the immense machinery and outfit that ruin historical painters; he never recognized sufficient faculty to attempt high-art, and therefore clung to easel painting.

At the beginning of December of that year, a season when the bourgeois of Paris periodically conceive the burlesque idea of perpetuating their forms and figures already too bulky in themselves, Pierre Grassou, who had risen early, prepared his palette, lighted his stove, ate a roll steeped in milk, and waited till the frost on his windows had melted enough to let the full light in. The weather was fine and dry. At that moment the artist, who ate his bread with that patient, resigned air that tells so much, heard and recognized the step of a man who had upon his life the influence such men have on nearly all artists—the step of Elie Magus, a picture-dealer, a usurer in canvas. The next moment Elie Magus entered and found the painter beginning his work in the tidy studio.

"How are you, old rascal?" said the painter.

Fougeres had the cross of the Legion of Honor, and Elie Magus bought his pictures at two and three hundred francs apiece, so he gave himself the airs of a fine artist.

"Business is very bad," replied Elie. "You artists have such pretensions! You talk of two hundred francs when you haven't put six sous' worth of color on a canvas. However, you are a good fellow; I'll say that. You are steady; and I've come to put a good bit of business in your way."

"Timeo Danaos et dona ferentes," said Fougeres. "Do you know Latin?"

"No."

"Well, it means the Greeks never proposed a good bit of business to the Trojans without getting their fair share. In the olden time they used to say, 'Take my horse.' Now we say, 'Take my bear.' Well, what do you want, Ulysses-Lagingeole-Elie Magus?"

These words show the mildness and wit with which Fougeres employed what painters call studio fun.

"Well, I don't deny that you are to paint me two pictures for nothing."

"Oh! oh!"

"I'll leave you to do it, or not; I don't ask it. But you're an honest man."

"Come, out with it!"

"Well, I'm prepared to bring you a father, mother, and only daughter."

"All for me?"

"Yes—they want their portraits taken. These bourgeois—they are crazy about art—have never dared to enter a studio. The girl has a dot of a hundred thousand francs. You can paint all three—perhaps they'll turn out family portraits."

And with that the old Dutch log of wood who passed for a man and who was called Elie Magus interrupted himself to laugh an uncanny laugh which frightened the painter. He fancied he heard Mephistopheles talking marriage.

"Portraits bring five hundred francs apiece," went on Elie; "so you can very well afford to paint me three pictures."

"True for you!" cried Fougeres, gleefully.

"And if you marry the girl, you won't forget me."

"Marry! I?" cried Pierre Grassou—"I, who have a habit of sleeping alone; who get up at cock-crow, and all my life arranged—"

"One hundred thousand francs," said Magus, "and a quiet girl, full of golden tones, as you call 'em, like a Titian."

"What class of people are they?"

"Retired merchants; just now in love with art; have a country-house at Ville d'Avray, and ten or twelve thousand francs a year."

"What business did they do?"

"Bottles."

"Now don't say that word; it makes me think of corks and sets my teeth on edge."

"Am I to bring them?"

"Three portraits—I could put them in the Salon; I might go in for portrait-painting. Well, yes!"

Old Elie descended the staircase to fetch the Vervelle family. To know to what extent this proposition would affect the painter, and what effect would be produced upon him by the Sieur and Dame Vervelle, adorned by their only daughter, it is necessary to cast an eye on the anterior life of Pierre Grassou of Fougeres.

When a pupil, Fougeres had studied drawing with Servin, thought a great draughtsman in academic circles. After that he went to Schinner's to learn the secrets of the powerful magnificent color that distinguished that master. Master and scholars were discreet; at any rate Pierre discovered none of their secrets. From there he went to Sommervieux' atelier to acquire that portion of the art called composition, but composition was shy and distant to him. Then he tried to snatch from Decamps and Granet the mystery of their interior effects. The two masters were not robbed. Finally Fougeres ended his education with Duval-Lecamus. During these studies Fougeres' habits and ways of life were tranquil and moral to a degree that furnished matter of jesting to the various ateliers where he sojourned; but everywhere he disarmed his comrades by his modesty and by the patience and gentleness of a lamblike nature. The masters, however, had no sympathy for the good lad; masters prefer bright fellows, eccentric spirits, droll or fiery, or else gloomy and deeply reflective, which argue future talent. Everything about Pierre Grassou smacked of mediocrity. His nickname "Fougeres" (that of the painter in the play of "The Eglantine") was the source of much teasing; but, by force of circumstances, he accepted the name of the town where he had first seen light.

Grassou of Fougeres resembled his name. Plump and of medium height, he had a dull complexion, brown eyes, black hair, a turned-up nose, rather wide mouth, and long ears. His gentle, passive, resigned air gave a certain relief to these leading features of a physiognomy full of health, but wanting in action. This young man, born to be a virtuous bourgeoise, having left his native place and come to Paris to be a clerk with a color-merchant (formerly of Mayenne and a distant connection of the Orgemonts) made himself a painter simply by the obstinacy that constitutes the Breton character. What he suffered, the manner in which he lived during those years of study, God only knows. He suffered as much as great men suffer when hounded by poverty and hunted like wild beasts by the pack of commonplace minds and troops of vanities athirst for vengeance.

As soon as he thought himself able to fly on his own wings, Fougeres took a studio in the upper rue des Martyrs, where he began to delve his way. He made his first appearance in 1819. The first picture he presented to the jury at the Louvre represented a village wedding laboriously copied from Greuze. It was rejected. When Fougeres heard of the decision, he did not fall into one of those fits of epileptic self-love to which strong natures give themselves up, sometimes ending in challenges to the director or even threats of assassination. Fougeres quietly fetched his canvas, wrapped it in a handkerchief, and brought it home, vowing he would still make himself a great painter. He placed his picture on the easel, and went to one of his former masters, a man of immense talent—Schinner—whose triumph at that year's Salon was complete. Fougeres asked him to criticize the rejected work. The great painter left everything and went at once. When Fougeres had placed the work before him, Schinner, after a glance, pressed Fougeres' hand.

"You are a fine fellow," he said; "you've a heart of gold, and I must not deceive you. Listen; you are fulfilling the promises you made in the studios. When you find such things at the tip of your brush, my good Fougeres, you had better leave colors with Brullon, and not take the canvas of others. Go home early, put on your cotton night-cap, and be in bed by nine o'clock. The next morning go to some government office, ask for a place, and give up art."

"My dear friend," said Fougeres, "my picture is already condemned; it is not a verdict I want of you, but the cause of that verdict."

"Well—you paint gray and sombre; you see nature as a crape veil; your drawing is heavy, pasty; your composition is a medley of Greuze, who only redeemed his defects by qualities you lack."

While detailing these faults Schinner saw on Fougeres' face so deep an expression of sadness that he carried him off to dinner and tried to console him. The next morning at seven Fougeres was at his easel working over the rejected picture; he warmed the colors; he made the corrections suggested by Schinner; he touched up his figures. Then, disgusted with such patching, he carried the picture to Elie Magus. Elie Magus, a sort of Dutch-Flemish-Belgian, had three reasons for being what he became—rich and avaricious. Coming last from Bordeaux, he was just starting in Paris, selling old pictures and living on the boulevard Bonne-Nouvelle. Fougeres, who relied on his palette to go to the baker's, bravely ate bread and nuts, or bread and milk, or bread and cherries, or bread and cheese, or bread and cherries, according to the seasons. Elie Magus, to whom Pierre offered his first picture, eyed it for some time and then gave him fifteen francs.

"With fifteen francs a year coming in, and a thousand francs for expenses," said Fougeres, smiling, "a man will go fast and far."

Elie Magus made a gesture; he bit his thumbs, thinking he might have had that picture for five francs.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Several days Pierre walked down from the rue des Martyrs and stationed himself at the corner of the boulevard opposite Elie's shop, where his eye rested upon his picture, which obtained no notice from passers. After a week the picture disappeared; Fougeres walked slowly to the dealer's shop. The Jew was at his door.

"Well, I see you have sold my picture."

"No, here it is," said Magus; "I've framed it, to show it to some one who fancies he knows about painting."

Fougeres had not the heart to return to the boulevard. He set about another picture, and spent two months upon it,--eating mouse's meals and working like a galley-slave.

One evening he went to the boulevard, his feet leading him to the dealer's shop. His picture was not to be seen.

"I've sold your picture," said Elie Magus, seeing him.

"For how much?"

"I got back what I gave and a small interest. Make me some Flemish interiors, a lesson of anatomy, landscapes, and such like, and I'll buy them of you," said Elie.

Fougeres would fain have taken old Magus in his arms; he regarded him as a father. He went home joyful; the great painter Schinner was mistaken after all! In that immense city of Paris there were some hearts that beat in unison with Pierre's; his talent was understood and appreciated. The poor fellow of twenty-seven had the innocence of a lad of sixteen. Another man, one of those distrustful, surly artists, would have noticed the diabolical look on Elie's face and seen the twitching of the hairs of his beard, the irony of his moustache, and the movement of his shoulders which betrayed the satisfaction of Walter Scott's Jew in swindling a Christian.

Fougeres marched along the boulevard in a state of joy which gave his honest face an expression of pride. He was like a schoolboy protecting a woman. He met Joseph Bridau, one of his comrades, and one of those eccentric geniuses destined to fame and sorrow. Joseph Bridau, who had, to use his own expression, a few sous in his pocket, took Fougeres to the Opera. But Fougeres didn't see the ballet, didn't hear the music; he was imagining pictures, he was painting. He left Joseph in the middle of the evening, and ran home to make sketches by lamp-light. He invented thirty pictures, all reminiscence, and felt himself a man of genius. The next day he bought colors, and canvases of various dimensions; he piled up bread and cheese on his table, filled a water-pot, and laid in a provision of wood for his stove; then, to use a studio expression, he dug at his pictures. He hired several models and Magus lent him stuffs.

After two months' seclusion the Breton had finished four pictures. Again he asked counsel of Schinner, this time adding Bridau to the invitation. The two painters saw in three of these paintings a servile imitation of Dutch interiors by Metzu, in the fourth a copy of Rembrandt's "Lesson of Anatomy."

"Still imitating!" said Schinner. "Ah! Fougeres can't manage to be original."

"You ought to do something else than painting," said Bridau.

Fougeres lowered his head like a sheep when it rains. Then he asked and obtained certain useful advice, and retouched his pictures before taking them to Elie Magus. Elie paid him twenty-five francs apiece. At that price of course Fougeres earned nothing; neither did he lose, thanks to his sober living. He made a few excursions to the boulevard to see what became of his pictures, and there he underwent a singular hallucination. His neat, clean paintings, hard as tin and shiny as porcelain, were covered with a sort of mist; they looked like old daubs. Magus was out, and Pierre could obtain no information on this phenomenon. He fancied something was wrong with his eyes.

The painter went back to his studio and made more pictures. After seven years of continued toil Fougeres managed to compose and execute quite passable work. He did as well as any artist of the second class. Elie bought and sold all the paintings of the poor Breton, who earned laboriously about two thousand francs a year while he spent but twelve hundred.

At the Exhibition of 1829, Leon de Lora, Schinner, and Bridau, who all three occupied a great position and were, in fact, at the head of the art movement, were filled with pity for the perseverance and the poverty of their old friend; and they caused to be admitted into the grand salon of the Exhibition, a picture by Fougeres. This picture, powerful in interest but derived from Vigneron as to sentiment and from Dubufe's first manner as to execution, represented a young man in prison, whose hair was being cut around the nape of the neck. On one side was a priest, on the other two women, one old, one young, in tears. A sheriff's clerk was reading aloud a document. On a wretched table was a meal, untouched. The light came in through the bars of a window near the ceiling. It was a picture fit to make the bourgeois shudder, and the bourgeois shuddered. Fougeres had simply been inspired by the masterpiece of Gerard Douw; he had turned the group of the "Dropsical Woman" toward the window, instead of presenting it full front. The condemned man was substituted for the dying woman--same pallor, same glance, same appeal to God. Instead of the Dutch doctor, he had painted the cold, official figure of the sheriff's clerk attired in black; but he had added an old woman to the young one of Gerard Douw. The cruelly simple and good-humored face of the executioner completed and dominated the group. This plagiarism, very cleverly disguised, was not discovered. The catalogue contained the following:--

     510. Grassou de Fougeres (Pierre), rue de Navarin, 2.
     Death-toilet of a Chouan, condemned to execution in 1809.

Though wholly second-rate, the picture had immense success, for it recalled the affair of the "chauffeurs," of Mortagne. A crowd collected every day before the now fashionable canvas; even Charles X. paused to look at it. "Madame," being told of the patient life of the poor Breton, became enthusiastic over him. The Duc d'Orleans asked the price of the picture. The clergy told Madame la Dauphine that the subject was suggestive of good thoughts; and there was, in truth, a most satisfying religious tone about it. Monseigneur the Dauphin admired the dust on the stone-floor,--a huge blunder, by the way, for Fougeres had painted greenish tones suggestive of mildew along the base of the walls. "Madame" finally bought the picture for a thousand francs, and the Dauphin ordered another like it. Charles X. gave the cross of the Legion of honor to this son of a peasant who had fought for the royal cause in 1799. (Joseph Bridau, the great painter, was not yet decorated.) The minister of the Interior ordered two church pictures of Fougeres.

This Salon of 1829 was to Pierre Grassou his whole fortune, fame, future, and life. Be original, invent, and you die by inches; copy, imitate, and you'll live. After this discovery of a gold mine, Grassou de Fougeres obtained his benefit of the fatal principle to which society owes the wretched mediocrities to whom are intrusted in these days the election of leaders in all social classes; who proceed, naturally, to elect themselves and who wage a bitter war against all true talent. The principle of election applied indiscriminately is false, and France will some day abandon it.

Nevertheless the modesty, simplicity, and genuine surprise of the good and gentle Fougeres silenced all envy and recriminations. Besides, he had on his side all of his clan who had succeeded, and all who expected to succeed. Some persons, touched by the persistent energy of a man whom nothing had discouraged, talked of Domenichino and said:--

"Perseverance in the arts should be rewarded. Grassou hasn't stolen his successes; he has delved for ten years, the poor dear man!"

That exclamation of "poor dear man!" counted for half in the support and the congratulations which the painter received. Pity sets up mediocrities as envy pulls down great talents, and in equal numbers. The newspapers, it is true, did not spare criticism, but the chevalier Fougeres digested them as he had digested the counsel of his friends, with angelic patience.

Possessing, by this time, fifteen thousand francs, laboriously earned, he furnished an apartment and studio in the rue de Navarin, and painted the picture ordered by Monseigneur the Dauphin, also the two church pictures, and delivered them at the time agreed on, with a punctuality that was very discomforting to the exchequer of the ministry, accustomed to a different course of action. But--admire the good fortune of men who are methodical--if Grassou, belated with his work, had been caught by the revolution of July he would not have got his money.

By the time he was thirty-seven Fougeres had manufactured for Elie Magus some two hundred pictures, all of them utterly unknown, by the help of which he had attained to that satisfying manner, that point of execution before which the true artist shrugs his shoulders and the bourgeoisie worships. Fougeres was dear to friends for rectitude of ideas, for steadiness of sentiment, absolute kindliness, and great loyalty; though they had no esteem for his palette, they loved the man who held it.

"What a misfortune it is that Fougeres has the vice of painting!" said his comrades.

But for all this, Grassou gave excellent counsel, like those feuilletonists incapable of writing a book who know very well where a book is wanting. There was this difference, however, between literary critics and Fougeres; he was eminently sensitive to beauties; he felt them, he acknowledged them, and his advice was instinct with a spirit of justice that made the justness of his remarks acceptable. After the revolution of July, Fougeres sent about ten pictures a year to the Salon, of which the jury admitted four or five. He lived with the most rigid economy, his household being managed solely by an old charwoman. For all amusement he visited his friends, he went to see works of art, he allowed himself a few little trips about France, and he planned to go to Switzerland in search of inspiration. This detestable artist was an excellent citizen; he mounted guard duly, went to reviews, and paid his rent and provision-bills with bourgeois punctuality.

Having lived all his life in toil and poverty, he had never had the time to love. Poor and a bachelor, until now he did not desire to complicate his simple life. Incapable of devising any means of increasing his little fortune, he carried, every three months, to his notary, Cardot, his quarterly earnings and economies. When the notary had received about three thousand francs he invested them in some first mortgage, the interest of which he drew himself and added to the quarterly payments made to him by Fougeres. The painter was awaiting the fortunate moment when his property thus laid by would give him the imposing income of two thousand francs, to allow himself the otium cum dignitate of the artist and paint pictures; but oh! what pictures! true pictures! each a finished picture! chouette, Koxnoff, chocnosoff! His future, his dreams of happiness, the superlative of his hopes--do you know what it was? To enter the Institute and obtain the grade of officer of the Legion of honor; to sit down beside Schinner and Leon de Lora, to reach the Academy before Bridau, to wear a rosette in his buttonhole! What a dream! It is only commonplace men who think of everything.

Hearing the sound of several steps on the staircase, Fougeres rubbed up his hair, buttoned his jacket of bottle-green velveteen, and was not a little amazed to see, entering his doorway, a simpleton face vulgarly called in studio slang a "melon." This fruit surmounted a pumpkin, clothed in blue cloth adorned with a bunch of tintinnabulating baubles. The melon puffed like a walrus; the pumpkin advanced on turnips, improperly called legs. A true painter would have turned the little bottle-vendor off at once, assuring him that he didn't paint vegetables. This painter looked at his client without a smile, for Monsieur Vervelle wore a three-thousand-franc diamond in the bosom of his shirt.

Fougeres glanced at Magus and said: "There's fat in it!" using a slang term then much in vogue in the studios.

Hearing those words Monsieur Vervelle frowned. The worthy bourgeois drew after him another complication of vegetables in the persons of his wife and daughter. The wife had a fine veneer of mahogany on her face, and in figure she resembled a cocoa-nut, surmounted by a head and tied in around the waist. She pivoted on her legs, which were tap-rooted, and her gown was yellow with black stripes. She proudly exhibited unutterable mittens on a puffy pair of hands; the plumes of a first-class funeral floated on an over-flowing bonnet; laces adorned her shoulders, as round behind as they were before; consequently, the spherical form of the cocoa-nut was perfect. Her feet, of a kind that painters call abatis, rose above the varnished leather of the shoes in a swelling that was some inches high. How the feet were ever got into the shoes, no one knows.

Following these vegetable parents was a young asparagus, who presented a tiny head with smoothly banded hair of the yellow-carroty tone that a Roman adores, long, stringy arms, a fairly white skin with reddish spots upon it, large innocent eyes, and white lashes, scarcely any brows, a leghorn bonnet bound with white satin and adorned with two honest bows of the same satin, hands virtuously red, and the feet of her mother. The faces of these three beings wore, as they looked round the studio, an air of happiness which bespoke in them a respectable enthusiasm for Art.

"So it is you, monsieur, who are going to take our likenesses?" said the father, assuming a jaunty air.

"Yes, monsieur," replied Grassou.

"Vervelle, he has the cross!" whispered the wife to the husband while the painter's back was turned.

"Should I be likely to have our portraits painted by an artist who wasn't decorated?" returned the former bottle-dealer.

Elie Magus here bowed to the Vervelle family and went away. Grassou accompanied him to the landing.

"There's no one but you who would fish up such whales."

"One hundred thousand francs of 'dot'!"

"Yes, but what a family!"

"Three hundred hundred thousand francs of expectations, a house in the rue Boucherat, and a country-house at Ville d'Avray!"

"Bottles and corks! bottles and corks!" said the painter; "they set my teeth on edge."

"Safe from want for the rest of your days," said Elie Magus as he departed.

That idea entered the head of Pierre Grassou as the daylight had burst into his garret that morning.

While he posed the father of the young person, he thought the bottle-dealer had a good countenance, and he admired the face full of violent tones. The mother and daughter hovered about the easel, marvelling at all his preparations; they evidently thought him a demigod. This visible admiration pleased Fougeres. The golden calf threw upon the family its fantastic reflections.

"You must earn lots of money; but of course you don't spend it as you get it," said the mother.

"No, madame," replied the painter; "I don't spend it; I have not the means to amuse myself. My notary invests my money; he knows what I have; as soon as I have taken him the money I never think of it again."

"I've always been told," cried old Vervelle, "that artists were baskets with holes in them."

"Who is your notary--if it is not indiscreet to ask?" said Madame Vervelle.

"A good fellow, all round," replied Grassou. "His name is Cardot."

"Well, well! if that isn't a joke!" exclaimed Vervelle. "Cardot is our notary too."

"Take care! don't move," said the painter.

"Do pray hold still, Antenor," said the wife. "If you move about you'll make monsieur miss; you should just see him working, and then you'd understand."

"Oh! why didn't you have me taught the arts?" said Mademoiselle Vervelle to her parents.

"Virginie," said her mother, "a young person ought not to learn certain things. When you are married--well, till then, keep quiet."

During this first sitting the Vervelle family became almost intimate with the worthy artist. They were to come again two days later. As they went away the father told Virginie to walk in front; but in spite of this separation, she overheard the following words, which naturally awakened her curiosity.

"Decorated--thirty-seven years old--an artist who gets orders--puts his money with our notary. We'll consult Cardot. Hein! Madame de Fougeres! not a bad name--doesn't look like a bad man either! One might prefer a merchant; but before a merchant retires from business one can never know what one's daughter may come to; whereas an economical artist--and then you know we love Art--Well, we'll see!"

While the Vervelle family discussed Pierre Grassou, Pierre Grassou discussed in his own mind the Vervelle family. He found it impossible to stay peacefully in his studio, so he took a walk on the boulevard, and looked at all the red-haired women who passed him. He made a series of the oddest reasonings to himself: gold was the handsomest of metals; a tawny yellow represented gold; the Romans were fond of red-haired women, and he turned Roman, etc. After two years of marriage what man would ever care about the color of his wife's hair? Beauty fades,--but ugliness remains! Money is one-half of all happiness. That night when he went to bed the painter had come to think Virginie Vervelle charming.

When the three Vervelles arrived on the day of the second sitting the artist received them with smiles. The rascal had shaved and put on clean linen; he had also arranged his hair in a pleasing manner, and chosen a very becoming pair of trousers and red leather slippers with pointed toes. The family replied with smiles as flattering as those of the artist. Virginie became the color of her hair, lowered her eyes, and turned aside her head to look at the sketches. Pierre Grassou thought these little affectations charming, Virginie had such grace; happily she didn't look like her father or her mother; but whom did she look like?

During this sitting there were little skirmishes between the family and the painter, who had the audacity to call pere Vervelle witty. This flattery brought the family on the double-quick to the heart of the artist; he gave a drawing to the daughter, and a sketch to the mother.

"What! for nothing?" they said.

Pierre Grassou could not help smiling.

"You shouldn't give away your pictures in that way; they are money," said old Vervelle.

At the third sitting pere Vervelle mentioned a fine gallery of pictures which he had in his country-house at Ville d'Avray--Rubens, Gerard Douw, Mieris, Terburg, Rembrandt, Titian, Paul Potter, etc.

"Monsieur Vervelle has been very extravagant," said Madame Vervelle, ostentatiously. "He has over one hundred thousand francs' worth of pictures."

"I love Art," said the former bottle-dealer.

When Madame Vervelle's portrait was begun that of her husband was nearly finished, and the enthusiasm of the family knew no bounds. The notary had spoken in the highest praise of the painter. Pierre Grassou was, he said, one of the most honest fellows on earth; he had laid by thirty-six thousand francs; his days of poverty were over; he now saved about ten thousand francs a year and capitalized the interest; in short, he was incapable of making a woman unhappy. This last remark had enormous weight in the scales. Vervelle's friends now heard of nothing but the celebrated painter Fougeres.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The day Fougeres began Mademoiselle Virginie's portrait he was already virtually son‑in‑law to the Vervelle family. The three Vervelles thrived in his studio, treating it like a residence; they were drawn to its neat, artistic order. Toward the end of the sitting the stairway shook and Joseph Bridau burst in, hair flying, his haggard face darting about. He paced, returned to Grassou, pulled his coat over his stomach and fumbled at a missing button.

"Wood is dear," he said to Grassou.

"Ah!"

"The British are after me," he added—creditors. "Gracious! do you paint such things as that?"

"Hold your tongue!"

"Ah! to be sure, yes."

The Vervelles flushed at the intrusion.

"Brings in, hey?" continued Joseph. "Any shot in your locker?"

"How much do you want?"

"Five hundred. I've got a bull‑dog dealer after me; if he gets his teeth in he won't let go while there's a bit of me left. What a crew!"

"I'll write you a line for my notary."

"Have you got a notary?"

"Yes."

"That explains why you still paint cheeks like a perfumer's sign."

Grassou colored; Virginie sat.

"Take Nature as you find her," resumed Bridau, lecturing. "Mademoiselle is red‑haired. Is that a sin? All things are magnificent in painting. Put some vermilion on your palette, warm those cheeks; touch the little brown spots; butter it in. Do you pretend to know more than Nature?"

"Look here," said Fougeres, "take my place while I go and write that note."

Vervelle rolled to the table and whispered to Grassou: "Won't that country lout spoil it?"

"If he would only paint the portrait of your Virginie it would be worth a thousand times more than mine," replied Fougeres vehemently.

The bourgeois withdrew to his wife, uneasy about his daughter's portrait. Bridau returned the palette and the note. "I won't thank you. I can go back to d'Arthez' chateau, where I'm doing a dining‑room and Leon de Lora the door‑tops — masterpieces! Come and see us." He left without taking leave, having seen enough of Virginie.

"Who is that man?" asked Madame Vervelle.

"A great artist," Grassou answered.

"Are you sure," said Virginie, "that he has done no harm? He frightened me."

"He has only done it good," replied Grassou.

"Well, if he is a great artist, I prefer a great artist like you," said Madame Vervelle.

The brusque ways of genius had ruffled these orderly bourgeois. Saint‑Martin's summer was beginning. Timid before a man of genius, Vervelle invited Fougeres to his country house the following Sunday. "You artists want emotions, great scenes, witty talk; but you'll find good wines, and my picture collection will compensate an artist like you for dining with mere merchants," he flattered.

This stroking of Pierre Grassou's ingenuous vanity—an honest, mediocre artist with a heart of gold, poor draughtsman, loyal soul, decorated with the Legion of Honor—sent him modestly to Ville‑d'Avray to enjoy the fine days of the year. He went by public conveyance, admiring the bottle‑dealer's villa: a five‑acre park atop Ville‑d'Avray with a noble view. He imagined marrying Virginie and owning that villa.

The Vervelles received him with enthusiastic bourgeois absurdity. It was a day of triumph. The prospective son‑in‑law was shown about on immaculate paths; the park and house proclaimed: "We have a great artist among us." Little Vervelle rolled like an apple through his park; the daughter meandered and the mother followed. For seven hours they attended Pierre Grassou. After a magnificent dinner, Monsieur and Madame Vervelle staged their theatrical effect—the opening of the picture gallery, lit by carefully arranged lamps. Three neighbors (retired merchants), an old uncle (expected to inherit), an elderly Demoiselle Vervelle, and other guests joined, eager to hear his opinion of Pere Vervelle's famed collection, whose value the host enjoyed inflating. The bottle‑merchant fancied himself rivaling King Louis‑Philippe and the galleries of Versailles.

The pictures, magnificently framed, bore labels in black letters on gold:
               Rubens
     Dance of fauns and nymphs

                         Rembrandt
     Interior of a dissecting room. The physician van Tromp
             instructing his pupils.

There were one hundred and fifty pictures, varnished and dusted; some were veiled by green baize curtains not drawn in the presence of young ladies.

Pierre stood with arms pendent and mouth agape as he recognized half his own pictures among them. He was Rubens, Rembrandt, Mieris, Metsu, Paul Potter, Gerard Dou—twenty great masters in one.

"What is the matter? You've turned pale!" someone cried.

"Daughter, a glass of water, quick!" Madame Vervelle exclaimed. The painter took Pere Vervelle by the button and led him aside, ostensibly to look at a Murillo—Spanish pictures being in vogue.

"You bought your pictures from Elie Magus?"

"Yes, all originals."

"Between ourselves, tell me what he made you pay for those I shall point out to you."

They walked the gallery together; the guests watched the gravity with which the artist and host examined each picture.

"Three thousand francs," whispered Vervelle as they reached the last, "but I tell everybody forty thousand."

"Forty thousand for a Titian!" said the artist aloud. "Why, it is nothing at all!"

"Didn't I tell you," said Vervelle, "that I had three hundred thousand francs' worth of pictures?"

"I painted those pictures," said Pierre Grassou in Vervelle's ear, "and I sold them one by one to Elie Magus for less than ten thousand francs the whole lot."

"Prove it to me," said the bottle‑dealer, "and I double my daughter's dot, for if it is so, you are Rubens, Rembrandt, Titian, Gerard Dou!"

"And Magus is a famous picture‑dealer!" said the painter, now understanding why his paintings had been aged in Elie's shop and why Magus had specified certain subjects.

Far from losing esteem, Monsieur de Fougeres (as the family called him) gained so much that, when the portraits were finished, he presented them gratuitously to his father‑in‑law, mother‑in‑law, and wife.

At the present day Pierre Grassou, who never misses exhibiting at the Salon, is considered in bourgeois circles a fine portrait‑painter. He earns some twenty thousand francs a year and spoils a thousand francs' worth of canvas. His wife brought six thousand francs' dowry, and he lives with his father‑in‑law. The Vervelles and the Grassous, who agree delightfully, keep a carriage and are content. Pierre never leaves the bourgeois world; no family portrait between the barrier du Trone and the rue du Temple escapes him; none costs less than five hundred francs. The chief reason bourgeois families employ him is: "Say what you will of him, he lays by twenty thousand francs a year with his notary."

Because Grassou took a creditable part in the May 12th riots he was made an officer of the Legion of Honor and is a major in the National Guard. The Museum of Versailles commissioned a battle‑piece from such an excellent citizen, and he walked about Paris telling old comrades, "The King has given me an order for the Museum of Versailles."

Madame de Fougeres adores her husband, who has presented her two children. Though a good father and husband, he cannot banish a fatal thought: artists laugh at his work, his name is a term of contempt in studios, and the feuilletons ignore his pictures. Yet he persists; he aims for the Academy, where he will doubtless enter. And — the vengeance that fills him — he buys works by celebrated artists short of means and substitutes these true works for the daubs in the Ville‑d'Avray collection.

Many mediocrities are more aggressive and mischievous than Pierre Grassou, who is anonymously benevolent and obliging.

ADDENDUM

MIDDLE OF THE STORY. MIDDLE OF THE STORY. This is the middle.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

There are houses in some provincial towns whose aspect brings a melancholy like that of sombre cloisters or ruins. Within them silence and barrenness prevail; life seems stagnant until a pale face peers from a window at an unaccustomed step.

Such sadness marked a dwelling in Saumur at the upper end of the steep street leading to the chateau. This street—now little frequented, hot in summer, cold in winter, and dark in places—is notable for the resonance of its pebbled pavement, its narrow tortuous roadway, and the quiet of houses overtopped by the ramparts. Many are three centuries old, built of wood, their ends carved into fantastic figures, blackened by time. Slate-covered beams trace a bluish line along frail walls; rotten shingles bend beneath the years. Blackened window-sills with faint sculpture barely bear the clay pots of poor working-women. Doors studded with enormous nails show domestic hieroglyphics of a lost meaning: here a Protestant attested his belief, there a Leaguer cursed Henry IV., elsewhere a bourgeois carved the insignia of his trade—the whole history of France is written there.

Next to a tottering artisan’s house rose a country gentleman’s mansion, its stone arch still bearing battered armorial bearings. In this hilly street the ground-floors are neither shops nor warehouses; they preserve the naive simplicity of an older age. These low rooms, without show-windows, are deep, dark, and unadorned. Their doors open in two parts; the upper half is fastened back, the lower half swings continually. Air and light reach the damp den through the upper door or an open space beneath a low front wall closed each evening by solid shutters and heavy iron bars.

This wall serves as a counter. No delusive display: only samples—tubs of cod and salt, bundles of sail-cloth, cordage, copper wire, iron hoops, a few cloth pieces on shelves. Enter; a neat girl in a white kerchief drops her knitting and calls her parents, who sell phlegmatically, civilly, or arrogantly. A cooper may sit twirling his thumbs, seeming to own little beyond a few boat-ribs and laths, yet his wood-yard below supplies the cooperage trade of Anjou. A hot season makes him rich, a rainy one ruins him; puncheons worth eleven francs may fall to six in a morning.

As in Touraine, atmospheric vicissitudes control commerce. Wine-growers, proprietors, wood-merchants, coopers, inn-keepers, mariners—all watch the sun. They dread frost, rain, wind, drought; a perpetual duel goes on between the heavens and terrestrial interests. The barometer smooths, saddens, or brightens their countenances. From end to end of this street, formerly the Grand’Rue de Saumur, men call: “Here’s golden weather,” or “It rains louis,” knowing what sun or rain will bring them.

On fine Saturdays these traders, with vineyards and provisions provided, close shop and spend two days in the country. Purchases and profits arranged, they have hours for pleasure, observation, criticism, and spying. A housewife cannot buy a partridge without neighbors asking if it was cooked right; a girl is seen by idling groups if she peeps at a window. Consciences are held in the light; the dark, silent houses hide no mysteries. Life is mostly in the open air; every household sits at its threshold to breakfast, dine, and quarrel. No one passes without being examined; formerly a stranger entering a provincial town was bantered from door to door—the origin of many good stories and the nickname copieux for Angers folk who excelled in such urban sarcasm.

The ancient mansions of Saumur’s old town are at the top of this street, once occupied by neighborhood nobility. The melancholy dwelling where the following events occur is one of these venerable relics. Follow the picturesque thoroughfare and you will find the dark recess where the door of Monsieur Grandet’s house is hidden. To explain the provincial expression—the house of Monsieur Grandet—we must give his biography.

Monsieur Grandet enjoyed a reputation in Saumur whose causes and effects must be understood provincially. In 1789—still called by some le Pere Grandet—he was a master-cooper, able to read, write, and cipher. When the Republic sold church property in the arrondissement, the forty-year-old cooper had just married the daughter of a rich wood-merchant. With about two thousand louis-d’or—his own fortune and his wife’s dowry—and help from two hundred double louis given by his father-in-law to the surly republican presiding over sales, Grandet bought for a song one of the finest vineyards in the arrondissement, an old abbey, and several farms. The inhabitants, so little revolutionary, thought Pere Grandet bold and patriotic; in truth his thought was open only to vineyards. Appointed to the administration of Saumur, his pacific influence showed itself politically and commercially: he protected the ci-devant nobles and prevented, where possible, the sale of emigres’ lands; he furnished the Republican armies with thousands of puncheons of white wine and took payment in splendid fields reserved for sale.

Under the Consulate Grandet became mayor and reaped better pickings. Under the Empire he was called Monsieur Grandet; Napoleon superseded him in office but Grandet left without regret. He had built roads to his properties; his house and lands, assessed advantageously, paid moderate taxes; and his vineyards, through his care, became the “head of the country,” a local term for the finest yielders. He might well have asked for the Legion of Honor.

In 1806 Monsieur Grandet was fifty-seven, his wife thirty-six, and an only daughter, the fruit of their love, was ten. That year he inherited three fortunes—those of Madame de la Gaudiniere, née de la Bertelliere, of old Monsieur de la Bertelliere (her grandfather), and of Madame Gentillet, her grandmother—a hoard amassed by avarice. Old Monsieur de la Bertelliere watched his gold more than any other interest. The inhabitants estimated his savings according to “the revenues of the sun’s wealth,” as they said.

Monsieur Grandet thus obtained a modern sort of nobility. He became the most imposing personage in the arrondissement. He farmed a hundred acres of vineyard, which in good years yielded seven or eight hundred hogsheads of wine; he owned thirteen farms, an old abbey whose windows he had walled to save expense, one hundred and twenty-seven acres of meadow with three thousand poplars planted in 1793, and the house in which he lived. This was his visible estate; as to other property, only two men could hazard a guess at its value: Monsieur Cruchot, a notary who handled Grandet’s usurious investments, and Monsieur des Grassins, Saumur’s richest banker, in whose profits Grandet had a secret share.

Though Cruchot and des Grassins were discreet, their public respect for Grandet led observers to estimate his wealth by their obsequiousness. No one in Saumur doubted Monsieur Grandet had a private treasure, a hiding-place of louis he nightly gazed upon. Avaricious people saw proof in his eyes, which seemed tinged by yellow metal. The glance of a man accustomed to draw enormous interest acquires furtive, eager, mysterious movements—an unspoken language of passion. Grandet inspired respect as one who owed no man anything: skillful cooper and experienced wine-grower, he could tell with precision whether to make a thousand puncheons or five hundred; he never failed in speculation and always had casks when casks were dear. He could store his vintage and sell at two hundred francs when small proprietors took five louis. His famous 1811 vintage, judiciously stored and slowly sold, brought him over two hundred and forty thousand francs.

Financially, Grandet was part tiger, part boa-constrictor. He could lie in wait, spring on his prey, swallow masses of louis, and rest like a snake digesting—impassible, methodical, and cold. No one passed without admiration mingled with fear: had not many felt the rending of those polished steel claws? For one man Cruchot procured money at eleven per cent; for another des Grassins discounted bills at frightful deductions. Few days passed without Grandet’s name in market talk. To some, his fortune was an object of patriotic pride. Innkeepers and merchants would tell strangers: “We have two or three millionaire establishments; but as for Monsieur Grandet, he does not himself know how much he is worth.”

In 1816 Saumur’s best reckoners put his landed property near four millions; having made on average a hundred thousand francs a year from 1793 to 1817, it was fair to presume he held cash nearly equal to his estate. After a game of boston or talk on vines, people said: “Le Pere Grandet? le Pere Grandet must have at least five or six millions.”

“You are cleverer than I; I have never been able to find out the amount,” Cruchot or des Grassins would answer when overheard.

If some Parisian mentioned Rothschild or Lafitte, Saumur folk asked if he were as rich as Monsieur Grandet. If the Parisian affirmed, they shook their heads incredulously. Such a fortune explained all his actions. Early peculiarities that once provoked ridicule had long died away. His slightest actions, like the blinking of his eyes, were law to the countryside, where men had studied him like a naturalist studies instinct.

“It will be a hard winter,” said one; “Pere Grandet has put on his fur gloves.”

“Pere Grandet is buying quantities of staves; there will be plenty of wine this year.”

Monsieur Grandet never bought bread or meat: his farmers supplied capons, chickens, eggs, butter, and his tithe of wheat. He owned a mill; the tenant took grain as part of rent and returned flour and bran. La Grande Nanon, his only servant, baked bread every Saturday. Grandet arranged with kitchen-gardeners, his tenants, to supply vegetables. He sold most of his fruits in the market. His firewood came from hedgerows and half-rotten sheds whose planks farmers carted to town, cut, and stacked in his wood-house, receiving his thanks. His only known expenses were consecrated bread, clothing for his wife and daughter, hire of their chairs in church, La Grande Nanon’s wages, tinning saucepans, lights, taxes, repairs, and industrial costs. He had six hundred acres of woodland recently purchased, which he put under a neighbor’s care for a promised indemnity. After acquiring this, he ate game for the first time.

Monsieur Grandet’s manners were simple. He spoke little, in short sententious phrases in a soft voice. Early on he stuttered when required to speak at length; this incoherence, attributed to defects of education, was really assumed and will be explained later. Four brief sentences sufficed him: “I don’t know; I cannot; I will not; I will see about it.” He never said yes or no, and never committed himself to writing. If people talked, he listened coldly, chin in his right hand, right elbow resting on the left, forming opinions he never changed. He reflected long before any business agreement; when his opponent confidently revealed purposes, Grandet answered: “I can decide nothing without consulting my wife.” His wife, reduced to helpless slavery, was a useful screen in business. He went nowhere among friends; he neither gave nor accepted dinners; he made no stir, economizing even in motion. He never disturbed others’ things out of respect for property, yet at home coarse language and habits of his nature surfaced where he controlled himself less.

Physically, Grandet was five feet high, thick-set, square-built, with calves twelve inches in circumference, knotted knees, and broad shoulders; his face was round, tanned, pitted by small-pox; his chin straight, lips uncurved, teeth white; his eyes had the calm, devouring expression of a basilisk; his forehead showed transverse wrinkles and significant protuberances; his yellow-gray hair was mockingly called silver and gold by young jesters. His nose, thick at the end, bore a veined wen the common people said was full of malice. His whole countenance showed dangerous cunning, an integrity without warmth, and the egotism of a man long used to concentrate every feeling on avarice’s enjoyments and upon the only human being who mattered to him—his daughter and sole heiress, Eugenie. Attitude, manners, bearing, all testified to the self-belief habit of success gives.

Thus, though outwardly unctuous, Monsieur Grandet’s nature was iron. His dress never varied since 1791: stout shoes tied with thongs, thick woollen stockings, short breeches of coarse maroon cloth with silver buckles, a velvet waistcoat of yellow and puce stripes, a large maroon coat with wide flaps, a black cravat, and a quaker’s hat. His gloves, thick as a gendarme’s, lasted twenty months; to preserve them he always laid them on his hat in one particular spot. Saumur knew nothing further.

Only six persons had the right of entrance to Grandet’s house. The most important of the first three was a nephew of Monsieur Cruchot. Since his appointment as president of the Civil courts of Saumur this young man had added Bonfons to Cruchot, signing himself C. de Bonfons. Any litigant so ill-advised as to call him Monsieur Cruchot would soon feel his folly in court. The magistrate protected those who called him Monsieur le president, but favored with smiles those who addressed him as Monsieur de Bonfons. Monsieur le president was thirty-three, owned Bonfons worth seven thousand francs a year; he expected to inherit his uncle the notary’s property and that of another uncle, Abbe Cruchot, a dignitary of Saint-Martin de Tours. These three Cruchots, backed by cousins and allies, formed a party like the Medici.

Madame des Grassins, mother of a son of twenty-three, came assiduously to play cards with Madame Grandet, hoping to marry her Adolphe to Mademoiselle Eugenie. Monsieur des Grassins promoted his wife’s schemes by services constantly rendered to the old miser, arriving in time upon the field of battle. The three des Grassins had adherents and cousins. On the Cruchot side, the abbe—the family Talleyrand—backed by his brother the notary, sharply contested every inch with his female adversary and tried to obtain the heiress for his nephew the president.

This secret warfare for Eugenie’s hand kept Saumur agitated. Would Mademoiselle Grandet marry Monsieur le president or Monsieur Adolphe des Grassins? Some said Monsieur Grandet would give his daughter to neither. The old cooper, eaten with ambition, was said to seek a peer of France, to whom an income of three hundred thousand francs would make the Grandet casks acceptable. Others replied the des Grassins were nobles and rich; Adolphe was personable; unless the old man had a papal nephew at his beck, such an alliance should satisfy a man who came from nothing—whom Saumur remembered with an adze in his hand and the bonnet rouge. Certain wise heads noted Cruchot de Bonfons had the right of entry at all times whereas his rival was received only on Sundays; others trusted Madame des Grassins’ intimacy with the women. “It is diamond cut diamond,” said a Saumur wit.

The oldest, wiser inhabitants declared the Grandets knew better than to let the property go out of the family, and that Mademoiselle Eugenie Grandet of Saumur would marry the son of Monsieur Grandet of Paris, a wealthy wholesale wine-merchant. To this the Cruchotines and Grassinists replied: the two brothers had seen each other only twice in thirty years; Monsieur Grandet of Paris, mayor and deputy and colonel of the National Guard, disowned the Saumur Grandets and meant to ally with some ducal family favored by Napoleon. In short, nothing was not said of the heiress, talked of for fifty miles and even on conveyances from Angers to Blois.

At the beginning of 1811, the Cruchotines won an advantage. The estate of Froidfond, notable for its park, mansion, farms, streams, ponds, and forests, worth about three millions, was put up for sale by the young Marquis de Froidfond, who had to liquidate. Maitre Cruchot, the president, and the abbe, aided by adherents, prevented sale in small lots. The notary concluded a bargain for the whole property, payable in gold, persuading the marquis that suits against purchasers of small lots would delay collection; it was better to sell the whole to Monsieur Grandet, solvent and able to pay in ready money. The marquisate of Froidfond was accordingly conveyed down the gullet of Monsieur Grandet, who, to Saumur’s astonishment, paid for it, under proper discount, with the usual formalities.

This affair echoed from Nantes to Orleans. Monsieur Grandet took advantage of a cart returning by way of Froidfond to see his chateau. After casting a master’s eye over the property, he returned to Saumur, satisfied he had invested at five per cent, and seized by the stupendous thought of extending the marquisate by concentrating his property there. Then, to fill his coffers, now nearly empty, he resolved to thin out his woods and sell the poplars in the meadows.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
One now understands what “the house of Monsieur Grandet” meant: that cold, silent, pale dwelling above the town, sheltered by ruined ramparts. The porte-cochere’s two pillars and arch were hewn from soft Loire tufa, its weathered holes giving the entrance a vermiculated, almost jail-like aspect. Above the arch a bas-relief of the four seasons, already crumbling and blackened, supported a projecting plinth where weeds—pellitory, bindweed, nettles, and even a little cherry-tree—had taken root.

The oak door beneath was brown, shrunken, split, fastened with iron bolts in symmetrical patterns; a small square grated panel, its bars red with rust, served as the knocker’s aperture. Through this grating one could see, at the far end of the dark vault, a few broken steps leading to a garden shut in by thick damp walls nourished with sickly herbage—the ramparts under which several neighboring gardens spread.

The principal room on the ground floor was a large hall entered directly from that vault. In towns of Anjou, Touraine, and Berry a hall is everything—antechamber, salon, office, boudoir, dining-room—the theatre of domestic life. There the barber came twice a year to cut Monsieur Grandet’s hair; there farmers, the cure, the under-prefect, and the miller’s boy came on business. The hall, with two street-facing windows, was entirely of wood: gray panels with ancient mouldings, exposed beams painted gray with the spaces between washed white and browned with age. An old brass clock with arabesques adorned the mantel of the ill-cut white stone chimney-piece; above hung a greenish mirror whose bevel reflected a thread of light along a gothic steel frame. Two copper-gilt candelabra, their side branches removable to make single candlesticks, stood on bluish marble pedestals. Antique chairs were covered with faded La Fontaine tapestries; colors and darning had blurred the scenes.

At the hall’s four corners were closets and buffets with dirty shelves. A marquetry card-table topped by a chessboard stood between the windows; above it an oval barometer, its gilding scoured by flies, looked as if it might yield its gilt at any moment. Opposite the chimney-piece were two pastel portraits said to represent Madame Grandet’s grandfather as a lieutenant in the French guard, and the deceased Madame Gentillet as a shepherdess. The windows were draped with red gros de Tours curtains, a luxury little in keeping with Monsieur Grandet’s habits but included in the purchase of the house with the pier-glass, tapestries, and rosewood buffets.

By the window nearest the door stood a straw chair on castors for Madame Grandet to see passers-by; a stained cherry-wood work-table filled the embrasure and Eugenie’s little armchair stood beside it. Here mother and daughter had lived peaceably for fifteen years in a round of labor from April to November; on November 1 they took their winter station by the chimney. Grandet permitted a fire only from November 1 to March 31, regardless of weather. A foot-warmer filled with embers saved mother and daughter from chill in April and October. They managed the family linen and daily tasks; if Eugenie wanted to embroider a collar she stole time from sleep and had to deceive her father to get light. For a long time the miser rationed tallow candles to his daughter and la Grande Nanon as he did bread and other necessities.

La Grande Nanon was perhaps the only person who accepted his despotism willingly. The town envied the Grandets for possessing her. She had lived with Monsieur Grandet thirty-five years and received sixty francs a year; by saving those sixty francs she had managed to invest four thousand francs in an annuity with Maître Cruchot. This seeming prosperity, the result of relentless thrift, made other servants jealous, who did not see the hard slavery behind it.

At twenty-two Nanon had been unable to find a situation because her face repelled employers—unjustly, for it would have suited a grenadier. Forced from a farm whose dwelling had burned, she came to Saumur robust and ready for work. Père Grandet, a cooper and a shrewd judge of strength, saw in her a workwoman shaped like a Hercules—firm on her feet, strong in the hips, square in the back, with cartman’s hands and unblemished honesty. He clothed and fed her, paid wages, and set her to work without cruelty. She attached herself gratefully, and from that day Grandet ruled and worked her with feudal authority.

Nanon did everything: she cooked, made lye, washed linen in the Loire and carried it home on her shoulders; she rose early, slept late, prepared food for the vine-dressers at harvest, watched market-people, protected her master’s property like a faithful dog, and obeyed his absurd exactions without murmur. In 1811, when the vintage had been especially difficult, Grandet resolved to give Nanon his old watch—the first present in twenty years; though he also turned over his old shoes, which were always worn out, to her. Necessity had made her stingy, and Grandet loved her as one loves a dog; she accepted his spiked collar of authority without complaint. If he cut bread with parsimony, she did not complain; she gladly shared the household’s severe regimen, and indeed no one was ever ill. Nanon laughed when Grandet laughed, felt gloomy or warm with him, and worked as he did. Pilfering fruit never occurred; any large yield was shared—“Come, fall-to, Nanon!” he would say when branches bent under fruit.

The cooper’s ambiguous laugh had been for her like a sunbeam. Her simple heart could hold only one feeling and one idea: gratitude to Père Grandet for having taken her from the street and set her to work. For thirty-five years she never ceased to see herself before his wood-yard barefoot, hearing him ask, “What do you want, young one?” His occasional pity—“Poor Nanon!”—was followed by a look from the servant that sealed a chain of devoted subjection. This compassion, cruel and pleasurable to the miser, was Nanon’s entire happiness. The townsfolk wondered how the Grandets had gained such devotion; she would go through fire and water for them.

Her kitchen—barred windows looking into the court—was always clean, neat, cold, a true miser’s kitchen where nothing went to waste. After locking up dinner remains and putting out her fire she spun hemp beside her masters; one tallow candle sufficed the family for the evening. She slept at the end of the passage in a closet lit by a fan-light; her robust health allowed it. She slept with one ear open like a watchdog, ready to rise.

Other parts of the house will be described as events require, but this sketch of the hall, where the household’s modest luxury appears, suggests the nakedness of the upper floors.

In 1819, on an evening in mid-November, la Grande Nanon lighted the fire for the first time that season. The autumn had been fine. This day was well known to the Cruchotines and the Grassinists: the six antagonists—Maitre Cruchot, the Abbe Cruchot, Monsieur C. de Bonfons, the des Grassins—prepared to meet at the Grandets and outdo one another in illustrated friendship. That morning everyone had seen Madame and Mademoiselle Grandet, accompanied by Nanon, on their way to Mass; it was Mademoiselle Eugenie’s birthday. Maitre Cruchot, the president, and Monsieur de Bonfons hurried to arrive before the des Grassins, each bringing enormous bouquets from their greenhouses; the president’s flowers were wound with white satin ribbon fringed with gold.

Monsieur Grandet, following the custom on Eugenie’s birthdays, went to her bedside and presented his paternal gift—regularly for thirteen years a gold piece. Madame Grandet gave her a winter or summer dress as needed. These gifts—two dresses and several gold pieces at birthdays, New Year’s, and her father’s fête—gave Eugenie a small revenue of about a hundred crowns. Grandet liked to see her amass this money; it was, in his mind, transferring coins from one strongbox to another and training his heiress’s thrift. He sometimes demanded an account of her treasure, saying, “It is to be your marriage dozen.”

The “marriage dozen” is an old custom still in parts of central France: at marriage a purse containing, according to means, twelve pieces, or twelve dozen, or twelve hundred pieces of gold is presented. Even the poorest bride receives some sort of dozen; the custom persisted in notable examples like Pope Clement VII.’s gift to Catherine de’ Medici.

During dinner, pleased to see Eugenie in a new gown, Grandet declared, “As it is Eugenie’s birthday let us have a fire; it will be a good omen.”

“Mademoiselle will be married this year, that’s certain,” said la Grande Nanon, carrying away the goose’s remains.

“I don’t see anyone suitable here,” Madame Grandet murmured, glancing timidly at her husband. Grandet, looking at his daughter, said gaily, “She is twenty-three to-day; we must soon begin to think of it.” Eugenie and her mother exchanged a knowing, silent glance.

Madame Grandet was a thin, dry woman, yellow as a quince—awkward, slow, made to be downtrodden. She had big bones, a big nose, a big forehead, big eyes; her mouth showed few blackened teeth. Yet she was a truly good woman: angelic sweetness, rare piety, an unalterable equanimity that made her pitied and respected. She had brought M. Grandet more than three hundred thousand francs through fortune and inheritances, yet felt humiliated by dependence and slavery and never asked for a penny. Grandet gave her no more than six francs at a time for personal expenses. When he sold his vintage each year he would stipulate “the wife’s pin-money,” the four or five louis visible as her annual revenue; but when he needed money he would sometimes ask, “Can you lend me a few sous?” and she, glad to help the man her confessor upheld as master, would return several crowns from that “pin-money.” When he handed Eugenie the monthly five-franc piece for small needs, he would ask, as he buttoned his pocket, “And you, mother, do you want anything?”

“My friend,” she would answer, moved by maternal dignity, “we will see about that later.” Such wasted dignity and submission fit the ironies of Providence.

After dinner Nanon fetched a bottle of black-currant ratafia from Monsieur Grandet’s bedroom and nearly stumbled on the steps.

“You great stupid!” her master said. “Are you going to tumble like other people?”

“It was that step which has given way,” she answered. Madame Grandet agreed it should have been mended; Eugenie had nearly twisted her ankle the day before. Grandet, mollified by the birthday and Nanon’s scare, filled a glass of ratafia for her and promised to mend the step. He went to the bakehouse for planks and tools, whistling songs of his youth as he worked; Nanon prepared to spin by the hearth so as not to intrude among guests.

At that moment the Cruchots arrived. Nanon opened the door and the hearth’s light let them find the way. The president, perfuming his bouquet with grandeur, kissed Eugenie’s neck with officious familiarity; the abbe kissed her hand. Maitre Cruchot, the notary, who never missed an opportunity, felt his courtship progress and hoped to be preferred to the des Grassins. Grandet removed the branches from the candelabra, fixed single tallow candles in their sockets, lit them with care, and sat down, looking alternately at friends, his daughter, and the two flames. Conversation ranged from how well fête-days were observed to inquiries about vintages.

“Have you sold your vintage?” asked de Bonfons.

“No; I shall keep it,” said the president, wishing to appear prudent and profitable.

“Is he driving some bargain?” Cruchot wondered. Grandet, whose pride swelled with talk of “all of them” when referring to his vintages, strutted about with the self-satisfaction of a man who calls his house his castle. Catching sight of Nanon by her candle in the passage he called her to join them: “Put out that fire and come sit with us. Are you not as good as the great people? They are descended from Adam, and so are you.”

While playfully parrying and boasting, the knocker announced the des Grassins. Madame des Grassins was a lively, plump little woman with pink-and-white skin, a fashion-setter in Saumur; her husband, formerly quartermaster in the Imperial Guard and wounded at Austerlitz, kept the air of an old soldier. They presented a rare Cape heather and a workbox with silver-gilt utensils from Paris; Adolphe des Grassins, a tall, blond, pale young man who had spent freely in Paris, kissed Eugenie and offered the gilt workbox monogrammed E.G. Eugenie, surprised and delighted, turned to her father as if for permission; Grandet coolly said, “Take it, my daughter.” The Cruchots felt humiliated as they perceived the bond forming between the heiress and Adolphe. Monsieur des Grassins offered snuff and the des Grassins’ ostentation irritated the notary and his circle.

Seating was arranged for loto at half-past eight; Madame des Grassins contrived to seat her son beside Eugenie. The party—two card-tables, bits of pasteboard colored and numbered, counters of blue glass—seemed trivial while each guest’s mind was on Grandet’s reputed millions. Grandet, inwardly self-conceited, watched Madame des Grassins’ feathers, the banker’s martial head, the faces of Adolphe, the president, the abbe, and the notary, thinking: “They are all after my money. Neither one nor the other shall have my daughter; but they are useful—useful as harpoons to fish with.”

This family gaiety in the old gray room, dimly lit by two tallow candles; the laughter, sincere only on Eugenie’s and her mother’s faces; the mingling of triviality and important stakes; the young girl, like certain birds sold because of their price, lured by proofs of friendship of which she was the dupe—altogether made the scene a melancholy comedy. It was a drama of all times: the figure of Grandet, playing his own game with false friendship and reaping profit, dominated the scene. The modern god, money, appeared in full force in his countenance. Tender sentiments existed but were secondary; only three pure hearts—Nanon’s, Eugenie’s, and her mother’s—were genuinely moved. Their ignorance of Grandet’s wealth made their feelings simple and innocent; in that ignorance lay their joy and their ruin.

At the moment Madame Grandet had just won a loto of sixteen sous—the largest ever pooled in that house—and while la Grande Nanon laughed, the knocker resounded on the door so loudly the women started.

“There is no man in Saumur who would knock like that,” said the notary.

“How can they bang in that way!” exclaimed Nanon. “Do they want to break in the door?”

“Who the devil is it?” cried Grandet.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Nanon took a candle and went to open the door, followed by her master.

“Grandet! Grandet!” cried his wife, alarmed, running to the room’s door.

The loto-players looked at one another.

“Suppose we all go?” said Monsieur des Grassins; “that knock seems ominous.”

A young man, accompanied by a coach-office porter with two trunks and a carpet-bag, soon appeared. Grandet turned sharply to his wife.

“Madame Grandet, go back to your loto; leave me to speak with monsieur.” Then he shut the door. The players resumed their seats but did not play.

“Is he from Saumur?” asked Madame des Grassins.

“No, a traveller,” answered Monsieur des Grassins.

“He must have come from Paris.”

“It’s nine o’clock; the Grand Bureau diligence is never late,” observed the notary.

“He looks young,” added the Abbe Cruchot.

“Nanon does not come back,” muttered Eugenie.

“It must be one of your relations,” remarked the president.

“Let us go on with our game,” Madame Grandet suggested softly. “I know from Monsieur Grandet’s tone that he is annoyed; perhaps he would not like us speaking of his affairs.”

Adolphe told his neighbor the newcomer was likely Eugenie’s cousin he had seen at the Baron de Nucingen’s ball. His mother scolded him to hold his tongue.

Grandet returned without la Grande Nanon; the porter’s steps echoed on the stairs. The traveller—whose arrival stirred all imaginations—came in, strikingly out of place among the provincial company.

“Sit near the fire,” Grandet said.

The young stranger saluted the company; the men inclined their heads, the women bowed. Madame Grandet offered that he must be cold; Grandet brusquely dismissed her, then Eugenie timidly offered something to warm him, met with her father’s curt remark that the visitor “has got a tongue.” The others were accustomed to the master’s despotism; the stranger, surprised, stood before the fire, lifted one foot to warm his boot, and thanked Eugenie, saying he had dined at Tours and was not tired.

“Has monsieur come from the capital?” asked Madame des Grassins.

The young man, called Monsieur Charles—Monsieur Grandet of Paris’s son—took an eye-glass and surveyed the room, ogling Madame des Grassins with easy impertinence. “Yes, madame. You are playing at loto, aunt. Do not let me interrupt you,” he said.

Madame des Grassins was sure he was the cousin and kept glancing between him and Eugenie, whose furtive looks at Charles betrayed melancholy forebodings. The company watched Charles with mixed curiosity and contempt; to their rustic eyes his Parisian elegance seemed absurd and yet impressive.

Charles Grandet, about twenty-two, displayed the air and trappings of a dandy: hunting-costume, best weapons, a variety of fine waistcoats and collars, a gold toilet-set from his mother, a pretty desk from a lady Annette, a little whip, and pistols—the whole apparatus of Parisian frivolity. He had travelled alone in the diligence, refreshed his curls in Tours, changed linen, put on a black satin cravat and round collar, a crossed cashmere waistcoat beneath a travelling great-coat, a short gold watch-chain, gray trousers with silk seams, gloves, and a cane with a chased gold knob. His airs were those of a young man confident in his charm.

The Cruchots, shabby and snuff-taking, with dingy shirts and twisted cravats, looked grotesque beside him. The Grassins, acquainted with fashion, cast satirical glances that seemed to say, “So this is Paris!” Yet Charles’s demeanor, with an eye-glass inspecting joists and fly-specks, made the loto-players regard him as a curiosity. Grandet, absorbed in a long letter, paid them no mind. Eugenie, unfamiliar with such perfection, breathed in the scent of his curls, desired the soft kid of his gloves, envied his small hands and refined features. To her, a girl who mended her father’s clothes, he seemed a seraphic being, an image from a keepsake. When he drew a handkerchief embroidered by a Scottish great lady, Eugenie watched to see if he would use it. His contemptuous glance at the coffer that had pleased her only enchanted her more. She dreamed of him.

The loto wound up slowly. La Grande Nanon entered and announced sheets for monsieur’s bed; Madame Grandet followed her out. Madame des Grassins suggested ending the game and each took two sous. The guests gathered around the fire.

“Have you finished your game?” Grandet asked without looking up.

“Yes, yes!” replied Madame des Grassins, seating herself near Charles.

Eugenie, moved by that young-girl impulse to care for a beloved one, left to help her mother and Nanon prepare the guest room. She was less concerned for her mother than eager to make her cousin’s room elegant. She found preparations undone and set about fixing them: insisting on a warming-pan between the sheets, covering a table with a cloth, persuading Nanon to change it daily and bring a pile of wood up the corridor without telling her father, and arranging small treasures on the chimney-piece—the lacquer tray from Monsieur de la Bertelliere, a crystal goblet, a gilt spoon, an antique flask. More ideas came to her in a quarter of an hour than ever before.

“Mamma, my cousin will never tolerate the smell of a tallow candle; shall we buy a wax one?” She produced a five-franc piece she had received for monthly expenses and urged Nanon to go. Madame Grandet protested: what would her husband say? Where would sugar come from? Nanon hesitated, aware of Grandet’s vigilance, but Eugenie, delighted at the first jest she had ever made, laughed and persuaded her. The girl’s tender, eager fluttering was a new spectacle in the austere house.

Meanwhile Madame des Grassins paid court to Charles. “You are very courageous to leave the pleasures of the capital and take up your abode in Saumur,” she cooed, suggesting the town could entertain him if he allowed it. She hinted that if he stayed too long with Monsieur Grandet, “what will become of you?”—painting the uncle as a sordid miser, the aunt as pious but dull, and the cousin Eugenie as “a little fool, without education,” destined to a life of darning towels.
