He called Eugenie, whom he did not see though she knelt beside him bathing his stiffening hand. “My father, bless me!” she entreated. “Take care of it all. You will render me an account yonder!” he said, proving by these last words that Christianity must always be the religion of misers.

Eugenie Grandet was now alone in that gray house, with none but Nanon to whom she could turn with certainty of being heard and understood—Nanon, the single being who loved her for herself and with whom she could speak of her sorrows. La Grande Nanon was a providence; not merely a servant but a humble friend. After her father’s death Eugenie learned from Maitre Cruchot that she possessed three hundred thousand francs of income from landed and personal property in the arrondissement of Saumur; six millions invested at three percent in the Funds (bought at sixty, then worth seventy-six francs); two millions in gold coin, and a hundred thousand francs in silver crown-pieces, besides unpaid interest. The sum total reached seventeen millions.

“Where is my cousin?” was her sole thought. When Maitre Cruchot handed her a clear schedule of the whole inheritance, Eugenie sat with Nanon by the vacant hall fireplace, where everything now was memory—from her mother’s chair on castors to the glass from which her cousin had drunk. “Nanon, we are alone—” she said. “Yes, mademoiselle; and if I knew where he was, the darling, I’d go on foot to find him.” Eugenie sighed: “The ocean is between us.” While she wept with the old servant in that cold, dark house that was to her the universe, the whole province from Nantes to Orléans rang with the news of her seventeen millions.

Among her first acts she settled an annuity of twelve hundred francs on Nanon, who, already possessed of six hundred more, became a rich and enviable match. Less than a month later that good soul passed from single life to wedded life under the protection of Antoine Cornoiller, appointed keeper of Mademoiselle Grandet’s estates. Madame Cornoiller, though fifty-nine, looked not more than forty; her strong features resisted time. Thanks to semi-conventual habits she laughed at old age with rosy skin and iron constitution. Perhaps she never looked so well as on her marriage-day. Big, fat, and strong, she gave an appearance of happiness that made Cornoiller’s bargain envied.

On her way to church, Nanon, beloved by the neighborhood, received many compliments. Eugenie had given her three dozen silver forks and spoons as a wedding present. Cornoiller, amazed, spoke of his mistress with tears in his eyes; he would willingly have been hacked in pieces for her. Madame Cornoiller, appointed housekeeper to Mademoiselle Grandet, took as much happiness from her new function as from her husband: she took charge of weekly accounts, locked up provisions and gave them out daily in the old manner; she ruled two servants, a cook and a maid to mend linen and make dresses. Cornoiller combined the functions of keeper and bailiff. The women-servants chosen were “perfect treasures.” Thus Mademoiselle Grandet had four devoted servants. The farmers perceived no change; the usages established by Monsieur Grandet were scrupulously carried out by Monsieur and Madame Cornoiller.

At thirty Eugenie knew none of life’s joys. Her pale, sad childhood had glided beside a mother always wounded by life’s bereavements. Her mother, leaving life joyfully, pitied her daughter and left in her some fugitive remorse and many lasting regrets. Eugenie’s first and only love had been a wellspring of sorrow. Meeting her lover for a few days she had given him her heart between two kisses; then he left and a whole world lay between them. This love, cursed by her father, had cost her mother’s life and brought Eugenie only suffering and frail hopes. Her upward spring toward happiness had wasted her strength and given nothing in exchange. The life of the soul needs to absorb another’s sentiments and assimilate them; without that respiration the heart perishes. Eugenie had begun to languish. Wealth was neither power nor consolation; she could not live except through love, religion, and faith in the future. Love explained to her the mysteries of eternity. Her heart and the Gospel taught her to know two worlds; she bathed in two infinite thoughts which may have had but one meaning. She drew back within herself, loving and believing herself beloved. For seven years her passion invaded everything. Her treasuries were not the millions but Charles’s dressing-case, the portraits above her bed, the jewels recovered from her father and spread upon wool in a drawer, the thimble of her aunt used by her mother, which she wore while embroidering—a Penelope’s web that presented that gold upon her finger.

It seemed unlikely Mademoiselle Grandet would marry during mourning. Her genuine piety was known. The Cruchots, guided by an old abbé, contented themselves with surrounding the heiress and paying affectionate attentions. Every evening the hall filled with devoted Cruchotines who sang the praises of their mistress in every key. She had her doctor in ordinary, her grand almoner, her chamberlain, her first lady of honor, her prime minister; above all, her chancellor, a chancellor who would fain have said much to her. If she had wished for a train-bearer, one would have been found. She was a queen, obsequiously flattered. Flattery never comes from noble souls; it is the gift of little minds who belittle themselves to worm into favor. So the people who nightly assembled in Mademoiselle Grandet’s house (they called her Mademoiselle de Froidfond) outdid each other in expressions of admiration. The novelty made her blush; but insensibly she grew accustomed to praise, and if any newcomer seemed to find her plain she would have felt the reproach far more than she might have eight years earlier. She ended by loving the incense she secretly laid at the feet of her idol. By degrees she grew used to being treated as a sovereign and to seeing her court press around her each evening.

Monsieur de Bonfons was the hero of the little circle: his wit, person, education, and amiability were perpetually praised. One or another remarked that in seven years he had largely increased his fortune; Bonfons brought in at least ten thousand francs a year, and was surrounded, like other possessions of the Cruchots, by the vast domains of the heiress. “Do you know, mademoiselle,” said a habitual visitor, “that the Cruchots have an income of forty thousand francs among them!” “And then, their savings!” exclaimed Mademoiselle de Gribeaucourt. “A gentleman from Paris has offered Monsieur Cruchot two hundred thousand francs for his practice,” said another. “He will sell it if appointed juge de paix.” “He wants to succeed Monsieur de Bonfons as president of the Civil courts, and is taking measures,” replied Madame d’Orsonval. “Monsieur le président will certainly be made councillor.” “Yes, he is a very distinguished man,” said another—“don’t you think so, mademoiselle?”

Monsieur de Bonfons tried to keep pace with the role he sought. In spite of forty years and withered features, he dressed in youthful fashions, toyed with a bamboo cane, avoided snuff in Mademoiselle de Froidfond’s house, and came in a white cravat and a pleated shirt frill, giving him a family resemblance to turkeys. He addressed the heiress familiarly as “Our dear Eugenie.” Except for more visitors, the change from loto to whist, and the absence of Monsieur and Madame Grandet, the scene remained as when the story opened. The pack still pursued Eugenie and her millions; but the hounds, more numerous, lay better on the scent and beset the prey more unitedly. If Charles had dropped from the Indian Isles he would have found the same people and the same interests. Madame des Grassins, to whom Eugenie was courteous, still tormented the Cruchots. Eugenie remained the central figure; and Charles, as before, would still have been sovereign. Yet there had been small progress. The flowers which the president formerly presented on her birthdays had become a daily institution. Every evening he brought the rich heiress a huge bouquet, which Madame Cornoiller placed conspicuously in a vase and secretly threw into a corner of the courtyard when the visitors had departed.

Early in spring Madame des Grassins tried to disturb the Cruchotines by speaking to Eugenie of the Marquis de Froidfond, whose ancient and ruined family might be restored if the heiress returned his estates through marriage. Madame des Grassins rang the changes on peerage and the marquise title until, mistaking Eugenie’s disdainful smile for acquiescence, she went about proclaiming that the marriage with “Monsieur Cruchot” was not as certain as people thought. “Though Monsieur de Froidfond is fifty,” she said, “he does not look older than Monsieur Cruchot. He is a widower and has children, that’s true. But then he is a marquis; he will be peer of France; and in times like these where will you find a better match? I know for a fact that Pere Grandet, when he put all his money into Froidfond, intended to graft himself upon that stock; he often told me so. He was a deep one, that old man!”

“Ah! Nanon,” Eugenie asked one night as she went to bed, “how is it that in seven years he has never once written to me?”
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
While events unfolded in Saumur, Charles was making his fortune in the Indies. His commercial outfit sold well; he realized six thousand dollars. Crossing the Line cleared many cobwebs from his brain; he perceived that the surest way to fortune in the tropics, as in Europe, was to buy and sell men. He went to the African coast and bought Negroes, combining that traffic with other profitable merchandise. He plunged into the business tirelessly, driven by the desire to return to Paris with the prestige of a large fortune and the hope of regaining a more brilliant position than the one he had lost.

Jostling with men, traveling through many lands, and observing conflicting customs modified his ideas and made him skeptical. He ceased to have fixed principles, since what one country called crime another praised as virtue. In the perpetual struggle of selfish interests his heart grew cold. The blood of the Grandets did its work: Charles became hard and eager for prey. He trafficked in Chinamen, Negroes, bird-nests, children, artists; practiced usury on a large scale; and, by habitually defrauding custom-houses, grew less scrupulous about others’ rights. He went to St. Thomas and bought, for a song, goods captured by pirates and took them to ports where he could sell them well. If Eugenie’s pure face had accompanied him on his first voyage, like an image of the Virgin on a mast, and if he attributed his first success to her prayers, later women—blacks, mulattoes, whites, Indian dancing-girls—orgies and adventures effaced recollection of his cousin, Saumur, the house, the bench, the stolen kiss. He remembered only the little garden behind the crumbling wall, where he had learned his fate; but he rejected his family. His uncle was an old dog who had filched his jewels; Eugénie had no place in his heart, though she remained in his accounts as a creditor for six thousand francs.

Such conduct explains Charles Grandet’s silence. In the Indies, at St. Thomas, on the African coast, at Lisbon, and in the United States, the adventurer used the name Shepherd so as not to compromise his family. As Charles Shepherd he could be indefatigable, bold, grasping, and greedy, like a man resolved to snatch his fortune quibuscumque viis and get the villainy over with to live honestly thereafter.

With such methods prosperity was rapid; and in 1827 Charles returned to Bordeaux on the brig Marie Caroline, belonging to a royalist house. He brought nineteen hundred thousand francs worth of gold-dust, from which he expected another seven or eight per cent at the Paris mint. On the brig he met Monsieur d’Aubrion, a gentleman-in-ordinary to Charles X., a worthy old man who had married a fashionable woman with a fortune from the West Indies. To meet Madame d’Aubrion’s extravagance he had gone to the Indies to sell property and was now returning with his family.

Monsieur and Madame d’Aubrion, of the old southern family d’Aubrion de Buch, were reduced to about twenty thousand francs income and had an ugly daughter whom the mother was resolved to marry off without a dot—the family fortune barely sufficing for her own expenses in Paris. The mother almost despaired of placing such a girl, but she made the best of her. Mademoiselle d’Aubrion was long and spare, with a disdainful mouth and a too-long nose, thick at the end, sallow normally and very red after a meal—an unpleasant feature in a pale, dull face. Madame d’Aubrion made her daughter appear distinguished: hygienic treatment kept the nose manageable; she taught the art of dressing, charming manners, melancholy glances that interest a man, and the coquettish peep of the foot beneath a petticoat at the precise moment the nose reddened. By full sleeves, deceptive pads, puffed dresses, and tight corsets she had achieved strange feminine developments worth showing as an example to other mothers.

Charles became intimate with Madame d’Aubrion precisely because she sought his intimacy. People on the brig said the handsome Madame spared no means to secure so rich a son-in-law. Landing at Bordeaux in June 1827, Monsieur, Madame, Mademoiselle d’Aubrion, and Charles lodged at the same hotel and set out together for Paris. The hotel d’Aubrion was hampered with mortgages; Charles was to free it. The mother told him she would gladly give the ground-floor to a son-in-law. Not sharing Monsieur d’Aubrion’s prejudices about nobility, she promised Charles that she would obtain a royal ordinance from Charles X. allowing him to take the d’Aubrion name and arms and, by buying the entailed estate for thirty-six thousand francs a year, to succeed to the titles of Captal de Buch and Marquis d’Aubrion. By uniting fortunes, living together, and profiting by sinecures, the two families might occupy the hotel d’Aubrion with an income exceeding a hundred thousand francs.

“And when a man has a hundred thousand francs a year, a name, a family, and a position at court—for I will get you appointed gentleman-of-the-bedchamber—you can do what you like,” she told Charles. “You can become master of the rolls, prefect, secretary to an embassy, ambassador if you like. Charles X. is fond of d’Aubrion; they have known each other from childhood.”

Intoxicated with ambition, Charles toyed with the hopes thus presented in confidences poured from heart to heart. Believing his father’s affairs settled by his uncle, he imagined himself anchored in the Faubourg Saint-Germain—reappearing as the Comte d’Aubrion, like the Dreux reappearing in Bréze. Dazzled by the Restoration’s prosperity, which had been tottering when he left France, fascinated by aristocratic splendor, his intoxication, begun on the brig, increased after he reached Paris, and he resolved to take the course his would-be mother-in-law indicated. His cousin counted for no more than a speck in this brilliant prospect; but he went to see Annette. A woman of the world, Annette advised him to marry and promised her support in his ambitious projects. In her heart she was delighted to fasten an ugly, uninteresting girl on Charles, whose West Indies life had made him very attractive: bronzed complexion, bold, decisive manners like a man used to sharp decisions, to rule, and to succeed. Charles breathed easier in Paris, conscious of his part to play.

Des Grassins, hearing of his return, his approaching marriage, and his fortune, came to see him and inquired about the three hundred thousand francs still required to settle his father’s debts. He found Grandet conferring with a goldsmith about jewels for Mademoiselle d’Aubrion’s corbeille. Charles had brought magnificent diamonds; the setting with plate and jewelry for the new establishment amounted to over two hundred thousand francs. When des Grassins, who had already called several times, presented himself, Charles treated him with the impertinence of a young man of fashion conscious of having killed four men in as many duels in the Indies. Coldly, and without fully grasping the plea, Charles replied:

“My father’s affairs are not mine. I am much obliged, monsieur, for the trouble you have been good enough to take—by which, however, I really cannot profit. I have not earned two millions by the sweat of my brow to fling them at the head of my father’s creditors.”

“But suppose that your father’s estate were within a few days to be declared bankrupt?”

“Monsieur, in a few days I shall be called the Comte d’Aubrion; you will understand, therefore, that what you threaten is of no consequence to me. Besides, you know as well as I do that when a man has an income of a hundred thousand francs his father has never failed.” So saying, he edged Monsieur des Grassins to the door.

At the beginning of August that year, Eugénie was sitting on the little wooden bench where her cousin had sworn to love her eternally, where she usually breakfasted when the weather was fine. The poor girl was happy for the moment in the fresh summer air, letting memory recall the great and little events of her love and the catastrophes that followed. The sun had just reached the angle of the ruined wall full of chinks that no one, by the mistress’s caprice, was allowed to touch, though Cornoiller often told his wife it would fall and crush somebody one day. At that moment the postman knocked and gave a letter to Madame Cornoiller, who ran into the garden, crying:

“Mademoiselle, a letter!” She gave it to her mistress, adding, “Is it the one you expected?”

The words rang as loudly in Eugénie’s heart as they echoed from wall to wall of the court and garden.

“Paris—from him—he has returned!”

Eugénie turned pale and held the letter for a moment. She trembled and could not break the seal. La Grande Nanon stood before her, hands on her hips, her joy showing in the lines of her face.

“Read it, mademoiselle!”

“Ah, Nanon, why did he return to Paris? He went from Saumur.”

“Read it, and you’ll find out.”

Eugénie opened the letter with trembling fingers. A cheque on the house of “Madame des Grassins and Coret, of Saumur,” fluttered out. Nanon picked it up.

The letter began in a formal tone—no longer the familiar “thou” of their youth—and in those first words Eugenie felt the change. He announced the success of his enterprise, his return rich, and the recent deaths of his uncle and aunt, news he had learned from Monsieur des Grassins. He recalled with a show of sentiment the little wooden seat, the passage, the gray hall, his attic chamber, and the night when, by her delicate kindness, she had once brightened his future. These recollections, he said, had sustained him.

But he was frank: traveling through many lands had altered his views; he no longer believed in youthful illusions. He declared that love in marriage was a delusion and that in marrying one must obey social laws and meet the world’s conventional demands. He said her customs, inclinations, education, and habits did not fit Parisian life or the future he intended. Then he revealed the reason for his change—he now possessed an income of eighty thousand francs. This fortune enabled him to marry into the d’Aubrion family, whose nineteen-year-old heiress would bring him a title, the place of gentleman-of-the-bedchamber, and a brilliant position. He admitted he did not love Mademoiselle d’Aubrion, but in marrying her he secured for his children a social rank whose advantages would become incalculable: his son, as Marquis d’Aubrion with an entailed estate yielding forty thousand francs a year, could obtain any State position he chose. “We owe ourselves to our children,” he wrote.

He tried to posture this as generosity toward Eugénie: having placed his heart, hopes, and fortune before her in good faith, he declared that if she still felt their youthful bond, he was putting himself in her hands. He claimed that if she demanded it, he would renounce his social ambitions and content himself with the simple happiness she could offer. But the tone, the calculations, and his abrupt transformation belied any real intention of renouncing his prospects.

Eugénie rose as if sitting on live coals and went to the stone steps of the court. She could not read the letter without feeling the change in every line; tears gathered. The cheque that fell from the letter was a postscript’s material proof of his choice. The P.S. enclosed a cheque on the des Grassins bank for eight thousand francs payable in gold, “which includes the capital and interest of the sum you were kind enough to lend me.” He added that he expected a case from Bordeaux containing “a few things” she must accept as a mark of his gratitude, and instructed that his dressing-case could be sent by diligence to the hotel d’Aubrion, rue Hillerin-Bertin.

“By the diligence!” cried Eugénie. “A thing for which I would have laid down my life!”

The letter destroyed every dream. For some deserted women, fury or flights to extremes are the response; others bow their heads and suffer in silence. This was Eugénie’s fate. She raised her eyes to heaven, thinking of her dying mother’s last words, which had seemed prophetic: nothing remained but to unfold her wings, stretch upward, and live in prayer until deliverance.

“My mother was right,” she said, weeping. “Suffer—and die!”
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Eugenie returned slowly from the garden to the house, avoiding the corridor where memories of her cousin lingered. The old hall held traces of him—the saucer and the Sèvres sugar-bowl she used each morning—and the atmosphere was heavy with the seriousness of the day. Nanon announced the parish curate, allied with the Cruchots and Monsieur de Bonfons’s interests. The old abbé had long urged the curate to speak to Mademoiselle Grandet about the duty of marriage for a woman of her station. Expecting a request for the thousand francs she gave monthly to the poor, Eugenie told Nanon to fetch the money, but the curate only smiled.

He came to speak of a “poor girl” who neglected her Christian duties. Eugenie, absorbed in her own sorrow, declared she could find refuge only in the Church and intended to withdraw from the world. The curate counseled patience: marriage was life, the religious veil, death. He argued, for both religious and practical reasons, that she owed society her example and stewardship of the fortune she held in trust; to bury herself in a convent would be selfish. He warned of the difficulties of managing vast estates alone and urged her to consider a husband as a safeguard for her property and duties.

Madame des Grassins arrived, driven by vengeance and despair. She confronted Eugenie, implying awareness of Charles’s return and his intended marriage to Mademoiselle d’Aubrion. Eugenie composed herself into the impassive demeanor inherited from her father. Madame des Grassins produced a letter recounting how Charles, returned from the Indies, had been in Paris about a month, and how the banns were published for his marriage. The marquise related that the Marquis d’Aubrion would not give his daughter to the son of a bankrupt, and that des Grassins intended to declare Guillaume Grandet bankrupt—revenge for her unpaid labors and a means to force the family into public disgrace.

Eugenie read the letter and, without outward passion, returned it unread to the woman. Nanon interrupted to collect eight thousand francs Charles had left, and Madame des Grassins complied. Eugenie asked the curate a pointed, ironic question—whether it would be sinful to remain a virgin after marriage—receiving a half-jesting promise to consult Sanchez on the matter. The curate withdrew. Eugenie retired to her father’s secret room and isolated herself all day, appearing only at the customary hour when guests arrived. Rumors of Charles’s return and treachery had spread, and the hall was full; yet Eugenie, expecting scrutiny, allowed no emotion to show, hiding her suffering beneath courtesy and a calm face.

As the evening’s company prepared to leave, an unexpected event startled Saumur. Eugenie called to Monsieur de Bonfons—the president—urging him to stay. The assembled guests, quick to pun, interpreted this as a formal engagement; provincial conventions treated such an address as a solemn promise. Left alone with the president, Eugenie spoke with emotion and candor. She asked him to swear that he would never claim from her the rights which marriage would give him; that he would leave her free throughout her life. She confessed she could offer only friendship to a husband and wished neither to wound him nor betray her own heart. But she added a condition: if he would accept the form of marriage without the claim to her person, he must perform a particular service.

Eugenie drew forth a certificate of a hundred Bank of France shares—fifteen hundred thousand francs—and commanded him to go at once to Paris. There he was to find des Grassins, learn the names of her uncle’s creditors, summon them, and pay them in full their claims, with interest at five percent from the date of each claim. He was to obtain full, legal receipts, properly notarized, and then carry those receipts and all papers to her cousin Grandet, also delivering a letter she entrusted to him. Only on his return, having secured those documents, would she keep her promise. The request was framed as a test of honor: he, a magistrate and a man of honor, would act as her agent. The president, overwhelmed, fell at her feet and vowed servitude.

Once he left, Eugenie could no longer contain herself and wept in private. The president hurried to Paris. The next morning he and des Grassins summoned the creditors to the notary’s office, where vouchers had been placed. All creditors attended; Monsieur de Bonfons, in Eugenie’s name, paid them the full amounts with interest. That refusal to stint on interest was remarkable in Parisian commerce. When receipts were registered and des Grassins received for his services the fifty thousand francs Eugenie had allowed him, the president went to the hôtel d’Aubrion and found Charles entering after a severe interview with his prospective father-in-law. The old marquis had insisted his daughter could not marry the son of a bankrupt until all her grandfather’s creditors were paid.

The president handed Charles a letter from Eugenie. In it she explained that Monsieur de Bonfons had placed in his hands acquittances for all claims and a receipt by which she acknowledged having received from Charles the sum total of those claims. She hinted at a “possible failure” and the social impossibility of a marriage between the son of a bankrupt and Mademoiselle d’Aubrion. She bade him farewell as the son of a man whose honor she would preserve; though she could not offer him worldly pleasures, she would not stand in the way of his marriage according to the social conventions he preferred. The letter closed with an assurance of continued faithful friendship.

Charles received the documents with astonishment when he learned of the sums involved. Monsieur de Bonfons observed that Eugenie’s fortune had recently been nineteen millions and was now seventeen, owing to the payments made; they estimated their combined income at seven hundred and fifty thousand francs upon marriage. Charles, recovering some audacity, suggested they might “push each other’s fortunes.” The president, with a cynical smile, noted the reduction in her wealth and delivered a small leather dressing-case entrusted to him.

Shortly afterward Madame d’Aubrion entered; the president was introduced as “his man of business,” and Mme d’Aubrion dismissed her husband’s objections. Heeding political and social appearances, the president returned to Saumur and soon announced his marriage to Eugenie. Six months later he was appointed counsellor in the Cour royale at Angers, later becoming president of the court. He conceived further ambitions—parliamentary election, a peerage—but his hopes came to little: he died eight days after his election as deputy of Saumur. His final elevation came at the height of the petty legal cleverness with which, as an exacting husband, he had drawn up the marriage contract. The contract contained a stipulation that, should the couple have no children, their entire combined property would pass to one another, “without exception or reservation, dispensing even with the formality of an inventory,” a clause designed to secure his eventual inheritance and explaining his scrupulous respect for Eugenie’s solitude.

Society, quick to find fault, pitied him and blamed Eugenie’s sorrow, excusing the president as a considerate and delicate man. Women whispered that she must be ill or capricious; others suggested she consult fashionable doctors in Paris or reproached her for denying an heir to her husband. Eugenie, meanwhile, with the clear-sightedness that solitude and suffering give, perceived the president’s motives. She divined that he coveted her death as the surest road to their fortune, heightened by the deaths of his own uncle the notary and his uncle the abbé. Her compassion for him was profound: he, constrained by selfish ambitions, respected the hopeless passion on which she wasted herself only because it served as his safeguard. To give a child to the husband would have cut off his hopes; thus his regard for her grief was rooted in self-preservation.

Providence seemed, in its inscrutable ways, to heap gold upon Eugenie—a woman indifferent to wealth and devoted to piety and secret benefactions. As Madame de Bonfons she became a widow at thirty-six. She retained a beauty sketched by sorrow: a placid, pale face, gentle voice, simple manners, saintly qualities unmarred by worldly contact, yet still bearing the rigid bearing and petty habits of an old maid shaped by provincial life. In spite of immense wealth, she lived frugally, keeping to the patterns of her childhood: the fire in her house in Saumur was never lit except by permission, and lights and comforts were arranged according to the old rules. She dressed modestly; the house remained shadowed and melancholy—an outward image of her soul. Although she appeared parsimonious, she disarmed such criticism by an honorable use of her fortune: she endowed pious and charitable institutions, founded an old-age hospital, financed Christian schools for children, and set up a public library. She contributed generously to the embellishment of Saumur’s churches. Her benefactions marked the nobility of her heart and provided tangible proof against charges of avarice.

Eugenie remained a woman “in the world but not of it,” created for domestic life yet deprived of husband, children, and family. She was spoken of with reverent respect—sometimes ironically as “mademoiselle”—and people speculated over her prospects. There were rumors that she might marry again, and talk of the Marquis de Froidfond and other suitors beginning to besiege the wealthy widow, as the Cruchots had once pursued the heiress. But such gossip overlooked the simple truth: Nanon and Cornoiller, faithful servants, had neither the mind nor the inclination for the intrigues of the world. Eugenie, whose heart beat only for the most tender emotions, continued her solitary piety, secretly stanching wounds in many families, directing her vast resources to good works, and moving through life with a saintly composure that redeemed the narrowness of her education and the petty habits of her early years.

############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############

##### MIDDLE OF THE STORY #####

############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

For souls given to effusiveness there is a delightful hour between day and night; twilight softens lights and reflections and invites a dreamy mood where light and shade play. The prevailing silence makes it especially loved by artists, who become contemplative, step back from pictures they can no longer work on, and judge, rapt by meanings that flash on the inner eye. He who has never stood pensive by a friend in such an hour can hardly grasp its soothingness. Favored by the clear-obscure, material skill used to produce illusion disappears. If the work is a painting, the figures seem to speak and walk; shade is shadow, light is day; flesh breathes, eyes move, blood flows, fabrics change sheen. Imagination heightens every realistic detail and sees only the work’s beauties. At that hour illusion rules; it wakens a mind’s night, peopled by dreams, bears the soul to a world of fancies where the artist forgets the real world—yesterday, tomorrow, all miseries and goods alike.

At this magic hour a young, talented painter who saw in art nothing but Art itself was perched on a step-ladder working on a large, nearly finished painting. Criticising and admiring himself, floating on the current of his thoughts, he lost himself in a meditative mood that elevated and soothed him. His reverie lasted long. Night fell. Whether he meant to come down or made some ill-judged movement—he did not know—the painter fell, struck his head on a footstool, lost consciousness, and lay motionless for an unknown time.

A sweet voice roused him. When he opened his eyes a bright light made him close them again; through the mist he heard two women whispering and felt two timid hands supporting his head. He soon recovered; by the light of an old-fashioned Argand lamp he saw the most charming face he had seen, a head that for an artist realized the ideal beauty he had formed: features recalling Prudhon’s delicacy and Girodet’s poetic sentiment—fresh temples, regular eyebrows, pure outline, virginal innocence on every feature. Her figure was slight and graceful; her dress simple and neat, showing neither wealth nor penury.

As he came to, the painter expressed admiration and fumbling thanks. A handkerchief pressed to his forehead; amid the studio smell he recognized the strong odor of ether used to revive him. He saw an old woman, looking like a marquise of the old school, holding the lamp and advising the younger woman.

“Monsieur,” said the young woman in answer to a few vague questions, “my mother and I heard the noise of your fall and fancied we heard a groan. The silence after the crash alarmed us, and we hurried up. Finding the key in the latch, we entered and found you lying motionless. My mother fetched what was needed to bathe your head and revive you. You have cut your forehead—there. Do you feel it?”

“Yes, I do now,” he replied.

“Oh, it will be nothing,” returned the elder. “Happily your head rested against this lay-figure.”

“I feel infinitely better,” replied the painter. “I need nothing further but a hackney cab to take me home. The porter’s wife will go for one.”

She tried to repeat his thanks, but the elder interrupted him at every sentence: “Tomorrow, monsieur, pray be careful to apply leeches, or to be bled, and drink a few cups of something healing. A fall may be dangerous.”

The young girl stole glances at the painter and the pictures. Her expression revealed perfect propriety; her curiosity seemed a gentle absence of mind, and her eyes spoke the natural interest women feel in suffering. The two strangers seemed to forget his works in his mishap. When he reassured them they left, looking at him with anxious kindness—free from insistence or familiarity, without indiscreet questions or invitations. Their noble and simple manners made a quiet impression on the painter; later, recalling the incident, he was greatly struck by them.

On the landing the old lady gently observed, “Adelaide, you left the door open.”

“That was to hear me fall,” said the painter with a grateful smile.

“You came down just now, mother,” replied the young girl with a blush.

“Would you like us to accompany you downstairs? The stairs are dark.”

“No, thank you, madame; I am much better.”

“Hold tightly by the rail.”

The two women remained to light him, listening to his steps.

To appreciate the interest this scene aroused, one must know something of the painter. A few days earlier he had established his studio in the attics of a house in the darker, muddier part of the Rue de Suresnes, near the Rue des Champs-Elysees and almost opposite the Church of the Madeleine. Fame had recently made him one of the artists most dear to his country; he was beginning to feel free from want and was, as he put it, enjoying his last privations. Instead of working in cheaper studios near the city gates he had spared himself the long walk and the loss of time now more valuable than before.

No man inspired more interest than Hippolyte Schinner, though he did not lightly reveal the secrets of his life. He was the idol of a necessitous mother who had raised him amid severe privations. Mademoiselle Schinner, daughter of an Alsatian farmer, had never married. Her gentle soul had been cruelly crushed long ago by a rich man who showed no delicacy in his love affairs. The day she lost her illusions she embraced reflection, religious thought, and resignation. She refused the betrayer’s alms, renounced the world, and made a glory of her shame. She devoted herself to maternal love, sought in it her joys instead of social pleasures, worked, and saved a treasure for her son. In after years one day amply repaid her sacrifices.

At the last exhibition her son received the Cross of the Legion of Honor. Newspapers hailed an unknown genius, artists acknowledged Schinner as a master, and dealers covered his canvases with gold. At twenty-five Hippolyte, to whom his mother had transmitted her woman’s soul, understood his standing. Eager to restore to his mother the pleasures society had denied her, he lived for her, hoping that fame and fortune would one day make her happy, rich, respected, and surrounded by people of mark. Schinner chose friends among the honorable and distinguished; fastidious in selection, he desired to raise further the position his talent had placed him in. His solitary work from boyhood had left him with beautiful beliefs that grace early days. His adolescent soul was not closed to the shy emotions that make a young man an apart being, full of poetry and virginal hopes—puerile to the worldly but deep in their singleness.

He had gentle, polite manners that spoke to the soul and fascinated even those who did not understand them. His voice, from the heart, stirred noble sentiments and testified to his modesty by ingenuous tones. Those who saw him felt an attraction of moral nature they could not analyze; they might call it galvanism or attempt to express it in formulas.

These details help explain why, in the absence of the porter whom he had sent to fetch a coach, Hippolyte did not question the porter’s wife closely about the two women who had tended him. He answered her natural inquiries about his accident and the fourth-floor tenants but could not stop her chatter, led by lodge policy and gossip.

“Ah,” she said, “they were, no doubt, Mademoiselle Leseigneur and her mother, who have lived here four years. We do not know exactly what these ladies do; in the morning an old woman who is half deaf comes to help them; in the evening, two or three old gentlemen with ribbon loops, like you, monsieur, come to see them and often stay very late. One comes in a carriage with servants; they say he has sixty thousand francs a year. However, they are very quiet tenants, as you are, monsieur; and economical! they live on nothing, and pay as soon as a letter is brought. It is queer, monsieur, the mother’s name is not the same as the daughter’s. Ah, but when they go for a walk in the Tuileries, mademoiselle is very smart, and she never goes out without being followed by a lot of young men; but she shuts the door in their face, and she is quite right. The proprietor would never allow—”

The coach came; Hippolyte heard no more and went home. His mother dressed his wound and would not permit him to return to the studio the next day. After advice and treatments, Hippolyte remained at home three days. During this enforced idleness his fancy recalled, bit by bit, the scene of his fainting fit. The young girl’s profile returned clearly; he saw the mother’s faded features again, felt Adelaide’s touch. He remembered some gesture whose grace memory enhanced; then an attitude, a tone of voice rising like objects from deep waters to the surface.

When he could resume work he went early to his studio; really he hurried to visit his neighbors. When passion wakes, inexplicable pleasures are felt; he mounted the fourth-floor stairs slowly, and his heart throbbed as he neared the humble brown door of Mademoiselle Leseigneur. He fancied similarity between their positions, imagined misfortunes of birth akin to his own. He lingered at the studio to think of them, dined there, then about seven o’clock went to call.

No painter of manners has sufficiently told the curious privacy of certain Parisian existences: fresh elegant toilettes and brilliant women whose homes betray doubtful comfort. If my picture is sometimes bold, blame the truth; the appearance of his neighbors’ rooms greatly influenced Hippolyte Schinner’s feelings and hopes.

The house belonged to one of those proprietors who abhor repairs and decoration, who regard being a Paris homeowner as business. Between miser and usurer, such men are allergic to moving a cupboard or opening an air-hole; when chimneys fall they deprive themselves of theatre nights “on account of repairs.” Hippolyte, who had seen a comical scene about decorative repairs, was not surprised by dark greasy paint, oily stains, and other signs of neglect. These stigmata of poverty are not without a sort of poetry to an artist.

Mademoiselle Leseigneur opened the door. Recognizing the young artist she bowed and, with Parisian tact and pride, turned to shut a glass partition through which Hippolyte might have seen linen hung over patent ironing stoves, an old camp-bed, charcoal and irons, a filter, household crockery—utensils of a small household. Muslin curtains screened this capharnaum, lighted by windows facing a neighboring yard.

With an artist’s quick eye Hippolyte saw the uses, furniture, and general condition of this cut-in-half first room. The more honorable half, serving as ante-room and dining-room, was hung with old salmon-rose paper with a flock border patched with wafers. Prints of the battles of Alexander in gilding-rubbed frames were arranged on the walls. In the middle stood a massive, old-fashioned mahogany table worn at the edges. A small stove with a thin pipe stood before the chimney, whose hearth was blocked by a cupboard. Chairs showed remains of former splendor—carved mahogany, red morocco seats, gilt nails—scarred like a veteran.

This room was a museum of objects that show both luxury and penury: a splendidly finished telescope hung over a discolored glass on the chimney. Between chimney and partition was a wretched painted sideboard pretending to be mahogany. Slippery red quarries, shabby rugs, and all the furniture shone with hard cleanliness that accentuated oldness. An indescribable odor mixed exhalations from the lumber-room, dining-room vapors, and stair smells, though the window was partly open. Street air fluttered dusty curtains, carefully drawn to hide a window bay where former tenants left ornamental additions—a sort of domestic fresco.

Adelaide hastened to open the inner room door and announced the painter with obvious pleasure. Hippolyte, who had earlier seen similar poverty in his mother’s home, noted these signs with an impression vivid as earliest memories and entered with sympathy rather than scorn.

“Well, monsieur, I hope you no longer feel the effects of your fall,” said the old lady, rising from an antique armchair and offering a seat.

“No, madame. I have come to thank you for the kind care you gave me, and above all mademoiselle, who heard me fall.”

As he spoke—words stamped with the exquisite awkwardness of first love—Hippolyte looked at the young girl. Adelaide lit the Argand lamp, replacing a tallow candle in a large copper candlestick, then placed the lamp on the chimney shelf and sat a little behind the painter to look at him at her ease while appearing interested in the lamp. The flame sputtered in the damp chimney. Seeing the large mirror on the chimney-piece, Hippolyte fixed his eyes on it to admire Adelaide; her stratagem only embarrassed them both.

While talking with Madame Leseigneur—Hippolyte called her so—he examined the room unobtrusively. Egyptian figures on iron fire-dogs were scarcely visible under cinders; two brands struggled before a sham log. An old Aubusson carpet, faded and mended, left the tiled floor cold. Walls were hung with reddish paper imitating figured silk. Opposite the windows he saw a crack and the paper outline of double doors closing a recess where Madame Leseigneur likely slept, poorly disguised by a sofa. Above a mahogany chest of drawers hung a portrait of an officer of rank, so dim he could not judge it well; from what he saw it must have been painted in China. Window-curtains of red silk were as faded as the furniture in red-and-yellow worsted work. On the chest’s marble top was a costly malachite tray with a dozen magnificently painted coffee cups, perhaps Sevres. On the chimney shelf stood an Empire clock—a warrior driving four horses on a chariot whose wheel bore the hours. Tall candlesticks held smoke-yellowed tapers; porcelain vases crowned with dusty artificial flowers stood in the corners.

In the middle of the room a card-table with new packs suggested a painful dilemma: either these two women were perfectly honest or they lived by intrigue and gambling. Yet in looking at Adelaide, so pure-minded, Schinner could not but believe in her innocence and attribute the furniture’s incoherence to honorable causes.

“My dear,” said the old lady to the young one, “I am cold; make a little fire, and give me my shawl.”

Adelaide fetched a cashmere shawl—once costly, now faded and darned—and wrapped her mother in it. She then brought a bundle of small wood from the lumber-room and threw it on the fire to revive it.

It is hard to reproduce the ensuing conversation. Guided by the tact born of early hardship, Hippolyte did not remark on his neighbors’ poverty; any direct question would be indiscreet. The simplest pity can offend, so his thoughts and words diverged. The two ladies began by talking of painting; women easily guess the embarrassment of a first call and supply devices to end it. Questioning him about his art emboldened him to talk, and the chat’s light nothings led Hippolyte to reflections that revealed his habits and mind. Trouble had prematurely faded the old lady’s face; only prominent features remained, an outline suggesting shrewdness with grace in the eyes—an expression of the old Court that can hint either at artifice or refined delicacy.

A woman’s face hides mystery: the line between frankness and duplicity, intrigue and heart, is inscrutable. The old lady’s face, like her room, made it hard to tell whether the squalor covered vice or highest virtue. But at Schinner’s age the heart’s first impulse is to believe in goodness. As he studied Adelaide’s noble, almost haughty brow and soulful eyes, he breathed the modest fragrance of virtue. He seized an opportunity to discuss portraits and thus examine the faded pastel whose chalk had fallen away.

“You are attached to that picture for the sake of the likeness, no doubt, mesdames, for the drawing is dreadful?” he said, looking at Adelaide.

“It was done at Calcutta, in great haste,” replied the mother, her voice agitated.

She gazed at the formless sketch with the absorbed melancholy of someone recalling happier days; in her face were traces of perennial regret. The painter interpreted her attitude as the mark of a once-happy life. He then sat down beside her.

“Madame,” he said, “in a very short time the colors of that pastel will disappear. The portrait will only survive in your memory. Will you allow me to reproduce the likeness on canvas? It will be more permanently recorded. Grant me, as a neighborly favor, the pleasure of doing you this service. There are times when an artist is glad of a respite from greater undertakings by doing work of less lofty pretension; it will be a recreation for me.”

The old lady flushed at the offer, and Adelaide shot a glance of deep feeling. Hippolyte wanted some tie to his neighbors, to gain a right to mingle in their life. His offer appealed to the heart’s affections, gratified his pride as an artist, and could not wound the ladies. Madame Leseigneur accepted, without eagerness or reluctance, with the self-possession of a noble soul, aware of the delicate nature of such an obligation.

“I fancy,” said the painter, “that the uniform is that of a naval officer.”

“Yes,” she said, “that of a captain in command. Monsieur de Rouville—my husband—died at Batavia from a wound received in a fight with an English ship off the Asiatic coast. He commanded a frigate of fifty-six guns; the Revenge carried ninety-six. The struggle was unequal, but he defended his ship bravely, held out till nightfall and got away. When I returned to France Bonaparte was not yet in power, and I was refused a pension. When I applied again, quite lately, I was sternly informed that if the Baron de Rouville had emigrated I should not have lost him; that by this time he would have been a rear-admiral; finally, his Excellency quoted degrees of forfeiture. I took this step, urged by friends, only for Adelaide’s sake. I have always hated holding out my hand as a beggar in the name of a grief that deprives a woman of voice and strength. I do not like this money valuation for blood irreparably spilt—”

“Dear mother, this subject always does you harm.”

The Baronne Leseigneur bowed and was silent.

“Monsieur,” said the young girl, “I had supposed a painter’s work was generally fairly quiet?”

Schinner colored, remembering the noise he had made. Adelaide rose at the sound of a carriage stopping at the door. She went into her own room and returned with a pair of tall gilt candlesticks with partly burnt wax candles, which she quickly lit. Without waiting for the bell she opened the outer door where she set the lamp down. The sound of a kiss given and received touched Hippolyte’s heart. His impatience to see the man who treated Adelaide familiarly was not immediately gratified; the newcomers conversed in undertone with the young girl.

At last Mademoiselle de Rouville returned, followed by two men whose costumes and countenances needed a long tale. The first, about sixty, wore one of those coats fashioned for Louis XVIII.: neither civil nor military, it might pass for either. Fleurs-de-lis embroidered the lapels and gilt buttons bore the same. Shoulder straps begged for useless epaulettes; a buttonhole blossomed with many colored ribbons. He probably always carried his three-cornered hat with a gold cord, for his powdered hair showed no traces of pressure. He might be taken for fifty and seemed robust. His frank, loyal emigre expression hinted also at libertine habits, the reckless gallantry of the Musketeers. His gestures and manner showed no intention of renouncing royalism, religion, or love affairs.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
A fantastic figure stood behind this specimen of “Louis XIV.’s light infantry”—the Bonapartists’ nickname for these venerable Monarchy survivors. He ought to have been the picture’s principal subject, but served only as an accessory. A lean, dry man dressed like the first, seeming his reflection or shadow: his coat older, his powder less white, the fleurs-de-lis dimmer, his shoulder-straps more hopeless and dog’s-eared; his intellect feebler, his life nearer its end. He realized Rivarol’s quip about Champcenetz, “He is the moonlight of me.” He was simply a paler double, the difference between a first and last impression of a lithograph.

The painter found this speechless old Chevalier a mystery. He did not speak; nobody spoke to him. Was he a friend, a poor relation, a companion who followed at the old gallant’s heels? Did he fill a place between dog, parrot, and friend? Had he saved his patron’s fortune, or only his life? Was he the Trim to a Captain Toby? At the Baronne de Rouville’s he provoked curiosity without satisfying it. Who after the Restoration could recall an attachment that, before the Revolution, had bound him to his friend’s wife, dead twenty years?

The leader, least dilapidated of these wrecks, gallantly kissed Madame de Rouville’s hand and sat down by her. The Chevalier bowed and placed himself a couple of chairs away from his model. Adelaide stood behind the old gentleman’s armchair, leaning her elbows on the back in an attitude unconsciously like Guerin’s depiction of Dido’s sister.

Though the gentleman’s familiarity was fatherly, his freedom seemed to annoy the girl. “What, are you sulky with me?” he said, then shot at Schinner one of those shrewd, diplomatic side-looks that ask, when they see a stranger, “Is he one of us?”

“This is our neighbor,” said the Baronne, pointing to Hippolyte. “Monsieur is a celebrated painter; his name must be known to you despite your indifference to the arts.”

The old man saw the intent in suppressing the name and bowed to the young man. “Certainly,” he said. “I heard much about his pictures at the last Salon. Talent has immense privileges,” he added, observing the artist’s red ribbon. “That distinction, which we must earn at the cost of our blood and long service, you win in your youth; but all glory is of the same kindred,” he said, laying his hand on his Cross of Saint-Louis.

Hippolyte murmured thanks and fell silent, satisfied to admire the beautiful girl’s head. He soon forgot the extreme misery of the dwelling; Adelaide’s face stood out against a luminous atmosphere. He answered questions briefly, his attention divided by that inward double consciousness when soul and ear attend different things. Hope poured out before him a thousand visions of happiness; he refused to analyze the evening’s incidents.

After a while the Baroness and her daughter began to play cards with the old gentleman. The Chevalier, faithful to his shadow role, stood behind his friend’s chair watching the game, answering mute inquiries with approving nods and repetition of gestures.

“Du Halga, I always lose,” said the gentleman.

“You discard badly,” replied the Baronne.

“For three months now I have never won a single game,” he said.

“Have you the aces?” asked the old lady.

“Yes, one more to mark,” he answered.

“Shall I come and advise you?” said Adelaide.

“No, no. Stay where I can see you. By Gad, it would be losing too much not to have you to look at!”

At last the game was over. The gentleman pulled out his purse and, throwing two louis-d’or on the table, not without temper, “Forty francs,” he exclaimed, “the exact sum.—Deuce take it! It is eleven o’clock.”

“It is eleven o’clock,” repeated the silent figure, looking at the painter.

Hippolyte, hearing this more distinctly, thought it time to retire. He found a few commonplace remarks to make, took leave of the Baroness, her daughter, and the two strangers, and went away wholly possessed by the first raptures of true love, without attempting to analyze the evening’s little incidents.

The next day the painter felt an ardent desire to see Adelaide again. He might have presented himself at six when he went to his studio, but waited until the afternoon. Blushing like a girl, he shyly asked Mademoiselle Leseigneur, who came to let him in, for the portrait of the Baron.

“But come in,” said Adelaide, who had no doubt heard him come down from the studio.

The painter entered, tongue-tied; happiness dulled his wit. To see Adelaide, hear the rustle of her skirt after longing all morning to be near her, was an intensity that made trifles precious. The old lady was not in the drawing-room. Adelaide brought a chair to stand on to take down the picture; finding she could not reach it without stepping on a chest of drawers, she turned to Hippolyte and, blushing, said, “I am not tall enough. Will you get it down?”

Her modesty was the true motive; he read this and returned one of those looks of intelligence that are the sweetest language of love. Adelaide cast down her eyes with maidenly reserve. Hippolyte, feeling almost timid, took down the picture, examined it in the window light, and then went away saying only, “I will return it soon.”

During that brief moment both felt a storm of agitation, as if a stone flung into a deep lake had set in motion long ripples in their hearts.

Hippolyte returned to his studio with the portrait. His easel awaited a fresh canvas; his palette set, brushes cleaned, the light chosen. He worked with the ardor artists throw into their whims until dinner, then returned that evening to the Baronne’s and stayed from nine till eleven. The same topics, the same arrivals, the same game, the same loss by the old man; only Hippolyte, a little bolder, ventured to chat with the young girl.

A week passed, and their feelings underwent the slow, delightful transformations that bring two souls to understanding. Every day Adelaide’s greeting grew more intimate, her voice more eager, her manner more familiar. They laughed and talked with the simplicity of children newly made friends, as if they had known one another for years. Schinner wished to be taught piquet; being a novice, he blundered and lost almost every game. Without a word of love the lovers knew they were everything to each other. Hippolyte enjoyed his power over his gentle little friend; Adelaide, timid and devoted, allowed many concessions, playing small caprices like a spoilt child. Familiarity with the old Count soon stopped: she perceived the painter’s melancholy from the abrupt tone of his words when the old man unceremoniously kissed her hands or throat.

Mademoiselle Leseigneur began to expect an account of the painter’s actions; she scolded him so effectually for absences that he gave up other friends and went nowhere else. Adelaide’s jealousy showed when she heard that sometimes at eleven he still had visits to pay in Paris’s brilliant drawing-rooms. She declared such a life bad for his health and argued passionately that a man who expended time and charms on several women at once could not love warmly. So the painter, driven by passion and the exactions of a girl in love, lived exclusively in the little apartment that enchanted him.

Never was there a purer or more ardent love. Both trusted and were delicate; their passion grew without the need for sacrifices. A spontaneous affinity made their union close. Two months after their first meeting their lives had become one: in the morning Adelaide could tell by footsteps that “He is there.” When Hippolyte went home to his mother at dinner he always looked in on his neighbors; in the evening he flew back with lover’s punctuality. Adelaide enjoyed the realization of the ideal she naturally dreamt of.

The old gentleman came less often; Hippolyte, who had been jealous, had taken his place at the green table and shared his constant ill-luck. Yet sometimes, amid his happiness, thought of Madame de Rouville’s desperate poverty haunted him. “Strange! twenty francs every evening?” he asked himself, and dared not confess his odious suspicions.

He spent two months on the portrait; when finished, varnished, and framed, he deemed it one of his best works. Madame de Rouville had not spoken of it again. Was this indifference or pride? He would not account for the silence. Joyfully plotting with Adelaide, he planned to hang it when the Baroness was out. One day, while her mother walked in the Tuileries, Adelaide went up to Hippolyte’s studio to see the portrait in its light. She stood speechless in ecstatic contemplation, all a woman’s feeling merged in admiration for the man she loved. The painter, uneasy, leaned forward; she held out her hand, unable to speak, and two tears fell. Hippolyte kissed her hand; for a minute they stood mute, longing to confess their love and not daring. The painter kept her hand in his; their hearts beat wildly. Overcome, Adelaide drew away and said simply, “You will make my mother very happy.”

“What, only your mother?” he asked.

“Oh, I am too happy.”

Frightened by the vehemence stirred in his heart, the painter bent his head. Both, sensing danger, went downstairs and hung the picture in its place. Hippolyte dined for the first time with the Baroness, who, overcome and in tears, embraced him.

That evening the old emigre, the Baron de Rouville’s comrade, paid a visit to announce he had just been promoted to vice-admiral; his land travels through Germany and Russia had been counted as naval campaigns. Seeing the portrait, he shook the painter’s hand and exclaimed, “By Gad! though my old hulk does not deserve to be perpetuated, I would gladly give five hundred pistoles to see myself as like as that is to my dear old Rouville.”

At this hint the Baroness looked at her young friend and smiled with sudden gratitude. Hippolyte suspected the admiral meant to offer the price for both portraits while paying for his own. His pride as an artist, and his jealousy, took offense; he replied, “Monsieur, if I were a portrait-painter I should not have done this one.” The admiral bit his lip and sat down to cards.

The painter stayed near Adelaide, who proposed a dozen hands of piquet, to which he agreed. As he played he noticed an unexpected eagerness in Madame de Rouville to win and finger the old gentleman’s gold. Evil suspicions troubled Hippolyte. Could the Baroness be living by gambling? Was she playing to pay a debt, to meet the rent? Why would this rich old man frequent a fourth-story room where he lost about forty francs each evening? Why had he been so familiar with Adelaide and then given up the rights he had acquired?

These reflections led him to watch the old man and the Baroness more closely. Their sidelong glances at Adelaide displeased him. “Am I being duped?” was his last, horrible thought. He resolved to stay after the departure of the two old men to confirm or dispel his suspicions. He drew out his purse to pay and, carried away by thought, laid it on the table and fell into a brief reverie; then, ashamed of his silence, he rose, answered some commonplace question, and went close to the Baroness to examine her withered features as she talked.

He left, racked by doubts. A few steps down, he turned back to fetch the forgotten purse. “I left my purse here!” he said to Adelaide.

“No,” she answered, reddening.

“I thought it was there,” he said, pointing to the card-table. Not finding it, shamed for Adelaide and the Baroness, he looked at them with blank amazement that made them laugh; he checked himself, felt his waistcoat, and said, “I must have made a mistake. I have it somewhere no doubt.”

In the purse were fifteen louis d’or and some small change. The theft seemed flagrant and denied with effrontery; Hippolyte no longer felt doubt about his neighbors’ morals. He stood on the stairs, legs shaking, dizzy, in a cold sweat, unable to walk, struggling with the shock of his destroyed hopes. He recalled various trifling observations that now corroborated his frightful suspicions and opened his eyes to the character and life of these two women.

Had they waited until the portrait was given them before robbing him? If so, the theft was more odious. He remembered Adelaide’s seeming curiosity at the worn silk netting—might she have been counting coins in his purse while making innocent jests to watch for a moment to steal? He briefly fancied other motives—perhaps the admiral hesitated to marry Adelaide—but checked himself with the thought that if the Baroness hoped to make him marry her daughter, they would not have robbed him.

Clinging to his illusion and the love already rooted, he tried to find an accident: “The purse must have fallen on the floor,” he told himself. He searched himself hastily but did not find it. His memory retraced the truth: he distinctly saw the purse on the green cloth. Even so, he excused Adelaide, thinking misfortune might explain a degrading action; there must be some secret. He refused to admit that that proud face could be a lie.

The next morning he had not slept. The heartache that follows the loss of unrealized bliss made rapid inroads. To lose the future we dreamed of, to renounce it, is a keener pang than losing known happiness. Hope can be better than memory. His spirit was plunged into a shoreless sea; such partial death can lead, in delicate natures, to ruin. Thus Hippolyte wandered under the Tuileries’ fresh shade, absorbed and forgetful.

There he met Francois Souchet, a school-fellow and studio-mate about to set out for Italy. “Why, Hippolyte, what ails you?” asked the sculptor.

“I am most unhappy,” replied Hippolyte gravely.

“Nothing but a love affair can cause you grief. Money, glory, respect—you lack nothing.”

Led imperceptibly into confidences, Hippolyte confessed his love. When Souchet heard “Rue de Suresnes, a young girl on the fourth floor,” he laughed and began to recall what all the young men knew: the mother’s appearance and the air that betrayed her station. “Do you not know her by the way she holds her bag?” he said. Several acquaintances joined; jokes and mockery, lively but painful, followed. Hippolyte’s reticence made him suffer as his heart’s secret was handled so carelessly. He pretended contradiction and asked for proofs, but the jesting resumed. “Come at four o’clock and study their walk,” Souchet advised. Torn by conflicting feelings, Hippolyte parted from his friends, still convinced Adelaide must be superior to such accusations and filled with remorse for suspecting her. He passed the door of their rooms, feeling a pain none could misread. His love was such that, despite the theft, he still worshiped her. He thought of playing the good genius who rescues a woman whose love others have abandoned—romantic and grand to a highly-strung soul.

He sat in his studio, gazed at his picture without working, tears in his eyes, brush in hand but not touching. Night fell; roused by darkness, he went downstairs, met the old admiral, looked darkly at him as he bowed, and fled. He had planned to see the ladies, but the admiral’s presence froze him. For the hundredth time he wondered what interest brought this prodigal, with eighty thousand francs a year, to a fourth story where he lost forty francs every evening; he imagined motives.

In the next days Hippolyte threw himself into his work to conquer passion by idea and composition. Study consoled him, though memories of tender hours with Adelaide persisted. One evening, leaving his studio, he saw the door of the ladies’ rooms half open and Adelaide standing in the window recess. The door and staircase made it impossible not to be seen. He bowed coldly, with a glance of supreme indifference; yet thinking of the pain such coldness would inflict, he shuddered inwardly. That week of disdain would have been terrible; perhaps the purse had been found and she had sought him nightly. This thought filled him with remorse; he asked whether the signs of attachment Adelaide showed did not deserve inquiry and at least justification. Ashamed of resisting his heart for a week, he called that evening on Madame de Rouville.

All his suspicions vanished at the sight of Adelaide, pale and thin. “Good heavens! what is the matter?” he asked, after greeting the Baroness.

Adelaide made no reply, but gave him a look of deep melancholy that pained him. “You must have thought us indifferent?” said the Baroness.

“I was wrong,” he replied. “There are forms of pain we know not how to confide to any one.”

“The sincerity and strength of friendship are not to be measured by time,” the Baroness observed sadly.

“What ails you?” he asked Adelaide.

“Oh, nothing,” said the Baroness, explaining Adelaide had sat up late finishing some woman’s work and would not listen when told a day more or less did not matter.

Hippolyte, blushing with shame at his suspicions, ascribed the loss of his purse to some accident. The evening was delicious to both. Adelaide could read his thoughts; though he could not confess his wrongs, he had come back more in love and affectionate, seeking tacit forgiveness. Yet at Madame de Rouville’s suggestion to play—“Let us have our little game; my old friend Kergarouet will not let me off”—his fears revived. He sat at the card-table; Adelaide said he needed a partner because he did not know piquet.

Throughout the game the Baroness and her daughter exchanged looks of intelligence, alarming Hippolyte more because he was winning. At last a final hand left the lovers in the old lady’s debt. Reaching for some money he saw before him a purse Adelaide had slipped in front of him without his noticing it; the poor child held the old one in her hand and was, to keep her countenance, looking into it for the money to pay her mother. The sight made the blood rush to his heart; he was near fainting.

The substituted purse contained his fifteen gold louis. It was worked with gilt beads; rings and tassels betrayed Adelaide’s taste and the small hoard she had spent on it. It was an impossible, delicate way to show the painter that his gift could only be repaid by some proof of affection.

Overcome, Hippolyte turned to Adelaide and her mother, saw their trembling pleasure, felt mean and foolish; tears rose. He sprang up, clasped Adelaide, pressed her to his heart, and stole a kiss; then, with the simple heartiness of an artist, “I ask for her for my wife!” he exclaimed, looking to the Baroness.

Adelaide looked half-wrathful, and Madame de Rouville, somewhat astonished, considered her reply when a bell rang. The old vice-admiral entered, followed by his shadow and Madame Schinner. Alarmed by calumnies against Adelaide, Hippolyte’s mother had inquired and reported them to the admiral, who declared he would crop all the scoundrels’ ears for them.

Prompted by his wrath, he told Madame Schinner his secret of losing intentionally at cards, because the Baroness’s pride left him no means but these ingenious ones to help her.

After Madame Schinner had paid respects to Madame de Rouville, the Baroness looked at the Comte de Kergarouet, the Chevalier du Halga—friend of the departed Comtesse de Kergarouet—Hippolyte, and Adelaide, and said with a gracious, heartfelt tone, “So we are a family party this evening.”

PARIS, May 1832

Produced by John Bickers, and Dagny

PIERRETTE

By Honore De Balzac

Translated by Katharine Prescott Wormeley

                             DEDICATION

  To Mademoiselle Anna Hanska:

  Dear Child,--You, the joy of the household, you, whose pink or
  white pelerine flutters in summer among the groves of
  Wierzschovnia like a will-o’-the-wisp, followed by the tender eyes
  of your father and your mother,--how can I dedicate to _you_ a
  story full of melancholy? And yet, ought not sorrows to be spoken
  of to a young girl idolized as you are, since the day may come
  when your sweet hands will be called to minister to them? It is
  difficult, Anna, to find in the history of our manners and morals
  a subject that is worthy of your eyes, that no choice has been
  left me; but perhaps you will be made to feel how fortunate your
  fate is when you read the story sent to you by
                                                    Your old friend,
                                                         De Balzac.

PIERRETTE

I. THE LORRAINS

At dawn one October day in 1827 a sixteen-year-old youth, plainly dressed, stood in a small square of Lower Provins. The mills along the river already whirred; their wheels, echoed from the Upper Town, deepened the morning silence so that even a distant diligence seemed near. The two long sides of the oblong square, separated by a linden avenue, bore the simple fronts of bourgeois life—few porte-cochères, little commerce; vines and roses climbed to second-story windows, scenting the air. One end opened on the main street, gardens touching the river; the other opened onto a street parallel to it.

At that quieter end the lad recognized the house he sought: a restored front of white grooved stone, closed gray blinds, slender iron balconies with yellow rosettes. Three dormer windows pierced the slate roof, the central one capped with a weather-vane of a hunter shooting a hare. Three stone steps led to the door; a leaden pipe poured sink-water into a gutter on one side, showing the kitchen; on the other, two closely shuttered windows with heart-shaped openings admitted light to the dining room. Vent-holes to the cellar had painted iron shutters cut in open-work. Everything looked new—too neat—suggesting the paltry taste and self-satisfaction of a retired petty shopkeeper.

The youth took pleasure tinged with sadness in noting these details; his eyes roved from the kitchen to the roof. A calico curtain at one garret window caught the rosy glow of the rising sun; the other attics lacked that sign of comfort, and his face brightened. He stepped back, leaned against a linden, and began to sing in the drawling tone of western France a Breton ditty often sung to newly married couples:

  “We’ve come to wish you happiness in marriage,
    To m’sieur your husband
    As well as to you:

  “You have just been bound, madam’ la mariee,
    With bonds of gold
    That only death unbinds:

  “You will go no more to balls or gay assemblies;
    You must stay at home
    While we shall go.

  “Have you thought well how you are pledged to be
    True to your spouse,
    And love him like yourself?

  “Receive these flowers our hands do now present you;
    Alas! your fleeting honors
    Will fade as they.”

This native air, tender and melancholy, would awaken a Breton maiden’s memories of her noble old land. As he finished the first couplet, the singer, never taking his eyes from the attic curtain, saw no sign of life. While he sang the second, the curtain stirred. At “Receive these flowers,” a face appeared; a white hand cautiously opened the casement, and a girl inclined her head as he closed with the melancholy thought, “Alas! your fleeting honors will fade as they.”

To her the young workman produced a yellow flower—furze, common in Brittany and sometimes found in La Brie.

“Is it really you, Brigaut?” she asked in a low voice.

“Yes, Pierrette, yes. I am in Paris. I have started to make my way; but I’m ready to settle here, near you.”

A first-floor window beneath Pierrette’s garret creaked. The girl showed terror and whispered: “Run away!”

The lad sprang like a frightened frog to a bend in the street near a projecting mill; but his hob-nailed shoes clattered on the stones, distinct from the mill’s music, and no doubt heard by the person who opened the window.

That person was an old maid. No man would have torn himself from a morning nap to hear a minstrel in a jacket; only a maid wakes to songs of love. She opened her blinds with the furtive motion of a bat, peered about, saw nothing, and only caught the sound of retreating footsteps. Few sights are more dreadful than the matutinal apparition of an ugly old maid at a window. Deprived of her public embellishments, her night-gown and black silk cap revealed a harsh, almost witch-like countenance—wrinkled temples and nape, a breast left exposed by a gaping garment, a long angular face whose lack of harmony suggested an unfeeling spirit. Usually softened by a commercial smirk in public, here she showed a keen, watchful severity. She owned the house with her brother, who slept so soundly that nothing could rouse him.

The old maid stretched her neck, raised pale blue eyes with short lashes toward the attic, tried to glimpse Pierrette, and, finding it useless, drew in like a tortoise and closed the blinds. Street silence resumed, broken only by peasants entering town or the rare early riser.

When there is an old maid in a house, watch-dogs are needless; not the slightest event escapes her. This trifling incident would thus awaken grave suppositions and set in motion one of those obscure family dramas—none the less terrible because secret.

Pierrette did not go back to bed. Brigaut’s arrival was an immense event to her. Night had been her only refuge from daily vexations and fault-findings; sleep seemed her paradise, waking her torment. Brigaut’s song had first stirred childhood memories, then, as she sprang from bed and reached the casement, recognition dispelled doubt. She saw the square Breton jacket with bobtails, printed cotton waistcoat, linen shirt fastened by a gold heart, the large collar, earrings, stout shoes, uneven blue-gray trousers—all the humble durable garments of Breton peasantry. The white-horn buttons made her heart beat. The broom filled her eyes with tears; then terror: perhaps her cousin sleeping below had heard her. The fear was justified; the old maid had appeared, and Pierrette made Brigaut a terrified sign which he obeyed without understanding—an ingenuous, absolute devotion that reveals rare childhood affection. Whoever saw him run would have loved the chivalry of his feeling.

Jacques Brigaut was worthy of Pierrette Lorrain, who was just fifteen. Two children, Pierrette could not help crying as she watched him flee. Then she sat in a shabby armchair before a small table topped by a mirror. Resting her elbows, head in hands, she thought for an hour, recalling the Marais, the village of Pen-Hoel, perilous pond voyages in a boat untied from an old willow by little Jacques; the faces of her grandparents and mother’s sufferings; Major Brigaut’s handsome face—her carefree childhood seemed a luminous joy against the gloomy present.

Her chestnut hair escaped in disorder from a cambric cap she had made; little ringlets loosened from gray curl-papers at each temple, a heavy braid hung half unplaited. Her whiteness betrayed the malady of girlhood—chlorosis—draining color, destroying appetite, disorganizing the body. Waxy tones marked her flesh; neck and shoulders showed wasted arms, flung forward and crossed upon the table. Bare feet and a night-gown barely reaching her knees revealed enervated legs, blue veins, and flaccid muscles. Cold, which she heeded not, turned her lips violet; a sad smile drew up the corners of a sensitive mouth with small ivory teeth. Despite the pallor, she was lovely: roundness of face, vivacious brown eyes with black flecks and golden reflections, brilliant pupils full of an eager, melancholy intelligence. Pierrette was made for gaiety but was sad. Her long eyelashes lay upon cheek-bones sharpened by suffering; paleness made her features finely distinct. The ear alone was a little masterpiece of modeling.

Her history explains how she came to this. Pierrette’s mother was a Demoiselle Auffray of Provins, half-sister by her father to Madame Rogron, mother of the present owners of the square house.
