Châtelet knew a visit at that hour meant only one thing: old M. de Bargeton was in bed. It was evidently Naïs who had set the feeble arm in motion. Châtelet, intimate in the house, took M. de Bargeton aside.

“Do you wish to speak to Stanislas?”

“Yes,” replied the old man, pleased to find a go‑between.

“Very well; go into Amélie’s bedroom,” said the controller of excise, pleased at the prospect of a duel that might make Mme. de Bargeton a widow and put a barrier between her and Lucien. Châtelet then went to M. de Chandour.

“Stanislas,” he said, “here comes Bargeton to call you to account, no doubt, for the things you have been saying about Naïs. Go into your wife’s room, and behave, both of you, like gentlemen. Keep it quiet and make a great show of politeness—behave with phlegmatic British dignity.”

In another minute Stanislas and Châtelet went to Bargeton.

“Sir,” said the injured husband, “do you say that you discovered Mme. de Bargeton and M. de Rubempré in an equivocal position?”

“M. Chardon,” corrected Stanislas with ironic emphasis; he did not take Bargeton seriously.

“So be it,” answered the other. “If you do not withdraw your assertions at once before the company now in your house, I must ask you to look for a second. My father‑in‑law, M. de Nègrepelisse, will wait upon you at four o’clock tomorrow morning. Both of us may as well make our final arrangements, for the only way out is the one I have indicated. I choose pistols, as the insulted party.”

The speech, long rehearsed, came out without excitement. Stanislas turned pale. “After all, what did I see?” he wondered.

Put between the shame of recanting and the mute terror this man inspired, Stanislas chose the more remote peril.

“All right. Tomorrow morning,” he said, thinking the matter might be arranged somehow.

They rejoined the room. Everybody scanned their faces; Châtelet smiled, M. de Bargeton looked in his element, but Stanislas looked ghastly. Some women guessed the conference’s nature, and the whisper, “They are going to fight!” circulated. Half the room thought Stanislas in the wrong—his white face and demeanor convicted him of lying; the other half admired Bargeton. Châtelet was solemn and mysterious. Bargeton stayed a few minutes, scrutinized faces, and retired.

“Have you pistols?” Châtelet asked Stanislas in a whisper; Stanislas shook from head to foot.

Amélie knew what it meant. She felt ill, and the women took her into her bedroom. There was a sensation; everybody talked at once. The men stopped in the drawing‑room, declaring with one voice that M. de Bargeton was within his right.

“Would you have thought the old fogy capable of acting like this?” asked M. de Saintot.

“But he was a crack shot when young,” said the pitiless Jacques. “My father often told me of Bargeton’s exploits.”

“Pooh! Put them at twenty paces, and they will miss each other if you give them cavalry pistols,” said Francis, addressing Châtelet.

Châtelet stayed after the rest had gone to reassure Stanislas and his wife, and to explain that all would go off well. In a duel between a man of sixty and a man of thirty‑five, all advantage lay with the latter.

Early next morning, as Lucien sat at breakfast with David, who had come back alone from Marsac, in came Mme. Chardon with a scared face.

“Well, Lucien,” she said, “have you heard the news? Everyone is talking of it, even the market. M. de Bargeton all but killed M. de Chandour this morning in M. Tulloy’s meadow; people are making puns on the name. It seems M. de Chandour said he found you with Mme. de Bargeton yesterday.”

“It is a lie! Mme. de Bargeton is innocent,” cried Lucien.

“I heard about the duel from a countryman who saw it from his cart. M. de Nègrepelisse came at three in the morning to be Bargeton’s second; he told Chandour that if anything happened to his son‑in‑law he would avenge him. A cavalry officer lent the pistols. Nègrepelisse tried them repeatedly. Du Châtelet tried to prevent practice, but they referred to the officer; he said that, unless they meant to behave like children, they ought to have working pistols. The seconds put them at twenty‑five paces. Bargeton looked as if out for a walk. He fired first; the ball lodged in Chandour’s neck, and he dropped before he could return the shot. The house‑surgeon says Chandour will have a wry neck for the rest of his days. I came to tell you how it ended, lest you go to Mme. de Bargeton’s or show yourself in Angoulême, for some of Chandour’s friends might call you out.”

As she spoke, the apprentice brought in Gentil, Bargeton’s footman. He had a note for Lucien from Louise.

“You have doubtless heard the news,” she wrote, “of the duel between Chandour and my husband. We shall not be at home today. Be careful; do not show yourself. I ask this in the name of the affection you bear me. Would it not be best to spend this melancholy day listening to your Beatrice, whose whole life has been changed by this event, who has a thousand things to say to you?”

“Luckily, my marriage is fixed for the day after tomorrow,” said David, “and you will have an excuse for not going to Mme. de Bargeton’s so often.”

“Dear David,” returned Lucien, “she asks me to go today; I ought to do as she wishes; she knows better than we do how I should act now.”

“Then is everything ready here?” asked Mme. Chardon.

“Come and see,” cried David, delighted to exhibit the transformation of the first floor. Everything was new and fresh, pervaded by the sweet influences of early married days, still crowned by the wreath of orange blossoms and the bridal veil; days when love’s springtide finds expression in material things, and everything is white and spotless.

“Eve’s home will be fit for a princess,” said the mother, “but you have spent too much, you have been reckless.”

David smiled, but Mme. Chardon had touched a sore spot. The cost far exceeded his estimates; he could not afford to build above the shed. His mother‑in‑law must wait awhile for the home he meant. Nothing is more painful to a generous nature than failing to keep such promises; it mortified the little vanities of affection. David hid his embarrassment to spare Lucien; he feared Lucien might be overwhelmed by the sacrifices made for his sake.

“Eve and her girl friends have been working hard,” said Mme. Chardon. “The wedding clothes and house linen are ready. The girls, without letting her know, covered the mattresses with white twill and rose‑colored piping. So pretty! It makes one wish one were going to be married.”

Mother and daughter had spent all their savings to furnish David’s home with things a young bachelor never thinks of. They knew he was furnishing splendidly; something had been said about ordering a Limoges dinner‑service, and the two women strove to make Eve’s contributions worthy of David. This emulation in love and generosity would bring husband and wife into difficulties at the outset of married life, yet everything looked homely and comfortable, positive luxury in a town so behind the times as Angoulême then was.

As soon as Lucien saw his mother and David enter the bedroom with blue‑and‑white draperies and neat furniture, he slipped away to Mme. de Bargeton. He found Naïs at table with her husband; Bargeton’s early morning walk had sharpened his appetite, and he breakfasted quite unconcernedly after all that had passed. Lucien saw the dignified face of M. de Nègrepelisse, the old provincial noble, a relic of the old French noblesse, sitting beside Naïs.

When Gentil announced M. de Rubempré, the white‑headed old man gave him a keen, curious glance; the father was anxious to form his opinion of the man his daughter singled out. Lucien’s beauty impressed him; he could not repress an approving glance, but seemed to regard the affair as a flirtation, a mere passing fancy. Breakfast over, Louise left her father and Bargeton together; she beckoned Lucien to follow.

“Dear,” she said, half glad, half melancholy, “I am going to Paris, and my father is taking Bargeton back with him to the Escarbas, where he will stay during my absence. Mme. d’Espard (a Blamont‑Chauvry) has great influence and influential relations. The d’Espards are connections of ours; if she acknowledges the relationship, I shall cultivate her; she may procure a place for Bargeton. At my solicitation, it might be desired at Court that he represent the Charente, a step toward his election here. If he were a deputy, it would further other steps I wish to take in Paris. You, my darling, have brought about this change in my life. After this morning’s duel, I must shut up my house for some time; people will side with the Chandours against us. In our position, in a small town, absence is the only way to soften bad feeling. But I shall either succeed and never see Angoulême again, or I shall not succeed and then wait in Paris until I can spend summers at the Escarbas and winters in Paris. It is the only life for a woman of quality; I have waited too long. One day will suffice for our preparations; tomorrow night I set out. You are coming with me, are you not? You shall start first. I will overtake you between Mansle and Ruffec, and we shall soon be in Paris. There is the life for a man with anything in him. We are only at our ease among equals; we are uncomfortable in other society. Paris, besides, is the capital of the intellectual world, the stage on which you will succeed; overleap the gulf that separates us quickly. You must not let your ideas grow rancid in the provinces; put yourself into communication at once with the great men of the nineteenth century. Try to stand well with the Court and those in power. No honor, no distinction, comes to talent that perishes for lack of light in a little town; tell me any great work executed in the provinces! See how Jean‑Jacques, sublime in his poverty, felt the irresistible attraction of that sun of the intellectual world which produces ever‑new glories and stimulates intellect—Paris, where men rub against one another. What is it but your duty to take your place in the succession of pléiades that rise generation after generation? You have no idea how much being brought into a high social light helps a clever young man. I will introduce you to Mme. d’Espard; it’s not easy to get into her set; but you meet all the greatest people at her house—cabinet ministers and ambassadors, great orators, peers and men of influence, wealthy or famous people. A young man with looks and more than sufficient genius could fail to excite interest only by very bad management.

“There is no pettiness about those who are truly great; they will support you; and when you have a high position, your work will rise in public opinion. The great problem for the artist is putting himself in evidence. There will be hundreds of chances of making your way, sinecures, a pension from the civil list. The Bourbons are fond of encouraging letters and the arts; you must be a religious poet and a Royalist poet at the same time. Not only is it the right course, but it is the way to get on. Do the Liberals give places and rewards and make the fortunes of men of letters? Take the right road and reach the goal of genius. You have my secret; do not breathe it, and prepare to follow me. Would you rather not go?” she added, surprised that he made no answer.

To Lucien, listening to these alluring words and bewildered by the rapid bird’s‑eye view of Paris, it seemed as if he had been using only half his brain and suddenly found the other half; his ideas widened. He saw himself stagnating in Angoulême like a frog under a stone. Paris and her splendors rose before him: the Eldorado of provincial imaginings, with golden robes and the royal diadem, arms outstretched to talent. Great men would greet him there. Everything smiled upon genius. There were no jealous booby‑squires to invent gibes and humiliate a man of letters; no stupid indifference to poetry. Paris was the fountain‑head of poetry; there the poet was brought into the light and paid. Publishers should no sooner read the opening pages of An Archer of Charles IX than open their cash‑boxes with “How much do you want?” And besides all this, he understood that this journey with Mme. de Bargeton would virtually give her to him; that they should live together.

At “Would you rather not go?” tears came to his eyes; he flung his arms about Louise, held her tightly, and kissed her passionately. Suddenly he checked himself, as if memory had struck.

“Great heavens!” he cried, “my sister is to be married the day after tomorrow!”

That exclamation was the last expiring cry of noble and single‑hearted boyhood. The strong ties that bind young hearts to home and early friendship were to be severed.

“Well,” cried the haughty Nègrepelisse, “and what has your sister’s marriage to do with the progress of our love? Have you set your mind so much on being best man at a wedding of tradespeople and workingmen that you cannot give up these exalted joys for my sake? A great sacrifice, indeed!” she went on, scornfully. “This morning I sent my husband out to fight in your quarrel. There, sir, go; I am mistaken in you.”

She sank fainting upon the sofa. Lucien went to her, entreating pardon, cursing his family, his sister, and David.

“I had such faith in you!” she said. “M. de Cante‑Croix had an adored mother; but to win a letter from me, and the words, ‘I am satisfied,’ he fell in the thick of the fight. And now, when I ask you to take a journey with me, you cannot think of giving up a wedding dinner for my sake.”

Lucien was ready to kill himself; his desperation was so unfeigned that Louise forgave him, though she made him feel he must redeem his mistake.

“Come, come,” she said, “be discreet, and to‑morrow at midnight be upon the road, a hundred paces out of Mansle.”

Lucien felt the globe shrink under his feet; he went back to David’s house, hopes pursuing him like Furies, for he had glimmerings of endless difficulties, summed up in the appalling words, “Where is the money to come from?”

He stood in such terror of David’s perspicacity that he locked himself into his pretty new study until he could recover; his head swam in this new position. So he must leave the rooms just furnished at such cost, and all the sacrifices had been made in vain. Then it occurred to him his mother might take the rooms and save David the heavy expense of building at the yard’s end; his departure would be a convenience. He found many urgent reasons for sudden flight; there is no such Jesuit as the desire of your heart. He hurried to tell his sister in L’Houmeau and take counsel. As he reached Postel’s shop, he bethought himself that if all else failed he could borrow enough to live on for a year from his father’s successor.

“Three francs per day will be abundance if I live with Louise,” he thought; “it is only a thousand francs for a year. And in six months’ time I shall have plenty of money.”

Under seal and promise of secrecy, Eve and her mother heard Lucien’s confidences. Both women began to cry as they heard his ambitious plans; when he asked why they were troubled, they said every penny had been spent on table‑linen, house‑linen, Eve’s wedding clothes, and many things David overlooked. They had been glad to do it, for David had made a marriage‑settlement of ten thousand francs on Eve. Lucien then spoke of a loan; Mme. Chardon undertook to ask M. Postel to lend a thousand francs for a year.

“But, Lucien,” said Eve, a thought clutching her heart, “you will not be here at my wedding! Oh! come back; I will put it off a few days. Surely she will give you leave to come back in a fortnight if you go now? Surely she would spare you a week when we brought you up for her? We shall have no luck if you are not at the wedding.... But will a thousand francs be enough?” she asked, interrupting. “Your coat suits you divinely, but you have only that one! Only two fine shirts; the other six are coarse; three of your white ties are common muslin, only two lawn cravats, your handkerchiefs not good. Where will you find a sister in Paris to get up your linen in one day? You will want much more. You have just one pair of new nankeen trousers; last year’s are tight; you will be obliged to have clothes made in Paris, and Paris prices are not Angoulême’s. You have only two presentable white waistcoats; I have mended the others already. Come, I advise you to take two thousand francs.”

David came in as she spoke and apparently heard the last two words; he looked at the brother and sister and said nothing.

“Do not keep anything from me,” he said at last.

“Well,” exclaimed Eve, “he is going away with her.”

Mme. Chardon came in again, and, not seeing David, began at once:

“Postel is willing to lend you the thousand francs, Lucien,” she said, “but only for six months; and he wants a bill endorsed by your brother‑in‑law, for he says you give him no security.”

She turned and saw David; there was a deep silence. The Chardons felt ashamed for having abused David’s goodness. Tears stood in the young printer’s eyes.

“Then you will not be here at our wedding,” he began. “You are not going to live with us! And here have I been squandering all that I had! Oh, Lucien, as I came along, bringing Eve her little bits of wedding jewelry, I did not think I should be sorry I spent the money on them.” He brushed his hand over his eyes as he drew the tiny morocco‑covered boxes from his pocket and set them on the table.

“Oh! why do you think so much for me?” protested Eve, giving him a divinely sweet smile that belied her words.

“Mamma, dear,” said David, “just tell M. Postel I will put my name to the bill, for I can tell from your face, Lucien, that you have quite made up your mind.”

Lucien’s head sank dejectedly; there was a pause, then he said, “Do not think hardly of me, my dear, good angels.”

He put his arms about Eve and David, drew them close, and held them tightly as he added, “Wait and see what comes of it, and you shall know how much I love you. What is the good of our high thinking, David, if it does not enable us to disregard petty ceremonial in which the law entangles our affections? Shall I not be with you in spirit despite distance? Shall we not be united in thought? Have I not a destiny to fulfil? Will publishers come here to seek my Archer of Charles IX and the Marguerites? A little sooner or later I shall be obliged to do as I am doing to‑day, should I not? And shall I ever find a better opportunity? Does not my success depend upon my entrance into life in Paris through the Marquise d’Espard’s salon?”

“He is right,” said Eve; “you yourself said he ought to go to Paris at once.”

David took Eve’s hand and led her into the narrow little room where she had slept for seven years.

“Love, you were saying just now that he would want two thousand francs?” he murmured in her ear. “Postel is only lending one thousand.”

Eve looked anguish at him; he read her meaning.

“Listen, my adored Eve, we are making a bad start. Yes, my expenses have taken all my capital; I have just two thousand francs left, and half will be needed to carry on the business. If we give your brother the thousand francs, it will mean giving away our bread; we shall live in anxiety. If I were alone, I know what I should do; but we are two. Decide for us.”

Eve, distraught, sprang to his arms and kissed him tenderly through tears: “Do as you would if you were alone; I will work to earn the money.”

Despite the most impassioned kiss ever given and taken by betrothed lovers, David left Eve overcome, and went out to Lucien.

“Do not worry,” he said; “you shall have your two thousand francs.”

“Go see Postel,” said Mme. Chardon, “for you must both sign the bill.”

When Lucien and David returned unexpectedly, they found Eve and her mother on their knees in prayer. The women felt sure Lucien’s return would bring realization of many hopes; at the moment they only felt how much they lost in his parting, and the coming days would be filled with fears for Lucien.

“If you could ever forget this sight,” David said in Lucien’s ear, “you would be the basest of men.”

David, no doubt, thought these brave words needed; Mme. de Bargeton’s influence seemed less to be feared than Lucien’s unlucky instability—he was so easily led. Eve soon packed Lucien’s clothes; the Fernando Cortez of literature carried but little baggage. He wore his best overcoat, his best waistcoat, and one of the two fine shirts. The whole of his linen, the celebrated coat, and his manuscript made a small package. To hide it from Mme. de Bargeton, David proposed to send it by coach to a paper merchant he dealt with and wrote asking him to keep the parcel until Lucien sent for it.

Despite Mme. de Bargeton’s precautions, Châtelet found she was leaving Angoulême; to discover whether she traveled alone or with Lucien, he sent his man to Ruffec to watch every carriage changing horses there.

“If she is taking her poet with her,” he thought, “I have her now.”

Lucien set out before daybreak the next morning. David went with him. David had hired a cabriolet, pretending he was going to Marsac on business, a deception that seemed probable. The two friends went to Marsac and spent part of the day with the old “bear.” As evening came they set out again, and at dawn waited on the road beyond Mansle for Mme. de Bargeton. When the seventy‑year‑old traveling carriage he had often seen in the coach‑house appeared, Lucien felt more deeply moved than ever; he sprang into David’s arms.

“God grant that this may be for your good!” said David; he climbed into the shabby cabriolet and drove away with dread clutching his heart; he had terrible presentiments of Lucien’s fate in Paris.

ADDENDUM

Note: Two Poets is part one of a trilogy. The second part is A Distinguished Provincial at Paris and details Lucien’s further adventures. Part three is titled Eve and David and continues their story. In other editions parts one and three are combined under the title Lost Illusions.

The following personages appear in other stories of the Human Comedy: Bargeton, Madame de; Cérizet; Chardon, Madame (née Rubempré); Châtelet, Sixte, Baron du; Châtelet, Marie‑Louise‑Anaïs de Nègrepelisse, Baronne du; Cointet, Boniface; Cointet, Jean; Courtois and Madame Courtois; Desplein; Gentil; Grozier, Abbé; Hautoy, Francis du; Maucombe, Comte de; Montriveau, General Marquis Armand de; Nègrepelisse, De; Petit‑Claud; Pimentel, Marquis and Marquise de; Postel; Prieur, Madame; Rastignac, Baron and Baronne de; Rastignac, Laure‑Rose and Agathe de; Rubempré, Lucien‑Chardon de; Séchard, Jérôme‑Nicolas; Séchard, David; Séchard, Madame David; Senonches, Jacques de and Madame Senonches; Stanhope, Lady Esther.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ### ##### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ##### ##### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #####
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

In late October 1800 a foreigner with a woman and a little girl stood for a long time before the Tuileries, near the ruins where the wing meant to join Catherine de’ Medici’s chateau to the Valois Louvre begins.

The man stood with folded arms and bowed head, glancing between the consular palace and his wife, who sat on a stone. The woman seemed occupied with the nine- or ten-year-old child whose long black hair she toyed with, but she watched every look her companion gave. Their bond was not mere love; misery—perhaps the strongest tie—drew their anxious attention.

The stranger had a broad, serious head, thick hair like those in Carracci paintings; the jet black was streaked with white. His noble, proud features were marred by a hardness. Though erect and strong, he looked over sixty. His worn clothes were foreign. His wife’s faded face showed deep sadness, though she forced smiles when he looked. The little girl, sun-tanned, bore the Italian cast: large black eyes beneath arched brows, native nobility, and simple grace. Passersby were moved; when the stranger noticed attention he looked so savage that bold loungers hurried off.

After standing some time the tall stranger wiped his face as if to clear his thoughts. He drew a dagger from his breast and gave it to his companion, saying in Italian, “I will see if the Bonapartes remember us.” He walked slowly to the palace entrance, where a consular guard stopped him and refused to permit discussion. Seeing his obstinacy, the sentinel presented his bayonet. Fortune favored the stranger: the guard was changing, and the corporal pointed to the post commander.

“Let Bonaparte know that Bartolomeo di Piombo wishes to speak with him,” the Italian said to the captain.

The officer insisted one could not see the First Consul without a written request. Bartolomeo persisted that the soldier must go to Bonaparte. The officer refused, citing regulations. Bartolomeo frowned, casting a terrible look as if charging him with the consequences of refusal. Then he remained under the portico between garden and courtyard. By chance a carriage drew up and Lucien Bonaparte, minister of the interior, emerged.

“Ah, Loucian, it is lucky for me I have met you!” cried the stranger in Corsican patois. Lucien recognized him and, after a word, took him away.

Murat, Lannes, and Rapp were in the First Consul’s cabinet. As Lucien entered with Piombo, conversation ceased. Lucien took Napoleon by the arm and led him to a window recess. Napoleon signaled Murat and Lannes to retire; Rapp pretended not to see and lingered. Bonaparte spoke sharply and Rapp left reluctantly, though he waited near the adjoining salon. The First Consul opened the door suddenly and found his aide-de-camp close to the wall.

“Do you choose not to understand me?” said the First Consul. “I wish to be alone with my compatriot.”

“A Corsican!” replied the aide-de-camp. “I distrust those fellows too much to—”

Bonaparte smiled and pushed him aside. “Well, what has brought you here, my poor Bartolomeo?” he asked.

“To ask asylum and protection from you, if you are a true Corsican,” replied Bartolomeo roughly.

“What ill fortune drove you from the island? You were the richest—”

“I have killed all the Portas,” said the Corsican in a deep voice, frowning.

The First Consul stepped back in surprise.

“Do you mean to betray me?” cried Bartolomeo, with a dark look at Bonaparte. “Do you know that there are still four Piombos in Corsica?”

Lucien seized his compatriot’s arm and shook it. “Did you come here to threaten the savior of France?” he said.

Bonaparte signaled Lucien to be silent, then looked at Piombo. “Why did you kill the Portas?”

“We had been friends,” the man replied. “The Barbantis reconciled us. The day after we drank to end our quarrels I left for Bastia. The Portas stayed in my house and set fire to my vineyard at Longone. They killed my son Gregorio. My daughter Ginevra and my wife had taken the sacrament that morning and escaped; the Virgin protected them. When I returned my house was ashes. I found Gregorio’s body by accident at night and knew the Portas had done it. I went to the woods, called together all the men who had ever served me—do you hear me, Bonaparte?—and we marched to the Portas’ vineyard. We arrived at five; at seven they were all before God. Giacomo says Eliza Vanni saved a child, Luigi. But I myself bound Luigi to his bed before setting fire to the house. I left the island with my wife and child without learning whether Luigi is alive.”

Bonaparte listened with curiosity but not surprise. Lucien asked how many there had been.

“Seven,” replied Piombo. “All of them persecuted you in olden times.”

The brothers showed no hatred. “Ha! you are no longer Corsicans!” cried Piombo in despair. “Farewell. In other days I protected you,” he added reproachfully. “Without me your mother would never have reached Marseille,” he said to Bonaparte, who stood silent and thoughtful, his elbow on a mantel-shelf.

“As a matter of duty, Piombo,” said Napoleon at last, “I cannot take you under my wing. I have become the leader of a great nation; I command the Republic; I am bound to execute the laws.”

“Ha! ha!” said Bartolomeo scornfully.

“But I can shut my eyes,” Bonaparte continued. “The tradition of the vendetta will long prevent law's reign in Corsica,” he added to himself. “But it must be destroyed, at any cost.”

After a pause he spoke more personally. “Live here, in Paris,” he said to Bartolomeo. “We will know nothing of this affair. I will cause your property in Corsica to be bought to give you enough to live on for the present. Later, we will think of you. But remember, no more vendetta! There are no woods here to fly to. If you play with daggers you must expect no mercy. Here, the law protects all citizens; no one is allowed to do justice for himself.”

“He has made himself the head of a singular nation,” said Bartolomeo, taking Lucien’s hand and pressing it. “But you have both recognized me in misfortune, and I am yours henceforth, for life or death. You may dispose as you will of the Piombos.”

At these words his Corsican brow unbent and he looked pleasantly about him. “You are not badly off here,” he said, smiling, “You are all in red, like a cardinal.”

“Your success depends on yourself; you can have a palace too,” Bonaparte replied, watching him keenly. “It will often happen that I shall need some faithful friend in whom I can confide.”

A sigh of joy heaved the Corsican’s chest. He held out his hand to the First Consul. “The Corsican is in you still.”

Bonaparte smiled and looked at the man who seemed to bring a breath from the island that had saved him—the land he would never see again. He signaled to his brother, who then took Piombo away. Lucien asked about the family’s finances. Piombo showed his wife and Ginevra sitting on a heap of stones.

“We came from Fontainebleau on foot; we have not a single penny,” he said.

Lucien gave him his purse and told him to come the next day so arrangements could be made for their comfort. The value of Piombo’s Corsican property, if sold, would scarcely maintain him honorably in Paris.

Fifteen years elapsed between Piombo’s arrival in Paris with his family and the following event, which would be scarcely intelligible without this account of the preceding circumstances.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Servin, one of our distinguished artists, was the first to conceive of opening a studio for young girls who wished to take painting lessons.

About forty, of strictly upright morals and wholly given to his art, he had married for inclination the dowerless daughter of a general. At first the mothers of his pupils brought their daughters themselves; then they were content to send them alone, reassured by the master’s principles and the pains he took to deserve their confidence.

He intended to take only young ladies of rich, well-placed families to avoid complaints about class composition, and even refused girls who wished to become professional artists, because such pupils required instruction beyond what he meant to give. Gradually his prudence, the skill with which he initiated pupils, the certainty each mother felt that her daughter was among well-bred girls, and his marriage gave him an excellent reputation in society. When a young girl wished to learn to draw, friends invariably advised: “Send her to Servin’s.”

Servin became thus a specialty for feminine art; like Herbault for bonnets or Leroy for gowns. A young woman trained by Servin could judge the Musée, make a striking portrait, copy an old master, or paint a genre picture. He sufficed for the educational needs of the aristocracy. Yet, despite relations with the best families in Paris, he remained independent and patriotic, keeping that easy, brilliant, half-ironical tone and freedom of judgment which characterizes painters.

He had carried scrupulous precaution into the arrangements of the locality where his pupils studied. The entrance to the attic above his apartments was walled up; to reach this retreat, as sacred as a harem, one had to go up a small spiral stair within his rooms. The studio, occupying nearly the whole attic under the roof, presented vast proportions that surprised visitors. It was profusely lighted from above through enormous panes with green linen shades arranged to manage the light. Caricatures, heads dashed in color or with a knife on gray-painted walls showed that, barring expression, distinguished young girls have as much fun and folly as men. A small stove with a large zigzagging pipe was the room’s necessary ornament. A shelf ran round the walls with plaster models, most covered with gray dust. Above it hung Niobe, a smiling Venus, a pauper’s outstretched hand; a few écorchés yellowed by smoke looked like limbs snatched from a grave. Pictures, drawings, lay figures, frames without paintings and paintings without frames gave the room that studio physiognomy of jumble and bareness, poverty and riches, care and neglect. The vast receptacle of an atelier, where all seems small, even man, has something of an Opera coulisse; ancient garments, gilded armor, fragments of stuff and machinery lie there. Yet there is something mysteriously grand: genius and death, Diana and Apollo beside a skull, beauty and destruction, colors glowing in shadows — a strange symbol of an artist’s head.

When this history begins, a brilliant July sun lit the studio, two rays tracing diaphanous gold lines where dust shimmered. A dozen easels raised sharp points like masts. Several young girls animated the scene by varied expressions, attitudes, and dresses. Strong shadows cast by the green serge curtains produced piquant light-and-shade effects. This group was the prettiest of all the pictures in the studio.

A fair young girl, simply dressed, sat at some distance, working bravely and seeming in dread of mishap. No one looked at or spoke to her; she was the prettiest, most modest, and seemed the least rich among them. Two principal groups, distinctly separated, showed two cliques, two minds even where rank and fortune might seem forgotten.

These young girls, amid color-boxes, brushes, dazzling palettes — painting, laughing, talking, singing — made a spectacle unknown to men. One, proud and capricious with black hair and fine hands, cast her glance here and there; another, light-hearted with chestnut hair, smiled and seemed thoughtless; a third dreamy and melancholy bent her head like a drooping flower; her neighbor, tall and indolent with long Asiatic eyes, said little and reflected. Another, like the jocoso of a Spanish play, full of wit and epigram, watched the rest, making them laugh and tossing up her head, and seemed to rule the first group — daughters of bankers, notaries and merchants — all rich, but aware of the cutting slights the aristocratic group put upon them.

The latter were led by Mademoiselle Amélie Thirion, daughter of one of the King’s ushers, a creature as silly as she was vain, proud of her father’s office at court. She pretended to understand the master at once, seemed to do her work as a favor, used an eyeglass, came very dressed and always late, and entreated companions to speak low. In this second group were several girls with exquisite figures and distinguished features but little simplicity or candor: elegant attitudes, graceful movements, faces lacking frankness; they belonged to a world where manners form character early and social pleasures destroy sentiment and develop egotism.

Yet when the whole class assembled childlike heads were seen among this bevy, ravishingly pure and virgin, faces with half-open lips and a flickering virgin smile. The studio then resembled not a mere atelier, but a group of angels on a cloud.

By mid-day on this occasion, Servin had not appeared. For days he had spent most of his time in another studio, giving the last touches to a picture for the Exposition. All at once Mademoiselle Amélie Thirion began to speak low and earnestly to her neighbor. A great silence fell on the patrician group, and the commercial party, curious, fell equally silent. Amélie rose, took an easel near hers, carried it away from the noble group and placed it close to a board partition which separated the studio from the attic’s extreme end, where broken casts, defaced canvases and winter wood were kept. She rolled aside the stool, the box of colors, and even the Prudhon picture her absent pupil was copying. After this coup d’état the Right began to work in silence, but the Left discoursed at length.

“What will Mademoiselle Piombo say to that?” asked a girl.

“She’s not one to say anything,” replied Matilde Roguin, the lively oracle of the banking group; “but fifty years hence she’ll remember the insult as if done last night, and revenge it. She is a person I don’t want to be at war with.”

“The slight these young ladies mean to put upon her is the more unkind,” said another, “because yesterday Mademoiselle Ginevra was very sad. Her father, they say, has just resigned. They ought not to add to her trouble.” Others suggested jealousy rather than pure party spite.

Matilde thought to take Mademoiselle Piombo’s easel and place it next to hers, rose, but sat down again. With a character like Ginevra’s, one couldn’t tell how civility would be taken; better wait events.

Footsteps on the narrow stairway were heard. “Here she comes!” passed from mouth to mouth, then absolute silence.

To understand Amélie’s ostracism, add that this scene took place toward the end of July, 1815. The second return of the Bourbons had shaken friendships that had held under the first Restoration. Families, almost all divided in opinion, renewed many deplorable scenes. Children, young girls, old men shared the monarchical fever. Discord glided beneath roofs; distrust dyed words and actions of the most intimate friends.

Ginevra Piombo loved Napoleon to idolatry; how could she conceal it? The emperor was her compatriot and the benefactor of her father. Baron di Piombo had been among those devoted servants who helped most in the return from Elba. Incapable of denying his political faith and anxious to confess it, the old baron remained in Paris among enemies. Ginevra made no secret of the grief the second Restoration caused her family. The only tears she had so far shed in life were drawn from her by news of Napoleon’s captivity on the Bellerophon and Labédoyère’s arrest.

The studio’s aristocratic girls belonged to the most devoted royalist families in Paris. The hatred of Bonapartists then prevalent made Amélie’s act a natural expression of prevailing sentiments. Ginevra, one of Servin’s first pupils, had occupied the place now taken from her since her first day. To drive her from a spot that in some sense belonged to her was not only to insult her but to inflict an artistic pain: all artists cherish a predilect spot where they work.

Nevertheless, political prejudice was not the chief influence on the Right clique. Ginevra, much the ablest of Servin’s pupils, was an object of intense jealousy. The master admired her talents and character; she concluded most comparisons. Without any one able to explain the ascendancy she exercised, she had a prestige almost like Bonaparte’s over his soldiers.

The aristocracy of the studio had resolved upon the fall of this queen, but none had yet openly avoided the Bonapartist. Mademoiselle Thirion’s act was a decisive stroke intended to force others to become accomplices in her hatred. Though loved by several royalists, most were indoctrinated at home and preferred to keep aloof.

On Ginevra’s arrival she was received in profound silence. Of all the young women who had come to Servin’s studio, she was the handsomest, tallest and best made. Her carriage and demeanor had a nobility and grace commanding respect. Her face, instinct with intelligence, seemed to radiate light, inspired by Corsican enthusiasm without losing calmness. Long hair, black eyes and lashes expressed passion; the corners of her mouth, softly defined, and lips a trifle too marked gave signs of the kindliness strong beings derive from conscious strength. A marble forehead, almost savage in pride, hinted at Corsican moral instincts. All the rest of her person, simple and gracefully Lombard, was so seductive that it was difficult to harm her. She had refused marriage out of love for her parents, feeling necessary to their comfort. Painting took the place of passions and interests that usually absorb her sex.

“You are very silent to-day, mesdemoiselles,” she said after advancing among her companions. “Good-morning, my little Laure,” she added, approaching the young girl painting apart. “That head is strong,—the flesh tints a little too rosy, but the drawing is excellent.”

Laure looked tenderly at Ginevra; their faces showed mutual affection. The Italian smiled faintly, seemed thoughtful, and walked to her easel, greeting the first group without noticing the curious silence. She passed before the patricians without a word and sat, opened her color-box, took up her brushes, arranged her apron and inspected her palette, apparently absorbed. All heads in the banking group turned toward her. The Thirion camp’s sidelong glances were none the less directed at Ginevra.

“She hasn’t noticed it!” said Mademoiselle Roguin.

Then Ginevra abandoned meditation and glanced toward the aristocrats. She measured the distance separating her from them but said nothing. Perhaps she truly had not noticed their move; perhaps she had other preoccupations. She rose abruptly, walked along the partition separating the adjoining closet, examined the sash through which her light came, mounted a chair to raise the green serge higher, and, through a narrow opening, cast a glance that compared to a miser discovering Aladdin’s treasure. She sprang down, returned to her place, pretended dissatisfaction with the light, pushed a table close to the partition, placed a chair on it, climbed lightly to the summit and again looked through the crevice. She saw something that made her totter.

“Take care, Mademoiselle Ginevra, you’ll fall!” cried Laure.

Fear of being seen gave Ginevra courage; she recovered and replied while balancing on the shaking chair: “Pooh! it is more solid than a throne!”

She then secured the curtain, came down, pushed the chair and table as far as possible from the partition, returned to her easel, and seemed to arrange it to suit the light. Her picture, however, was not in her mind; she was wholly bent on getting near the closet door where she finally settled. Sitting there she could distinctly hear a sound that had excited her curiosity the evening before and had whirled her imagination across vast fields of conjecture. She recognized the firm, regular breathing of a man she had just seen asleep. Her curiosity was satisfied beyond expectation, but she felt saddled with an immense responsibility. Through the opening she had seen the Imperial eagle; upon the flock bed, faintly lighted from above, lay the form of an officer of the Guard. She guessed all. Servin was hiding a proscribed man!

She trembled lest any companion come near and, by the regular breath or some deeper one, betray the political victim. She resolved to keep her place by that door, trusting her wits to baffle danger.

“Better that I should be here,” she thought, “to prevent some luckless accident, than leave that poor man at the mercy of a heedless betrayal.”

This secret explained her apparent indifference to the move of her easel: the change enabled her to gratify curiosity naturally; besides, she was too preoccupied to perceive the reason for her removal.

Nothing is more mortifying to young girls than to see mischief or insult fail through the intended victim’s indifference. Hatred seems to grow as the enemy is raised above us. Ginevra’s behavior was an enigma to all; friends and enemies were equally surprised; her friends claimed every virtue for her except forgiveness of injuries. Yet Mademoiselle Roguin, interpreting Ginevra’s silence as grandeur, rallied the banking circle to humiliate the aristocrats by a fire of sarcasm which humbled the Right coterie.

Madame Servin’s arrival put a stop to the struggle. Amélie Thirion had noticed the extreme preoccupation of Ginevra, which prevented her from hearing the bitterly polite war of words. The vengeance inflicted by the banking circle drove the ultras to search for the cause of the silence. Ginevra became the center of all glances, watched by friends and foes alike.

It is difficult to hide even a slight emotion from fifteen inquisitive, idle girls whose wits and mischief ask for secrets to guess. Madame Servin, entering, turned her eyes to the door near which Ginevra sat. Under present circumstances this glance was noticed; later Amélie remembered it and read in it fear and mystery.

“Mesdemoiselles,” Madame Servin said, “Monsieur Servin cannot come to-day.”

She then went round complimenting each girl, receiving those feminine caresses given as much by tone and looks as by gestures. She reached Ginevra with uneasiness she tried to disguise. They nodded in a friendly way but said nothing; one painted, the other stood looking at the painting. The soldier’s breathing could be distinctly heard, but Madame Servin appeared not to notice it; her feigned ignorance was so obvious that Ginevra recognized wilful deafness. The unknown man turned on his pallet.

The Italian then looked fixedly at Madame Servin, who said without change of face: “Your copy is as fine as the original; if I had to choose between the two I should be puzzled.”

“Monsieur Servin has not taken his wife into his confidence as to this mystery,” thought Ginevra, who, after replying with a gentle smile of incredulity, began to hum a Corsican canzonetta to cover the noise made by the prisoner. It was unusual to hear her sing in the studio; all the other girls looked up in surprise. Later this circumstance served as proof to charitable suppositions of jealousy.

Madame Servin soon went away, and the session ended without further events; Ginevra allowed her companions to depart and seemed to intend to work later. Unconsciously she betrayed a desire to be left alone by impatient, ill-disguised glances at pupils slow in leaving. Mademoiselle Thirion, cruel to the girl who excelled her, guessed her rival’s industry hid some purpose. By dint of watching she was struck by the attentive air with which Ginevra seemed to be listening to sounds no one else had heard. The expression of impatience she detected was like a flash of light.

Amélie was the last to leave; she went down to Madame Servin’s apartment and spoke for a moment, then pretended to have left her bag, ran softly back to the studio, and found Ginevra once more mounted on her frail scaffolding, absorbed and not hearing the slight noise of her companion’s footsteps. Amélie stepped as if on eggs, withdrew outside the door and coughed. Ginevra quivered, turned, saw her enemy, blushed, hastened to alter the shade to give meaning to her position and came down leisurely. She left the studio, bearing in memory the image of a man’s head as beauteous as Girodet’s Endymion, a masterpiece she had lately copied.

“To banish so young a man! Who can he be? for he is not Marshal Ney—”

These two sentences expressed the many ideas that turned over in Ginevra’s mind for two days. On the third day, despite haste to be first at the studio, she found Mademoiselle Thirion already there, having come in a carriage.

Ginevra and her enemy observed each other for a long time. Amélie had seen the handsome head of the mysterious man, but the Imperial eagles and uniform were hidden by the crevice; she was lost in conjectures. Suddenly Servin came in, much earlier than usual.

“Mademoiselle Ginevra,” he said after glancing round, “why have you placed yourself there? The light is bad. Come nearer to the rest and pull down that curtain a little.”

Then he sat down near Laure, whose work earned his cordial attention.

“Well, well!” he cried; “here is a head extremely well done. You’ll be another Ginevra.”

The master went from easel to easel, scolding, flattering, jesting, his jests more dreaded than his reprimands. Ginevra had not obeyed his order but remained at her post, resolved not to quit it. She took a sheet of paper and sketched in sepia the head of the hidden man. A work done under emotion has its own stamp: passion often takes the place of genius. Under her circumstances the intuition lent her a supernatural touch. The head was dashed on the paper amid an awkward tremble she mistook for fear. From time to time she glanced furtively at companions to hide the sketch if any came near. But there was a moment when she did not see Amélie’s eyeglass turned full upon the drawing from a great portfolio. Mademoiselle Thirion, recognizing the portrait of the mysterious man, showed herself abruptly, and Ginevra hastily covered the sheet.

“Why do you stay there in spite of my advice, mademoiselle?” asked the professor gravely.

The pupil turned her easel so only the master could see the sketch, which she placed upon it, and said in an agitated voice: “Do you not think, as I do, that the light is very good? Had I not better remain here?”

Servin turned pale. As nothing escapes malice’s piercing eyes, Mademoiselle Thirion became as it were a sharer in the sudden emotion of master and pupil.

“You are right,” said Servin; “but really,” he added with a forced laugh, “you will soon know more than I do.”

A pause followed while the professor studied the officer’s head drawing.

“It is a masterpiece! worthy of Salvator Rosa!” he exclaimed with the energy of an artist.

All the pupils rose, and Mademoiselle Thirion darted forward with the velocity of a tiger. At that instant the prisoner, awakened perhaps by the noise, began to move. Ginevra knocked over her stool, said a few incoherent sentences, and began to laugh; she had thrown the portrait into her portfolio before Amélie could reach her. The easel was now surrounded; Servin descanted on the beauty of the copy his favorite was making, and the whole class was duped by this stratagem, except Amélie, who, slipping behind companions, attempted to open the portfolio where she had seen Ginevra throw the sketch. The latter took it up without a word and placed it before her. The two girls then looked at each other fixedly, in silence.

“Come, mesdemoiselles, take your places,” said Servin. “If you wish to do as well as Mademoiselle di Piombo, you mustn’t be always talking fashions and balls, trifling your time away.”

When they were reseated, Servin sat down beside Ginevra.

“Was it not better that I should be the one to discover the mystery rather than the others?” asked the girl in a low voice.

“Yes,” replied the painter, “you are one of us, a patriot; but even if you were not, I should have confided the matter to you.”

Master and pupil understood one another, and Ginevra no longer feared to ask: “Who is he?”

“An intimate friend of Labédoyère, who contributed more than any other man, except the unfortunate colonel, to unite the 7th regiment with the grenadiers of Elba. He was a major in the Imperial Guard and was at Waterloo.”

“Why not have burned his uniform and shako and given him citizen’s clothes?” asked Ginevra impatiently.

“He will have them to-night.”

“You ought to have closed the studio for some days.”

“He is going away.”

“Then they’ll kill him,” said the girl. “Let him stay here with you till the storm is over. Paris is still where a man can be hidden safely. Is he a friend of yours?” she asked.

“No; he has no claim on me but ill-luck. He came into my hands by my father-in-law, who returned to the army during the campaign and very cleverly rescued him from those who captured Labédoyère. He came here to defend the general, a foolish fellow!”

“Do you call him that!” cried Ginevra, astonished.

“My father-in-law is too closely watched to keep him in his own house,” Servin resumed. “So he brought him to me by night about a week ago. I hoped to keep him out of sight in this corner, the only spot in the house where he could be safe.”

“If I can be useful, employ me,” said Ginevra. “I know the Maréchal de Fétre.”

“Well, we’ll see,” replied the painter.

This conversation lasted too long to escape the other girls. Servin left Ginevra, went round once more to each easel, and gave such long lessons that he was still there at the hour pupils usually left.

“You are forgetting your bag, Mademoiselle Thirion,” said the professor, running after the girl now condescending to spy to satisfy jealousy.

The baffled pupil returned for the bag, feigning surprise at her carelessness; but Servin’s act was fresh proof to her of a mystery’s existence and importance. She now noisily ran down the staircase and slammed the door into the Servins’ apartment to give the impression she had gone; then she softly returned and stationed herself outside the studio door.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
When the painter and Ginevra believed themselves alone, Servin rapped on the garret door, which opened on its rusty hinges. Ginevra saw a tall, well-made young man in an Imperial uniform; one arm was in a sling and his pallor betrayed suffering. He recoiled at the sight of an unknown woman.

Amelie, unable to remain and unwilling to look through the closed door, slipped away after hearing the garret open.

“Fear nothing,” said the painter to the officer. “Mademoiselle is the daughter of a faithful friend of the Emperor, the Baron di Piombo.”

At once the soldier accepted Ginevra’s patriotism.

“You are wounded,” she said.

“Oh! it is nothing, mademoiselle,” he replied; “the wound is healing.”

From the street came the vendors’ cry: “Condemned to death!” They all shuddered, and the soldier heard a name that blanched him.

“Labedoyere!” he cried, collapsing into a stool.

They sat in heavy silence. Drops stood on the young man’s livid forehead; he seized his hair in despair and rested his elbow on Ginevra’s easel.

“After all,” he said, rising abruptly, “Labedoyere and I knew what we were doing. We were certain of the fate awaiting us, whether triumph or defeat. He dies for the Cause, and here am I hiding myself!”

He rushed to the studio door; Ginevra reached it first and barred him.

“Can you restore the Emperor?” she said. “Do you expect to raise that giant who could not maintain himself?”

“But what can I do?” he asked the two friends fate had sent him. “I have no relation in the world. Labedoyere was my protector and friend; without him I am alone. Tomorrow I myself may be condemned; my only fortune was my pay. I spent my last penny to come here and try to snatch Labedoyere from his fate; death is, therefore, a necessity for me. When a man decides to die he ought to know how to sell his life to the executioner. I was thinking just now that the life of an honest man is worth that of two traitors, and the blow of a dagger well placed may give immortality.”

This outburst alarmed Servin and even Ginevra, who understood the young man’s nature. She admired his handsome face and sweet voice, which kept fervor even in anger. She tried to soothe him.

“Monsieur,” she said, “as for your pecuniary distress, permit me to offer my savings. My father is rich; I am his only child; he loves me, and I am sure he will never blame me. Have no scruple in accepting my offer; our property comes from the Emperor; we do not own a penny that is not the result of his munificence. Is it not gratitude to assist his faithful soldiers? Take what you need as indifferently as I offer it. It is only money!” she added with contempt. “Now, as for friends—those you shall have.”

She raised her head proudly; her eyes shone.

“The head which falls tomorrow before a dozen muskets will save yours,” she went on. “Wait till the storm is over; you can then escape and take service in foreign countries if you are not forgotten here; or in the French army, if you are.”

There is always a particular delicacy in consolation from a woman: maternal, foreseeing, complete. When words of hope are spoken with graceful gesture by a beautiful benefactress, a young man rarely resists. The prisoner breathed in love through all his senses. A rosy tinge warmed his white cheeks; his eyes lost some of their dulled sadness.

“You are an angel of goodness—But Labedoyere!” he added. “Oh, Labedoyere!”

At that cry they looked at one another in silence, each understanding the others’ thought. They were no longer mere acquaintances: the links of friendship knit them as if for years.

“Dear friend,” said Servin, “can you save him?”

“I can avenge him.”

Ginevra quivered. Though the stranger was handsome, what most touched her was pity for suffering that was not ignoble; but a cry of vengeance, a proscribed soul devoted to Napoleon, Corsican generosity—this stirred her deeply. She looked at the officer with respectful emotion that shook his heart. For the first time a man had produced a keen sensation in her. At once her sympathy deepened into something more: she put the stranger’s soul on a par with his noble beauty and the proportions she admired as an artist. Curiosity turned to pity, pity to powerful interest; she felt profound sensations that made her uneasy.

“Until tomorrow, then,” she said, giving him a gentle parting smile.

Seeing that smile illuminated her features anew; the stranger forgot all else for a moment.

“Tomorrow,” he said sadly; “but tomorrow, Labedoyere—”

Ginevra put a finger to her lips and looked at him, as if saying: “Be calm, be prudent.”

The young man cried in his own language: “Ah! Dio! che non vorrei vivere dopo averla veduta?—who would not wish to live after seeing her?”

The peculiar accent made Ginevra quiver.

“Are you Corsican?” she cried, returning toward him with a beating heart.

“I was born in Corsica,” he replied; “but I was brought, while very young, to Genoa, and as soon as old enough for military service I enlisted.”

The young man’s beauty, his attachment to the Emperor, his wound and misfortunes—all blended into a new, delightful sentiment in Ginevra. This persecuted man was a child of Corsica; he spoke its cherished language. She stood motionless, held by a magical sensation; before her eyes he was a living portrait, each feeling lending vivid color. At Servin’s invitation the officer sat on a divan; the painter, after removing the sling, undid the bandages to dress the wound. Ginevra shuddered at the long, broad sabre gash across his forearm and a moan escaped her. The stranger raised his head and smiled at her. Servin lifted the lint and touched the lacerated flesh with delicate care; the wounded man’s pale face expressed, as he looked at the girl, more pleasure than suffering. An artist might have admired the contrast of white linen and bared arm against the red and blue uniform.

A soft half-light pervaded the studio; a parting evening ray lit where the soldier sat, bathing his blanched face, black hair, and clothes. To Ginevra’s Italian imagination it was an omen. He seemed a celestial messenger, speaking her language and unconsciously conjuring childhood memories; in her heart dawned a feeling as fresh and pure as innocence. For a short moment she was motionless and dreamy, then blushed, exchanged a soft glance with the wounded man, and fled with his image before her eyes.

The next day, though not a class day, Ginevra came to the studio and the prisoner sat beside her easel. Servin, with a sketch to finish, played mentor as the two talked chiefly in Corsican. The soldier related the sufferings of the retreat from Moscow; at nineteen he had made the passage of the Beresins, and was almost the last of his regiment. He described with fiery words the disaster of Waterloo. His voice was music to the Italian girl. Brought up as a Corsican, Ginevra was, in some sense, a child of Nature; falseness was unknown to her; she gave herself up without reserve to her impressions, without the coquetry of Parisian girls. That day she often sat with palette and brushes in her hands without touching a color, eyes fixed on the officer, lips slightly parted, listening as if painting a stroke never painted. She was not surprised at the softness in his eyes; she felt her own despite a resolve to remain stern. After pauses she painted diligently without raising her head, because he sat near and watched. The first time he sat by her in silence she said after a long pause:

“Does it amuse you to see me paint?”

That day she learned his name was Luigi. They agreed that on class days, when they could not see each other, any important political event would be signaled by Ginevra singing certain Corsican melodies.

The following day Mademoiselle Thirion informed the class, under pledge of secrecy, that Ginevra di Piombo had a lover, a young man who came during lesson hours and concealed himself in the garret.

“You, who take her part,” she said to Mademoiselle Roguin, “watch her carefully, and you will see how she spends her time.”

Ginevra was observed with malicious attention. They listened to her songs and watched her glances. At times, when she supposed herself unseen, a dozen pairs of eyes were furtively upon her. Enlightened thus, the girls interpreted truly the emotions crossing the beautiful Italian’s features—her gestures, the tones in which she hummed, and the attention with which she listened to sounds only she could hear through the partition.

By the end of a week, Laure was the only one of Servin’s fifteen pupils who had resisted peeking at Luigi through the crevice; she still defended her friend. Mademoiselle Roguin tried to make her wait on the staircase after class to prove the intimacy by entering the studio and surprising them, but Laure refused such espionage, and thus became the object of reprobation.

Soon Mademoiselle Thirion declared it improper to attend classes with a painter whose opinions were tainted with patriotism and Bonapartism (then synonymous), and she ceased to attend. Though she forgot Ginevra, the harm she planted bore fruit. Little by little the other young girls told their mothers of the strange events at the studio. One day Matilde Roguin did not come; the next another was missing, and so on, until the last three or four left. Ginevra and Laure were the sole occupants of the deserted studio for several days.

Ginevra did not notice the falling off nor ask the cause. As soon as she had invented means of communicating with Luigi she lived in the studio in a delightful solitude, thinking only of the officer and the dangers that threatened him. Though a sincere admirer of noble characters who never betray their political faiths, she urged Luigi to recognize royal authority to be released and remain in France. He refused. If passions are born and nourished under romantic causes, never were so many such circumstances conjoined to unite two young souls. Their friendship advanced in a month more than a society friendship would in ten years. Adversity tested and revealed character. Ginevra studied Luigi and soon esteemed him. Older than Luigi, she found charm in being courted by a youth so grand, so tried by fate—a youth who combined manly experience with adolescent graces. Luigi felt unspeakable pleasure in being apparently protected by a woman of twenty-five. Was it not proof of love? The union of gentleness and pride, strength and tenderness in Ginevra was irresistible, and he was subjugated. In short, they loved each other so profoundly there was no need to deny or proclaim it.

One evening Ginevra heard the accustomed signal. Luigi scratched with a pin on the woodwork, making no more noise than a spider fastening its thread. The signal meant he wished to come out.

Ginevra glanced around, and not seeing Laure, opened the door; but Luigi caught sight of the pupil and abruptly retired. Surprised, Ginevra saw Laure and said as she went to her easel:

“You are staying late, my dear. That head seems finished; you only want a highlight—see! on that knot of hair.”

“You would do me a great kindness,” said Laure in a trembling voice, “if you would give this copy a few touches; then I could carry away something to remind me of you.”

“Willingly,” said Ginevra, painting a few strokes. “But I thought it was a long way from your home, and it is late.”

“Oh! Ginevra, I am going away, never to return,” cried the poor girl sadly.

“You mean to leave Monsieur Servin!” exclaimed Ginevra, less affected than she might have been a month earlier.

“Haven’t you noticed, Ginevra, that for some days past you and I have been alone in the studio?”

“True,” said Ginevra, as if struck by recollection. “Are all those young ladies ill, or going to be married, or are their fathers on duty at court?”

“They have left Monsieur Servin,” replied Laure.

“Why?”

“On your account, Ginevra.”

“My account!” repeated the Corsican, springing up, brow threatening and eyes flashing.

“Oh! don’t be angry, my kind Ginevra,” cried Laure in distress. “My mother insists on my leaving. The young ladies say you have some intrigue, and that Monsieur Servin allows the young man you love to stay in the dark attic. I never believed these calumnies nor told my mother. But last night Madame Roguin met her at a ball and asked if she still sent me here. When my mother answered yes, Madame Roguin told her the falsehoods. Mamma scolded me severely; she said I must have known all, and that I failed in proper confidence between mother and daughter. Oh! my dear Ginevra! I, who took you for my model, how grieved I am that I can’t be your companion.”

“We shall meet again in life; girls marry—” said Ginevra.

“When they are rich,” sighed Laure.

“Come and see me; my father has a fortune—”

“Ginevra,” Laure added tenderly, “Madame Roguin and my mother are coming to see Monsieur Servin tomorrow to reproach him; hadn’t you better warn him?”

A thunderbolt could not have surprised Ginevra more.

“What matter is it to them?” she asked naively.

“Everybody thinks it very wrong. Mamma says it is immoral.”

“And you, Laure, what do you say?”

Laure looked up and their thoughts joined. She could no longer hold back tears; she flung herself on Ginevra’s breast and sobbed. At this moment Servin entered.

“Mademoiselle Ginevra,” he cried with enthusiasm, “I have finished my picture! It is now being varnished. What have you been doing meanwhile? Where are the young ladies; are they on holiday or in the country?”

Laure dried her tears, bowed to Monsieur Servin, and went away.

“The studio has been deserted for some days,” replied Ginevra, “and the young ladies are not coming back.”

“Pooh!” said the artist.

“Oh! don’t laugh,” said Ginevra. “Listen: I am the involuntary cause of the loss of your reputation—”

The artist smiled and interrupted:

“My reputation? Why, in a few days my picture will make it at the Exposition.”

“That relates to your talent,” replied the girl. “I speak of your morality. Those young ladies have told their mothers that Luigi was shut up here, and that you lent yourself— to—our love.”

“There is some truth in that, mademoiselle,” replied the professor. “The mothers of those young ladies are foolish; had they come to me I should have explained. But I don’t care a straw! Life is short.”

And the painter snapped his fingers above his head. Luigi, who had heard part of the conversation, came in.

“You have lost all your scholars,” he cried. “I have ruined you!”

The artist took Luigi’s hand and Ginevra’s and joined them.

“Marry one another, my children,” he said with fatherly kindness.

They dropped their eyes; their silence was the first avowal of love.

“You will surely be happy,” said Servin. “There is nothing to equal the happiness of two beings like yourselves when bound together in love.”

Luigi pressed his protector’s hand and presently, with emotion, said:

“To you I owe it all.”

“Be happy! I bless and wed you,” said the painter with comic unction, laying his hands on the lovers’ heads.

This jest ended the strained feeling. All three looked at one another and laughed. Ginevra pressed Luigi’s hand with the simple ardor of her native habits.

“Ah ca, my dear children,” resumed Servin, “you think all will go right now, but you are much mistaken.”

The lovers looked astonished.

“Don’t be anxious. I’m the only one your romance will harm. But Madame Servin is a little straitlaced; I don’t see how to settle it with her.”

“Heavens! and I forgot to tell you,” exclaimed Ginevra, “that Madame Roguin and Laure’s mother are coming here tomorrow to—”

“I understand,” said the painter.

“But you can easily justify yourself,” continued the girl, proudly. “Monsieur Luigi,” she added to him with an arch look, “will no longer object to entering the royal service. Well then,” after his smile, “tomorrow morning I will send a petition to a most influential man at the War ministry—a man who will refuse nothing to the daughter of the Baron di Piombo. We shall obtain a ‘tacit’ pardon for Captain Luigi, for, of course, they will not allow him the rank of major. And then,” she added to Servin, “you can confound the mothers of my charitable companions by telling them the truth.”

“You are an angel!” cried Servin.

While this passed at the studio, Ginevra’s parents grew anxious at her delay.

“It is six o’clock, and Ginevra not yet home!” cried Bartolomeo.

“She was never so late before,” said his wife.

The two old people looked at one another with unusual anxiety. Too restless to remain, Bartolomeo paced the salon with an active step for a man over seventy-seven. Thanks to a robust constitution he had changed little since arriving in Paris; despite his tall figure he walked erect. His white sparse hair left uncovered a broad protuberant skull suggesting character and firmness. His face, seamed with deep wrinkles, had taken with age a nobler expression, preserving pallid tones that inspire veneration. The ardor of his passions lived in the fire of his eyes; his still-not-wholly-white eyebrows retained mobility. His head’s aspect was stern but conveyed that Piombo had a right to be so. His kindness and gentleness were known only to his wife and daughter. In functions, or before strangers, he never laid aside the majesty time had impressed; the habit of frowning with heavy eyebrows and giving his eyes a Napoleonic fixity made his manner icy.

During his political life he had been so generally feared he seemed unsocial. The life, morals, and fidelity of Piombo made him obnoxious to courtiers. Despite delicate missions entrusted to him which would have been lucrative to others, he possessed an income of not more than thirty thousand francs from an investment in the Grand Livre. Considering cheap government securities under the Empire and Napoleon’s liberality toward faithful servants, the Baron di Piombo must have been a man of stern integrity. He owed his barony to the Emperor’s need to give him a title before sending him on missions to foreign courts.

Bartolomeo always professed hatred for the traitors Napoleon surrounded himself with. It is told that he took three steps to the door of the Emperor’s cabinet after advising him to get rid of three men in France on the eve of the 1814 campaign. After the second Bourbon return Bartolomeo ceased wearing the Legion of Honor. No man better represented those old Republicans, incorruptible friends to the Empire, relics of two energetic governments. Though he displeased courtiers, he had Darus, Drouots, and Carnots as friends. As for other politicians, he cared not a whiff of his cigar’s smoke for them, especially since Waterloo.

Bartolomeo had bought, for the moderate sum Madame Mère had paid him for his Corsican estates, the old Portenduere mansion, in which he made no changes. Lodged usually at government expense, he did not occupy it until after Fontainebleau. Following simple habits, the baron and his wife gave no heed to outward splendor; their furniture was what they bought with the mansion. The grand apartments, lofty, sombre, and bare, the wide mirrors in blackened gilded frames, and Louis XIV–period furniture suited Bartolomeo and his wife, personages worthy of antiquity.

Under the Empire and during the Hundred Days, while exercising well-rewarded functions, the old Corsican maintained a great establishment, more to honor his office than to shine himself. His and his wife’s lives were so frugal and tranquil that their modest fortune sufficed. To them their daughter Ginevra was more precious than all wealth. When in May, 1814, the Baron di Piombo resigned, dismissed his servants, and closed his stable, Ginevra, quiet and unpretending like her parents, saw nothing to regret. Like all great souls, she found luxury in strength of feeling and happiness in quiet and work. These three loved one another too well for externals to matter.

Often, especially after Napoleon’s second fall, Bartolomeo and his wife passed delightful evenings with their daughter, listening while she sang and played. They took vast secret pleasure in her slightest word; their eyes followed her with tender anxiety; they heard her step in the court-yard. Like lovers, the three often sat silently together, understanding better than by speech the eloquence of their souls. This profound sentiment animated their every thought. If memories of Napoleon’s benefits or public events distracted them, they could always return to this source without affecting their community of thought, for Ginevra shared their political passions. What more natural than the ardor with which they found refuge in their only child?

Until now public life had absorbed Bartolomeo’s energy; after leaving office he felt the need to cast it into the last sentiment remaining. Apart from parentage, there may have been another reason for their intense reciprocal love: it was undivided love. Ginevra’s whole heart belonged to her father, as his belonged to her; and if we are bound by defects more than virtues, Ginevra echoed her father’s youthful passions. This was the sole imperfection of their triple life. Ginevra was born unyielding, vindictive, and passionate, like her father.

The Corsican delighted in developing these savage sentiments in his daughter, as a lion teaches lion-cubs to spring at prey. But this apprenticeship to vengeance having no outlet in family life, the child turned the principle against her father; as a child she forgave him nothing, and he yielded. Piombo saw nothing more than childish nonsense in these quarrels, but Ginevra acquired a habit of ruling her parents. Amid the tempests he loved to excite, a look or word of tenderness sufficed to pacify them, and often they were nearest a kiss when threatening vehemently.

Nevertheless, for five years Ginevra, grown wiser, avoided such scenes. Her faithfulness, devotion, love filling her every thought, and admirable good sense overcame her temper. Yet a great evil resulted from her training; she lived with her parents on an equality always dangerous.

Piombo and his wife, without education, allowed Ginevra to study as she pleased. Following youthful caprices she studied many things for a time, abandoning each train of thought until painting proved her dominant passion. Ginevra would have made a noble woman had her mother guided her studies and harmonized her gifts; her defects came from the fatal education the old Corsican delighted in giving her.

After pacing and ringing the bell, a servant entered.

“Go and meet Mademoiselle Ginevra,” said his master.

“I always regret our carriage on her account,” remarked the baroness.

“She said she did not want one,” replied Piombo, looking at his wife, who, used to obedience, lowered her eyes and said no more.

Already a septuagenarian, tall, withered, pale, and wrinkled, the baroness resembled those old women Schnetz paints in Italian genre scenes. She was habitually silent, sometimes seeming another Mrs. Shandy; occasionally a word or look betrayed that her feelings retained youthful vigor. Her dress, devoid of coquetry, was often in bad taste. She usually sat passive, buried in a low sofa like a Sultana Valide, awaiting or admiring her Ginevra. All was well with her if Ginevra was happy. Her hair was white, and a few strands only showed above her wrinkled forehead and hollow cheeks.

“It is now fifteen days,” she said, “since Ginevra made a practice of being late.”

“Jean is so slow!” cried the impatient old man, buttoning his blue coat and seizing his hat, which he dashed on as he took his cane and departed.

“You will not get far,” said his wife, calling after him.

As she spoke the porte-cochere opened and shut, and the old mother heard Ginevra’s steps in the court-yard. Bartolomeo almost instantly reappeared, carrying his daughter, who struggled in his arms.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“Here she is, my Ginevra, Ginevrettina, Ginevrola, mia Ginevra bella!” cried the old man.

“Oh, father, you hurt me!”

Ginevra was at once set down respectfully. She nodded to her mother, who had been frightened by the cry, as if to say, “Don’t be alarmed; it was only a trick to get away.” The pale baroness regained color; Piombo rubbed his hands violently—his token of joy; when his face relaxed every wrinkle became kindly. The two old people looked like a drooping plant refreshed by a little water.

“Now, to dinner! to dinner!” cried the baron, offering his large hand to his daughter, whom he called “Signora Piombellina.” Ginevra smiled.

“Ah ca!” said Piombo as they left the table. “Your mother tells me you have stayed at the studio much longer than usual. It seems painting is more to you than your parents—”

“Oh, father!”

“Ginevra is preparing some surprise for us, I think,” said the mother.

“A picture of your own! will you bring us that?” cried the Corsican, clapping his hands.

“Yes, I am very much occupied at the studio,” replied Ginevra slowly.

“What is the matter, Ginevra? You are turning pale!” cried her mother.

“No!” exclaimed the girl with resolution, “no! it shall never be said that Ginevra Piombo acted a lie.”

Piombo and his wife looked at her in astonishment.

“I love a young man,” she added, her voice full of feeling, lowering her eyelids as if to veil the fire of her eyes.

“Is he a prince?” asked her father ironically.

“No, father,” she said gently, “he is a young man without fortune.”

“Is he very handsome?”

“He is very unfortunate.”

“What is he?”

“Labedoyere’s comrade; he was proscribed, without refuge; Servin concealed him, and—”

“Servin is a good fellow,” cried Piombo, “but you, my daughter, you do wrong to love any man except your father.”

“It does not depend on me to love, or not to love,” replied Ginevra.

“I flattered myself,” continued her father, “that my Ginevra would be faithful to me until I died; and that my love and that of her mother would suffice her till then; I did not expect our tenderness would find a rival in her soul, and—”
